glossary.def: A major global economic downturn that severely impacted the retirement savings and pension plans of many workers, forcing numerous older adults to delay retirement due to insufficient financial resources.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The conventional standard for full-time employment in the United States, consisting of 40 hours of work per week. In reality, only 4 in 10 U.S. workers actually work this schedule. The average work week for many Americans is approximately 47 hours, and 39% work 50 or more hours per week.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A blood test used to diagnose and monitor diabetes by measuring the average blood glucose levels over the past 3 months. A normal A1C is under 5.7; 5.7–6.4 indicates prediabetes; 6.5 or higher indicates diabetes. An A1C of 5.0 corresponds to approximately 97 mg/dL, and a 6.0 corresponds to approximately 126 mg/dL.

The American Association of Retired Persons, a nonprofit organization advocating for adults over 50. An AARP survey (Goyer, 2010) identified the primary reasons grandparents assume custody of grandchildren, including parental drug/alcohol problems, mental illness, incarceration, divorce, chronic illness, homelessness, abuse, neglect, military deployment, and death.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Generativity in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The habitual pattern of absence from work or other duty. Middle-aged workers with higher job satisfaction tend to exhibit lower rates of absenteeism compared to younger workers, contributing to their greater productivity and reliability in the workplace.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The habitual or frequent absence from work. Older workers demonstrate lower rates of absenteeism compared to younger workers, reflecting greater reliability and investment in their employment.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A key requirement for achieving integrity in late adulthood. Involves accepting one's life as it was lived, including both accomplishments and failures, accepting the inevitability of death, and accepting the deficiencies of others including one's parents.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Integrity vs. Despair | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The traditional societal basis on which men are evaluated, including their accomplishments, skill, and power. Because men are valued for these attributes rather than reproductive capacity, they are not considered socially 'old' until they are physically incapable of working, creating a stark contrast with how aging women are perceived.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A common term for heartburn or pyrosis, referring to the discomfort caused when stomach acid backs up into the esophagus. It is often used interchangeably with heartburn.
Researchers who found that a family history of violence makes older women more vulnerable to elder abuse, particularly physical and sexual abuse.

glossary.term: Active Engagement in Social and Productive Activities
glossary.def: The third criterion of Rowe and Kahn's successful aging model, which emphasizes continued participation in meaningful social relationships and activities that contribute to society or personal well-being. This criterion reflects the importance of purpose, connection, and contribution in later life.

The number of years a person can expect to live in good health and with functional independence. Active life expectancy can be promoted through stress reduction, exercise, healthy diet, smoking cessation, and limiting alcohol use.

A lifestyle that includes physical activity equivalent to walking more than 3 miles per day at 3 to 4 miles per hour, in addition to the activities of independent living. Individuals with active lifestyles have the highest caloric needs and gain the most extensive health benefits from their activity levels.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: ACTIVE Study (Advanced Cognitive Training for Independent and Vital Elderly)
glossary.def: A major study conducted between 1999 and 2001 involving 2,802 racially diverse participants aged 65–94. Participants received 10 group training sessions and 4 follow-up sessions focused on memory, reasoning, and processing speed. Results showed cognitive improvements lasting at least 5 years, though improvements were task-specific and did not generalize to other abilities or delay Alzheimer's disease.

Five basic self-care tasks that are essential for independent living: eating, bathing, toileting, dressing, and transferring from a bed to a chair. Inability to perform any of these tasks is considered a sign of frailty in older adults.

Basic self-care tasks such as bathing, dressing, eating, and toileting. Nearly 50% of the oldest-old (85+) require some assistance with these activities, reflecting their higher level of functional dependence compared to younger older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A theory of aging that proposes elderly people want and need to remain active across a variety of social spheres—with relatives, friends, and community groups—and that withdrawal occurs only unwillingly as a result of ageism and social exclusion, not because of a natural desire to disengage.
A theory of aging suggesting that remaining socially active and engaged is key to maintaining psychological well-being and life satisfaction in late adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A gerontological theory proposed by Lemon, Bengston, and Peterson (1972) stating that greater life satisfaction occurs among older adults who remain active and engaged rather than withdrawing from social roles. Posits that replacing lost roles and activities with new ones supports positive aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Integrity vs. Despair | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Short-term pain that is typically associated with tissue damage or illness. Individuals who have suffered acute pain in the past week experience approximately 14 minutes of sleep debt per night.

The ability to adjust goals, strategies, and behaviors in response to changing life circumstances. The young-old demonstrate stronger adaptive flexibility in daily living compared to those 85 and older (Riediger, Freund, & Baltes, 2005).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The specific, learned immune defense system that includes the tonsils, spleen, bone marrow, thymus, circulatory system, and lymphatic system. It produces and transports T-cells that fight bacteria, viruses, and foreign threats, and creates immunological memory.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Devices, tools, and systems designed to assist older adults or individuals with disabilities in maintaining independence and quality of life. These include hearing aids, mobility aids, smart home technology, and other assistive devices. The growing older adult population has increased demand for and development of such technologies.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Sugars and syrups added to foods or beverages during processing or preparation, as distinguished from naturally occurring sugars. Dietary guidelines recommend that adults consume less than 10% of daily calories from added sugars. Currently, about 15% of calories in the American adult diet come from added sugars, equivalent to approximately 22 teaspoons per day. Excess added sugar contributes to weight gain, diabetes, and other health problems.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The primary energy currency of the cell, produced by mitochondria through the conversion of oxygen. When mitochondria are damaged, ATP production decreases, reducing energy available to cells and contributing to cellular deterioration and aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: ADHD, Specific Learning Disorders, and ASD
glossary.def: ADHD (Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder): great difficulty concentrating for more than a few moments; also inattentive, impulsive, and overactive. Rates in the U.S. are higher than most other nations; concerns about misdiagnosis and overmedication. SPECIFIC LEARNING DISORDER: marked deficit in a particular area not caused by intellectual disability or unusual stress. DYSLEXIA: unusual difficulty with reading — a processing problem, not an intelligence problem. DYSCALCULIA: unusual difficulty with math. AUTISM SPECTRUM DISORDER (ASD): difficulty with social communication and interaction; restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior. Social interaction is ALWAYS impaired. NEURODIVERSITY perspective: human brains make people diverse in abilities and needs.

glossary.term: Adolescent Brain: Lopsided Growth and Body Rhythms
glossary.def: The limbic system (fear, emotional impulses) matures BEFORE the prefrontal cortex (planning, emotional regulation). This lopsided growth makes teenagers vulnerable to social pressures. NUCLEUS ACCUMBENS: region of the ventral striatum connected to the limbic system; heightened arousal in this reward center during adolescence. POSITIVE aspects of adolescent brain development: increased myelination decreases reaction time; enhanced dopamine activity promotes pleasurable experiences; synaptic growth enhances moral development. CIRCADIAN SHIFT: puberty causes a shift toward 'eveningness' — later sleep-wake cycles. Three of every four high school seniors are sleep deprived.

glossary.term: Adolescent Egocentrism: Imaginary Audience and Personal Fable
glossary.def: ADOLESCENT EGOCENTRISM (ages 10–13): characteristic of adolescent thinking that leads young people to focus on themselves to the exclusion of others. IMAGINARY AUDIENCE: thinking intensely about themselves and what other people think of them; interpreting everyone else's behavior as a personal judgment; feeling more unique, special, admired, or disliked than they actually are. PERSONAL FABLE: belief that one's thoughts, feelings, or experiences are unique, more wonderful or awful than anyone else's. INVINCIBILITY FABLE: egocentric conviction that one cannot be overcome or harmed by anything that might defeat a normal mortal (leads to unprotected sex, drug use, reckless driving).

The pattern in which adult children of divorced older parents tend to provide more support and care to their mothers than their fathers, as documented in studies from both the United States (Lin, 2008) and England (Glaser et al., 2008). Divorced older men, despite often being better off financially and more likely to repartner, may receive less support from their adult children.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A parenting orientation identified by Greene et al. (2003) in which a divorced parent expects that children can adapt and should accommodate parental wishes regarding repartnering. Anderson and Greene (2011) found that adult-focused custodial mothers tended to be older, more educated, employed, had been married longer, reported less rapport with their children, and spent less time in joint activities with their children.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A circadian rhythm disorder common in older adults in which the sleep-wake cycle shifts earlier, causing individuals to fall asleep and wake up earlier than younger adults. It is based on age-related changes in circadian rhythm regulation.

Exercise that uses large muscle groups in a rhythmic, continuous fashion and relies on oxygen for energy production. Includes walking, jogging, cycling, and swimming. Per guidelines, sessions should last at least 10 minutes and be spread throughout the week. Aerobic activity benefits cardiovascular health, weight management, and stress reduction.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The age demographic that spends less time on leisure activities than any other age group aged 15 or older in the United States (U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, 2016). This is attributed to the likelihood that individuals in this group are actively parenting and advancing in their careers simultaneously.

The conventional threshold age defining the onset of late adulthood in the United States. It is also historically associated with eligibility for full Social Security retirement benefits and Medicare. According to the U.S. Census Bureau, individuals reaching age 65 in 2015 can expect to live approximately 19 more years.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The practice of treating an applicant or employee less favorably because of their age. In the context of midlife employment, employers may prefer younger workers who start at lower pay scales and have more years of work ahead of them, making it harder for middle-aged unemployed adults to find new jobs.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A U.S. federal law originally passed in 1967 and amended in 1986 to eliminate mandatory retirement for most workers, protecting individuals from being forced to retire due to age. Exceptions remain for specific occupations such as pilots, air traffic controllers, federal law enforcement officers, national park rangers, and firefighters.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Flat, brown spots that appear on the skin of older adults, caused primarily by cumulative years of sun exposure. Previously called liver spots, they are now referred to as age spots and are considered harmless, though they are a visible marker of photoaging and prolonged UV radiation exposure.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A term used to describe the normal, mild decline in memory functioning that occurs with advancing age but does not meet diagnostic criteria for dementia or mild cognitive impairment. It includes slowed retrieval and reduced efficiency in encoding new information, and is distinguished from pathological memory loss.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The gradual reduction in cognitive functioning that occurs across the adult life span, affecting processes such as memory, processing speed, attention, and executive function. Research by Kramer and Erickson (2007) has demonstrated that this decline may be slowed through physical exercise and lifestyle interventions.

The phenomenon whereby older adults outperform younger adults on certain memory tasks due to accumulated knowledge, expertise, and experience. This advantage is most pronounced in areas such as vocabulary, semantic knowledge, and domain-specific expertise (e.g., chess, typing, grocery pricing decisions).
The death rate observed within a specific age group of a population. It is one of the primary inputs used by the WHO to calculate Healthy Life Expectancy across different regions and countries.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Prejudice or discrimination against individuals on the basis of their age. In the context of women in midlife, ageism is closely linked to the double standard of aging, where women face harsher social judgments about growing older compared to men.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Prejudice, discrimination, and stereotyping directed at older adults based on their age. Ageism adds to stratification and can take many forms, including devaluing older people in youth-oriented cultures or making assumptions about their capabilities.

Prejudice, stereotyping, or discrimination against individuals based on their age, particularly directed toward older adults. Ageism can manifest in social attitudes, workplace policies, healthcare practices, and cultural representations.

Prejudice, stereotyping, or discrimination against individuals on the basis of their age, particularly older adults. In the context of sexuality, ageist myths portray elderly individuals as genderless and asexual, and these myths can become internalized by older adults, making it more difficult for them to accept and express their own sexuality.

Prejudice or discrimination based on a person's age, particularly directed toward older adults. The term was first coined in 1969 and, according to Nelson (2016), remains one of the most institutionalized forms of prejudice in contemporary society. Ageism is reflected in everyday conversations, media portrayals, and cultural artifacts such as greeting cards that mock aging.

Prejudice, stereotyping, or discrimination against individuals based on their age, particularly targeting older adults. For gay and lesbian elders, ageism can intersect with other forms of discrimination to produce compounded negative effects on physical and mental health.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gay and Lesbian Elders | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The relationship between advancing age and specialized knowledge, in which accumulated experience can confer advantages in familiar domains; however, expertise cannot fully compensate for age-related declines in general cognitive functioning, and the benefits of additional practice diminish with age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The practice of remaining in the same home and community throughout later life, making adjustments as needed to accommodate health changes rather than relocating to institutional care. It is associated with maintaining social connections and identity.

The preference and practice of older adults remaining in their current homes and communities as they age, rather than relocating to retirement communities or care facilities. The majority of older adults wish to live independently in their own homes for as long as possible.

A population demographic trend in which the median age of the population increases and the proportion of older individuals grows relative to younger age groups. In the United States, this is reflected in the rising percentage of adults 65 and older, projected to reach over 20% by 2030.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A contemporary focus in gerontology and public health emphasizing healthy, active, and engaged aging. Promoted through physical activity, social involvement, and purposeful engagement; aligns with Erikson's concept of integrity and the Activity Theory's tenets.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Integrity vs. Despair | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: One of the Big Five personality traits characterized by cooperativeness, trustworthiness, empathy, and prosocial behavior. Generative men and women were found to score high on agreeableness, consistent with the caring orientation that defines the generativity stage.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A chronic disease characterized by uncontrolled drinking and preoccupation with alcohol, leading to significant physical, psychological, and social harm. Among older adults, widowers over the age of 75 have the highest rate of alcoholism in the U.S. Nearly 50% of nursing home residents have alcohol-related problems.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Substance Abuse and the Elderly | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A recessive genetic condition affecting approximately 1 in 1,600 to 5,000 people that increases risk for COPD. AAT is a protein normally made in the liver that protects organs, especially the lungs, from harmful proteins. In those with the defect, the AAT protein is misshapen and cannot leave the liver, increasing risk for both lung disease and liver cirrhosis.

An example of compensation in Selective Optimization with Compensation theory, where an older adult who can no longer drive finds other means of transportation (e.g., public transit, ride-sharing, family assistance) to maintain mobility and independence. It illustrates how goal achievement can be preserved through alternative strategies.

A subcategory of single adults who have never been married, as distinguished from those who are single due to divorce or widowhood. Research by DePaulo (2014) suggests that always-single individuals show little difference in emotional well-being or health compared to married individuals, particularly when compared to those married four or more years.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by memory loss, cognitive decline, and behavioral changes. Research by Chang et al. (2016) found that higher BMI in midlife is associated with earlier onset of Alzheimer's disease, with each one-point increase in BMI at age 50 associated with developing the disease six to seven months earlier. Scientists speculate that fat cells may produce inflammatory chemicals that damage brain vasculature.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A progressive neurodegenerative disorder and the most common form of dementia, characterized by memory loss, cognitive decline, and behavioral changes. Chang et al. (2016) found that midlife adiposity predicts earlier onset, neuropathology, and presymptomatic cerebral amyloid accumulation.

The most common neurocognitive disorder, affecting approximately 50% of all neurocognitive disorder cases. Characterized by gradual onset with progressive memory loss, personality changes, language deterioration, loss of problem-solving ability, physical coordination decline, and eventual inability to respond to the environment or control movement. As of 2016, affected approximately 5.4 million Americans and is the 6th leading cause of death in the United States.

A progressive neurodegenerative disorder and the most common form of dementia, characterized by memory loss, cognitive decline, personality changes, and eventual inability to perform daily activities. Pathologically marked by amyloid plaques and neurofibrillary tangles in the brain. The Alzheimer's Association identifies 10 warning signs for early detection.

A professional medical organization that establishes clinical guidelines and standards for sleep medicine. The AASM recommends that adults receive at least 7 hours of sleep per night to avoid the health risks associated with chronic sleep deprivation.

A U.S. Census Bureau survey that provides demographic and social data. The 2014 survey found that over 5.5 million children under the age of 18 were living in families headed by a grandparent, an increase of more than half a million from 2010.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Generativity in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: An almond-shaped structure in the brain's limbic system responsible for processing emotions, particularly fear and negative stimuli. In middle and older adulthood, the amygdala demonstrates reduced reactivity to negative stimuli compared to younger adults.

Abnormal formations of protein pieces called beta-amyloid that accumulate between neurons in the brains of individuals with Alzheimer's disease. These plaques block cell communication and may trigger an inflammatory response in the immune system, contributing to neuronal death and cognitive decline.

Hormones responsible for building and maintaining muscle mass and other tissues, including testosterone and growth hormone. Their decline with aging is a key contributing factor to sarcopenia and the associated loss of muscle mass and strength.

Researchers who studied post-divorce dating behavior and parental gatekeeping. They found that most divorced adults had dated by one year after filing for divorce, over half were in a serious relationship by one year post-filing, and identified three gatekeeping approaches used by post-divorce parents when introducing new romantic partners to their children.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Also known as late-onset hypogonadism, this condition refers to a significant decline in testosterone levels in aging men, particularly after age 50. Symptoms include low sex drive, erectile dysfunction, fatigue, loss of muscle mass, loss of body hair, and breast enlargement. It is analogous to menopause but less universal and more gradual.

Chest pain or discomfort caused by reduced blood flow to the heart muscle, often a symptom of cardiovascular disease. It typically occurs when the heart is working harder than usual (e.g., during exercise or stress) and is an indication that the coronary arteries may be narrowed or partially blocked.
The total loss of the sense of smell. Considered extremely rare. Distinguished from partial or gradual smell loss, such as presbyosmia.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A commercial sector that markets products, procedures, and cosmetic surgeries aimed at reducing or reversing visible signs of aging. The media and anti-aging industry significantly influence how women perceive their own aging by promoting standards of youthful beauty and selling products to help women 'look younger,' as noted by Gilleard & Higgs (2000).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A molecule that inhibits the oxidation of other molecules and neutralizes free radicals, thereby preventing cell damage and slowing aging. Estrogens function as antioxidants in females, contributing to their longer life expectancy.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A combination of specific antioxidant vitamins and minerals studied as a potential intervention for dry macular degeneration. Research results are mixed; this approach does not cure the disease or restore lost vision but may slow visual deterioration in some patients.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Substances that can donate an electron to neutralize free radicals, thereby reducing oxidative damage to cells and tissues. Adding antioxidants to the diet has been studied as a potential countermeasure to free radical damage, though research on their effectiveness is not conclusive.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A class of drugs used to treat HIV/AIDS by reducing viral load in the body. Increased access to antiretroviral medication in the WHO African Region was a primary factor contributing to the 9.4-year increase in life expectancy between 2000 and 2015 in that region.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researcher who, along with Kahn (1980) and independently (2001), developed and elaborated the Convoy Model of Social Relations, which conceptualizes social relationships as a convoy of connections held together by exchanges of social support, differentiated by levels of closeness and stability across the lifespan.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Social Networks in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A psychological condition involving excessive worry or fear that can affect older adults. Research by Pilkington, Windsor, and Crisp (2012) found that volunteering and the social contact it provides are linked to lower rates of anxiety among older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Generativity in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Quick assessment of newborn health, completed at 1 minute AND 5 minutes after birth. Rates five categories: heart rate, breathing, muscle tone, color, and reflexes. Each scored 0, 1, or 2; maximum is 10. Score of 7 or above indicates a healthy transition to extrauterine life; below 7 means the newborn may need assistance. Developed by Virginia Apgar in 1952.

Programmed cell death in which a cell stops replicating by dying. It is one of three outcomes when a telomere becomes critically short, alongside cellular senescence and abnormal division. Apoptosis is a normal and controlled part of an organism's development and aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A reduction in the desire to eat, commonly observed in older adults due to sensory changes, medication side effects, dental problems, social isolation, and depression. Appetite decline can contribute to malnutrition and unintentional weight loss.

An early researcher who found that boomerang children and adult children who failed to exit the home placed greater financial hardship on parents, and that parents reported more negative perceptions of this living arrangement. This early research contrasts with more recent findings showing greater parental tolerance for adult children returning home.

glossary.term: Arenas of Identity: Religious, Political, Ethnic, Gender
glossary.def: RELIGIOUS identity: most adolescents accept broad outlines of parental and cultural religious identity; specific beliefs may be questioned. POLITICAL identity: more 21st-century adolescents identify as independent; they tend to be more liberal than parents. ETHNIC identity: establishing a solid ethnic identity is especially difficult for multiracial adolescents or immigrants; pride in ethnic identity correlates with academic achievement and well-being. GENDER IDENTITY: DSM-5 replaced 'gender identity disorder' with GENDER DYSPHORIA — people distressed at their biological gender. This shift from 'disorder' to 'dysphoria' means the problem is in the distress, which can be mitigated by social conditions.

An abnormal heart rhythm in which the heartbeat may be too fast, too slow, or irregular. Arrhythmias are a common cause of sudden cardiac arrest. In a healthy heart, a fatal arrhythmia is unlikely to develop without an outside trigger.

The reduction in elasticity and flexibility of the arteries that occurs with aging. As arteries become less flexible, the cardiovascular system must work harder to circulate blood, contributing to increased blood pressure and greater risk of cardiovascular disease in older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A broad term referring to inflammation of the joints. Arthritis and other rheumatic conditions are the most common cause of disability among U.S. adults for the past 15 years. Approximately 62% of adults with arthritis are aged 65 or older, and almost 1 in 2 older adults with arthritis have some degree of mobility limitations.

A chronic inflammatory joint condition characterized by pain, stiffness, and reduced range of motion. In the context of older adult sexuality, arthritis can cause joint pain that limits the ability to engage in sexual activity comfortably.

A broad term referring to inflammation of one or more joints, causing pain, stiffness, and reduced range of motion. The most common forms in older adults are osteoarthritis (wear-and-tear of cartilage) and rheumatoid arthritis (an autoimmune condition). It is one of the most prevalent chronic conditions in older populations.

The widespread but inaccurate cultural belief that older adults no longer engage in or desire sexual activity. This stereotype is contradicted by research showing that 72% of men and 45.5% of women aged 52–72 report being sexually active, and 20–30% remain sexually active into their 80s.

ASSIMILATION: new experiences are interpreted to fit into existing schemas (old ideas). ACCOMMODATION: old ideas are restructured to include new experiences that do not fit. Process: Equilibrium → New experience → Disequilibrium → Adaptation (assimilation or accommodation) → New Equilibrium. Disequilibrium leads to cognitive growth.

A living arrangement for elderly people that combines privacy and independence with medical supervision. Assisted-living facilities range from small group homes for a few elderly people to large apartment or townhouse developments for hundreds of residents, and their number has increased as nursing home usage has declined.

ASTHMA: chronic disease of the respiratory system in which inflammation narrows the airways, causing wheezing, shortness of breath, chest tightness, and coughing. HYGIENE HYPOTHESIS: the idea that early exposure to microbes and allergens helps develop natural resistance. Two pieces of evidence: (1) first-born children are more likely to develop asthma than later-born children; (2) farm children have much lower rates of asthma and allergies. Suggests that reduced outdoor exposure and cleaner environments may be increasing asthma incidence.

A model developed by Robert Atchley (1994) identifying six phases individuals may experience when transitioning into retirement: (1) Remote pre-retirement phase—fantasizing about retirement; (2) Immediate pre-retirement phase—establishing concrete plans; (3) Actual retirement; (4) Honeymoon phase—engaging in travel and deferred activities; (5) Disenchantment phase—experiencing emotional letdown; and (6) Reorientation phase—adjusting to a routine. Not all individuals experience every stage.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The buildup of fatty plaque in the arteries, considered the most common cause of cardiovascular disease. The plaque thickens the artery walls and restricts blood flow to organs and tissues. Atherosclerosis is also a major contributing factor in heart attacks, as the plaque can form the basis for blood clots that block coronary arteries.

A cardiovascular condition involving the buildup of plaque—composed of fat, cholesterol, calcium, and other substances—in the arterial walls. Over time the plaque hardens and narrows the arteries, restricting blood flow and oxygenation to organs including the penis, making it a leading medical cause of erectile dysfunction.

The buildup of fatty deposits or plaques in the walls of arteries, which is the most common type of heart disease. As plaque accumulates, blood cannot flow normally to deliver oxygen throughout the body, and depending on location, can cause a heart attack, leg pain, or stroke. Atherosclerosis is not considered a normal part of aging.

A disease characterized by the buildup of plaque in arterial walls, associated with free radical damage, elevated blood fat levels, and cholesterol. It is one of the disorders linked to oxidative stress and aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The wasting or decrease in size of a body tissue or organ due to disease, disuse, or aging. In the context of aging, muscle atrophy occurs when muscles are not used sufficiently or when sarcopenia progresses, leading to reduced strength and mobility.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: ATTACHMENT: a lasting emotional bond that one person has with another, beginning in early infancy and influencing close relationships throughout life. Demonstrated through proximity-seeking (approaching and following) and contact-maintaining (touching, snuggling). Measured using the Strange Situation. Four types: SECURE (Type B) — explores freely, misses caregiver, joyfully welcomes return; INSECURE-AVOIDANT (Type A) — indifferent to caregiver; INSECURE-RESISTANT (Type C) — clingy and hard to comfort; DISORGANIZED (Type D) — confused, contradictory responses.

A cognitive resource that governs the ability to selectively focus on relevant information while ignoring irrelevant stimuli. Aging affects multiple components of attention differently: selective attention shows modest decline, divided attention is more significantly impaired, and sustained attention is relatively preserved.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The ability to redirect attention between different objects, locations, or tasks. Research by Tales, Muir, Bayer, and Snowden (2002) found that older adults have greater difficulty shifting their attention compared to younger adults, contributing to broader cognitive challenges in late adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A condition in which the immune system mistakenly attacks the body's own tissues. Women are slightly more prone to autoimmune and inflammatory diseases (e.g., rheumatoid arthritis) than men, though their overall immune system deteriorates more slowly with age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A feature of expert performance in which complex thoughts and actions become more routine and instinctive over time. Expertise allows individuals to process information faster and more effectively, making responses appear instinctive (Crawford & Channon, 2002).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A dimension of psychological well-being referring to an individual's sense of self-determination and independence. Formal religious participation was associated with lower levels of autonomy, while spirituality was associated with higher levels of autonomy.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: In the context of Lang and Schütze's research from the Berlin Aging Study, adult children who were more emotionally autonomous (less dependent on their parents) tended to have more emotionally meaningful relationships with their aging parents, as reported by both the parents and the adult children themselves.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Social Networks in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The first criterion of Rowe and Kahn's successful aging model, referring to the relative absence of disease, disability, and associated risk factors such as high blood pressure, smoking, and obesity. It is described as 'relative' because complete avoidance is not always possible but minimizing risk factors is achievable.

A generational cohort in the United States consisting of individuals born between 1946 and 1964, following the end of World War II. This cohort is characterized by a significant spike in birth rates during that period. The aging of the Baby Boom cohort into middle and late adulthood has led to increased research interest and funding directed toward understanding the developmental stage of middle adulthood.

Individuals born between 1946 and 1964 following World War II. As this large cohort ages into late adulthood, it significantly increases the number of older adults in the United States and has major implications for healthcare, social security, and public policy.

The demographic cohort born approximately between 1946 and 1964, known for higher lifetime exposure to and use of psychoactive substances compared to previous generations. This history of substance use is a key reason why future rates of elderly substance abuse are expected to increase significantly.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Substance Abuse and the Elderly | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The generation of approximately 78 million Americans born between approximately 1946 and 1964. Promoting exercise and healthy habits among baby boomers is considered one of the most effective strategies for reducing health care costs and improving quality of life, as cited by Shure and Cahan (1998).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The large generational cohort born roughly between 1946 and 1964. In 2002, baby boomers were aged 38–56, representing the prime employment group. By 2012, the youngest were 48 and the oldest had just reached retirement age (66). Their aging is a major factor in the declining overall workforce participation rate.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A generational cohort of Americans born between 1946 and 1964, representing a large post-World War II surge in birth rates. The first baby boomers turned 65 in 2011, and approximately 10,000 baby boomers reach age 65 every day, making them a primary driver of the Graying of America.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The demographic cohort born between approximately 1946 and 1964, representing a large generational wave currently entering late adulthood. Their increasing numbers are expected to significantly raise demand for long-term care services in the coming decades.

Individuals born approximately between 1946 and 1964 who came of age during the social upheaval of the 1960s and 1970s. Gay and lesbian baby boomers began to witness the repeal of laws criminalizing homosexual behavior, representing an improvement in legal protections compared to older cohorts.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gay and Lesbian Elders | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The ability to maintain stable posture and orientation during sitting, standing, and movement. Controlled by the brain receiving signals from shifting hair cells in the inner ear. This function declines with age, leading to increased risk of falls and instability in older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A collaborative research contribution by Paul Baltes and Ute Kunzmann examining the determinants of wisdom. They proposed that occupations and experiences emphasizing others rather than self, combined with personality characteristics such as openness to experience and generativity, are more likely to build wisdom than age alone.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A research collaboration examining the nature of wisdom in aging, concluding that wisdom is rare and that its development is not simply a product of growing older but requires specific types of experiences and personality factors.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who developed and elaborated the Selective Optimization with Compensation (SOC) model as a framework for understanding how older adults successfully adapt to age-related losses. Their work emphasizes that aging can be navigated through strategic adjustments in goal selection, resource optimization, and compensatory behaviors.

Researchers who conducted longitudinal studies proposing that deficits in sensory functioning (vision and hearing) account for age-related differences in a variety of cognitive abilities, highlighting the link between perceptual and cognitive aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A long-term scientific study used in the Chang et al. (2016) research that tracked 1,394 men and women with an average age of approximately 60 for 14 years. The study provided data linking higher BMI in midlife to earlier onset of Alzheimer's disease and greater brain changes typical of the disease.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A landmark longitudinal research study begun in 1958 by the National Institute on Aging that has tracked the aging process in approximately 1,400 individuals ranging from age 20 to 90. Key findings include that aging varies significantly between individuals and organ systems, and that changes such as heart muscle thickening, arterial stiffening, and declining lung capacity are common features of normal aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A scholarly source cited in this content that elaborates on Erikson's Integrity vs. Despair stage, noting that those in late adulthood need to achieve both acceptance of their life and acceptance of the inevitability of their death, and that prior to Erikson, old age was seen primarily through physical and social restriction lenses.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Integrity vs. Despair | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A region of the brain that provides cholinergic (acetylcholine-releasing) projections to the cortex and hippocampus. Synaptic loss in the basal forebrain region during aging contributes to memory and attentional deficits.

A group of subcortical nuclei involved in the regulation of voluntary motor movements, procedural learning, and habit formation. In Parkinson's disease, the loss of dopaminergic input from the substantia nigra to the basal ganglia disrupts normal motor activity, causing the hallmark motor symptoms.

AUTHORITARIAN: high behavioral standards, strict punishment, little communication. Children tend to become conscientious and obedient but not especially happy; may feel guilty or depressed. PERMISSIVE: high nurturance and communication but little discipline or control. Children are unhappy, lack self-control, are immature, tend to remain dependent. AUTHORITATIVE: parents set limits and enforce rules but are flexible and listen to their children. Children are successful, articulate, happy, generous, well-liked. NEGLECTFUL/UNINVOLVED: parents are indifferent and unaware of what is going on in children's lives — distinct from permissive. Cultural and cohort context always matters.

A multigenerational family structure characterized by multiple generations (sometimes four or five) but only a few members in each generation, reflecting trends of longer life expectancy and lower birth rates. Relationships span more generations vertically but are narrower horizontally.

The degree to which an individual's bedtime fluctuates from night to night. High bedtime variability exposes the body to varying levels of light, disrupting the circadian clock and impairing glucose metabolism and energy homeostasis.

A researcher who challenged the assumption that single individuals fare worse emotionally and in health than married individuals. She argued that most studies create a bias by comparing 'married' versus 'not married' without separating always-single individuals from the divorced or widowed, and found little meaningful difference between always-single and married individuals once the honeymoon phase ends.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A perspective proposed by Vern Bengtson (2001) suggesting that grandparents do not maintain a single fixed grandparenting style across all relationships. Instead, they may adopt different styles with different grandchildren and change their approach over time as family circumstances evolve, such as during crises or when stepping in as sole care providers.

A non-cancerous growth of tissue that does not invade nearby tissues and, when removed, usually does not grow back. Benign tumors can sometimes be quite large and are generally not life threatening—with the exception of benign brain tumors, which can be life threatening due to limited space within the skull.

A class of tranquilizing prescription medications that act on the central nervous system, commonly prescribed to older adults for anxiety, insomnia, and pain management. They are the most commonly misused and abused prescription medications among older adults. Approximately 17 million benzodiazepine prescriptions are written for older adults each year in the United States.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Substance Abuse and the Elderly | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A large longitudinal and interdisciplinary study of aging conducted in Berlin, Germany. Lang and Schütze used this study to survey adult children (mean age 54) and their aging parents (mean age 84), finding that emotional support from adult children — such as showing tenderness or cheering parents up — was associated with greater life satisfaction in older parents, whereas informational support (advice-giving) was associated with lower life satisfaction.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Social Networks in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A sticky protein fragment derived from a larger protein found in the fatty membrane surrounding nerve cells. In Alzheimer's disease, beta-amyloid accumulates into plaques between neurons, blocking cell-to-cell communication and triggering an inflammatory immune response that leads to further neuronal death.

A widely used model of personality consisting of five dimensions: Openness to Experience, Conscientiousness, Extraversion, Agreeableness, and Neuroticism (OCEAN). Research by de St. Aubin & McAdams (1995) and Peterson, Smirles & Wentworth (1997) found that highly generative individuals scored high on conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, and openness to experience, and low on neuroticism.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The use of both hemispheres of the brain to perform cognitive tasks. Older adults who perform best on cognitive tasks are more likely to demonstrate bilateralization compared to those who perform worst, suggesting it is an adaptive compensatory strategy.

A digestive fluid produced by the liver and stored in the gallbladder, used to digest fats. Bile pigments are one of the components that can crystallize to form gallstones; increased cholesterol in bile (e.g., due to obesity) raises the risk of gallstone formation.

A tubular structure that carries bile from the gallbladder to the small intestine. Gallstones that become lodged in the bile duct can block the flow of bile or pancreatic juices, causing pain, infection, and potentially life-threatening complications requiring surgical or medical intervention.

Colored compounds derived from the breakdown of red blood cells (primarily bilirubin) that are secreted into bile. Bile pigments are one of the three main components—along with fatty materials and calcium deposits—that can crystallize and contribute to gallstone formation.

The consumption of excessive amounts of alcohol in a short period of time, defined clinically as reaching a blood alcohol concentration of 0.08 g/dL or higher. Lesbian and gay adults are more likely to binge drink compared to heterosexuals, contributing to disparate physical health outcomes in this population.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gay and Lesbian Elders | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A stress-reduction technique in which an individual is provided with real-time information about normally unconscious bodily processes (e.g., heart rate) and then taught strategies to consciously alter these signals. Has shown promise in reducing heart disease, hypertension, and other serious conditions (Moravec, 2008; Patel, Marmot, & Terry, 1981).

A measure of aging based on an individual's physical condition, health status, and physiological functioning rather than chronological years lived. Used alongside other dimensions to understand late adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The progressive, time-dependent decline in the physiological functioning of the body at the molecular, cellular, tissue, and organ levels. Biological aging underlies primary aging and includes processes such as telomere shortening, DNA damage accumulation, reduced cellular repair mechanisms, and oxidative stress.

Negative emotional states associated with unresolved conflicts in relationships and life events. According to Erikson, persistent bitterness and resentment in late adulthood are key contributors to the experience of despair rather than integrity.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Integrity vs. Despair | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: In 2012, Black women had the highest labor force participation rate among women at 58%, compared to approximately 57% for Hispanic and Asian women, and 55% for White women. Despite leading female participation rates, rates for all groups were projected to decline by 2022.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Age-related changes in the urinary bladder that reduce its capacity to store urine effectively. This can lead to increased urinary frequency, urgency, and incontinence in older adults and is considered a normal physiological change of late adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A researcher who studied interpersonal problem solving across adulthood (2007) and found that older adults use more effective strategies than younger adults when navigating social and emotional problems, demonstrating preserved or enhanced practical wisdom in late life.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who found that adults aged 50–64 (baby boomers) were more likely to use cannabis than adults aged 65 and older. Their study identified that the highest cannabis users among this age group were men, those who were unmarried or unpartnered, and those with depression.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Substance Abuse and the Elderly | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The force exerted by circulating blood on the walls of blood vessels. High blood pressure (hypertension) is associated with high sodium intake, obesity, and excessive fat consumption. Reducing sodium intake, especially to 1,500 mg per day for those already hypertensive, is associated with corresponding decreases in blood pressure.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: BMI (Body Mass Index): ratio of weight to height. CHILDHOOD OVERWEIGHT: BMI above the 85th percentile for a child's age. CHILDHOOD OBESITY: BMI above the 95th percentile for a child's age. In 2016, 18% of 6-to-11-year-olds in the United States were obese. Obesity now causes more deaths worldwide than malnutrition. Childhood overweight correlates with asthma, high blood pressure, elevated cholesterol, and Type II diabetes. Obesity can have a genetic component but environmental factors (family diet, lifestyle) are major influences.

The proportion of fat, muscle, bone, and other tissues that make up the human body. With aging, body composition changes significantly: lean muscle tissue decreases, body fat increases (particularly around the center of the body and internal organs), and bone density declines, affecting metabolism, strength, and overall health.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: Body Image and Sexuality in Older Women
glossary.def: The psychological impact of societal emphasis on youthful physical appearance on older women's sexual self-concept and enjoyment. Women who are unhappy with their aging appearance may believe their partners no longer find them attractive, negatively affecting sexual performance and satisfaction.

A numerical measure derived from an individual's height and weight, used to categorize underweight, normal weight, overweight, and obesity. BMI was measured alongside insulin resistance in the SWAN Sleep Study to assess the metabolic impact of irregular sleep schedules.

A numerical measure derived from a person's weight and height, used to categorize individuals as underweight, normal weight, overweight, or obese. In the Chang et al. (2016) study, each one-point increase in BMI at age 50 was associated with developing Alzheimer's disease approximately six to seven months earlier.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The amount of mineral matter per unit volume of bone, used as an indicator of bone strength and fracture risk. Exercise, particularly weight-bearing and resistance activities, is known to increase bone density. Maintaining adequate bone density is especially important in midlife to prevent osteoporosis and fractures in later adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Young adults who return to live in their parents' home after having previously lived independently. Motivations for returning include financial hardship, emotional distress, and mental health issues. Research indicates that those who return are more likely to have had good relationships with their parents previously, and today's parents are more tolerant of this arrangement than in past decades due to its increasing normalization.

A researcher who documented the differential pattern of cognitive aging, noting that verbal skills show minimal or no age-related decline while performance test scores, which measure problem-solving speed, decline with age. This pattern mirrors the distinction between crystallized and fluid intelligence.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A researcher who conducted a comprehensive review of the research literature on the empty nest and post-parental period, finding that few parents reported significant loneliness or loss after children left home, and that marital satisfaction commonly increased during the launching phase and persisted over time, supporting the role strain relief hypothesis over the empty nest syndrome concept.

The gradual loss of brain volume and weight that occurs with aging, amounting to 5–10% between ages 20 and 90. It results from shrinkage of neurons, reduction in the number of synapses, and shortening of axon lengths, contributing to cognitive decline over the lifespan.

The cognitive and neurological activity of the brain during the middle adult years, approximately ages 40-65. Research indicates that brain functioning in middle adulthood reflects both gains and losses — while processing speed and certain memory functions may begin to decline, expertise, vocabulary, and emotional regulation often remain stable or improve. Neuroplasticity continues throughout this period.

The specialization of cognitive functions in one hemisphere of the brain. Younger adults tend to show greater lateralization (e.g., using only the right prefrontal cortex for memory tasks), while older adults show decreased lateralization by using both prefrontal areas, which appears to assist cognitive functioning in aging.

The brain's ability to reorganize itself by forming new neural connections throughout life. In middle age, the brain continues to rewire itself based on experiences, demonstrating that cognitive development does not cease after young adulthood.

BRAIN STEM: controls automatic responses like breathing — present at birth. MIDBRAIN: affects emotions and memory. CORTEX: outer layers where most thinking, feeling, and sensing occurs — continues developing through adolescence into young adulthood. PREFRONTAL CORTEX: at the very front; specializes in anticipation, planning, and impulse control — LAST area to mature. LIMBIC SYSTEM: produces emotions — amygdala, hypothalamus, hippocampus.

A sudden, severe onset of pain that occurs while a patient is already on a long-acting pain medication regimen. Particularly concerning in cancer patients. Can be psychologically distressing and cause anticipatory anxiety about future episodes.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Part-time or transitional employment that occurs between a person's primary career and full retirement. Approximately 60% of workers transition to bridge jobs rather than retiring abruptly, often serving as a financial or psychological buffer before complete withdrawal from the workforce.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who documented that women experience more fear, anxiety, and concern about their identity as they age compared to men, and may feel pressure to prove themselves as productive and valuable members of society, particularly during and after midlife.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who coined the term 'graying of divorce,' noting that while the overall U.S. divorce rate has declined since the 1990s, the divorce rate among adults 50 and older has doubled. They also found the rate of remarriage dropped more for younger adults than for middle-aged and older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The theory proposed by Cohen and Wills (1985) that social support moderates (buffers) the negative effects of stress on psychological and physical health, particularly during high-stress conditions.

BULLYING: repeated, systematic efforts to inflict harm through physical, verbal, or social attack on a weaker person. BULLY-VICTIM: someone who both attacks others and is attacked (also called a provocative victim). FOUR TYPES: Physical (hitting, pinching, kicking), Verbal (teasing, taunting, name-calling), Relational (destroying peer acceptance and friendship — often more devastating than physical bullying), Cyber (using electronic means to harm another). CAUSES: genetic predisposition, parenting/caregiving environment, peers. CONSEQUENCES: depression, impaired social understanding, lower school achievement. PREVENTION: the WHOLE SCHOOL must be involved; intervention is more effective in earlier grades.

The researcher credited with establishing the distinction between primary aging and secondary aging. Ewald Busse's classification framework helped differentiate between inevitable biological aging processes and those caused by modifiable lifestyle and environmental factors, providing a foundation for health promotion in middle and late adulthood.

A stimulant found in coffee, tea, and certain beverages that is a known trigger for heartburn. Caffeine can relax the lower esophageal sphincter, making it easier for stomach acid to reflux into the esophagus.

Accumulations of calcium salts that can combine with bile pigments and fatty materials in the gallbladder to form gallstones. Calcium-rich gallstones (pigment stones) are one type of gallstone composition.

The number of calories required per day to maintain body weight, which varies by age, sex, and physical activity level. Aging brings about a reduction in the number of calories required. For example, a sedentary 36-40 year old male requires approximately 2,400 calories per day, while a sedentary 36-40 year old female requires approximately 1,800 calories per day, with needs decreasing further with age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A developed North American nation with 16.5% of its population aged 65 and older in 2012, projected to rise to 25% by 2030 and 26.5% by 2050, reflecting trends common to most developed nations driven by low fertility and increased longevity.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A collection of more than 100 related diseases characterized by uncontrolled cell division and the spread of abnormal cells into surrounding tissues. Cancer cells do not mature into specific cell types, ignore signals to stop dividing, evade the immune system, prompt nearby cells to form blood vessels that supply tumors, and can metastasize. Cancer was the second leading cause of death in the United States in 2013, accounting for 22.5% of all deaths.

The disproportionate occurrence of cancer in older adults, as documented by Berger et al. (2006) and Balducci and Extermann (2000). Age is a major risk factor for most cancers; treatment is complicated by comorbid conditions, functional status, and physiological changes of aging. Gay men and prostate cancer represent a specific example of invisible diversity in this population (Blank, 2005).

A psychoactive plant-derived substance containing THC, used recreationally and medically. Among older adults aged 50–64 (baby boomers), use is more prevalent than in those 65 and older. Negative effects include panic reactions, anxiety, perceptual distortions, and exacerbation of mood and psychotic disorders. Medical benefits include treatment of multiple sclerosis, Parkinson's disease, chronic pain, and chemotherapy-induced nausea and fatigue.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Substance Abuse and the Elderly | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A waste gas normally expelled by the lungs during exhalation. With age-related declines in lung capacity caused by rib cage changes and diaphragm weakening, carbon dioxide levels in the blood increase while oxygen levels decrease, contributing to shortness of breath and fatigue.

A subset of heart disease involving narrowed, blocked, or stiffened blood vessels that prevent adequate blood flow to the body and heart. The most common cause is atherosclerosis. Cardiovascular disease can lead to heart attack, angina (chest pain), or stroke. Risk factors include high blood pressure, high cholesterol, diabetes, obesity, smoking, poor diet, advanced age, male sex, family history, stress, and poor hygiene.

The health of the heart and blood vessels, including functioning related to blood pressure, circulation, and risk of conditions such as heart attack and stroke. Stress and anxiety associated with ageism and negative stereotyping have been found to negatively impact cardiovascular health in older adults.

The mechanism by which estrogen levels in women protect the heart and circulatory system from disease and damage. This hormonal protection is considered one of the primary biological reasons women have lower rates of early cardiovascular disease and longer life expectancy compared to men.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The virtue associated with the successful resolution of Erikson's seventh stage of generativity vs. stagnation. Erikson defined it as the obligation to 'take care of the persons, the products, and the ideas one has learned to care for' (Erikson, 1982, p. 67). It represents the psychosocial strength gained from mastering this stage.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The point at which individuals often reach the highest level of professional achievement, which frequently occurs during middle adulthood due to the accumulation of expertise, experience, and domain-specific knowledge over many years.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The physical, emotional, financial, and social strain experienced by individuals providing care for a disabled or ill family member. Beach et al. (2000) found both negative and positive health effects of caring for a disabled spouse.

The physical, emotional, and financial strain experienced by individuals who provide care for a chronically ill or disabled loved one. In the context of late adulthood, caring for an ill spouse can result in significant stress, and the spouse's death may paradoxically bring a sense of relief to the caregiver.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Elevated levels of emotional and physical stress experienced by a caregiver that result in negative health outcomes. Research by Beach et al. (2000) found that caregivers who experienced strain had the poorest perceived health, increased health-risk behaviors, and elevated anxiety and depression. In contrast, those who provided spousal care without strain had mental health similar to non-caregivers.

The provision of physical, emotional, and practical support to individuals who cannot fully care for themselves, often older adults. As the U.S. population ages, caregiving — whether by family members, paid professionals, or institutional facilities — becomes an increasingly significant social and economic concern.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The physical and psychological burden experienced by individuals who provide unpaid care to a disabled child, spouse, parent, or other family member. Caregiving is associated with poorer global health, increased physical symptoms, and higher rates of chronic conditions. The typical caregiver in the U.S. is a 49-year-old female caring for a 69-year-old female with a long-term physical condition.

A researcher who articulated the contrast between how men and women are socially valued as they age: men for achievements, competence, and power (considered old only when unable to work), versus women for reproductive capabilities (considered old once postmenopausal), contributing to the double standard of aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Developmental psychologist who developed Socioemotional Selectivity Theory (SST) in 1993. Her research, including work with Gottman and Levenson (1995) and Isaacowitz and Charles (1999), demonstrated that older adults shift motivational goals toward emotional optimization, resulting in smaller, more emotionally meaningful social networks and more positive relational interactions in later life.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Social Networks in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A surgical procedure to remove the eye's clouded natural lens and typically replace it with an artificial intraocular lens. It is one of the most common surgical procedures performed on older adults. Individuals who have undergone cataract surgery are at increased risk for developing floaters.

A clouding of the lens of the eye caused by the clumping of lens proteins (normally precisely arranged to keep the lens clear). Results in reduced clarity, discoloration of the lens (yellowing then browning), and difficulty distinguishing dark colors. Affects 70% of adults by age 75. Risk factors include diabetes, high blood pressure, obesity, smoking, UV exposure, steroid use, eye trauma, and family history. Treated with surgical lens removal and replacement with a synthetic lens; the leading cause of blindness in developing nations.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A clouding of the lens of the eye that occurs commonly with aging, leading to blurry vision, sensitivity to glare, and reduced color perception. Cataracts are the leading cause of blindness worldwide and are typically treated with surgical replacement of the clouded lens with an artificial lens.

A biological theory of aging suggesting that aging is caused by the fact that normal cells cannot divide indefinitely due to the Hayflick limit. The shortening of telomeres with each replication is the proposed mechanism underlying this limit.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A state in which a cell permanently stops replicating but does not die. Senescent cells continue to interact with surrounding cells and can increase disease risk, particularly with advancing age. In youth, senescent cells may reduce cancer risk; in older age they can increase risk of disease.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: An individual who has reached 100 years of age or older. Contributing factors include genetics, diet, physical activity, social engagement, low stress, and access to healthcare. The number of centenarians is growing worldwide.

Individuals who are 100 years of age or older. They represent a segment of the oldest-old and are notable for typically having been healthier than peers throughout aging, often delaying serious disease or disability until their 90s. In 2015 there were nearly half a million centenarians worldwide.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A research center that conducted the National Survey of Sexual Health and Behavior (NSSHB) in 2010, providing data on sexual behaviors across various age groups in the United States. Their findings highlighted the continued sexual activity of middle-aged adults and the low rates of condom use among adults over 40.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)
glossary.def: A U.S. federal public health agency responsible for tracking disease incidence and promoting public health. The CDC reported in 2014 that adults aged 50 and older accounted for 16.7% of new HIV diagnoses, highlighting growing STI risks in older populations.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The supervisory subsystem of working memory that oversees and coordinates the phonological loop and visuospatial sketchpad. It allocates cognitive resources, monitors the effectiveness of cognitive strategies, and manages the division of attention between multiple tasks. The central executive is the component most negatively impacted by aging.
A U.S. government publication that ranks countries by various metrics including life expectancy. As of 2016, the United States ranked 43rd in the world for life expectancy.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The degree to which the brain and spinal cord respond to drugs or other stimuli. CNS sensitivity to drugs increases with age, meaning older adults experience stronger and more prolonged effects from the same dose of a psychoactive substance compared to younger adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Substance Abuse and the Elderly | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Two patterns of prenatal and postnatal growth. CEPHALOCAUDAL: development proceeds from head downward (e.g., infants gain head and hand control before walking). PROXIMODISTAL: development proceeds from the center of the body outward (e.g., trunk before extremities). Both patterns continue after birth.

The buildup of amyloid plaques in the brain, a hallmark pathological feature of Alzheimer's disease. Research by Chang et al. (2016) linked midlife obesity to earlier presymptomatic accumulation of these plaques.

The pathological mechanism underlying Vascular Neurocognitive Disorder, in which obstructed blood flow to a specific region of the brain results in localized damage and associated cognitive decline, as opposed to the diffuse neuronal loss seen in Alzheimer's disease.

Researchers who reviewed literature on aging and expertise, concluding that the superior performance of older experts compared to younger novices is task specific, and that the rate of return for practice effort diminishes as individuals age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A decline in cognitive function associated with chemotherapy treatment for cancer, sometimes called 'chemo brain.' In elderly patients, chemotherapy may further exacerbate existing dementia and age-related cognitive declines.

Researchers who described and categorized three principal styles of grandparenting — Remote, Companionate, and Involved — based on the degree of contact and level of authority grandparents exercised in their grandchildren's lives. Their typology remains a foundational framework in the study of grandparent-grandchild relationships.

glossary.term: Child Maltreatment: Abuse, Neglect, and Prevention
glossary.def: CHILD MALTREATMENT: intentional harm to or avoidable endangerment of anyone under 18. CHILD ABUSE: deliberate action harmful to a child's physical, emotional, or sexual well-being. CHILD NEGLECT: failure to meet a child's basic physical, educational, or emotional needs. REPORTED maltreatment: someone has notified authorities. SUBSTANTIATED maltreatment: reported, investigated, and verified. THREE PREVENTION LEVELS: Primary (macrosystem/exosystem — stable neighborhoods, family cohesion), Secondary (identifying insecure attachment), Tertiary (limiting harm after maltreatment — permanency planning, foster care, kinship care, adoption). PTSD may develop as a delayed reaction to shocking or frightening events.

A parenting orientation identified by Greene et al. (2003) in which a divorced parent allows the child's views, reactions, and needs to significantly influence the repartnering process and selection of a new partner.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A condition in which a child carries excess body weight for their height and age. The rate of childhood obesity tripled from 1971 to 2011 in the United States, and it is associated with high blood pressure, type 2 diabetes, elevated blood cholesterol levels, low self-esteem, negative body image, and depression.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The world's most populous nation, which by 2050 is projected to have an older population larger than the total current U.S. population. China's enormous population size means its aging demographic will have profound global implications for long-term care and economic support systems.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Older adults in China, particularly in rural areas, who experience greater loneliness and depression after their children leave home compared to American samples.
