This more negative response is attributed to the cultural tradition of family support for the elderly, loss of income when children move to cities for education and work, and the practical hardships associated with children's departure. Urban Chinese empty-nesters did not report the same degree of distress.

One of the most common bacterial sexually transmitted infections, caused by Chlamydia trachomatis. It is often asymptomatic but can lead to serious reproductive complications if untreated. Older adults who do not use condoms remain at risk for contacting chlamydia.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who theorized that biological differences in sex chromosomes and patterns of gene expression contribute to females living longer than males, including the roles of X chromosome inactivation, the SRY gene, and developmental biology in the gender longevity gap.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A phenomenon associated with matchmaking websites in which having so many possible romantic options makes a thoughtful, satisfying choice difficult or overwhelming.

A waxy, fatty substance carried by lipoprotein molecules in the blood. It is produced by the body to create hormones and to aid in the digestion of fatty foods, and is also found in many foods. While the body requires cholesterol for normal function, excessively high levels can lead to plaque formation in the arteries, increasing the risk of heart disease and stroke. Total cholesterol is calculated as HDL + LDL + 20% of triglycerides.

A long-term inflammation of the bronchial tubes characterized by persistent cough and mucus production. Individuals with chronic bronchitis experience more pronounced effects of normal age-related lung changes, resulting in greater reductions in lung capacity and oxygen exchange than non-affected individuals.

A component condition of COPD characterized by chronic inflammation and narrowing of the bronchial tubes, resulting in persistent cough and mucus production. Like emphysema, it is strongly associated with cigarette smoking.

Long-term health conditions that persist over extended periods and typically cannot be cured but can be managed. Examples relevant to middle adulthood include diabetes and arthritis. Chronic diseases contribute to secondary aging by reducing overall health, physical functioning, and quality of life, and may accelerate other age-related declines.

Long-lasting health conditions that can be controlled but not cured. Common examples in late adulthood include cardiovascular disease, hypertension, cancer, congestive heart failure, osteoporosis, and dementia. Many are linked to lifestyle choices and can be prevented, lessened, or managed.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A long-lasting health condition that persists for a year or more, requires ongoing medical attention, and may limit daily activities. Examples include diabetes, heart disease, arthritis, and cancer. The CDC reports that a substantial proportion of U.S. adults 55 and over have one or more chronic conditions, making chronic disease management a central concern of geriatric care.

Long-term medical conditions such as diabetes, cardiovascular disease, osteoporosis, and hypertension that are prevalent in older adults. Proper nutrition plays a significant role in the management and mitigation of these conditions.

An ongoing, generally incurable condition that requires continuing medical attention and affects daily life. Chronic illnesses become more prevalent with age; less than 50% of adults aged 50-64 have a chronic condition, while 90% of adults aged 75 and older do.

A long-lasting health condition that typically cannot be cured but can be managed. More than 40% of lesbian and gay adults ages 50 and over suffer from at least one chronic illness or disability, a rate higher than that seen in comparable heterosexual populations.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gay and Lesbian Elders | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Persistent health conditions that negatively impact sexual activity in older adults. Examples include arthritis (joint pain limiting movement), diabetes (contributing to erectile dysfunction), heart disease (difficulty achieving orgasm), stroke (causing paralysis), and dementia (potentially causing inappropriate sexual behavior).

A progressive lung disease in which the airways become damaged, making it difficult to breathe. It includes conditions such as emphysema and chronic bronchitis, kills more than 120,000 people per year, and has no cure since the lung damage cannot be reversed. Treatments aim at slowing further damage.

Persistent or long-lasting pain that significantly impacts daily functioning. Individuals with chronic pain experience an average of 42 minutes of sleep debt per night, contributing substantially to overall sleep deficiency.

Persistent pain lasting beyond normal healing time. Reported by approximately 60–75% of adults over 65, with even higher rates among nursing home residents. Caused most commonly by arthritis, cancer, diabetes, joint pain, sciatica, and shingles. Despite its high prevalence in older adults, older individuals are actually less sensitive to pain than younger adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Persistent pain lasting longer than three to six months, often associated with medical conditions common in older adults. Chronic pain is a major risk factor for substance abuse in the elderly, as physicians frequently prescribe opioids and benzodiazepines rather than non-pharmacological alternatives to manage it.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Substance Abuse and the Elderly | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A persistent inability to maintain employment that contributes to an adult child's financial and housing dependency on elderly parents, increasing the risk that the adult child will engage in abusive behavior toward the elder.

A person's age as measured by the calendar from the date of birth. In gerontology, chronological age alone is considered insufficient for understanding an individual's health or functional status in late adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Age as measured by the calendar in years from birth. One of the dimensions used to categorize individuals in late adulthood, though it does not fully capture functional or biological status.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A pattern in young adult romantic relationships in which partners break up and get back together again repeatedly. Churning correlates with the stormiest relationships; in the Toledo, Ohio study, 44 percent of participants were churners and half of churners fought both physically and verbally.

Tiny muscle fibers that surround the lens of the eye and control its shape by contracting and relaxing. When viewing near objects, these muscles contract to increase the curvature (bending) of the lens. With aging, these muscles weaken, contributing to presbyopia and the reduced ability to focus on close objects.

The body's internal biological timing system that regulates sleep-wake cycles and many other physiological processes. Light is the most important timing cue for the circadian clock, and disruptions caused by variable bedtimes can impair glucose metabolism and energy homeostasis.

The internal biological clock that regulates the sleep-wake cycle, hormone release, and other bodily functions on a roughly 24-hour cycle. In older adults, changes in circadian rhythms contribute to advanced sleep phase syndrome and overall disrupted sleep patterns.

The process by which the body's circadian clock aligns physiological functions, including sleep, hormone release, and metabolism, with the 24-hour light-dark cycle. Irregular bedtime schedules disrupt circadian timing by exposing the body to varying levels of light at inconsistent times.

A disease in which the liver becomes scarred and does not function properly. In the context of AAT deficiency, excess accumulation of misshapen AAT protein in the liver can lead to cirrhosis over time.

The population of individuals aged 16 and older who are employed and not confined to institutions such as prisons or care facilities. This group forms the basis for U.S. labor force participation statistics. The participation rate peaked at 67% in the late 1990s and was projected to decline to 62% by 2022.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A form of implicit learning and memory in which a neutral stimulus becomes associated with a reflexive response after repeated pairings with an unconditioned stimulus. As a component of implicit memory, classical conditioning shows relatively little decline with normal aging.

A process in which a person or animal learns to associate a neutral stimulus with a meaningful stimulus, gradually reacting to the neutral stimulus with the same response as to the meaningful one. Focuses on a specific response to a specific stimulus. Also called respondent conditioning.

The period of hormonal and reproductive change that occurs in both females and males during middle adulthood. In females, it refers to the transition leading to menopause, including perimenopause and menopause itself, characterized by declining estrogen levels. In males, it refers to a gradual decline in testosterone levels. The climacteric involves physical, hormonal, and sometimes psychological changes.

The midlife transition period during which fertility declines in both men and women. It is biologically based but also influenced by environmental factors. In women, it culminates in menopause; in men, it involves a gradual reduction in reproductive capacity and hormonal changes.

The broad transitional phase in both men and women during middle adulthood involving hormonal and physiological changes related to reproductive aging. In women, it encompasses perimenopause, menopause, and postmenopause; in men, it involves a gradual decline in testosterone. It is associated with changes in sexual functioning, mood, and physical health.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Research studies conducted with human participants to evaluate the effectiveness and safety of medical interventions, treatments, or drugs. Older adults are often excluded from clinical trials due to physician bias — doctors who view illnesses as natural consequences of aging are less likely to refer older patients to participate, limiting research generalizability and treatment access.

The study of how cognitive abilities change across the adult lifespan. Research shows a complex pattern in which some abilities such as vocabulary and inductive reasoning improve into the 70s, while others such as perceptual speed and processing speed decline, influenced by both biological aging and accumulated experience.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The gradual changes in cognitive functioning that occur as a normal part of aging, including slower processing speed, reduced working memory capacity, and increased tip-of-the-tongue experiences, while some abilities such as vocabulary and semantic knowledge may remain stable or improve. Paul Baltes described both the potential and limits of the aging mind.

The process by which individuals offset or make up for age-related cognitive declines in one area using preserved or enhanced abilities in another area. For example, pragmatics of intelligence may compensate for declines in mechanics of intelligence, and experience and knowledge can offset processing speed decrements.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The gradual reduction in cognitive abilities such as memory, processing speed, executive function, and attention that accompanies normal aging. It is associated with structural and functional brain changes including synaptic loss, reduced neurotransmitter receptor activity, and decreased white matter integrity.

A general reduction in cognitive abilities — including memory, processing speed, attention, and executive function — associated with aging. While real, the extent of cognitive decline in late adulthood is often exaggerated due to methodological issues such as use of meaningless tasks, cross-sectional designs, and uncontrolled sensory deficits.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A deterioration in cognitive abilities such as memory, reasoning, attention, and processing speed. While some cognitive changes occur with aging, research by Nelson (2016) demonstrates that the internalization of negative stereotypes about aging accelerates cognitive decline, whereas older adults in cultures with positive views of aging show fewer cognitive deficits.

The specific areas of mental functioning evaluated in the diagnosis of neurocognitive disorders, including memory, language, abstract thinking, reasoning, decision making, and problem-solving. Decline in one or more of these domains is required for a neurocognitive disorder diagnosis.

The broad set of mental abilities including memory, processing speed, attention, reasoning, and problem solving. Expertise cannot fully compensate for all losses in general cognitive functioning that occur with aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A decline in mental functions including memory, communication, and reasoning ability. Considered the greatest single risk factor for elder abuse, as it reduces the elder's ability to recognize, report, or resist abusive situations.

A condition characterized by difficulties with memory, concentration, and other thinking skills. Symptoms overlap with signs of substance use disorder in older adults—including memory deficits, confusion, depression, agitation, motor problems, and hostility—leading to potential misdiagnosis and undertreatment of substance use disorders.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Substance Abuse and the Elderly | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The documented phenomenon in which some middle-aged individuals show actual gains in cognitive functioning rather than decline. Those with cognitive improvements tend to be more physically, cognitively, and socially active.

The brain's resilience or capacity to cope with neural damage or aging-related decline. Activities such as physical exercise, learning new skills, and socializing are thought to build cognitive reserve, minimizing the effects of primary aging of the brain.

A broad phenomenon in which the speed of mental operations decreases with age, affecting perception, response time, and complex reasoning. Cognitive slowing is considered one of the primary mechanisms underlying age-related changes in memory, attention, and problem solving, as proposed by the general slowing hypothesis.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: Cognitive Theory and Piaget's Four Stages
glossary.def: Thoughts and expectations profoundly affect actions, attitudes, beliefs, and assumptions. Piaget's four stages: SENSORIMOTOR (0-2, senses and motor skills; object permanence), PREOPERATIONAL (2-6, symbolic thinking; egocentric), CONCRETE OPERATIONAL (6-11, logic applied to real experiences; conservation), FORMAL OPERATIONAL (12+, abstract and hypothetical thinking; ethics). Intellectual advancement occurs lifelong because humans seek cognitive equilibrium.

The degree of individual differences in cognitive performance within an age group. Greater cognitive variability occurs among middle-aged adults compared to both younger and older adults, reflecting diverse trajectories of cognitive aging.

Living with an unrelated person—typically a romantic partner—to whom one is not married. Most young adults in the United States, England, and northern Europe cohabit rather than marry before age 25; half of all cohabitating couples in the United States plan on marrying eventually. Education increases the chance of marriage and marital childbearing.

An arrangement in which two people in a romantic relationship live together without being formally married. Teachman (2008) found that more than two-thirds of women under age 45 had cohabited with a partner between their first and second marriages. Cohabitation has contributed to declining marriage and remarriage rates.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The arrangement in which two individuals live together in a romantic relationship without being legally married. Cohabitation is increasing among older adults and is associated with more positive outcomes in later adulthood than in younger age groups. Motivations include desire for companionship, protection of family wealth, and avoidance of legal marriage entanglements. In 2014, approximately 2% of adults aged 65+ were cohabitating.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: People born within the same historical period who move through life together, experiencing the same events, technologies, and cultural shifts at the same ages. The effect of any experience varies depending on a person's cohort.

A group of people who share a common characteristic, typically birth year or birth period, and who move through historical time together. In developmental research, cohort effects refer to the influence of shared historical and cultural experiences on development. The Baby Boom cohort (born 1946-1964) is a well-known cohort that has significantly influenced research on middle adulthood.

A group of individuals who share a defining statistical or demographic characteristic, particularly birth year. Baby boomers (born 1946–1964) are a cohort whose size and aging trajectory have had and will continue to have profound effects on American society, including healthcare, Social Security, and housing.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The influence that being born and raised in a particular historical period has on individuals' development, attitudes, and experiences. A major criticism of Levinson's research is that because all participants were of similar ages and likely shared historical experiences, the findings may reflect cohort-specific trends rather than universal developmental patterns.

The influence of generational or historical experiences on attitudes and behaviors. In the context of religion and spirituality, a cohort effect explains why older individuals tend to be more religious and spiritual than younger individuals — older cohorts were socialized more strongly to be religious during their formative years.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The influence of the historical period and social context in which a group of people came of age on their attitudes, experiences, and development. Gay and lesbian elders from different generational cohorts (e.g., those who came of age in the 1950s vs. baby boomers of the 1960s–1970s) had vastly different legal and social environments shaping their identities and health outcomes.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gay and Lesbian Elders | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A research methodology used in longitudinal studies, such as the Seattle Longitudinal Study, that combines cross-sectional and longitudinal approaches by testing multiple age cohorts at multiple time points to distinguish age effects from cohort and period effects.

A form of generativity described by Erikson in which individuals contribute to and engage with the broader society beyond the family and workplace. It represents a way of extending care and contribution to the wider community during middle adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Local groups and institutions, including faith-based and volunteer organizations, that provide older adults with structured opportunities for social engagement, meaningful activity, and role replacement. These outlets support the Activity Theory and promote integrity in late adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Integrity vs. Despair | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The simultaneous presence of two or more chronic conditions in the same patient. In elderly cancer patients, comorbidities such as anemia, coronary artery disease, congestive heart failure, COPD, renal insufficiency, and arthritis complicate treatment decisions and outcomes.

One of three grandparenting styles identified by Cherlin and Furstenberg (1986). Approximately 55% of grandparents fall into this category — making it the most common style. These grandparents spend time with and enjoy activities alongside their grandchildren but exercise little authority or control over them. They prefer to avoid interfering with parenting decisions and tend to act more like friends to their grandchildren.

The primary motivation for many older adults entering romantic relationships, emphasizing emotional closeness, shared activities, and mutual support rather than procreation or financial merging. Research by Carr (2004a) found that older adults with strong emotional support from friends were less likely to seek new romantic relationships.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A strategy used by older adults to adapt to losses in functioning by finding alternative means to achieve goals. In the context of aging, compensation involves using new resources, technologies, or reorganizing activities when previous methods of functioning are no longer effective or possible.

A neurological adaptation in which older adults recruit additional or alternative brain regions to maintain cognitive performance, compensating for age-related neural changes. Cabeza et al. (2002) documented that high-performing older adults show increased bilateral brain activation compared to younger adults, suggesting neural compensation.

A category of divorced individuals identified by Hetherington and Kelly (2002) who used their divorce experience to grow emotionally but chose to remain single. Like enhancers, the majority of competent loners were women.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Piaget's third stage — ability to reason logically about direct experiences and perceptions. Key abilities: CLASSIFICATION: organizing things into groups according to shared characteristics (by age 8, most children can classify). SERIATION: knowledge that things can be arranged in logical series. CONSERVATION: now mastered. Logic is applied to concrete (direct, real) experiences but NOT yet to abstract ideas. Culture affects content and method of learning (Vygotsky's contrast). Children are 'apprentices in learning.'

The practice of using a barrier contraceptive device (condom) during sexual activity to reduce the risk of pregnancy and sexually transmitted infections. Adults over the age of 40 have been identified as having the lowest rates of condom use among sexually active populations, increasing their STI risk.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A close trusted person in whom one confides personal feelings and experiences. Research by Bookwala et al. (2014) found that having a friend (not a family member) as a confidante was specifically protective against the health impacts of widowhood, reducing somatic depressive symptoms, improving self-rated health, and decreasing sick days.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Social Networks in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A chronic condition in which the heart does not pump blood as efficiently as needed. It is one of the most common reasons for hospitalization among the oldest-old (85+).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: One of the Big Five personality traits characterized by organization, dependability, self-discipline, and goal-directed behavior. Generative adults of both sexes were found to score high on conscientiousness in research studies linking personality to generativity.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The principle that the amount of a substance remains the same (is conserved) when its appearance changes. Preoperational children fail conservation tasks because they center on appearance, use static reasoning, and cannot reverse the process mentally. Classic tasks: liquid poured into a taller, narrower glass appears to be more; candy spread farther apart appears to be more; a rolled-out ball of dough appears to have more. Children typically master conservation around ages 6–7 in the concrete operational stage.

glossary.term: Conservative Religious Communities and LGBT Elders
glossary.def: Gay and lesbian elders who belong to or reside within conservative religious communities may face heightened discrimination and rejection related to their sexual orientation, adding to the cumulative stressors that affect their physical and mental health.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gay and Lesbian Elders | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The Western ideal of love described by Robert Sternberg that combines all three dimensions: passion, intimacy, and commitment. It is difficult to achieve; passion may fade, intimacy may grow and stabilize, and commitment may deepen over time.

Educational programs available to seniors who are not necessarily seeking a formal degree, covering topics such as computer skills, foreign languages, budgeting, and holistic medicine. Participation in continuing education is associated with higher quality of life and cognitive engagement, with those having higher prior education levels being more likely to participate.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Methods used to prevent pregnancy during sexual activity. Women who have not yet reached full menopause (defined as 12 consecutive months without menstruation) remain at risk for pregnancy and should continue using contraception, even if their cycles have become irregular.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Higher-order cognitive mechanisms involved in regulating and directing other mental functions, including selective attention, strategic judgment, and appropriate action. In late adulthood, control processes are particularly impaired, especially in retrieval, and older adults increasingly rely on prior knowledge, general principles, and rules of thumb rather than analytical forethought.

Higher-order cognitive mechanisms involved in the regulation and coordination of information processing, including selective attention, strategic judgment, and appropriate action. These are considered the underlying basis for cognitive impairment in late adulthood, especially impaired retrieval.

A theoretical framework developed by Kahn and Antonucci (1980) and expanded by Antonucci (2001) that conceptualizes social relationships as a convoy of connections differing in levels of closeness. The model proposes that relationships are held together by exchanges of social support, with inner-circle relationships (spouse, close family) remaining stable throughout life, while peripheral relationships (coworkers, neighbors, acquaintances) are less stable and more vulnerable to life changes such as job changes, relocation, or shifts in social roles.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Social Networks in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A theory of social relationships across the lifespan proposing that individuals move through life surrounded by a convoy of close relationships that provide social support, and that the composition of this convoy shifts over time.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The cognitive and behavioral efforts made by an individual to manage the internal and external demands of a stressor that taxes or exceeds their resources. Classified into two main categories: problem-focused coping (addressing the stressor directly) and emotion-focused coping (managing the emotional response to the stressor).

Cognitive and behavioral efforts used to manage internal and external demands perceived as stressful. Carver, Scheier & Weintraub (1989) categorized coping as problem-focused (addressing the stressor directly) or emotion-focused (regulating emotional response). Billings & Moos (1981) also contributed foundational work.

CORE KNOWLEDGE: innate understanding that gravity makes objects fall, moving objects are stopped by solid walls, and adult gaze signals important information. GAZE-FOLLOWING: babies instinctively know that what caregivers look at is important. OWN-RACE EFFECT: infants are much better at recognizing individuals from their own ethnic group than from other groups — related to the fusiform face area; appears by 6 months.

CORPUS CALLOSUM: a long, thick band of nerve fibers that connects the left and right hemispheres of the brain and allows communication between them. As myelination progresses, signals between the hemispheres become quicker and clearer, enabling better thinking and less clumsiness. LATERALIZATION: specialization in certain functions by each side of the brain, with one side dominant for each activity. The left side controls the right side of the body and vice versa. Contemporary research shows the distinction is exaggerated — both sides are involved in almost every skill.

A stress hormone produced by the adrenal glands that, when chronically elevated due to insufficient sleep, keeps the body in a state of alertness and hyperarousal, thereby increasing blood pressure and compounding the negative effects of sleep deprivation.

A stress hormone produced by the adrenal glands. Unlike most hormones that decrease with age, cortisol levels do not decline and can increase with stress exposure. Excess cortisol is associated with hypothalamic damage and contributes to metabolic and hormonal problems seen in aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Surgical procedures performed to alter or improve physical appearance, often used by women experiencing societal pressure to maintain a youthful look. The prevalence of cosmetic surgery is partly driven by media support and anti-aging cultural norms that disproportionately affect women in midlife.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers cited in the context of automatic processing, noting that expertise allows individuals to process information faster and more effectively, making complex responses appear instinctive over time.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The ability to generate novel, useful, or original ideas and solutions. In middle adulthood, creativity is linked to the concept of flow and tends to reflect accumulated experience and crystallized knowledge rather than solely raw processing speed.

The ability to generate novel and valuable ideas, products, or solutions. Csikszentmihalyi (1996) linked creativity to the experience of flow and identified characteristics of highly creative people including curiosity, drive, intellectual values, and the ability to transcend the sense of self. He disputed the notion that creative people are typically unhappy or tortured.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: One of the three components of generativity described by Erikson, referring to the generation of new ideas, inventions, artistic works, or innovations. It contributes to cultural advancement and represents a form of symbolic legacy beyond biological reproduction.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Historical laws in the United States that made same-sex sexual conduct illegal. Gay and lesbian adults who came of age in the 1950s faced these laws without legal protection from victimization; baby boomers witnessed their gradual repeal in various states during the 1960s and 1970s.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gay and Lesbian Elders | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A time when a particular type of developmental growth in body or behavior MUST happen if it is ever going to happen. If the necessary experience or biological event does not occur during this window, the development will not occur at all.

A chronic inflammatory bowel disease that can affect any part of the gastrointestinal tract. It is a recognized risk factor for gallstone formation, likely due to impaired bile acid absorption in the affected small intestine.

A research method that collects data from different individuals at different ages at a single point in time. Vaillant criticized Levinson's use of this design for adult development research, arguing it can lead to erroneous conclusions such as the inevitability of a midlife crisis because it does not track the same individuals over time.

A research design that compares different age groups at a single point in time. In the context of aging and cognition, cross-sectional studies tend to exaggerate cognitive loss because they do not account for cohort differences, education, or historical effects.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The accumulated knowledge, skills, and information acquired over a lifetime through experience and education. Crystallized intelligence tends to increase or remain stable throughout middle and older adulthood, in contrast to fluid intelligence.

The accumulated knowledge of the world acquired throughout one's lifetime, including semantic knowledge, vocabulary, language, history, and geography. Crystallized intelligence increases with age and reflects the wisdom and experience gained over time, giving older adults advantages in knowledge-based tasks.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Accumulated knowledge, vocabulary, and skills gained through experience and education. It remains relatively strong in the young-old (65-84) compared to other cognitive abilities and tends to be preserved longer into aging than fluid intelligence.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A dimension of intelligence that reflects accumulated knowledge, vocabulary, verbal skills, and the application of learned information. Crystallized intelligence tends to be maintained and can even increase with age into late adulthood, representing the benefits of a lifetime of learning and experience.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A type of intelligence that encompasses abilities drawing upon accumulated experience and knowledge. It includes skills such as vocabulary, solving number problems, and understanding texts. Crystallized intelligence shows minimal or no decline with age and may even increase throughout adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A distinction made in intelligence research where crystallized intelligence reflects accumulated knowledge and experience (vocabulary, problem-solving with prior knowledge) while fluid intelligence reflects real-time information processing (logical reasoning, spatial ability, reaction time). Crystallized intelligence is relatively preserved with aging; fluid intelligence declines.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The variation in how menopause is perceived, described, and experienced across different cultures and ethnicities. Cultures that lack specific language for symptoms like hot flashes (e.g., Hmong, Mayan) tend not to report those symptoms. Some cultures, such as Indian and Japanese, view menopause positively as a transition to greater freedom and social status.

Societal and ethnic values that shape perceptions of what constitutes abusive behavior. Research by Dakin and Pearlmutter found that working class White women did not consider verbal abuse as elder abuse, and higher socioeconomic status African American and White women did not consider financial abuse as a form of elder abuse, demonstrating how cultural context affects abuse recognition.

The differences across cultures in how aging individuals are perceived, treated, and valued. Research shows that attitudes about aging women vary by race, ethnicity, culture, and sexual orientation—for example, Black and Latina women hold fewer negative stereotypes about aging than white women, and Asian women may gain greater household authority as they age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The compounding negative effect experienced when an individual faces multiple simultaneous forms of discrimination (e.g., ageism, racism, heterocentrism, sexism). Research by David and Knight (2008) and Hillman and Hinrichsen (2014) highlights that the combined impact exceeds the sum of each individual form of discrimination.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gay and Lesbian Elders | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Age-related decreases in tactile sensitivity, including touch, pressure, vibration, and temperature perception in the skin. Bowden and McNulty (2013) documented age-related changes in cutaneous sensation in the healthy human hand, indicating progressive sensory decline with important implications for safety and quality of life.

CYBERBULLYING: bullying that occurs via Internet insults and rumors, texting, anonymous phone calls, and video embarrassment. Anonymity often brings out the worst in people. Particularly prevalent between ages 11 and 14. Adolescent victims are likely to suffer from depression and may commit suicide. SEXTING: sending sexual photographs; dangers include pictures being forwarded without the subject's knowledge. Adolescent imaginary audience can go viral — thousands can see a private message instantly. Most parents and teachers are unaware of cyberbullying; few laws successfully prevent it.

Minor, everyday stressors such as getting stuck in traffic or arguing with a friend. Research by DeLongis, Folkman, and Lazarus (1988) and Twisk et al. (1999) demonstrated that even small daily hassles can raise blood pressure, alter stress hormones, and suppress immune system function, contributing to illness risk.

The practice of praying every day, used as a measure of individual religious devotion. In the United States, 64% of women report praying daily compared to 47% of men. In Israel's Orthodox Jewish community, a higher percentage of men than women report daily prayer due to religious requirements.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A category of modern biological aging theories that emphasize environmental factors causing cumulative damage to organisms over time. Examples include DNA damage, free radical damage, and mitochondrial damage.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: American psychologist who published 'The Seasons of a Man's Life' in 1978, proposing a stage theory of adult development based on in-depth interviews with 40 men aged 35-45. He argued that a midlife crisis was a normal and expected part of adult development, though his methodology has been widely criticized.

Brown or discolored patches on the skin that develop with aging, primarily as a result of long-term sun exposure. They occur due to uneven distribution and clumping of melanin in the skin and are also referred to as age spots or liver spots. They are a common sign of photodamage accumulated over the lifespan.

Researchers who studied intersecting forms of discrimination among older gay Black men, finding that this group reported higher rates of racism than younger gay Black men and higher levels of perceived ageism than older gay white men, illustrating the compounding effect of multiple minority statuses.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gay and Lesbian Elders | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Authors of a UK-based study examining the rate of hearing problems across adulthood. Their research found that the rate of hearing problems doubled between ages 40 and 55 and tripled by age 64, providing key epidemiological data on the prevalence of hearing decline in middle adulthood.

Researchers who investigated the relationship between generativity and the Big Five personality traits, finding that generative individuals scored high on conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, and openness to experience, and low on neuroticism.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A neurosurgical treatment for Parkinson's disease involving the insertion of an electrode into specific brain regions to provide electrical stimulation. DBS has been shown to result in improved motor functioning and is used when medication alone is insufficient.

A stage of slow-wave, non-REM sleep during which growth hormone is released. This growth hormone stimulates protein synthesis, breaks down fat for energy, and promotes cell division. Decreases in deep sleep are associated with reduced growth hormone secretion and physical decline in aging.

In developmental biology, the sex that an embryo will develop into in the absence of specific molecular signals. Females are considered the default sex in humans, meaning that additional molecular events (initiated by the SRY gene) are required to produce a male individual.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The decision by older adults to continue working beyond traditional retirement age, often motivated by economic factors such as insufficient savings or pension losses, or by psychological benefits such as social contact, purpose, and health improvements. Research indicates that delaying retirement by even one year is associated with a decreased risk of death from all causes.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Intentional, effortful practice of skills aimed at maintaining or improving performance. As adults age, they must be more deliberate in their practice of skills in order to maintain them, because the passive accumulation of experience is less sufficient with advancing age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A historical clinical term used to describe difficulties with memory, language, abstract thinking, reasoning, decision making, and problem-solving, particularly in older adults. Replaced in DSM-5 by the term neurocognitive disorder.

A neurocognitive disorder that can cause inappropriate sexual behavior in older adults as a result of disinhibition and impaired judgment. Dementia is listed by the NIA as a chronic illness adversely affecting sexual functioning.

Statistical estimates of future population characteristics based on current trends in birth rates, death rates, immigration, and age distribution. For example, the U.S. Census Bureau projected that 20.3% of the U.S. population would be 65 or older by 2030, up from 13.7% in 2012.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A significant change in the composition or distribution of a population over time. In the U.S. labor force context, this refers to the aging of the workforce: by 2022, adults aged 55+ were projected to make up 38% of the population, up from 26% in 1992, while young adult labor force entrants (ages 16–24) were projected to shrink by nearly half a percent.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A change in the composition of a population over time. In the context of late adulthood, this refers to the increasing proportion of older adults in the United States and worldwide due to rising life expectancy and declining birth rates.

A model describing the shift from high birth and death rates to low birth and death rates as a country develops economically. Falling fertility rates in both developed and less developed countries have contributed to demographic aging, a hallmark of this transition's later stages.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The formation of new dendritic branches on neurons in adulthood, which is variable in rate and extent. Dendrite growth is considered evidence of lifelong neural plasticity and contributes to cognitive adaptability in older adults.

The extension of dendritic branches on neurons that allows for greater synaptic connectivity. Evidence of dendrite growth in adulthood supports the concept of lifelong neural plasticity and cognitive adaptability.

The reliance of an abusive adult child or other caregiver on the elder for financial resources, housing, or emotional support. This dependency, often exacerbated by substance use, mental illness, or chronic unemployment, is a significant factor that increases the likelihood of elder abuse.

A mood disorder characterized by persistent sadness, loss of interest, and impaired daily functioning. In older adults, depression is closely linked to social isolation caused by bereavement, retirement, and reduced social engagement, and it is a primary risk factor for the development or worsening of substance use disorders.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Substance Abuse and the Elderly | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A mood disorder characterized by persistent sadness, loss of interest, and other emotional and physical symptoms, which older adults are at risk for due to factors such as social isolation, health decline, and loss of loved ones. Volunteering has been associated with lower rates of depression in older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Generativity in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: Depression, Suicide, and Delinquency in Adolescence
glossary.def: DEPRESSION: self-esteem dips at puberty for both sexes; familism is protective. MAJOR DEPRESSIVE DISORDER: deep sadness, hopelessness, lethargy, and worthlessness. LGBT youth have higher risk of depression and anxiety. CLUSTER SUICIDES: several suicides committed by members of a group within a brief period. PARASUICIDE: any potentially lethal action against self that does not result in death — common; completed suicide is not. Girls attempt more; boys complete more. LIFE-COURSE-PERSISTENT OFFENDER: criminal activity begins in early adolescence and continues throughout life. ADOLESCENCE-LIMITED OFFENDER: criminal activity stops by age 21. Delinquency pathway: stubbornness → defiance → shoplifting → more serious crime.

The negative outcome of Erikson's final psychosocial stage, experienced when individuals are unable to accept their life, relationships, or the inevitability of death. Characterized by bitterness, resentment, and regret over missed opportunities and unfulfilled goals.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Integrity vs. Despair | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Nations with advanced economies, high standards of living, and established infrastructure. In the context of global aging, developed countries such as the United States, Japan, Germany, Italy, Canada, and Russia are experiencing significant increases in the proportion of citizens aged 65 and older, largely due to fertility rates that fell below replacement level by the 1970s.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Positive changes, improvements, or growth in abilities, knowledge, or functioning that occur across the lifespan during a given developmental stage. In middle adulthood, developmental gains may include increased wisdom, expertise, emotional regulation, and social competence, even as some physical capacities begin to decline.

Declines or reductions in abilities, physical capacity, or functioning that occur during a given developmental stage. In middle adulthood, developmental losses may include declines in peak physical performance, changes in sensory acuity, and shifts in certain cognitive abilities, though these are often offset by gains in other areas.

The psychological, social, and emotional needs unique to individuals in late adulthood. Erikson's theory was groundbreaking in establishing that older adults have ongoing developmental needs, countering the prior view that old age was merely a period of social restriction and physical decline.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Integrity vs. Despair | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A researcher or clinician who studies the psychological growth and change of individuals across the lifespan, including cognitive, emotional, social, and physical development from birth through late adulthood.

DEVELOPMENTAL PSYCHOPATHOLOGY: links the study of typical development with the study of disorders. General principles: abnormality is normal; disability changes year by year; life may be better or worse in adulthood; diagnosis and treatment reflect social context. MULTIFINALITY: one cause can have many (multiple) final manifestations. EQUIFINALITY: one symptom can have many causes. COMORBIDITY: presence of two or more unrelated disease conditions at the same time in the same person. These principles should lead to caution in diagnosis and treatment.

A group of ideas, assumptions, and generalizations that interpret and illuminate the thousands of observations made about human growth. Provides a framework for understanding how and why people change as they grow older. Theories produce hypotheses, generate discoveries, and offer practical guidance.

A metabolic disease characterized by high blood sugar levels, either due to insufficient insulin production (Type 1) or insulin resistance (Type 2). Diabetes is a recognized risk factor for gallstone development, possibly related to altered bile composition and gallbladder motility.

A metabolic disorder characterized by insulin resistance and elevated blood glucose levels. The American Diabetes Association (2016) standards of medical care emphasize lifestyle modification and pharmacological management. CDC (2014d) reported national statistics on diabetes prevalence.

Diabetes mellitus can adversely affect sexual functioning in older adults, particularly by contributing to erectile dysfunction in men through damage to blood vessels and nerves involved in sexual arousal and response.

A chronic disease in which the body does not properly regulate the amount of glucose in the blood, either due to insufficient insulin production or improper use of insulin by the body's cells. In adults, 90–95% of diagnosed cases are Type 2. As of 2012, 29 million Americans (over 9% of the population) had diabetes. Diabetes is associated with serious complications including cardiovascular disease, diabetic retinopathy, kidney failure, neuropathy, limb amputations, and hearing loss.

Nerve damage caused by long-term high blood glucose levels in people with diabetes. It can cause pain, tingling, numbness, and loss of sensation, particularly in the extremities. Neuropathy is a contributing factor in the approximately 60% of non-traumatic limb amputations that occur in people with diabetes.

Damage to the small blood vessels in the retina caused by diabetes, which may lead to vision loss or blindness. Among diabetics aged 40 or older, 28.5% showed signs of diabetic retinopathy, and more than 4% had the advanced form. It is one of the leading causes of blindness in adults.

glossary.term: Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, 5th Edition (DSM-5)
glossary.def: The standard classification manual of mental disorders published by the American Psychiatric Association in 2013, used by mental health professionals to diagnose substance use disorders and other psychiatric conditions. Its criteria can be difficult to apply to older adults due to age-specific behavioral and social differences.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Substance Abuse and the Elderly | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The primary muscle responsible for breathing, located beneath the lungs. With aging, the diaphragm weakens as part of broader age-related muscle changes, which reduces lung capacity and can contribute to shortness of breath and fatigue.

The second (bottom) number in a blood pressure reading, representing the pressure in the blood vessels when the heart is at rest between beats. Normal diastolic pressure is under 80 mmHg; 80–89 indicates prehypertension; 90 or higher indicates hypertension.

The variety of different foods consumed across food groups. Maintaining dietary diversity is important for ensuring adequate intake of all essential nutrients and is challenged in older adults by sensory decline, tooth loss, and socioeconomic factors.

Federal nutrition recommendations jointly published by the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services (USHHS) and U.S. Department of Agriculture (USDA), most recently cited in this content as the 2015-2020 edition. They provide science-based advice on sodium, fat, added sugars, caloric intake, and physical activity for Americans aged 2 and older.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The mistaken belief that a deviation from some norm is necessarily inferior to the behavior or characteristics that meet the standard. A common error in cross-cultural comparisons that fails to recognize cultural variation as equally valid.

Sensitivity to any particular experience differs from one person to another because of the particular genes each person has inherited, or what happened to them in the past. Some people are more vulnerable than others to certain experiences, usually due to genetic differences.

A neuropsychological test of working memory that requires participants to repeat sequences of digits either forward or backward. It primarily engages the phonological loop component of working memory and shows minimal age-related decline, as it does not heavily tax the central executive.

Researchers who studied sexuality in older adults and highlighted the importance of sexual activity and intimacy throughout the lifespan, emphasizing that many older adults remain highly interested in and engaged in sexual activity.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The phenomenon, noted by Charness and Krampe (2006), in which increasing practice does not yield the same advances in older adults as similar efforts produce in younger individuals. The rate of return for effort diminishes as we age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A research paradigm in which participants are instructed to forget or ignore certain information (e.g., a word list) and remember other information. Older adults show impaired directed forgetting, recalling more words from the 'forget' list than younger adults, which serves as evidence for inhibition theory.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A memory phenomenon in which individuals intentionally suppress or forget specific information when instructed to do so. Age-related differences in directed forgetting in working memory have been studied by Andrés, Van der Linden, and Parmentier (2004), who found older adults show reduced ability to intentionally forget information.

The probability of experiencing a physical or mental impairment that limits daily functioning. Disability risk is one of three factors — along with mortality and morbidity — used by the WHO to calculate Healthy Life Expectancy.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A level of hearing impairment severe enough to interfere with daily communication and functioning. Affects nearly 1 in 4 adults aged 65–74 and approximately 1 in 2 adults aged 75 and older according to NIH data.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A stage in Atchley's retirement model in which retirees experience an emotional letdown or disappointment after the initial excitement of retirement fades, often realizing that retirement does not meet their earlier idealized expectations.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The process by which older adults withdraw from social roles and interactions. Contrasted with Activity Theory; disengagement is associated with lower life satisfaction and more negative views on aging, according to the research supporting Activity Theory.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Integrity vs. Despair | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A theory of aging that proposes aging naturally and inevitably makes a person's social sphere increasingly narrow, resulting in role relinquishment, withdrawal from social roles, and passivity, and that this mutual withdrawal between older adults and society is a natural and appropriate process.

The recognition that older adults are not a homogeneous group but vary widely in health, functioning, ethnicity, gender, socioeconomic status, culture, and lived experience, making generalizations about this population difficult and often misleading.

The capacity to simultaneously attend to and process multiple streams of information or tasks. Divided attention is one of the most significantly impaired attentional processes in older adults, as it places high demands on cognitive resources that diminish with age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The legal dissolution of marriage occurring among individuals aged 65 and older. The rise in divorce rates within this age group, paired with declining remarriage rates, has resulted in slightly more older men living alone than in previous generations.

Livingston (2014) found that 27% of adults aged 45–54 were divorced, with 57% of divorced adults being women, reflecting men's higher likelihood of remarriage. Brown and Lin (2013) noted that while the overall U.S. divorce rate has declined since the 1990s, the rate among those 50 and older has doubled.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A damage/error theory of aging proposing that accumulating, irreparable damage to DNA over time causes cells to deteriorate and malfunction, contributing to the aging process. Sources of DNA damage include ultraviolet radiation, cigarette smoking, and exposure to hydrocarbons such as auto exhaust and coal.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A neurotransmitter involved in motor control, reward, motivation, and executive functioning. In Parkinson's disease, the degeneration of dopamine-producing neurons in the substantia nigra leads to the motor symptoms characteristic of the disorder. L-dopa treatment helps restore dopamine levels.

A societal phenomenon in which aging men are viewed as 'distinguished' while aging women are viewed as 'old.' Men are traditionally valued for achievements, competence, and power, so they are not considered old until physically unable to work, whereas women have been valued for reproductive capabilities and are considered old once postmenopausal. First described by Teuscher & Teuscher (2006) in the context of Western society.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Unauthorized immigrants to the United States who meet five specific criteria: under age 31, entered the U.S. before age 16, lived continuously in the U.S. for at least five years, never convicted of a felony or serious misdemeanor, and graduated from a U.S. high school or served in the armed forces. They cannot become citizens and differ from peers in their original nations.

Drug use becomes widespread from ages 10 to 25, then decreases. Drug use before age 15 is the best predictor of later drug use. ALCOHOL: most frequently abused drug among North American teenagers; heavy drinking may permanently impair memory and self-control by damaging the hippocampus and prefrontal cortex. MARIJUANA: adolescents who regularly smoke are more likely to drop out, become teenage parents, and be unemployed. TOBACCO: slows growth, reduces appetite, causes protein/vitamin deficiencies, damages developing hearts and lungs. GENERATIONAL FORGETTING: each new generation forgets what the previous generation learned about the harm drugs do. Drug use excites the limbic system and interferes with the prefrontal cortex — users are more emotional and less reflective.
Tiny pieces of a fatty protein that accumulate beneath the retina in individuals with dry macular degeneration. Their buildup causes the macula to thin and eventually stop functioning properly.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A condition that occurs when the eyes do not produce sufficient tears, or when tears evaporate too quickly due to incorrect consistency, leading to dryness, irritation, and potential surface damage to the eye. Although it can affect people at any age, nearly 5 million Americans over age 50 experience it. It affects women more than men, particularly after menopause, and women who experienced early menopause are at heightened risk.

The Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders, Fifth Edition, published by the American Psychiatric Association in 2013. The authoritative reference used by clinicians to classify and diagnose mental disorders, including neurocognitive disorders.

glossary.term: Dual Processing: Intuitive vs. Analytic Thought
glossary.def: INTUITIVE THOUGHT: thought arising from emotion or hunch, beyond rational explanation; influenced by past experiences and cultural assumptions. ANALYTIC THOUGHT: thought resulting from analysis — systematic ranking of pros and cons, risks and consequences, possibilities and facts; depends on logic and rationality. Adolescents rely MORE on intuitive thinking than adults. With maturity, adolescents gradually balance formal analytic thinking and emotional, experiential thinking. Brain basis: limbic system activated by puberty; prefrontal cortex matures more gradually — producing the dual processing gap.

The simultaneous loss of both vision and hearing, which increases significantly in midlife and older adulthood. Dawes et al. (2014) documented vision impairment and dual sensory problems in middle age.

The ability to learn or perform two tasks simultaneously. Research (Göthe, Oberauer, & Kliegl, 2007) demonstrated that older adults are significantly impaired in dual-task learning compared to younger adults, who can eventually master both tasks with practice. This deficit is attributed to the reduced capacity of the central executive in older adults.

A Vygotsky-derived assessment approach that measures learning potential rather than current performance. Unlike static IQ tests, the assessor actively intervenes during testing (providing hints, coaching, feedback) to reveal a student's ZPD and capacity to learn from instruction. DA is both diagnostic and developmental — it promotes development while assessing it.

Persistent or recurrent pain during vaginal intercourse, often experienced by women during and after menopause due to reduced estrogen levels causing vaginal atrophy and decreased lubrication. It can significantly affect sexual functioning and quality of life.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A developmental orientation identified by Levinson in which younger adults are primarily motivated by aspirations and plans for the future. This future orientation gives way in midlife to a greater emphasis on the present and on current quality of life, contributing to the psychological shift characteristic of the midlife transition.

glossary.term: Early Childhood Education: Programs and Approaches
glossary.def: CHILD-CENTERED programs (Piaget/Vygotsky influence): stress children's natural inclination to learn through play; self-paced exploration; includes Montessori (individual pride, literacy tasks), Reggio Emilia (creativity in carefully designed setting, originated in Reggio Emilia, Italy), and Waldorf (creativity, social understanding, emotional growth). TEACHER-DIRECTED programs: stress academic subjects taught by a teacher; clear work/play distinction; higher child-adult ratio; less expensive. HEAD START: most widespread U.S. early childhood program, begun in 1965; strongest benefits for children in poverty or with disabilities; vocabulary gains persisted longest. Long-term intensive programs (Perry/High Scope, Abecedarian) show lasting gains.

A form of Alzheimer's disease that develops in individuals younger than age 65, representing approximately 5% of all Alzheimer's cases. Often associated with rare inherited deterministic genes that directly cause the disease, leading to symptom onset in the 30s, 40s, or 50s.

A behavioral risk factor for heartburn and GERD. Lying down shortly after eating reduces the effect of gravity in keeping stomach acid from entering the esophagus, increasing the likelihood of acid reflux.

glossary.term: Eating Disorders: Anorexia, Bulimia, Binge Eating
glossary.def: ANOREXIA NERVOSA: characterized by self-starvation; individual voluntarily undereats and often overexercises. DSM-5 diagnosis requires: significantly low body weight (BMI 17 or lower), intense fear of weight gain, disturbed body perception. Can be fatal. BULIMIA NERVOSA (binge-purge syndrome): bingeing and subsequent purging through induced vomiting and/or laxatives. DSM-5 requires: bingeing and purging at least once a week for three months, uncontrollable urges to overeat, sense of self tied to body shape. BINGE EATING DISORDER: introduced in DSM-5, recognizes bingeing sometimes associated with anorexia. Origins: cultural image, stress, puberty, hormones, childhood and family patterns.

Approach taken by most developmentalists, in which aspects of each of the various theories of development are selectively applied rather than adhering exclusively to one theory. Helps guard against bias and facilitates open-mindedness. Recognizes that no single theory explains everything.

Each person is affected by many social contexts arranged in nested levels: MICROSYSTEM (immediate, direct: family, school, peers, neighborhood), MESOSYSTEM (interactions between microsystems), EXOSYSTEM (external networks: community structures, media, medical institutions), MACROSYSTEM (cultural patterns, political philosophies, economic policies), CHRONOSYSTEM (dimension of time). Each level interacts with all others.

The degree to which cognitive tests and research measures reflect real-world functioning and the abilities needed in everyday life. Ecological validity is particularly important when assessing cognition in the elderly, as standard tests may not accurately capture practical cognitive abilities.

The degree to which research or testing conditions and measured abilities reflect real-life settings and practical, everyday cognitive demands. It is considered key to accurately measuring cognition in the elderly, as standard tests may be normed on younger adults.

A period of significant decline in economic activity, typically involving rising unemployment, reduced consumer spending, and financial instability. The U.S. experienced notable recessions starting in 2001 and again in 2008. During the most recent recession, 55% of adults reported workplace problems such as fewer hours, pay cuts, or switching to part-time work.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Mistreatment of older adults by caregivers or others, which is more likely to occur when the caregiver suffers from emotional problems or substance abuse, the care receiver is frail, confused, and demanding, and the care setting is isolated with few visitors. It can include physical, emotional, financial, and neglect-related harm.

Any knowing, intentional, or negligent act by a caregiver or any other person that causes harm or a serious risk of harm to a vulnerable older adult. Includes physical, sexual, psychological/emotional, financial, and neglect/abandonment forms. Affects at least 1 in 10 older Americans, approximately 4.3 million per year.

Intentional or negligent acts by a caregiver or trusted individual that cause harm or serious risk of harm to a vulnerable older adult. Includes emotional, physical, sexual, and financial abuse, as well as neglect. The National Elder Mistreatment Study (Acierno et al., 2010) examined prevalence and correlates in the United States.

Weeks 3 through 8. Primitive streak becomes the neural tube, forming the brain and spine (CNS). Head takes shape; eyes, ears, nose, and mouth form; heart begins to pulsate; extremities develop. KEY FACT: by week 8, ALL body organs and systems have formed in rudimentary form. After this point development focuses on maturation and growth of each system.

A coping strategy that regulates the emotional response to a stressor rather than addressing the stressor directly. For example, watching a funny movie to reduce exam anxiety. More effective in the short term or when stressors are uncontrollable (e.g., death of a loved one). Long-term reliance on emotion-focused coping is less effective for mental wellness than problem-focused coping.

6 weeks: social smile evoked by human faces. 3-4 months: laughter, often associated with curiosity. 6 months: anger as a healthy response to frustration; sadness appears with cortisol increase. 9 months: fear emerges; stranger wariness (infant cries at unfamiliar faces); separation anxiety (distress when caregiver leaves — if strong after age 3, may be an emotional disorder). Toddlerhood: new emotions including pride, shame, embarrassment, disgust, and guilt, which require self-awareness.

A condition in which an individual lacks intimate emotional connections, even if social contacts are present. Emotional isolation is a primary cause of loneliness particularly among men in late adulthood, according to Brehm et al. (2002).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Social Networks in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A developmental characteristic associated with older adulthood, described by Kemp and Kemp (2002) as contributing to greater stability in late-life remarriages. Emotional maturity leads to more realistic expectations about marital relationships and is associated with positive outcomes in both remarriage and cohabitation.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The ability to manage and modulate one's emotional responses to stimuli. Middle-aged adults demonstrate improved emotional regulation, including greater calmness, reduced neuroticism, and better ability to navigate social situations.

The ability to control when and how emotions are expressed. Possibly due to connections between the limbic system and prefrontal cortex. The MOST IMPORTANT psychosocial accomplishment between ages 2 and 6. EFFORTFUL CONTROL: the ability to regulate one's emotions and actions through effort, not simply through natural inclination. Is a function of executive function. Maturation, learning, and culture all contribute.

A form of social support involving the expression of empathy, affection, encouragement, and comfort. In the context of aging, emotional support from adult children (e.g., showing tenderness, cheering parents up) is associated with greater life satisfaction in older adults, consistent with Socioemotional Selectivity Theory.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Social Networks in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A subjective sense of happiness, life satisfaction, and positive affect. Research indicates that those in the young-old category (65-84) report better emotional well-being than at any other period of adulthood (Carstensen, Fung, & Charles, 2003; George, 2009; Robins & Trzesniewski, 2005).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A component condition of COPD in which the air sacs in the lungs are damaged, reducing the surface area available for gas exchange and making it increasingly difficult to breathe. It is most commonly caused by long-term cigarette smoking.

Also called the post-parental period, this refers to the stage of life when children have grown up and left the family home, typically occurring during midlife. It is considered a normative developmental event because parents generally anticipate that their children will eventually leave. The empty nest creates both positive and negative emotional responses depending on the individual and cultural context.

A concept referring to the great emotional distress, loneliness, and sense of loss experienced by some parents, typically mothers, after their children have left home. It is linked to the absence of alternative roles through which the parent could establish their identity. Research by Bouchard (2013) found that relatively few parents actually report significant loneliness or loss after children leave, questioning the prevalence of this syndrome.

A psychological condition experienced by parents, particularly mothers, when their children leave home. Characterized by feelings of loss, sadness, and purposelessness. Research shows many parents adapt positively, and some experience improved marital satisfaction.
Inflammation or swelling of the brain. In rare cases, shingles can cause encephalitis as a serious complication, particularly in older or immunocompromised individuals.

The first stage of memory processing in which incoming information is transformed into a mental representation suitable for storage. Encoding efficiency tends to decline with age, particularly for deep, elaborative encoding strategies, contributing to poorer subsequent recall in older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A second career pursued after retiring from a primary career, typically in a different field. Encore careers may be motivated by financial need, personal fulfillment, or a desire for continued engagement and purpose in late adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The physiological balance between energy intake and energy expenditure. Disrupted circadian timing due to irregular sleep schedules can impair energy homeostasis, contributing to metabolic dysfunction and weight gain.

The overall vitality and capacity for physical and mental activity. Nutritional adequacy, particularly in terms of caloric intake and micronutrient sufficiency, directly influences the energy levels of older adults.

A category of divorced individuals identified by Hetherington and Kelly (2002) who used the divorce experience as an opportunity for self-improvement and to seek more productive intimate relationships. The overwhelming majority of enhancers were women.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: Entity vs. Incremental Approach to Intelligence
glossary.def: ENTITY APPROACH: sees ability as innate, a fixed quantity present at birth; rejects the idea that effort enhances achievement. Students with this view give up when challenged. INCREMENTAL APPROACH: holds that intelligence can be directly increased by effort; suggests children can master whatever they seek to learn if they pay attention, participate, study, and complete homework. School transitions (middle school, high school) often increase bullying, decrease achievement, depression, and eating disorders — particularly stressful when combined with puberty. HIGH-STAKES TESTS: evaluations critical in determining whether a student will graduate or be promoted.

A dimension of psychological well-being referring to an individual's ability to manage and adapt to their surrounding environment effectively. It is one of the outcomes positively associated with higher levels of spirituality in the National Survey of Midlife data.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Referring to the effects of environmental forces on the expression of an individual's or species' genetic inheritance. All important human characteristics are epigenetic — every trait, psychological as well as physical, is influenced by the interaction of genes and environment.

A type of long-term memory that involves the recall of specific personal experiences, events, and contextual details (e.g., what you had for breakfast, a childhood birthday). Episodic memory shows significant decline with aging, particularly for source memory (remembering where or when information was learned) and free recall tasks.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A type of long-term explicit memory that stores personally experienced events along with their spatiotemporal context—what happened, when, and where. Episodic memory shows greater age-related declines than semantic memory, likely because it requires encoding and retrieving at least two types of information: the event itself and its temporal/spatial context.
