Researchers cited for the finding that the majority of older adults remain in the same location—often the same house—where they lived before retiring, and that the housing crisis has kept many older adults in their suburban homes.

Researchers who noted that if there is a decline in sexual activity for a heterosexual older couple, it is typically attributable to a decline in the male partner's physical health. They also identified that older men who become widowed or divorced tend to pair with younger women, reducing the pool of available partners for older women.

The inability to achieve or maintain an erection sufficient for sexual intercourse, either consistently or intermittently. It becomes increasingly common with age: approximately 4% of men in their 40s, 17% in their 60s, and 47% of men over 75 experience chronic ED. About 50% of men aged 40–70 experience intermittent ED. Medical conditions account for 70% of chronic cases and psychological factors for 10–20%.

A medical condition in men characterized by the persistent inability to achieve or maintain an erection sufficient for satisfactory sexual intercourse. It becomes more common with age and can be associated with conditions such as diabetes, cardiovascular disease, hormonal changes, and psychological factors.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The inability to achieve or maintain an erection sufficient for sexual intercourse. It is a common sexual problem among older men, can be caused by chronic illnesses such as diabetes, and is a significant contributor to decline in sexual activity among older heterosexual couples.

Researchers who emphasized that expertise is a long process resulting from extensive experience and practice over time, highlighting that becoming an expert requires sustained, deliberate effort rather than innate talent alone.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: German-American developmental psychologist and psychoanalyst (1902–1994) who proposed an eight-stage theory of psychosocial development spanning the entire lifespan. His theory was the first to formally address development in old age and coined the stage of Integrity vs. Despair.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Integrity vs. Despair | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Erikson's third psychosocial crisis, occurring during early childhood (ages 3–6). Children undertake new skills and activities and feel GUILTY when they do not succeed at them. GUILT: self-blame people experience when they do something wrong. SHAME: people's feeling that others blame them, disapprove of them, or are disappointed in them. SELF-CONCEPT: a person's understanding of who he or she is, incorporating self-esteem, physical appearance, personality, and personal traits. PROTECTIVE OPTIMISM: young children are not realistic — they believe they are strong, smart, and able to achieve any goal; this confidence helps them persist.

Erikson's fourth psychosocial crisis, occurring in middle childhood (ages 6–11). Characterized by tension between productivity and incompetence. Children attempt to master culturally valued skills and develop a sense of themselves as either industrious or inferior, competent or incompetent. Self-pride depends not just on actual accomplishments but on how others, especially peers, view those accomplishments. SOCIAL COMPARISON: comparing one's attributes to those of others — helps children value themselves and abandon the rosy self-evaluation of preschoolers. GROWTH MINDSET develops. EFFORTFUL CONTROL: ability to regulate emotions and actions through effort.

glossary.term: Erikson Stage 5: Identity vs. Role Confusion
glossary.def: Erikson's fifth psychosocial crisis, occurring during adolescence. The central question: 'Who am I?' IDENTITY: consistent definition of oneself as a unique individual in terms of roles, attitudes, beliefs, and aspirations. IDENTITY ACHIEVEMENT: Erikson's term for the attainment of identity — understanding who one is in accord with past experiences and future plans. ROLE CONFUSION (identity diffusion): adolescent does not seem to know or care what his or her identity is. Several aspects of identity search — especially sexual and vocational — have become more arduous than when Erikson first described them.

TRUST VS. MISTRUST (year 1): infants learn basic trust if the world is a secure place where basic needs are met. Failure leads to a suspicious, pessimistic adult. AUTONOMY VS. SHAME AND DOUBT (year 2/toddlerhood): toddlers succeed or fail in gaining self-rule over their actions and bodies. Failure leads to an adult who is easily shamed and has insufficient autonomy.

Eight developmental stages, each characterized by a challenging developmental crisis. Built on Freud but differed: stages emphasize family and culture rather than sexual urges, and three additional adult stages beyond adolescence. First two infant stages: Trust vs. Mistrust (year 1) and Autonomy vs. Shame and Doubt (year 2).

The seventh of Erikson's eight psychosocial stages, occurring during middle adulthood. In this stage, individuals face the challenge of contributing positively to society through family, work, and community involvement versus becoming self-absorbed and stagnant. Erikson believed this was the longest of all his developmental stages.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Positive psychological strengths or qualities that Erikson believed individuals achieve upon successfully resolving each of his eight psychosocial stages. Each stage has a corresponding virtue; for example, the virtue of Stage 7 (Generativity vs. Stagnation) is Care.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A malignancy of the esophagus described as one of the most lethal forms of cancer in the United States. It is a serious complication that can result from prolonged, untreated heartburn or GERD, as chronic acid exposure damages the esophageal lining over time.

The muscular tube connecting the throat (pharynx) to the stomach through which food passes during swallowing. It is the structure most affected in heartburn and GERD, as prolonged contact with stomach acid can injure its lining.

A primary female sex hormone produced largely by the ovaries. During and after menopause, estrogen levels decline significantly, contributing to symptoms such as hot flashes, vaginal dryness, sleep disturbances, and increased risk of osteoporosis. Estrogen is also essential for bone formation and growth.

A group of steroid hormones produced primarily in the ovaries in females. Estrogen appears to have a protective effect on the heart and circulatory system, and also possesses antioxidant properties that protect cells against free radical damage, slow the aging process, and contribute to females' longer average lifespan.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A form of hormone therapy that supplements estrogen alone, previously used commonly to treat menopausal symptoms. Its use has declined due to associations with breast cancer, stroke, and blood clot formation. Lower doses may be prescribed with more frequent monitoring for women with severe symptoms.

Variations in the number, health outcomes, and life expectancy of older adults across different racial and ethnic groups. Disparities are influenced by socioeconomic status, access to healthcare, cultural practices, and historical inequities.

The variation in rates of multigenerational living across racial and ethnic groups in the U.S. In 2010, Asian families had the highest rate (25%), followed by Black families (23%), Hispanic families (22%), and White families (13%), reflecting cultural values around intergenerational family living.

The differential access to resources and life opportunities based on ethnicity, which creates large discrepancies in income, health, and social status by old age. Immigrant elders often face multiple compounding challenges related to cultural differences in care, housing, and stereotyping.

A subtype of prospective memory that involves remembering to perform an action when a specific external event or cue occurs (e.g., giving someone a message when you see them). This type shows less age-related decline than time-based prospective memory and older adults can perform comparably or slightly better than younger adults in real-world settings.

The application of cognitive and social skills to resolve real-life challenges. Unlike laboratory tasks requiring rapid processing of meaningless information, everyday problem solving in older adults is often preserved or even superior, as older adults tend to rely on expertise, social input, and more effective strategies.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Integrates explanations for many issues in human development. Humans have two long-standing biologically based drives: survival and reproduction. Proposes selective adaptation — genetic variations that are particularly beneficial when the environment changes become more prevalent across generations. Focuses more on the nature side of development.

A theory rooted in evolutionary psychology suggesting that genes associated with aging-related disorders (e.g., Alzheimer's disease) are not eliminated by natural selection because they manifest only after an individual's primary reproductive years, making them invisible to selective pressure.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A set of higher-level cognitive processes managed by the prefrontal cortex, including selective attention, strategic judgment, inhibition of unwanted responses, planning, and coordinating thoughts. Executive function declines with age, particularly in tasks requiring rapid shifting between tasks.

A set of higher-order cognitive processes managed largely by the prefrontal cortex, including selective attention, strategic judgment, planning, inhibition, and coordinating thoughts. Executive function declines with age and is part of control processes.

The cognitive ability to organize and prioritize the many thoughts that arise from various parts of the brain, allowing a person to anticipate, strategize, and plan behavior. Combines three essential abilities: MEMORY (typically short-term or working memory at this age), INHIBITION (ability to control responses and stop before acting), and FLEXIBILITY or shifting (ability to see things from different perspectives, share, take turns). Becomes evident by age 2 and develops throughout the lifespan. Considered foundational — not inborn but developed through family interactions and experience.

A set of higher-order cognitive processes including response inhibition, abstract thinking, and cognitive flexibility. Research by Malone et al. (2016) found that men rated higher in generativity at midlife demonstrated stronger executive functioning in late adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Physical activity performed to improve or maintain health and fitness. In midlife adults, exercise builds muscle, increases metabolism, helps control blood sugar, increases bone density, and relieves stress. Despite its benefits, fewer than half of midlife adults exercise, and only about 20% exercise frequently and strenuously enough to achieve meaningful health benefits.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Physical activity recognized as an effective and straightforward stress-reduction strategy with numerous health benefits. Cited by Salmon (2001) as a great stress reduction activity that does not require complex interventions. Exercise reduces physiological arousal and contributes to overall mental and physical health.

Physical activity and structured exercise during the middle adult years, considered one of the most important behavioral factors for maintaining health and counteracting secondary aging. Regular exercise in midlife is associated with reduced risk of cardiovascular disease, improved mental health, better sleep, maintained muscle mass and bone density, and enhanced cognitive functioning.

EXPERIENCE-EXPECTANT: brain growth requiring certain universal experiences for normal maturation to occur. Must happen for typical development. EXPERIENCE-DEPENDENT: growth from individual experiences that might or might not happen — enriches but not required for normal development. Romanian orphan research demonstrated that lack of experience-expectant interactions causes long-term cognitive and emotional deficits.

An individual who has acquired extensive domain-specific knowledge, skills, and automated processes through years of practice and experience. Experts differ from novices in that they process information more efficiently, recognize patterns more quickly, and apply intuitive judgment in their field.

Specialized skills and knowledge that pertain to a particular topic or activity, developed through extensive experience and practice over time. Expert thought is characterized as intuitive, automatic, strategic, and flexible. Everyone develops some level of selective expertise in personally meaningful areas.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The highly developed knowledge and skills acquired through extensive practice and experience in a specific domain. Charness & Krampe (2006) and Ericsson et al. (2006) examined how aging and deliberate practice interact to shape expert performance.

A body of research demonstrating that older adults with domain-specific expertise can compensate for age-related cognitive declines through strategic adaptation. Examples include older typists looking farther ahead in text to compensate for slower motor speed, and older chess players focusing on a smaller, more efficient set of possible moves.

A mechanism by which older adults use accumulated knowledge, experience, and efficient strategies to compensate for age-related cognitive declines, particularly in work contexts. This explains why older workers rarely perform poorer on the job despite slowing in basic processing tasks.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Conscious, intentional recollection of facts and events (also called declarative memory). Explicit memory loss in late adulthood is generally greater than implicit memory loss, making it a more prominent feature of normal cognitive aging.

Conscious, intentional recollection of facts and events (also called declarative memory). Explicit memory shows greater decline in late adulthood compared to implicit memory and includes episodic memory for specific events and semantic memory for general knowledge.

A type of long-term memory that requires conscious, intentional recollection of information, including episodic and semantic memory. Explicit memory, particularly episodic memory, shows notable decline with age, especially under conditions of free recall without cues.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A type of long-term memory that requires conscious, deliberate effort to encode and retrieve. It includes both semantic memory (general knowledge) and episodic memory (personal events). Explicit memory is more vulnerable to age-related decline than implicit memory.

Physical, emotional, and financial resources provided outside the immediate family to help caregivers and older adults manage the challenges of aging and long-term care. As the ratio of elderly to potential caregivers increases, families will increasingly need such external support.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: One of the Big Five personality traits characterized by sociability, assertiveness, positive emotionality, and outward energy. Research found that highly generative individuals scored high on extraversion, suggesting that social engagement is associated with generative development.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: An external incentive such as money, grades, or praise that motivates behavior. In the context of flow theory, Csikszentmihalyi noted that individuals in a state of flow are not concerned with extrinsic rewards, as the activity itself provides sufficient satisfaction.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The personality trait of extroversion, characterized by sociability and outward orientation, which is associated with better adjustment following widowhood. McCrae and Costa (1988) found that older adults who are more extroverted tend to fare better after the loss of a spouse, likely due to their greater engagement with social support networks.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A form of Alzheimer's disease caused by deterministic (inherited) genes. Individuals who inherit these rare genes tend to develop early-onset symptoms in their 30s, 40s, or 50s. Accounts for some cases of early-onset Alzheimer's disease.

A cultural value system that prioritizes family loyalty, solidarity, and mutual obligation, prompting family caregiving among all relatives. Familism strongly influences the degree of filial responsibility felt by adult children in many cultures.

A role identified by Neugarten and Weinstein (1964) in which a grandparent provides guidance, wisdom, and counsel to family members across generations. Like the family historian role, this was more commonly reported by grandfathers than grandmothers.

A role identified by Neugarten and Weinstein (1964) in which a grandparent serves as the keeper and transmitter of family history, stories, traditions, and cultural heritage to younger generations. This role was found to be more commonly adopted by grandfathers than grandmothers.

Non-biological, non-legal individuals who are considered part of one's family due to close emotional bonds and mutual support. Lesbian and gay elders disproportionately rely on these chosen family networks for social support, given they are twice as likely to live alone and four times less likely to have adult children compared to heterosexuals.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gay and Lesbian Elders | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: FAMILY STRUCTURE: legal and genetic relationships among relatives living in the same home (nuclear, single-parent, extended, stepfamily, polygamous, etc.). FAMILY FUNCTION: the way a family works to meet the needs of its members — providing basic material necessities, encouraging learning, helping develop self-respect, nurturing friendships, fostering harmony and stability. FAMILY-STRESS MODEL: two factors increase the likelihood of dysfunction in every structure, ethnic group, and nation: LOW INCOME/POVERTY and HIGH CONFLICT. Effects of poverty are cumulative; low SES is especially damaging during middle childhood. Shared vs. nonshared environments: influence of shared environment shrinks with age; effect of nonshared environment (friends, schools) increases.

A blood test used to diagnose diabetes or prediabetes by measuring blood glucose levels after a period of fasting (typically overnight). Normal fasting glucose is under 100 mg/dL. Levels between 100–125 mg/dL indicate prediabetes, and levels of 126 mg/dL or higher on two separate tests indicate diabetes.

Dairy items such as milk, cheese, and yogurt with reduced fat content that provide calcium, vitamin D, and protein. These are recommended for older adults as part of the 25% protein-rich food group to support bone health while limiting saturated fat intake.

Foods high in fat content that can trigger or worsen heartburn and GERD by relaxing the lower esophageal sphincter and delaying stomach emptying, thereby increasing the likelihood of acid reflux into the esophagus.

Psychological responses experienced more commonly by women than men as they age, driven by societal emphasis on youthful beauty, the double standard of aging, and concerns about identity and social value. Bromberger, Kravitz, & Chang (2013) noted that women experience heightened fear, anxiety, and concern about their identity as they age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: An anticipatory anxiety about growing older, physical decline, and mortality. While popularly assumed to be the main cause of midlife crises, MacArthur Foundation research found that the crisis was more commonly triggered by specific major life events than by fear of aging itself.

A form of age-related hair loss in women in which hair becomes less dense overall and the scalp becomes increasingly visible. Unlike male-pattern baldness, it does not typically follow a receding hairline pattern but rather a general thinning across the scalp.

The average number of live births per woman over her lifetime in a given population. Declining fertility rates are one of the two primary drivers of global population aging. In more developed countries, fertility dropped from nearly 3 children per woman around 1950 to below 2 by the 1970s. In less developed countries, it dropped from an average of 6 children in 1950 to 2 or 3 by 2005.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A biological stress response in which the body mobilizes physiological resources (e.g., increased heart rate, hormonal changes) in preparation to either confront or flee from a perceived threat. It is one of the recognized sources of stress and contributes to heightened physiological arousal.

The cultural and moral obligation of adult children to care for their aging parents. The extent and nature of filial responsibility varies worldwide and by culture, and it can differ between sons (who tend to feel stronger obligation) and daughters (who tend to feel stronger affection).

The illegal or improper use of an elder's funds, property, or assets without their informed consent. Costs seniors nearly 3 billion dollars per year in the United States, affecting approximately 5% of elders. It is the second most common form of elder abuse after psychological/emotional abuse. Prosecution is difficult due to victim reluctance and crowded court dockets.
Research finding that adults in middle adulthood make their best financial decisions around age 53, demonstrating better economic understanding and judgment than both younger and older adults.

The increased economic burden experienced by sandwich generation adults who are simultaneously supporting aging parents and their own children. Only 28% of those supporting both parents and children reported living comfortably, compared to 41% of those who were not supporting parents. About one-third were just making ends meet, compared to 17% of those without the additional burden of aging parents.

Economic difficulty experienced by older adults, particularly those living alone. Only 33% of older adults living alone report living comfortably, compared to 49% of those living with others. Additionally, 12% of those living alone report lacking money for basic needs, versus 5% of those living with others.

A characteristic of expert cognition in which experts across all fields demonstrate greater curiosity and creativity, enjoy challenges, and experiment with new ideas or procedures. Experts must continue to take on challenging rather than routine tasks to continue growing their knowledge.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Small spots, specks, or 'cobwebs' that drift across the field of vision. They occur when the vitreous humor, the gel-like substance filling the interior of the eye, slowly shrinks with age and becomes stringy. These strands cast tiny shadows on the retina, which are perceived as floaters. Most are harmless, but sudden onset, darkening, or vision obscuration may indicate retinal tearing, infection, or inflammation. People who are highly myopic, have diabetes, or have had cataract surgery are at increased risk.

A state of deep, focused engagement in an intrinsically motivating activity, first described by Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi. Achieving flow is associated with enhanced creativity, life satisfaction, and psychological well-being, and is particularly relevant in middle adulthood.

A mental state described by Csikszentmihalyi (1990) in which an individual is completely present and fully absorbed in a task. Characteristics include blocking outside distractions, experiencing great joy or intellectual satisfaction, pursuing intrinsic rather than extrinsic rewards, and losing one's sense of self. Flow is considered an important factor in creativity and is well-developed in middle adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A concept developed by Mihaly Csikszentmihalyi (1990) describing a state of optimal experience in which a person is fully immersed and engaged in an activity, experiencing deep enjoyment, creativity, and a sense of fulfillment. Associated with intrinsic motivation and peak performance.

The capacity to reason and solve novel problems independently of previously acquired knowledge. Fluid intelligence is associated with working memory and processing speed and tends to decline in middle and later adulthood.

The capacity to learn new ways of solving problems and performing activities quickly and abstractly, without relying on previously acquired knowledge. Fluid intelligence tends to decrease with age, particularly in late adulthood, and favors younger individuals in tasks requiring speed and novel problem-solving.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A dimension of intelligence that reflects the capacity for novel problem solving, pattern recognition, logical reasoning, and adaptation to new situations independent of prior knowledge. Fluid intelligence shows significant decline with aging, particularly after middle adulthood, as it depends heavily on processing speed and working memory.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A type of intelligence referring to information processing abilities, including logical reasoning, remembering lists, spatial ability, and reaction time. Fluid intelligence is dependent on neurological functioning and declines with age, particularly in later adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The legal process by which a lender takes possession of a property when the borrower fails to make mortgage payments. During the 2008 recession, increased foreclosures significantly affected middle-aged adults, compounding the financial stress caused by job losses and declining retirement savings.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Sexual activity that precedes intercourse, including kissing, touching, and oral stimulation. Middle-aged and older couples may engage in more foreplay as a means of compensating for age-related changes in sexual response, such as the need for more direct stimulation or reduced lubrication.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A traditional, structured approach to grandparenting in which grandparents maintain clear generational boundaries and avoid interfering with parenting. According to Hayslip, Henderson, and Shore (2003), fewer grandparents today are adopting this more formal role as more take on active caregiving responsibilities.

Piaget's fourth and final stage of cognitive development. Characterized by: systematic logic, ability to think about ABSTRACT ideas, and HYPOTHETICAL THOUGHT (reasoning using propositions and possibilities that may not reflect reality). DEDUCTIVE reasoning (top-down): reasoning from a general statement or principle through logical steps to deduce specifics. INDUCTIVE reasoning (bottom-up): reasoning from one or more specific experiences to a general conclusion; may be less cognitively advanced than deduction. Examples: adolescents can multiply unreal numbers, consider gross national product, and test hypotheses in labs — not just concrete real-world operations.

The approach used by novices in which they follow explicit, step-by-step rules and procedures to solve problems, in contrast to the more fluid and intuitive approach of experts who draw on accumulated experience.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: 1. INSTRUMENTAL aggression: hurting someone to get or keep an object, territory, or privilege (goal-oriented). 2. REACTIVE aggression: an angry, retaliation response to being hurt or frustrated. 3. RELATIONAL aggression: attempting to hurt someone's peer relationships by excluding them, spreading rumors, or using social rejection. 4. BULLYING aggression: repeated, systematic efforts to inflict harm on a weaker person. PROSOCIAL behavior (empathy, compassion) and antisocial behavior are both innate and universal; antisocial behavior generally diminishes over the preschool years.

A conceptual life stage referring to adults aged 85 and older (the oldest-old), characterized by increased physical decline, greater dependence, more serious chronic illnesses, higher rates of nursing home residence, and decreased social engagement compared to the third age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The final years of adulthood characterized by significant physical and cognitive decline, increased dependency on others, and proximity to death. Contrasts with the Third Age (active retirement years). Coined within gerontological literature to describe the period of advanced old age and frailty.

People over age 65, and often over age 85, who are physically infirm, very ill, or cognitively disabled. Frailty is most common in the months preceding death (terminal decline), and most older adults become frail if they live long enough, though North Americans who live to 95 are independent for most of those years.

A clinical syndrome associated with aging, characterized by weakness, reduced physical activity, exhaustion, slow walking speed, and unintentional weight loss. Sarcopenia is considered a significant contributing factor to frailty and functional impairment in older adults.

A clinical state characterized by limited life expectancy and near-exhausted functional reserves. Frail individuals are not considered candidates for more toxic forms of chemotherapy due to their reduced ability to tolerate aggressive treatment.

A clinical syndrome common in older adults characterized by decreased physiological reserve, weakness, exhaustion, weight loss, slowness, and low physical activity. Sarcopenia is a major contributing factor to frailty, which increases vulnerability to adverse health outcomes such as falls, fractures, hospitalization, and mortality.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who found that more than 80% of lesbian and gay older adults engage in some form of wellness or spiritual activity, highlighting resilience factors within this population despite significant health and social disparities.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gay and Lesbian Elders | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Unstable molecules produced during normal metabolism that can damage cell components, cause mutations in DNA, and contribute to the aging process. Estrogens in females act as antioxidants that neutralize free radicals, providing women with a cellular protective advantage over men.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Unstable, potentially harmful byproducts — specifically oxygen free radicals — produced when mitochondria use oxygen to generate energy. Free radicals are missing an electron and destabilize surrounding molecules by stealing electrons, creating a chain reaction of molecular damage. They are associated with cancer, atherosclerosis, cataracts, and neurodegeneration.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A memory retrieval process in which a person must reproduce previously learned information without any external cues or prompts. Free recall shows significant age-related decline and is considered a sensitive measure of episodic memory deterioration in aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Two cardiologists who in the 1959 identified Type A Behavior among their heart patients. They found that this behavioral pattern — characterized by competitiveness, impatience, hostility, and time urgency — was associated with double the risk of heart disease compared to individuals without these traits (Type B Behavior).

The region of the brain responsible for judgment, decision-making, impulse control, and evaluation of an action's consequences. The frontal lobe develops more slowly in boys and young men than in females, contributing to poorer judgment, increased risk-taking, and higher rates of death from smoking, excessive drinking, accidents, drunk driving, and violence in males.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Plant-based foods rich in vitamins, minerals, antioxidants, and fiber. MyPlate for Older Adults recommends these comprise 50% of an older adult's diet, supporting disease prevention, digestive health, and immune function.

A decline in an individual's ability to perform everyday activities and tasks independently. In the context of aging, sarcopenia-related muscle loss is a significant cause of functional impairment and is associated with decreased quality of life and increased healthcare costs.

A researcher who noted cultural variation in aging attitudes, specifically that as Asian women age, they attain greater respect and have greater authority within the household—contrasting with Western norms that tend to diminish older women's perceived value and status.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The primary inhibitory neurotransmitter in the central nervous system. Reduced GABA receptor activity in aging is associated with cognitive decline and is implicated in the normal age-related deterioration of brain function alongside changes in glutamate signaling.
A small organ located beneath the liver that stores and concentrates bile. It is the site where gallstones develop from accumulated fatty materials, bile pigments, and calcium deposits.

Hard particles that develop in the gallbladder, composed of fatty materials, bile pigments, and calcium deposits. They range in size from a grain of sand to a golf ball and typically take years to develop, though they can form over a few months in some individuals. About 75% cause no symptoms, but symptomatic stones can cause upper abdominal pain and, if lodged in ducts, may be life-threatening.

A chronic digestive condition in which the lower esophageal sphincter (LES) relaxes too frequently, allowing stomach acid to flow back into the esophagus repeatedly. GERD is a more severe and frequent form of heartburn and can lead to serious complications including esophageal cancer if left untreated.

The practice by which post-divorce parents regulate when and how they introduce their new romantic partner to their children. Anderson et al. (2004) identified three approaches: gradual introduction (47%), open and transparent disclosure from the outset (40%), and withholding disclosure until cohabitation or remarriage is likely (13%).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers cited in the study of late adulthood who identified late adulthood (age 65 and older) as the fastest growing age division of the United States population, providing a foundational reference for understanding demographic trends in aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Older adults aged 50 and over who identify as lesbian or gay. Approximately 3 million such individuals exist in the United States as of 2014, with projections exceeding 7 million by 2025. They represent one of the least researched demographic groups in gerontology and face unique physical, mental, and social health challenges compared to heterosexual older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gay and Lesbian Elders | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The observed phenomenon in which women tend to outlive men in most countries, resulting in a greater proportion of older women than older men in late adulthood. Biological, behavioral, and social factors all contribute to this disparity.

The process by which individuals learn and internalize gender-specific behaviors, norms, and values from society. In the context of religion, women have been socialized to internalize behaviors linked with religious values such as cooperation and nurturance more than men, contributing to gender differences in religious participation.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The unequal distribution of resources and opportunities based on gender that continues into late adulthood. Examples include irrational fear limiting women's independence, the pattern of women marrying older men, and men seeking medical help less frequently than women.

The process by which information encoded in a gene is used to produce a functional product (typically a protein). Differences in patterns of gene expression between males and females, particularly related to X-linked genes, are theorized to contribute to the female longevity advantage.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A three-phase model of the stress response developed by Hans Selye (1946). The three phases are: (1) mobilization of physiological resources (alarm phase), (2) coping phase (resistance), and (3) exhaustion phase, in which the organism fails to cope adequately and depletes its resources, leading to potential health breakdown.

A theory proposed to explain age-related cognitive decline, stating that a pervasive reduction in the speed of neural processing underlies most observed declines in cognitive performance in older adults. It suggests that slower processing affects virtually all cognitive tasks to varying degrees.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The extent to which research findings can be applied or extended to populations, settings, or circumstances beyond those directly studied. Levinson's study was criticized for limited generalizability due to its small, homogeneous, all-male sample.

The projected trend in which children born in the United States today may have a shorter lifespan than their parents — a reversal of the historical pattern of increasing life expectancy — primarily attributed to rising rates of obesity and sedentary behavior.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A concept described by Erikson referring to the concern for establishing and guiding the next generation. It encompasses procreativity (having children), productivity (work and career contributions), and creativity (generating new ideas and products). It is the central developmental task of middle adulthood in Erikson's psychosocial theory.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Erik Erikson's seventh psychosocial stage, occurring in middle adulthood, in which individuals focus on contributing to society through raising children, mentoring others, productivity, and creativity. Failure to achieve generativity results in stagnation.

Erikson's concept of the drive to create, nurture, and contribute to future generations. In late adulthood, generativity can be fulfilled through work, volunteer activities, grandparenting, and mentoring, and it is a key psychological benefit of remaining active.

A personality characteristic, originally described by Erik Erikson, referring to a concern for establishing and guiding the next generation and contributing to society beyond oneself. According to Baltes and Kunzmann, generativity is one of the personality factors that contributes to the development of wisdom.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A psychosocial concern, identified by Erikson, for establishing and guiding the next generation. Generativity continues into late adulthood through mentoring, volunteering, caregiving, and legacy-building activities.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A psychosocial concept, originally proposed by Erik Erikson, referring to the concern for establishing and guiding the next generation. In the context of late adulthood, generativity extends beyond midlife and includes activities such as volunteering, mentoring, and raising grandchildren, reflecting an ongoing investment in the well-being of future generations.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Generativity in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Erikson's concept originally associated with the midlife stage (Generativity vs. Stagnation), referring to a concern for establishing and guiding the next generation and contributing to society. Erikson noted this concern can extend into late adulthood, motivating older adults to remain productive and engaged.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Integrity vs. Despair | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Erikson's seventh psychosocial stage, associated with middle adulthood. Generativity involves a concern for establishing and guiding the next generation through parenting, mentoring, or contributing to society. Stagnation occurs when an individual becomes self-absorbed and fails to contribute meaningfully to others or society.

The degree to which an organism's genetic expression and developmental processes are buffered against errors, mutations, or disturbances. Females exhibit greater genetic and developmental stability than males due to their ability to select the better X chromosome and inactivate the inferior one.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A common sexually transmitted infection caused by the herpes simplex virus (HSV), characterized by recurrent outbreaks of sores or blisters in the genital area. Older adults remain susceptible and can contract and transmit the virus regardless of age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A sexually transmitted infection caused by certain strains of the human papillomavirus (HPV), presenting as warts on the genital or anal areas. They are transmitted through skin-to-skin sexual contact and remain a risk for sexually active older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The complete set of genetic material (DNA) in an organism. Researchers scan the entire genome to compare genetic variants in individuals with longer versus average or shorter lifespans in order to identify genes that promote longevity.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The physical distance between a grandparent's home and the grandchildren's home, which significantly influences the degree and type of grandparent involvement. In developed nations, high societal mobility often results in grandparents living far from grandchildren, which can reduce in-person contact and push grandparents toward remote or technology-mediated interaction.

A prominent researcher and one of the main investigators of the Harvard Study of Adult Development, who argued that longitudinal rather than cross-sectional research designs are necessary to understand adult development. He considered the midlife crisis to be a rare occurrence among his study participants and challenged Levinson's conclusions.

The branch of medicine focused on health care of elderly people. Due to the complexity of treating cancer alongside age-related conditions, expertise in geriatrics alongside oncology is recommended for those caring for elderly cancer patients.

A developed European nation with 20% of its population aged 65 and older in 2012, projected to increase to 27.9% by 2030 and 30% by 2050, making it one of the most rapidly aging societies in the world.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The multidisciplinary scientific study of aging and older adults, encompassing biological, psychological, social, and cultural dimensions of the aging process.

The scientific study of aging and the biological, psychological, and social changes that occur with advancing age. Gerontologists study factors including stress, genetics, immune function, and hormonal changes to understand the aging process.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who studied kinkeeping and found that kinkeepers provide approximately 34 hours of kinkeeping activities per month. They also found that the more activities a kinkeeper took on and the more kin they helped, the greater the stress and higher the levels of depression experienced. Notably, their study included caregiving activities such as providing transportation, making repairs, and preparing meals alongside traditional kinkeeping activities.

Researchers who examined the role of media in shaping perceptions of aging, noting that the media influences how women view aging by selling anti-aging products and supporting cosmetic surgeries designed to help women appear younger.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Increased difficulty seeing clearly in the presence of bright or contrasting light sources, such as car headlights or streetlights, particularly in low-light conditions. It is a common age-related change resulting from reduced pupillary response and lens changes, making nighttime driving especially challenging for older adults.

An unofficial, invisible barrier representing organizational discrimination in the workplace that limits the career advancement of women and minorities beyond a certain level, regardless of their qualifications or achievements. It is cited as a reason why women employed at large corporations are twice as likely as men to quit their jobs.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: An invisible barrier that prevents women and minorities from advancing to upper-level positions in organizations despite qualifications. Barreto, Ryan, & Schmitt (2009) examined this phenomenon in the 21st century, documenting persistent structural barriers to gender equality in the workplace.

A term coined by Elsesser & Peplau (2006) referring to obstacles that hinder the formation of cross-sex friendships in the workplace, including concerns about sexual misinterpretation and organizational norms.

An eye disease characterized by loss of peripheral vision, most commonly due to a buildup of fluid in the eye that increases intraocular pressure and damages the optic nerve. Progresses from peripheral to central visual field loss; can result in total blindness in advanced stages. The most common cause of blindness in the United States. Higher risk in African Americans over 40 and all adults over 60, those with diabetes, or those with a family history. No cure, but progression can be slowed with early diagnosis, medicated eye drops, and routine eye exams.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A widespread, general deterioration across multiple cognitive domains. Research on aging suggests that global cognitive decline is not typical in normal aging; rather, certain abilities such as processing speed and fluid intelligence decline while others such as vocabulary and crystallized knowledge are preserved or increase.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A broad measure of cognitive ability encompassing memory, attention, and calculation. Malone et al. (2016) found that men with higher generativity ratings at midlife had stronger global cognitive functioning in late adulthood, suggesting long-term cognitive benefits of generative engagement.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The competitive pressure that American workers and businesses face from workers and companies in other countries. Advances in technology and globalization have increased this competition, and workers who can adapt to technological changes or are willing to relocate have better employment prospects, particularly in midlife.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Statistical estimates of future population composition based on current trends in fertility, mortality, and migration. According to the NIA, the proportion of the global population aged 65 and older is expected to grow from 8% to 16% by 2050, with 524 million currently over age 65.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The biochemical process by which the body converts glucose into energy. Sleep disturbances, particularly irregular sleep schedules, can impair glucose metabolism in middle adulthood, increasing the risk of metabolic disorders including type 2 diabetes.

An excitatory neurotransmitter involved in synaptic plasticity, learning, and memory. Reduced receptor activity for glutamate in aging brains is associated with declines in memory storage and cognitive flexibility.

Researchers who found that having children residing with their mothers reduces the mothers' likelihood of marriage, specifically with respect to marrying a man without children. They also found that having experienced family divorce as a child is associated with a greater willingness to marry a partner with children.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A researcher cited in the context of older adult sexuality who noted that ageist myths about elderly asexuality can become internalized, leading older adults to have greater difficulty accepting their own sexuality.

Researchers who studied differences in divorce motivations by age. They found that early adulthood divorces tended to be more angry and conflictual, with partners blaming each other, while midlife divorces were more characterized by emotional distance, having grown apart, or a cooling of the relationship.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: An AARP researcher who surveyed grandparents raising grandchildren and identified the primary reasons for this arrangement, including parental drug and alcohol problems, mental illness, incarceration, divorce, chronic illness, homelessness, child neglect or abuse, military deployment, and parental death.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Generativity in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The grandparenting role as experienced by men, which has historically been understudied compared to the grandmother role. Research (Sorenson & Cooper, 2010; Waldrop et al., 1999) shows that grandfathers value their grandchild relationships just as strongly as grandmothers. For many men, being a grandfather offers an opportunity to express affection and engage in nurturing behaviors that societal norms around masculinity and fatherhood had previously discouraged. Grandfathers more commonly adopted the roles of family historian and advisor.

A parent of one's parent; an individual who takes on a new familial role often in middle adulthood. The nature and extent of this role varies widely based on culture, geography, family structure, and personal preference, ranging from distant contact to daily caregiving.

The degree to which a grandparent participates in and influences a grandchild's daily life. Involvement is shaped by multiple factors including geographic proximity, cultural background, family structure, the grandparent's health and motivation, and crisis situations within the family. Higher involvement may include co-residence, caregiving, and authority over child-rearing decisions.

The functions and responsibilities assumed by grandparents in contemporary families. These roles have evolved and vary widely, ranging from surrogate parents raising grandchildren to more distant, ceremonial figures. Cultural context significantly shapes grandparenting expectations and involvement.

A framework developed by Cherlin and Furstenberg (1986) categorizing grandparents into three types based on frequency of contact and degree of authority: Remote (30%), Companionate (55%), and Involved (15%). Bengtson (2001) later noted that grandparents may adopt different styles with different grandchildren and may shift styles over time as family circumstances change.

A family arrangement in which grandparents assume primary caregiving and legal responsibility for their grandchildren, often due to parental inability to care for the children. According to the 2014 American Community Survey, over 5.5 million children under 18 were living in grandparent-headed families in the United States.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Generativity in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A term referring to the increasing trend of divorce among adults in mid-to-late life. While still less common than divorce in younger age groups, the rate of gray divorce has risen in recent decades, posing distinct financial, social, and health challenges particularly for older women.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The trend, documented by Brown and Lin (2013), of rising divorce rates among middle-aged and older adults (aged 50+) in the United States between 1990 and 2010, contrasting with declining divorce rates in younger cohorts.

A natural consequence of aging in which hair follicles produce decreasing amounts of melanin, causing hair to lose pigment. Graying typically begins at the temples and spreads throughout the head. It commonly starts in the 30s, occurs earlier in white populations, and later in Asian populations.

Brain tissue composed primarily of neuronal cell bodies and dendrites, crucial for processing new experiences. Its volume is reduced in every part of the aging brain, in part because the cortex becomes thinner with each decade of adulthood.

Brain tissue composed primarily of neuronal cell bodies that is crucial for processing new experiences. Gray matter volume decreases in every part of the brain during adulthood, partly because the cortex thins with each decade.

A demographic term referring to the rapid and significant increase in the proportion of older adults (65+) in the United States population. This trend is driven largely by the aging of the baby boomer generation and increased life expectancy, and has wide-ranging effects on work, healthcare, housing, social security, caregiving, and adaptive technologies.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A term coined by Brown and Lin (2013) referring to the increasing divorce rate among adults aged 50 and older. While the overall U.S. divorce rate has declined since the 1990s, the divorce rate among those 50 and older has doubled. Contributing factors include reduced social stigma around divorce, increased financial independence of older women, and greater human longevity making the prospect of remaining in an incompatible marriage less appealing.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The trend noted by Livingston (2014) in which adults aged 55 and older are remarrying at higher rates than in the past. In 2013, 67% of adults aged 55–64 and 50% of adults 65 and older had remarried, compared to 55% and 34% respectively in 1960.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A demographic phenomenon referring to the global increase in the proportion of older adults (aged 65 and over) relative to the total population, driven by declining fertility rates and improvements in longevity. This trend is occurring in both developed and less developed nations, though at different rates and magnitudes.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who studied the concept of linked lives by examining middle-aged parents and their adult children. They found that parents whose adult children were dealing with personal problems (such as alcoholism, mental health issues, trouble with the law, or poor social relationships) reported more negative affect, lower self-acceptance, poorer parent-child interactions, and more family relationship stress, with single parents faring the worst.

Researchers who defined the distinction between religiosity and spirituality and analyzed 2005 MIDUS data to find that higher spirituality was associated with better psychological well-being outcomes including lower negative affect, higher positive affect, personal growth, purpose in life, positive relationships, self-acceptance, environmental mastery, and autonomy.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A complex emotional response experienced by some surviving spouses after the death of an ill partner. The surviving spouse may feel relief that caregiving responsibilities have ended and that the spouse is no longer suffering, but this relief is often accompanied by guilt for having such feelings. The emotional issues are described as complex and intermingled.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A hormone released during deep sleep that plays critical roles in stimulating protein synthesis, breaking down fat to supply energy, and stimulating cell division. A decline in deep sleep leads to decreased growth hormone release, contributing to physical aging.

GROWTH SPURT: relatively sudden and rapid physical growth during puberty. Sequence: WEIGHT increase usually precedes HEIGHT increase; then a MUSCLE SPURT occurs; growth of limbs precedes growth of the torso. Organ growth: lungs triple in weight; heart doubles in size and heartbeat slows. PRIMARY SEX CHARACTERISTICS: parts of the body directly involved in reproduction (vagina, uterus, ovaries, testicles, penis). SECONDARY SEX CHARACTERISTICS: physical traits not directly involved in reproduction but indicating sexual maturity (body shape, hair patterns, breasts, voice changes).

Researchers who found that both personal health behaviors (regular exercise, healthy weight, not smoking) and social behaviors (including involvement in religious-related activities) are positively related to optimal health outcomes.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: Guardians and Legal Professionals as Abusers
glossary.def: Paid professionals such as guardians and lawyers who are trusted to make decisions on behalf of elders but who may exploit their position to financially or otherwise abuse the elder. Recognized as a category of perpetrators beyond family members.

The sense of taste. Occurs when molecules released by chewing food stimulate taste buds on the tongue, roof of the mouth, and throat lining. Ages relatively well compared to other senses; significant changes are typically not noticed until the 60s. After age 50, taste sensory cells begin to gradually decrease.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The sense of taste, mediated by taste buds on the tongue and palate. Age-related decline in gustation reduces the ability to perceive sweet, salty, bitter, sour, and umami flavors, often leading older adults to add excessive salt or sugar to food.

Sensory cells located in the inner ear that detect movement and fluid shifts to signal the brain about body position and orientation, contributing to balance. Their function declines with age, contributing to balance problems in late adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A small organ in the skin that produces hair strands and contains melanocytes responsible for producing the pigment melanin. With aging, hair follicles produce less melanin, slow hair growth, shrink in size, and may eventually stop producing new hairs altogether.

Small structures in the skin from which individual hairs grow. With aging, many hair follicles stop producing new hairs, resulting in a slowed rate of hair growth and progressive hair loss. The loss of pigment-producing cells (melanocytes) in follicles causes graying of hair.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A near-universal phenomenon of aging in which the rate of hair growth slows, many hair follicles stop producing new hairs, and hair strands become smaller and finer. The extent and pattern of hair loss are largely genetically determined, differing between men (male-pattern baldness) and women (female-pattern baldness).

A researcher who was the first to use the word 'stress' in a psychological manner. While studying the effects of an ovarian hormone on rats, he discovered that nearly any injected hormone produced similar sickness, and concluded that the aversive experience of handling and injection — not the hormone — caused physiological arousal and health problems such as ulcers. He coined the term 'stressor' and developed the General Adaptation Syndrome model.

A landmark 75-year longitudinal study conducted by Harvard University researchers, including George Vaillant, designed to identify the factors associated with optimum health and potential across the adult lifespan. Vaillant found that midlife crises were rare among participants, contradicting Levinson's claims.

Researchers who proposed the Inhibition Theory (1988) of cognitive aging, arguing that deficits in inhibitory control — specifically the inability to suppress attention to irrelevant information — are a primary cause of cognitive decline in older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Hawaii had the highest Healthy Life Expectancy among U.S. states, with individuals at age 65 expected to experience 16.2 additional years of good health, as determined from 2007–2009 CDC data.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The phenomenon by which normal human cells can only divide a finite number of times — approximately 40 to 60 times — before they stop dividing. Named after Leonard Hayflick, it is the basis of the Cellular Clock Theory of aging and is evidenced in cells studied in vitro.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The maximum number of times a normal human somatic cell can divide before it stops dividing due to telomere shortening. Named after Leonard Hayflick, this concept is foundational to cellular theories of biological aging and explains why cells eventually lose their capacity to replicate.

Researchers who examined evolving grandparent roles and found that as more grandparents step in as primary or sole caregivers, fewer are adopting a traditional, formal grandparenting role. Their work reflects the dynamic and changing nature of grandparenting in contemporary society.

High-density lipoprotein cholesterol, often called 'good' cholesterol because it helps remove other forms of cholesterol from the bloodstream. Berglund et al. (1999) studied HDL-subpopulation patterns in response to dietary fat reduction.

Authors of a demographic study reporting that in 2000 there were about 10 people aged 85 and older for every 100 persons between ages 50 and 64, and projecting that the number of older adults requiring support from their children is expected to more than double by 2040.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A biological mechanism that protects the brain when malnutrition disrupts body growth. The brain is the LAST part of the body to be damaged by malnutrition. A lifelong process — in adults, blood flow is diverted to major organs during physical assaults like major bleeding events.

The wide-ranging effects that an increasingly older population has on the healthcare system, including increased demand for medical services, long-term care, geriatric specialists, and chronic disease management. The Graying of America is expected to significantly strain healthcare infrastructure and resources.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The degree to which individuals of a given sex access and use medical services. Men are less likely than women to have health insurance, establish a regular relationship with a physician, or seek treatment for medical conditions, leading to delayed diagnoses and poorer health outcomes that contribute to shorter male lifespans.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A WHO metric that measures the average number of equivalent years of full health a newborn baby is expected to have, taking into account current age-specific mortality, morbidity, and disability risks. In 2015, the global HLE was 63.1 years, up from 58.5 years in 2000.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A potential methodological confound in research on volunteering and health outcomes, whereby older adults who volunteer may already be healthier than those who do not, making it difficult to determine whether volunteering causes better health or whether healthier individuals are simply more able to volunteer.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Generativity in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The APA (2016) reported that hearing loss is significantly associated with mental health issues including depression, social isolation, and cognitive decline, highlighting the need for early intervention and support.

A progressive decline in auditory function that becomes increasingly prevalent during middle adulthood. According to UK research (Dawes et al., 2014), rates of hearing problems double between ages 40 and 55 and triple by age 64. In U.S. samples, hearing problems jump from about 5.5% before age 40 to 19% among adults aged 40–69. Challenges include difficulty understanding speech in noisy environments and reduced ability to hear high-frequency sounds.

A broad category of conditions affecting the heart, including heart defects, heart rhythm problems, and narrowed, blocked, or stiffened blood vessels (cardiovascular disease). It is the number one cause of death in the United States and worldwide, accounting for 23.5% of all U.S. deaths in 2013. Heart disease typically develops slowly over time and first appears in midlife. Symptoms differ by sex: males more commonly experience chest pain, while females more often experience shortness of breath, nausea, and extreme fatigue.

Cardiovascular disease can interfere with sexual functioning in older adults by making it difficult for both men and women to achieve orgasm, due to reduced circulation and associated physical limitations or fear of exertion.

A complication of heart disease in which the heart cannot pump enough blood to meet the body's needs. It can result from various conditions that weaken or damage the heart, including coronary artery disease, hypertension, and heart attacks. It is distinct from a heart attack and represents a chronic, progressive condition.

Also called acid indigestion or pyrosis, heartburn is a common digestive problem in adults caused by stomach acid backing up into the esophagus. Prolonged contact with digestive juices injures the esophageal lining and causes burning discomfort. In the U.S., 60 million people experience it at least once a month and 15 million experience it daily.

Researchers who studied tacit knowledge, finding that it is practical, experience-based, and increases with age. Their work contributed to understanding how non-academic, real-world problem-solving skills develop across the lifespan.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The gradual decrease in stature that occurs universally across all races and sexes as part of the normal aging process. People typically lose nearly one-half inch every 10 years after age 40, with more rapid loss after age 70, resulting in a total loss of 1 to 3 inches over a lifetime. It is related to changes in bones, muscles, and joints, including spinal compression and reduced muscle tone.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Parents who hover over their emerging-adult child and are ready to swoop down if any problem arises. This differential treatment may cause sibling rivalry or resentment.

Researchers who found that while the number of older adults volunteering declines with age, the number of hours that older adults volunteer does not show significant decline until they are in their late 70s.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Generativity in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The assumption that heterosexuality is the default or superior sexual orientation, leading to the marginalization of lesbian, gay, bisexual, and other non-heterosexual individuals. In older adults, heterocentrism can compound other forms of discrimination such as ageism and racism, negatively affecting health and well-being.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gay and Lesbian Elders | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Referring to an organism that produces two types of gametes with respect to sex chromosomes. In humans, males are heterogametic because they carry one X and one Y chromosome (XY), meaning their sex-determining gametes differ from one another.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A group that is diverse and varied in its characteristics, experiences, and outcomes. Gay and lesbian elders are described as a heterogeneous population because their development is shaped by differing cohort experiences, racial and ethnic backgrounds, geographic locations, and religious affiliations.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gay and Lesbian Elders | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A chronic medical condition in which the force of blood against artery walls is consistently too high (typically defined as 130/80 mmHg or above), increasing risk of heart disease, stroke, and kidney disease. Documented extensively by the CDC (2014a, 2014b, 2014c).

Often called 'good' cholesterol, HDL absorbs cholesterol and carries it back to the liver, where it is flushed from the body. Higher levels of HDL reduce the risk of heart attack and stroke. HDL is factored into the total cholesterol calculation along with LDL and triglycerides.

An age-related auditory change in which the ability to detect sounds at higher pitches (frequencies) diminishes over time. It is a hallmark feature of age-related hearing decline and contributes to difficulty understanding speech, particularly consonant sounds that are higher in frequency.

Advanced cognitive processes that develop through social interaction and cultural influence, as distinguished from innate elementary mental functions. Characteristics: consciously aware, voluntarily controlled, mediated by cultural tools, and social in origin. Examples: language, logical reasoning, problem-solving, planning, self-regulation, metacognition.

Researchers who extensively documented the physical and mental health disparities, social support differences, and cumulative discrimination faced by lesbian and gay older adults. Their work is a foundational reference for understanding this population in gerontology.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gay and Lesbian Elders | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who introduced and elaborated on the concept of triple jeopardy to describe the compounded forms of discrimination — ageism, racism, and sexism — experienced simultaneously by older minority women, which can severely and adversely affect their wellbeing in late adulthood.

A brain structure that serves as the primary memory center and is one of the main sites of adult neurogenesis. It is particularly vulnerable to age-related shrinkage and is critically involved in the formation and retrieval of explicit memories.

A brain structure critical for memory formation and consolidation. It is one of the primary sites of neurogenesis in adulthood and experiences notable shrinkage during senescence, contributing to memory decline in late adulthood.

A brain structure in the medial temporal lobe critical for the formation and retrieval of memories. With aging, synaptic loss specifically in the hippocampus contributes to memory impairment. It is also affected in Alzheimer's disease.

A brain region critically involved in learning and memory formation. In Alzheimer's disease, the brain cells in the hippocampus are often the first to be damaged, which is why memory loss typically appears as one of the earliest symptoms of the disease.

The statistical phenomenon in which Hispanic Americans have the highest life expectancy among U.S. demographic groups despite other socioeconomic challenges. In 2012, Hispanic females had a life expectancy of 83.7 years and Hispanic males 78.9 years.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Among all racial and ethnic groups, Hispanic males had the highest rate of labor force participation in 2012 at 76%, compared to 71% of White males, 72% of Asian males, and 64% of Black males ages 16 and older. All racial and ethnic group participation rates were expected to decline by 2022.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A virus that attacks the immune system and, if untreated, can lead to AIDS (Acquired Immunodeficiency Syndrome). In 2014, people aged 50 and older accounted for 16.7% of new HIV diagnoses in the United States (7,391 of 44,071), up from 15.4% in 2005, according to the CDC.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Gay older adults have an elevated risk of infection from HIV and other sexually transmitted illnesses compared to heterosexual older adults, as reported by the CDC (2008). This health disparity is a key consideration in the clinical care of aging gay men.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gay and Lesbian Elders | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: HIV infection among adults aged 50 and older is a growing public health concern. CDC (2014e) reported diagnoses of HIV infection in this age group in the United States from 2010–2014, highlighting that older adults are often overlooked in prevention and testing efforts.

A significant public health concern in late adulthood. According to the Office on Women's Health (2010), nearly 25% of people living with HIV/AIDS in the United States are aged 50 or older. Only 25% of single or newly partnered adults 50+ reported using a condom at last sexual contact, and 40% had never been tested for HIV.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A concept in self theory describing how most older people feel their personalities and attitudes have remained stable over their life span, even as they recognize physical changes. Objects and places become more precious to older adults as a means of maintaining identity and continuity of self.

A model of long-term care that provides services to older adults in their own homes or local community settings rather than in institutional facilities. This approach is considered the least restrictive care alternative and has become increasingly preferred over nursing home placement.

The body's tendency to maintain internal physiological equilibrium or stability. Stress is defined in part as a response that disturbs homeostasis, requiring the organism to mobilize resources in order to restore balance.

Data describing the sex of homicide victims. According to the FBI (2014), of 11,961 homicides in the U.S. in 2014, 77% of victims were male, illustrating the disproportionate exposure of males to lethal violence.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Referring to an organism that produces only one type of gamete with respect to sex chromosomes. In humans, females are homogametic because they carry two X chromosomes (XX), meaning all their gametes contain an X chromosome.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A stage in Atchley's retirement model that immediately follows actual retirement, during which retirees travel, pursue hobbies, and engage in activities they were unable to do while working. This phase is marked by enthusiasm and high activity.
