glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who found that religious organizations are the primary settings for encouraging and providing volunteer opportunities for older adults, and documented that approximately 40% of adults in their 60s engage in volunteerism.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Generativity in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Age-related shifts in hormone levels, such as decreased estrogen in postmenopausal women and reduced testosterone in older men, that can affect libido, lubrication, erectile function, and overall sexual satisfaction in later life.

glossary.term: Hormonal Stress Theory (Neuroendocrine Theory of Aging)
glossary.def: A theory of aging proposing that the hypothalamus gradually loses its ability to regulate hormones as we age, leading to metabolic problems. This decline is linked to excess cortisol from chronic stress, which damages the hypothalamus and is associated with age-related conditions such as diabetes, thyroid problems, osteoporosis, and orthostatic hypotension.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A medical treatment used to supplement declining levels of estrogen and/or progesterone in menopausal women to alleviate symptoms. HRT has been associated with increased risks of breast cancer, stroke, and blood clots, leading to more cautious prescribing practices. Most women do not have symptoms severe enough to require HRT.

Researchers who provided foundational documentation of the distinction between fluid and crystallized intelligence across the lifespan, demonstrating that crystallized intelligence increases with age while fluid intelligence tends to decrease. Their work is referenced in studies of cognitive aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A personality trait and component of Type A behavior characterized by being quick to get upset and experience angry arousal. Research has identified hostility and competitiveness as the components of Type A behavior most harmful to heart health. Hostile individuals also tend to lack health-protective social support networks due to their negative personality style.

A common vasomotor symptom of perimenopause and menopause characterized by a sudden surge of adrenaline that awakens the brain from sleep, produces sweating, and causes a rapid change in body temperature. Hot flashes can be highly disruptive to sleep and comfort, and may take significant time to subside.

A societal concern arising from the Graying of America, encompassing the need for age-appropriate, accessible, and affordable housing options for older adults. This includes independent living communities, assisted living facilities, nursing homes, and modifications to existing homes to support aging in place.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A state of heightened physiological and psychological alertness caused by elevated cortisol levels, often resulting from insufficient sleep. Hyperarousal is associated with increased blood pressure and difficulty falling or staying asleep.

A chronic medical condition defined as blood pressure of 140/90 mmHg or higher, in which blood flows through the vessels with greater force than normal. It affects approximately 1 in 3 American adults (70 million people), with only half having it under control. Often called the 'silent killer' because most people experience no symptoms. It strains the heart, increases the risk of heart attack and stroke, can damage the kidneys, and when uncontrolled in early and middle adulthood may damage brain white matter and lead to cognitive problems later in life.
Chronically elevated blood pressure. Sleep deprivation contributes to hypertension through mechanisms including elevated cortisol levels that maintain hyperarousal and increase vascular pressure.

A region of the brain that regulates hormonal balance in the body. According to the Hormonal Stress Theory, the hypothalamus loses its regulatory efficiency with age, leading to hormonal imbalances and metabolic problems including diabetes, thyroid dysfunction, and osteoporosis.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The process of forming possible explanations or diagnoses based on available information. Both skilled and novice doctors generate several hypotheses within minutes of a patient encounter, but skilled clinicians produce more accurate hypotheses because they can filter out misleading symptoms and distractors (Norman, 2005).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The psychosocial achievement of Stage 5 in Erikson's theory (Identity vs. Role Confusion), typically resolved during adolescence. Erikson argued that resolving identity issues was a prerequisite for achieving generativity in middle adulthood, as unresolved identity conflicts could impede generative development.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The psychological challenge of maintaining a coherent sense of self in the face of age-related changes. Women in midlife may experience heightened concern about their identity as they age, feeling pressure to prove themselves as productive and valuable members of society (Bromberger, Kravitz, & Chang, 2013).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The psychological process by which a widowed older adult must redefine their sense of self after years of identifying as a husband or wife. This process is challenging because much of the older adult's identity may have been intertwined with their role as a spouse, making the transition particularly disorienting.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The capacity of the body's immune system to identify and combat pathogens, disease, and foreign substances. Chronic stress and anxiety — which can be exacerbated by experiencing ageism and stereotype threat — can adversely affect immune functioning, making individuals more susceptible to illness.

A reduction in the effectiveness of the immune system's ability to fight off infections and disease. Chronic sleep deprivation is a known cause of immune suppression, leaving individuals more vulnerable to illness.

The gradual decline in immune function that occurs with aging. Males experience faster deterioration of cellular immunity with age than females, making men more susceptible to viral and bacterial infections over time and contributing to shorter male lifespans.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The capacity of the body's immune defenses to resist infection and disease. Adequate nutrition in older adults is linked to improved immune function, including better antibody response and reduced susceptibility to illness.

The gradual deterioration of the immune system that occurs with aging, resulting in reduced immune response to infections, decreased vaccine efficacy, and increased susceptibility to diseases. Research indicates sex and gender differences in immunosenescence may partly explain the longer average lifespan observed in women compared to men.

Unconscious memory that influences behavior without deliberate recollection, including procedural skills and conditioned responses. Implicit memory is relatively more preserved in late adulthood compared to explicit memory.

Unconscious memory that influences behavior without deliberate recollection, including procedural skills and conditioned responses. Implicit memory is more resistant to age-related decline than explicit memory in late adulthood.

A form of long-term memory that operates unconsciously and does not require deliberate recollection. It includes procedural memory (skills, habits) and priming effects. Implicit memory is largely preserved in older adults compared to explicit memory systems.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A type of long-term memory that operates without conscious awareness or effort, encompassing procedural skills, classical conditioning, and priming effects. Implicit memory shows few declines with normal aging and remains relatively intact in older adults.

A qualitative research method involving detailed, open-ended conversations with participants to explore their experiences, perceptions, and feelings. Levinson used in-depth interviews as the primary data collection method for his study, which, while rich in detail, contributed to limitations in sample size and objectivity.

The socioeconomic inequality that accumulates over a lifetime based on employment, savings, and benefits such as Social Security, which creates large disparities in financial resources in old age. Poverty magnifies the stratification effects of gender, ethnicity, and age.

The ability of an older adult to live autonomously without requiring full-time care or assistance. Maintaining independent living is a central goal of the Selective Optimization with Compensation model, and strategies such as compensation and optimization are employed specifically to sustain this independence as long as possible.

A living situation in which an older adult resides on their own or with a spouse without dependence on institutional care. The majority of older adults prefer and achieve independent living, with only a small percentage requiring institutional placement.

One of the world's two most populous nations alongside China. By 2050, India is projected to have a larger older population than any of the developed nations studied, though smaller than China's older population, reflecting the global scale of demographic aging in less developed regions.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The considerable variation among people of the same age group in their physical, cognitive, and psychosocial characteristics and development. Middle adulthood is particularly notable for wide individual differences due to the cumulative effects of different genetic backgrounds, lifestyle choices, cultural factors, and life experiences over decades.

The cognitive ability to generalize from particular examples to broader principles or patterns. According to the Seattle Longitudinal Study, inductive reasoning increases with age until one's 70s and is one of four cognitive tasks on which middle-aged adults outperform their younger selves.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Classic theory (Freud): infants store no memories in the first year. Modern view: very young infants can remember when experimental conditions are similar to real life, motivation is high, and special measures aid retrieval. Repeated reminders are more powerful than single reminders. Context is crucial for infants younger than 9 months. Rovee-Collier's mobile experiment showed 3-month-olds could remember after 2 weeks with a reminder session.

A biological response involving immune activation in tissues, which can be triggered by excess fat cells. Scientists hypothesize that fat cells may produce harmful chemicals that promote inflammation in blood vessels throughout the body, including the brain, potentially contributing to the development of Alzheimer's disease in obese individuals.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: Information Processing: Knowledge Base, Control Processes, Automatization
glossary.def: Information-processing theory compares human thinking to a computer: sense → seek relevant info → analyze → express conclusions. KNOWLEDGE BASE: extensive prior knowledge makes it easier to master new, related information. Control processes (executive processes): mechanisms combining memory, processing speed, and knowledge to regulate information flow — include SELECTIVE ATTENTION, METACOGNITION, and METAMEMORY. AUTOMATIZATION: some processes become so well-practiced they no longer require conscious thought. HUBS develop where massive numbers of axons meet, linking domains like reading and social skills.

A form of social support involving the provision of advice, guidance, or information. Research from the Berlin Aging Study found that older adults whose adult children primarily provided informational support (such as giving advice) reported lower life satisfaction compared to those who received emotional support.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Social Networks in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A theory proposed by Hasher and Zacks (1988) arguing that cognitive decline in older adults is primarily due to difficulty with inhibitory functioning — the inability to suppress attention to less relevant information. Evidence comes from directed forgetting research showing older adults recall more 'forget' list words than younger adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The cognitive ability to suppress irrelevant, distracting, or no-longer-relevant information from entering or remaining in working memory. Inhibitory control declines with age and is thought to contribute to increased susceptibility to distraction and poorer performance on complex memory and attention tasks in older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The cognitive capacity to suppress attention to less pertinent or irrelevant information while focusing on task-relevant information. According to inhibition theory, older adults experience deficits in this capacity, making it harder for them to block out distracting or unwanted information.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: Injury Control and Three Levels of Prevention
glossary.def: Practices that anticipate, control, and prevent dangerous activities given children's physical abilities and impulsiveness. THREE LEVELS: PRIMARY prevention — changes overall background conditions (e.g., safety surfaces, car seats, bike helmets, medication safety containers). SECONDARY prevention — averts harm in a high-risk situation (e.g., stopping a car before hitting a pedestrian, traffic lights). TERTIARY prevention — actions taken after an adverse event to reduce harm or prevent disability (the 'golden hour' of immediate medical treatment).

The non-specific, first-line immune defense system composed of the skin, mucous membranes, cough reflex, stomach acid, and specialized cells that alert the body to threats. With age, these cells lose effectiveness in communication, making it harder for the body to mobilize its defenses against pathogens.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: In the Convoy Model of Social Relations, the innermost layer of an individual's social convoy, consisting of the closest and most stable relationships such as a spouse and immediate family members. These relationships are expected to remain stable across the lifespan regardless of situational changes.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Social Networks in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Silent, internalized thought that develops from private speech (around age 7+). For Vygotsky, inner speech is the culmination of language development — the mechanism used for planning, reasoning, self-regulation, and abstract thinking. It represents the full internalization of the language structures used in social life.

A sleep disorder characterized by persistent difficulty falling asleep, staying asleep, or experiencing restorative sleep. Among Americans diagnosed with a sleep disorder, 24% suffer from insomnia according to the National Sleep Foundation (2015).

The most common sleep disorder in adults aged 60 and older, characterized by difficulty falling asleep and/or staying asleep. Causes in older adults include medications, pain, medical or psychiatric conditions, and anxiety about sleep. Treatment with over-the-counter aids is typically only effective short-term.

Placement of an older adult in a residential care facility such as a nursing home. As of 2015, only 3.2% of adults over 65 lived in institutions, but this rate increases sharply with age: 1% for ages 65–74, 3% for ages 75–84, and 10% for ages 85 and older.

A form of prejudice that is embedded in the norms, policies, and practices of social institutions such as healthcare, employment, and media, rather than existing only at the individual level. According to Nelson (2016), ageism is one of the most institutionalized forms of prejudice in contemporary society.

More complex actions that are important to independent living and require some intellectual competence and forethought, such as paying bills, driving a car, managing medications, and preparing meals. The ability to perform IADLs may be even more critical to self-sufficiency than ADL ability.

A hormone produced by the pancreas that helps glucose in the blood enter cells to provide them with energy. In Type 2 diabetes, cells become resistant to insulin's effects, and the pancreas may eventually lose the ability to produce sufficient quantities. When pancreatic beta cells cease functioning in Type 2 diabetics, insulin injections become necessary.

A condition in which cells in the muscles, liver, and fat tissue do not respond properly to insulin, reducing the uptake of glucose from the blood. It is the typical initiating mechanism of Type 2 diabetes. As resistance increases, the pancreas must produce more insulin to compensate, and over time the beta cells may become exhausted and fail.

A condition in which cells in the body do not respond effectively to insulin, leading to elevated blood glucose levels. Irregular sleep schedules in midlife women are associated with increased insulin resistance, an important indicator of metabolic health and diabetes risk.

The positive outcome of Erikson's final psychosocial stage, characterized by acceptance of one's life, accomplishments, and imperfections, as well as acceptance of the inevitability of death and others' deficiencies. Results in a sense of contentment and wisdom.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Integrity vs. Despair | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Erikson's eighth and final psychosocial stage, occurring in late adulthood, in which individuals reflect on their lives through a life review. Those who feel their life was meaningful and well-lived achieve integrity, while those who feel regret or believe their life was wasted experience despair.

Erikson's eighth and final psychosocial stage, occurring in late adulthood, in which individuals reflect on their lives and either achieve a sense of fulfillment and acceptance (integrity) or experience regret and bitterness over missed opportunities (despair).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Erikson's eighth and final psychosocial stage, occurring in late adulthood. It involves a retrospective accounting of one's life, evaluating how well it was lived. Successful resolution leads to wisdom and acceptance; unsuccessful resolution leads to despair, bitterness, and regret over missed opportunities.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Integrity vs. Despair | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The eighth and final psychosocial stage in Erik Erikson's theory of development, occurring in late adulthood. Individuals reflect on their lives; those who feel satisfaction and fulfillment achieve ego integrity, while those who feel regret and disappointment experience despair. Successful resolution results in the virtue of wisdom.

Social and familial connections between people of different generations, including parents and adult children, grandparents and grandchildren. These relationships are shaped by factors including need, geographical proximity, childhood memories, cultural values, and emotional closeness, and can include both positive bonds and ambivalence.

Relationships and interactions between individuals of different generations, including grandparent-grandchild bonds, parent-adult child relationships, and multigenerational family dynamics. Studied extensively by Bengtson (2001) and others in the context of kinkeeping and family support.

The process by which externally mediated social activities are transformed into internal mental processes. For Vygotsky, higher mental functions originate in social interaction and are absorbed inward through participation in culturally meaningful activities. It is the bridge between social learning and individual cognition.

The process by which older adults absorb and accept negative cultural stereotypes about aging—including beliefs about asexuality—as applying to themselves, which can diminish their sexual self-concept and willingness to engage in sexual activity.

A United Nations agency that sets international labor standards, develops policies, and devises programs promoting decent work for all. In the context of midlife work, the ILO is cited to note that most developed nations legally guarantee a minimum number of paid vacation days, unlike the United States.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: TIMSS (Trends in Math and Science Study): international assessment of math and science skills of fourth- and eighth-graders. PIRLS (Progress in International Reading Literacy Study): measures reading ability of fourth-graders; girls ahead of boys in verbal skills in every nation; boys ahead in math/science. PISA (Programme for International Student Assessment): measures problem-solving and cognition in daily life for 15-year-olds in 50+ nations; U.S. students score worse than on PIRLS or TIMSS. HIDDEN CURRICULUM: unofficial, unstated patterns within a school that influence what children learn — influenced by teacher ethnicity and expectations.

The ability to navigate social and emotional challenges through effective strategies. Research by Blanchard-Fields (2007) found that older adults use more effective strategies than younger adults in resolving social and emotional problems, suggesting experience and wisdom can compensate for processing speed declines.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The psychosocial achievement of Stage 6 in Erikson's theory (Intimacy vs. Isolation), typically resolved during early adulthood. Erikson argued that successfully resolving intimacy was necessary before an individual could fully achieve generativity in middle adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Erikson's sixth psychosocial stage, which emphasizes that humans are social creatures. Social isolation is harmful at every age and in every culture. Intimacy progresses from attraction to close connection to ongoing commitment.

Fat that accumulates around internal organs in the abdominal region. An increase in intra-abdominal fat, combined with loss of lean body mass, is associated with weight gain following menopause and may necessitate lifestyle changes to manage.

The fluid pressure inside the eye. When this pressure increases abnormally, it can damage the optic nerve and lead to glaucoma. Measured during routine eye exams; elevated pressure is managed with medicated eye drops to reduce risk or slow progression of glaucoma.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Occurring within the mind or psyche. In the definition of spirituality, the intrapsychic sense of connection refers to an internal, subjective experience of connectedness to something transcendent rather than an externally organized or institutionalized practice.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The natural, genetically programmed aging of the skin and other body systems that occurs over time, independent of environmental factors. It results in thinning skin, loss of elasticity, reduced collagen production, and other structural changes distinct from photoaging caused by sun exposure.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Motivation driven by internal rewards such as joy, intellectual satisfaction, and personal fulfillment rather than external incentives. In Csikszentmihalyi's theory of flow, individuals in a flow state are not concerned with extrinsic rewards, reflecting pure intrinsic motivation.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A characteristic of expert cognition in which experts can draw upon a vast store of knowledge and past experience to make decisions or solve problems without consciously following explicit rules or steps. In contrast to novices, experts' actions appear more intuitive than formulaic.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: One of three grandparenting styles identified by Cherlin and Furstenberg (1986). Approximately 15% of grandparents fall into this category. These grandparents take a very active, authoritative role in the grandchild's life, with frequent contact and sometimes even co-residence. Grandmothers are more likely than grandfathers to adopt this style.

glossary.term: IQ Tests, Flynn Effect, and Multiple Intelligences
glossary.def: APTITUDE: potential to master a specific skill or learn a certain body of knowledge. IQ TEST: designed to measure intellectual aptitude, or ability to learn in school. ACHIEVEMENT TEST: measures mastery or proficiency in a subject. FLYNN EFFECT: the rise in average IQ scores over decades in many nations — better prenatal care and maternal health are factors. STERNBERG: three types of intelligence — academic, creative, and practical. GARDNER: originally seven intelligences (linguistic, logical-mathematical, musical, spatial, bodily-kinesthetic, interpersonal, intrapersonal), later added naturalistic and spiritual/existential — each associated with a brain region.

The withdrawal from social engagement and relationships in older adulthood. Associated with negative aging outcomes including poorer physical health and lower life satisfaction, contrasting with the active engagement promoted by Activity Theory.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Integrity vs. Despair | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A developed European nation with 20% of its population aged 65 and older in 2012, projected to increase to 25.5% by 2030 and 31% by 2050, reflecting significant demographic aging driven by low fertility and improved longevity.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The developed nation with the highest percentage of elderly population globally. In 2012, 24% of Japan's population was aged 65 or older. Japan is projected to continue having the oldest population in 2030 (32.2%) and 2050 (40%), according to Census Bureau data.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A French woman documented by Guinness World Records (2016) as the longest-living person on record, having lived 122 years and 164 days. She serves as the most extreme documented example of centenarian longevity.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers (2015) who studied selective attention in older adults and found that while older adults are less able to selectively focus on information while ignoring distractors, when given double the time to complete attention tasks, they could perform at young adult levels.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A state of chronic stress that leads to physical and emotional exhaustion, cynicism, detachment, and feelings of ineffectiveness and lack of accomplishment. It involves becoming disillusioned and frustrated at work. American workers are considered more susceptible to burnout partly because the U.S. does not legally guarantee paid vacation days, unlike most other developed nations.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The practice of changing jobs frequently, often in pursuit of better pay, conditions, or advancement. Younger workers are more likely to engage in job hopping, while middle-aged adults with higher job satisfaction tend to demonstrate greater job stability and loyalty to their employers.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A positive emotional state resulting from one's appraisal of one's job or job experiences. Research indicates that job satisfaction tends to peak during middle adulthood due to higher wages, greater decision-making involvement, increased competence, and lowered career expectations. High job satisfaction is associated with lower absenteeism, greater productivity, and less job hopping.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The degree of contentment an employee feels about their work. Research (Kühnel & Sonnentag, 2011) indicates that leisure activities can improve job satisfaction and workplace productivity by allowing mental and emotional recovery.

A state of chronic physical and emotional exhaustion resulting from prolonged workplace stress. Research (Nimrod, Kleiber, & Berdychevesky, 2012) indicates that taking leisure time away from work helps reduce job stress burnout.

Researcher who initiated and led the Seattle Longitudinal Study beginning in 1956. His work documented age-related changes in multiple cognitive abilities across the adult lifespan, providing foundational evidence for how specific cognitive skills change with age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who conducted longitudinal research finding a strong link between volunteering and better health outcomes in later adulthood, contributing to the evidence base supporting the health benefits of prosocial behavior in older populations.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Generativity in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who originally proposed the Convoy Model of Social Relations, conceptualizing social support networks as a convoy of relationships with varying levels of closeness. Inner-circle relationships (spouse, family) were proposed to remain stable across the lifespan, while peripheral relationships were seen as more vulnerable to change.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Social Networks in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A researcher who identified and described the cultural stereotype that older adults are genderless and asexual, and who highlighted how these stereotypes reflect broader ageist attitudes in society.

Researchers who analyzed data from the National Social Life, Health, and Aging Project, finding that 72% of men and 45.5% of women aged 52–72 were sexually active, and that changes in marital status differentially affected sexual behavior in older men versus women.

An age-related reduction in the kidneys' ability to filter and remove waste products from the blood. This decreased renal function is a normal part of aging and can affect the body's ability to regulate fluid balance, electrolytes, and excretion of metabolic waste and medications.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A person within a family who maintains family connections, promotes solidarity and continuity, organizes family gatherings, preserves family history, provides advice and support, and strengthens bonds between family members. Research shows that 86% of kinkeepers are women, and mothers, maternal grandmothers, and paternal grandmothers are more likely to serve this role than fathers, young adult children, and grandfathers combined.

The set of activities performed by a kinkeeper to maintain family cohesion and connectedness, including organizing gatherings, preserving family history, providing emotional support, and facilitating family communication. On average, kinkeepers devote approximately 34 hours per month (almost a full work week) to these activities. When combined with caregiving tasks, it can be a significant source of stress and depression.

The role, often assumed by women, of maintaining family connections, organizing gatherings, facilitating communication among family members, and preserving intergenerational relationships. Studied by Bengtson (2001) and Brown & DeRycke (2010).

Three levels of moral reasoning with two stages each. PRECONVENTIONAL (personal): emphasizes rewards and punishments — what benefits me. CONVENTIONAL (communal): emphasizes social rules — what others expect and social norms. POSTCONVENTIONAL (worldwide): emphasizes moral principles — abstract justice beyond social rules. Kohlberg judged moral development not by answers but by the REASONS for the answers. Criticism: culture and gender ignored; family not included; differences between child and adult morality not addressed. Current research: raising moral issues and letting children talk about them advances morality — not immediately but soon.
A researcher who found that men were less likely than women to report elder abuse, contributing to underreporting and potentially skewed prevalence statistics.

Researchers who provided empirical evidence demonstrating that age-related declines in cognitive functioning across the adult life span may be slowed through physical exercise and lifestyle interventions, supporting the physical and cognitive maintenance criterion of Rowe and Kahn's successful aging model.

A progressive curvature of the spine resulting in a hunched-over posture, caused by weakening of the spinal bones due to osteoporosis. Over time, a bent spine can make it difficult to walk or even sit up. Adults gradually lose height as the spinal bones weaken.

The percentage of the civilian non-institutionalized population aged 16 and older that is currently employed. This rate has been declining since its peak in the late 1990s, dropping from 67% to 64% by 2012, with further decline projected to 62% by 2022.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers associated with the Berlin Aging Study (BASE) who studied the relationship between adult children and aging parents. Their findings indicated that older parents (mean age 84) reported greater life satisfaction when their adult children (mean age 54) provided emotional support rather than informational support, and that more autonomous adult children tended to have more emotionally meaningful relationships with their aging parents.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Social Networks in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: Language Development: Babbling to Naming Explosion
glossary.def: Before birth: language learning via brain organization. Newborn: preference for speech sounds and mother's language. About 6 months: babbling begins (ba-ba-ba); experience-expectant. About 10 months: pointing emerges. About 12 months: holophrases (single words as complete thoughts); understand 10 times more words than produced. About 18 months (50-word threshold): naming explosion begins at 50-100 new words per month. 21-month-olds say twice as many words as 18-month-olds.

Average child knows ~500 words at age 2 and more than 10,000 at age 6. Comprehension is always greater than production. VOCABULARY EXPLOSION: rapid expansion with mastery of verbs, adjectives, adverbs, conjunctions, and nouns. FAST-MAPPING: speedy and sometimes imprecise way children learn new words by tentatively placing them in mental categories according to perceived meaning. LOGICAL EXTENSION: using words to describe other objects in the same category. OVERREGULARIZATION: applying rules of grammar even when exceptions occur (e.g., 'foots,' 'tooths,' 'mouses') — evidence of increasing grammatical knowledge.

glossary.term: Language in Middle Childhood: Pragmatics and Code-Switching
glossary.def: School-age children comprehend and enjoy metaphors, puns, and unexpected answers — new cognitive flexibility makes these funny. PRAGMATICS: ability to use words and devices to communicate in various contexts; allows children to change formal and informal codes to fit their audience (CODE-SWITCHING). Children may switch between formal code (academic contexts), informal code (with friends), and a third code (texting with numbers, abbreviations, emoticons). ELL (English Language Learners): children whose English proficiency is below a cutoff. BILINGUALISM: code-switching ability correlates with school achievement.

A researcher who identified the 'third age' concept, defining the post-employment, pre-decline stage of life (roughly ages 65-84) as a distinct and positive life period following childhood and work/parenting.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The developmental stage spanning from approximately the mid-sixties until death. It is considered the longest developmental stage across the entire human lifespan and encompasses significant physical, cognitive, and socioemotional changes.

The stage of human development encompassing individuals aged 65 years and older. It is currently the fastest growing age division of the United States population, characterized by increased longevity, retirement, potential health challenges, and significant societal impacts on healthcare, housing, social security, and caregiving.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The final broad stage of the human lifespan, generally beginning at age 65, characterized by biological, psychological, social, and chronological changes associated with aging. It is subdivided into young-old (65-84), oldest-old (85-99), and centenarians (100+).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The developmental stage typically beginning around age 65, characterized by retirement, potential physical and cognitive changes, and shifts in social roles. Individuals in late adulthood are the primary targets of ageist attitudes and stereotypes in American culture.

The stage of human development generally defined as age 65 and older, characterized by retirement, potential health decline, and changes in living arrangements, social roles, and cognitive functioning.

The developmental stage generally defined as beginning around age 65, characterized by retirement, potential health decline, and continued psychosocial development including generativity, wisdom, and legacy concerns.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Generativity in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The final stage of the human lifespan, typically beginning around age 65. In Erikson's framework, this is the period during which individuals face the psychosocial crisis of Integrity vs. Despair, reflecting on the totality of their lives.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Integrity vs. Despair | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The developmental stage generally defined as age 65 and older, characterized by significant physiological, psychological, and social changes. Substance abuse in this population is underdiagnosed and undertreated due to overlapping symptoms with normal aging, physician bias, stigma, and inapplicable DSM-5 diagnostic criteria.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Substance Abuse and the Elderly | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The engagement of older adults in romantic and dating relationships following widowhood or divorce. Social norms have shifted to make this more acceptable. Research by Carr (2004a) found that 18 months after the death of a spouse, 37% of men and 15% of women aged 65+ expressed interest in dating.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The dissolution of marriage occurring in adults aged 65 and older. Although divorce rates among older adults are rising compared to prior generations, older adults are still less likely to divorce than middle-aged or young adults. Older women face greater financial hardship and are more likely to remain single post-divorce compared to older men.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Psychological and mental health difficulties experienced by older adults that emerge or are exacerbated in later life, often as a result of internalized continual emotional mistreatment and abuse experienced over time.

The act of marrying again after divorce or widowhood in late adulthood. Remarriages among older adults tend to be more stable than those of younger adults, partly due to greater emotional maturity, more realistic expectations of relationships, and a primary motivation of companionship rather than child-rearing.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A clinical condition in men in which the testes produce insufficient testosterone, typically occurring in middle to older adulthood. It is diagnosed when low testosterone is accompanied by negative symptoms. Associated with medical conditions such as diabetes, obesity, high blood pressure, and testicular cancer.

The transitional period during the empty nest when children begin leaving home, which research has found to be associated with increased marital satisfaction for many couples. This satisfaction has been shown to endure long after the last child has left home, supporting the role strain relief hypothesis.

Researchers who studied kinkeeping and found that 86% of respondents named a woman as their family's kinkeeper, providing empirical support for the gendered nature of kinkeeping roles within families.

glossary.term: Lean Body Mass to Fat Ratio
glossary.def: A physiological measure describing the proportion of muscle and other lean tissue relative to fat tissue in the body. With aging, this ratio decreases—meaning a higher proportion of fat relative to lean mass—which leads to greater sensitivity to alcohol and other substances because alcohol distributes primarily in lean body mass and water.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Substance Abuse and the Elderly | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Body tissue that is primarily composed of muscle, bone, and organs rather than fat. After age 30, the body tends to lose lean tissue as cells in muscles, the liver, kidneys, and other organs are gradually lost, contributing to changes in body composition, metabolism, and strength over time.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A care philosophy that emphasizes providing services in the setting that maximally preserves an individual's independence and freedom, favoring home and community-based care over institutionalization whenever possible.

Researchers who found that even among the oldest-old, the death rate of those who volunteer is half that of non-volunteers, highlighting the significant association between volunteerism and longevity in advanced age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Generativity in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: In the context of Erikson's generativity stage, legacy refers to the lasting positive contributions an individual leaves for future generations through parenthood, work, creative output, and community involvement. Leaving a positive legacy is described as the primary concern of individuals at midlife.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Non-work, freely chosen activities engaged in for enjoyment and personal fulfillment. Leisure is considered important to mental health and is linked to a successful transition into and experience of retirement.

Time free from work and other obligatory duties, used for relaxation, recreation, or personal enjoyment. In middle adulthood, leisure activities are shaped by work demands, family obligations, and cultural factors. The most common leisure activity worldwide among middle-aged adults is watching television.

The relationship between free-time activities and psychological well-being. Research by Qian, Yarnal, and Almeida (2013) found that leisure time improves mental health outcomes among adults, supporting the importance of work-life balance.

The relationship between recreational activities and bodily well-being. Studies (Stern & Konno, 2009; Lee et al., 2015) indicate that leisure time, particularly when combined with moderate physical activity, improves physical health in middle-aged adults.

The difference in the amount of leisure time enjoyed by men versus women. In the United States, men spend approximately 5 hours more per week in leisure activities than women, with the gap narrowing to about 3 hours per week among parents with children under age 18 (Drake, 2013; U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, 2016).

Researchers who formulated the Activity Theory of aging in 1972, providing the empirical and theoretical basis for the claim that remaining active in late adulthood leads to greater life satisfaction compared to social withdrawal and disengagement.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Integrity vs. Despair | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The physical property of the eye's crystalline lens that allows it to change shape (become more or less curved) in order to focus light on the retina. With age, the lens becomes stiffer and less elastic, impairing the ability to focus on nearby objects, a process central to the development of presbyopia.

Nations with emerging or lower-income economies that are also experiencing demographic aging, though starting from different baseline fertility rates. Between 2010 and 2050, the number of older people in less developed countries is projected to increase more than 250%, compared with a 71% increase in developed countries.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The primary pharmacological treatment for Parkinson's disease. L-dopa is a precursor to dopamine that can cross the blood-brain barrier, where it is converted into dopamine in the brain, partially restoring the dopamine levels lost due to substantia nigra degeneration.

A researcher whose work on stereotype threat in aging demonstrated that older adults who internalize negative aging stereotypes exhibit worse memory performance, worse physical performance, and reduced self-efficacy compared to those who do not internalize such stereotypes.

Microscopic protein deposits found within neurons, identified postmortem. They disrupt brain chemicals affecting thinking, movement, behavior, and mood. Can occur in both the cortex and brain stem, producing both cognitive and motor symptoms. Associated with Neurocognitive Disorder with Lewy Bodies and also found in Parkinson's disease.

Memory for words and their meanings, related to vocabulary knowledge. Studies (Dahlgren, 1998) have found that older adults often outperform younger adults on lexical memory tasks, reflecting accumulated linguistic knowledge over a lifetime.
A researcher cited in the context of financial abuse and exploitation of elders, noting that financial abuse costs seniors nearly 3 billion dollars per year.

A theoretical framework in developmental psychology that examines how individual lives unfold over time and are shaped by social, historical, and relational contexts. Central to this perspective is the concept of linked lives, which emphasizes the mutual influence between individuals across generations and across the lifespan.

A specific major occurrence or experience, such as job loss, divorce, or death of a loved one, that precipitates a midlife crisis in those who experience one. Research from the MacArthur Foundation study found that when midlife crises did occur, they were typically triggered by such events rather than by a generalized fear of aging.

The average number of years a person born in a specific time and place can expect to live, based on current mortality rates. Life expectancy varies by gender, ethnicity, geography, and socioeconomic factors.

The average number of additional years a person of a given age is statistically expected to live. According to the U.S. Census Bureau (2014), a person who turned 65 in 2015 can expect to live an additional 19 years — 5.5 years longer than someone who turned 65 in 1950, reflecting significant improvements in medicine and public health.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The average number of years that members of a population or species are expected to live. Global life expectancy at birth in 2015 was 71.4 years according to the WHO, with females averaging 73.8 years and males averaging 69.1 years.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The average number of years a person is expected to live, calculated from birth or a given age. In humans, females consistently live longer than males across nearly all populations, a difference attributed to both biological and lifestyle factors.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A reflective process in late adulthood, described by Erikson, in which older adults look back on their lives, attempt to integrate their unique experiences with a broader vision of community, and evaluate whether their life had meaning and purpose.

A cognitive evaluation of the quality of one's life as a whole, often used as a measure of subjective well-being. In older adults, higher life satisfaction is linked to greater social contact, including participation in volunteering.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Generativity in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The maximum number of years a member of a species has been known to live. For humans, this is generally cited as approximately 122 years, based on the oldest documented human lifespan.

An approach to studying human development that takes into account all phases of life, not just childhood or adulthood. Development is viewed as multidirectional, multicontextual, multicultural, multidisciplinary, and plastic.

Medical interventions designed to prolong life in individuals who might otherwise die, such as mechanical ventilation, dialysis, or resuscitation. Physicians who hold ageist beliefs that illness is a natural part of aging may be less likely to recommend or administer life-sustaining treatment to older patients.

The ongoing, voluntary pursuit of education and skill development throughout the lifespan. Middle adults increasingly return to college or seek advanced training to meet changing workforce demands or personal development goals.
The greatest age reached by any member of a given population or species. For humans, the current maximum lifespan is estimated to be between 120 and 125 years.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A theoretical framework, pioneered by Erikson, that views development as a continuous process occurring across all stages of life from birth to death. Erikson's was the first major theory to propose that development does not end in adolescence or young adulthood but continues through old age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Integrity vs. Despair | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A field of study and theoretical perspective that examines human development across the entire span of life, from conception through death. It recognizes that development is a lifelong, multidirectional process involving both gains and losses at every stage, and that no single period — including middle adulthood — is more important than others.
The scientific study of human growth, change, and stability across the entire life span, from conception through death, encompassing biological, cognitive, and psychosocial domains.
The scientific study of human growth and change across all stages of life, from conception through death, including biological, cognitive, and psychosocial processes at each stage.

The scientific study of human growth, change, and stability across the entire life span from conception through death. It encompasses physical, cognitive, emotional, and social development across all stages including late adulthood, which is increasingly prominent given demographic trends in aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A theoretical framework articulated by Baltes, Staudinger, & Lindenberger (1999) emphasizing that development is a lifelong, multidirectional, multidimensional process involving both gains and losses, and that intellectual functioning changes across the lifespan in complex ways.

Behavioral and social choices or circumstances that influence health outcomes and longevity. For males, relevant lifestyle factors contributing to shorter lifespans include working in dangerous occupations, higher rates of violence exposure, greater likelihood of committing suicide, less frequent use of healthcare, lower rates of religious participation, and greater social isolation.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Health-related behaviors that influence length and quality of life in older adulthood, including regular exercise, stress reduction, smoking cessation, limiting alcohol consumption, and eating more fruits and vegetables, as identified by He et al. (2005).

Behavioral changes made to daily habits and routines — such as increasing physical activity, improving diet, or reducing risk factors like smoking — that have been shown to mitigate age-related physical and cognitive decline. They are a key component of achieving successful aging as defined by Rowe and Kahn.

The concept that the success, health, and well-being of each family member are connected to those of every other family member, explaining why parents of all income levels in the United States and globally continue to support their adult children.

A concept from the life course perspective proposing that individuals in important relationships, such as parents and children, mutually influence each other's developmental pathways. This bidirectional relationship means that the problems faced by adult children can significantly affect their parents' emotional health, well-being, and relationship quality, even when the children no longer live at home.

The process by which liver enzymes break down substances such as alcohol and drugs. With age, these enzymes become less efficient, causing psychoactive substances to remain in the body longer and at higher concentrations, increasing the risk of toxicity and dependence in older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Substance Abuse and the Elderly | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A living arrangement in which an older adult resides independently without a spouse, family member, or non-relative. Women make up the majority of older adults living alone in the U.S. The prevalence of living alone among older adults increased from 1900 to 1990 and has since declined.

A researcher who reported that 27% of adults aged 45–54 were divorced, that 57% of divorced adults were women, and that older adults are more likely to have remarried. Also noted that adults aged 55 and older are remarrying at higher rates than in the past.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A component of fluid intelligence referring to the ability to analyze information, identify patterns, and draw conclusions without relying on prior knowledge. Like other aspects of fluid intelligence, logical reasoning tends to decline with age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The subjective discrepancy between the social contact a person has and the social contact a person desires (Brehm, Miller, Perlman, & Campbell, 2002). Loneliness can result from social isolation (lack of social contacts) or emotional isolation (lack of intimate connection). It is associated with lack of self-worth, impatience, desperation, and depression. Women are more likely to experience loneliness from social isolation; men from emotional isolation.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Social Networks in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Research by Ash et al. (2015) examining whether members of families with exceptional longevity are healthier than equally long-lived peers not from such families. The Long Life Family Study investigated the role of genetic and environmental factors in exceptional longevity, with implications for understanding heritable components of healthy aging.

Nursing home and residential care that provides continuous medical supervision and support for elderly individuals who can no longer live independently. Good long-term care allows independence, individual choice, and privacy while providing continuity of care from well-trained staff.

A range of services and support for individuals who need assistance with daily activities over an extended period due to age, disability, or chronic illness. As global populations age, concerns are growing about the availability of caregivers and resources to meet the long-term care needs of older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A range of services and supports for individuals who need assistance with daily living activities over an extended period. The oldest-old are more likely than the young-old to require long-term care, with nearly 50% of those 85 and older needing some assistance with daily living activities (APA, 2016).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A range of services and supports needed by individuals who have functional limitations over an extended period of time, including nursing home care, assisted living, and home-based care. Demand is projected to grow from 12 million in 2010 to 27 million in 2050 due to the aging baby boomer population.

The relatively permanent memory system that stores information over extended periods, ranging from minutes to a lifetime. It is divided into explicit memory (semantic and episodic) and implicit memory (procedural, priming, classical conditioning). Different subdivisions of long-term memory show varying degrees of age-related change.
Length of life. Research by Dew et al. (2003) found that older adults with better sleep patterns lived longer, establishing a link between sleep quality and overall lifespan.

The length of an individual's life or lifespan. Advances in medicine, nutrition, and public health have significantly increased longevity in the United States, with those turning 65 in 2015 expected to live 5.5 years longer than those who turned 65 in 1950.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The length of an individual's life or the general increase in life expectancy within a population. Improvements in longevity, along with declines in fertility, account for the growing percentage of persons aged 65 and older worldwide.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The length of an individual's life. Increases in longevity have contributed to the growth of the oldest-old and centenarian populations and necessitated subcategories within late adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A research design that follows the same individuals over an extended period of time. When cognitive ability is studied longitudinally rather than cross-sectionally, the amount of observed cognitive loss with age diminishes, suggesting that cross-sectional studies overestimate decline.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A type of research study that follows the same participants over an extended period of time to observe changes and developments. In the context of late adulthood, longitudinal research has established strong links between volunteering and improved health outcomes.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Generativity in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A research design in which the same participants are studied repeatedly over a long period of time to track changes and developments. The Seattle Longitudinal Study is a prime example, tracking cognitive abilities every seven years since 1956 across approximately 6,000 participants.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A research design in which the same individuals are observed and measured repeatedly over an extended period of time. Vaillant advocated for longitudinal studies over cross-sectional designs for studying adult development, as they track changes within individuals across the full lifespan and reduce cohort effects.

A component of the Selective Optimization with Compensation model referring to the process by which older adults adapt to a specific loss of functioning by first attempting to maintain their skills using new resources, technologies, or continued practice, and eventually selecting new goals or activities when prior ones are no longer achievable.

LBW: less than 2,500 grams (5.5 lbs) at birth. VLBW: under 1,500 grams. ELBW: under 1,000 grams. Preterm: birth at 35 or fewer weeks. NEWBORN REFLEXES are unlearned, involuntary responses. Survival reflexes: breathing, hiccupping, sneezing, crying, shivering, sucking, rooting, swallowing. Signaling reflexes (brain/body function): Babinski (toes fan up when foot stroked), stepping, palmar grasping, Moro startle (arms fling out then close on chest).

Age-related decline in testosterone levels in men, sometimes called male menopause or andropause. Associated with decreased energy, libido, muscle mass, and mood changes. Discussed by Berkeley Wellness (2011).

Often called 'bad' cholesterol, LDL makes up the majority of the body's cholesterol. At high levels, LDL can form plaque in the arteries, narrowing them and increasing the risk of heart attack and stroke. Nearly 32% of American adults have high LDL cholesterol levels. Risk factors for high LDL include a diet high in saturated fats, trans fats, and cholesterol, physical inactivity, obesity, diabetes, and family history.

A muscular valve located at the junction of the esophagus and stomach that normally prevents stomach acid from entering the esophagus. In individuals with GERD, this sphincter relaxes too frequently, allowing acid reflux to occur.

glossary.term: Lowered Expectations Theory of Midlife Satisfaction
glossary.def: A theory proposed by Tangri, Thomas, and Mednick (2003) suggesting that one reason middle-aged adults report higher job satisfaction is that they have adjusted and lowered their career expectations and goals, having recognized the realistic ceiling of their career advancement, which reduces disappointment and increases contentment.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The maximum amount of air the lungs can hold or expel. It decreases with aging due to two primary mechanisms: thinning and reshaping of the rib cage bones that limits lung expansion, and weakening of respiratory muscles such as the diaphragm. This reduction leads to lower blood oxygen levels and higher carbon dioxide levels.

glossary.term: MacArthur Foundation Research Network on Successful Midlife Development
glossary.def: A research organization that conducted a major 10-year study on midlife adults in the United States, based on telephone interviews with over 3,000 participants between the ages of 40 and 60. Their findings indicated that midlife is generally a time of well-being and that only 23% of participants reported experiencing a midlife crisis.

The primary nutrients required in large quantities for energy and body function, including carbohydrates, proteins, and fats. In older adults, the balance of macronutrients is critical for maintaining muscle mass, energy levels, and immune function.
The central portion of the retina responsible for sharp, central vision. Deterioration of the macula causes age-related macular degeneration, resulting in loss of the central visual field.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: An age-related eye disease involving deterioration of the macula, the central portion of the retina responsible for sharp, central vision. It is a leading cause of vision loss in older adults and exists in two forms: dry (gradual atrophy) and wet (abnormal blood vessel growth). Also called age-related macular degeneration (AMD).

Deterioration of the macula (center of the retina) leading to loss of clarity in the central field of vision. Does not typically cause total blindness but significantly impairs daily functioning. Two types exist: dry (most common, caused by drusen deposits beneath the retina) and wet (caused by abnormal blood vessel growth beneath the retina, causing faster vision loss). Risk factors include smoking (doubles risk), Caucasian race, high cholesterol, and family history. No effective treatment for dry type; wet type may be treated with medications or laser surgery with only 25% seeing improvement.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The second criterion of Rowe and Kahn's successful aging model, referring to the preservation of mental abilities including memory, attention, processing speed, and executive function. Research by Kramer and Erickson (2007) suggests that age-related cognitive declines can be slowed through physical exercise and lifestyle interventions.

The second criterion of Rowe and Kahn's successful aging model, referring to the preservation of physical capacities and abilities that support independent living and activity. This includes strength, mobility, cardiovascular health, and other bodily functions that decline with age but can be mitigated through exercise and healthy lifestyle choices.

A DSM-5 diagnosis characterized by a significant cognitive decline from a previous level of performance in one or more cognitive domains that interferes with independent functioning in everyday activities. Previously referred to as dementia.

A genetically and hormonally influenced form of hair loss in men characterized by a receding hairline followed by hair loss at the top of the head. It is related to the hormone testosterone and can begin as early as the 30s, with some men being nearly bald by age 60.

A cancerous tumor capable of invading nearby tissues and potentially metastasizing to other parts of the body via the lymph system or bloodstream. When surgically removed, malignant tumors may grow back. Malignant tumors can prompt the formation of new blood vessels to supply them with oxygen and nutrients.

A condition of inadequate nutrition that has higher prevalence and risk in cancer patients. In elderly cancer patients, malnutrition risk is compounded by diminished sensory function and the metabolic demands of the disease.

A condition resulting from inadequate intake or absorption of nutrients, common in older populations. It can lead to muscle wasting, weakened immunity, slower recovery, and increased mortality. Contributing factors include sensory decline, tooth loss, poverty, and social isolation.

Researchers who found that men rated higher in generativity at midlife were associated with stronger global cognitive functioning, stronger executive functioning, and lower levels of depression in late adulthood, demonstrating the long-term psychological and cognitive benefits of generativity.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A policy requiring workers to retire upon reaching a specific age. In the United States, mandatory retirement was eliminated for most workers in 1986 through the amendment of the Age Discrimination in Employment Act (ADEA), though certain high-risk occupations retain enforced retirement ages.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: James Marcia described and measured four ways adolescents cope with identity: 1. ROLE CONFUSION (identity diffusion): does not know or care what their identity is. 2. FORECLOSURE: premature identity formation — adolescent adopts parents' or society's roles and values wholesale, without questioning or analysis. 3. MORATORIUM: adolescent's choice of a socially acceptable way to postpone identity decisions (going to college is a common example). 4. IDENTITY ACHIEVEMENT: the goal — having explored and committed to an identity. It is now considered unusual for teenagers to fully resolve this crisis and reach identity achievement.

The degree to which individuals feel content and fulfilled within their marriage. Research (Landsford et al., 2005) indicates that marital satisfaction tends to increase for many couples in midlife, often as children leave the home. However, some researchers (Umberson et al., 2005) argue this apparent increase is due to selection bias, as unhappy couples are likely to have already divorced.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The level of happiness and fulfillment experienced within a marriage. Research consistently shows that raising children has a negative impact on the quality of marital relationships, and that marital satisfaction often increases during the launching phase of the empty nest period. Multiple studies confirm that this increased satisfaction endures long after the last child has left home.

glossary.term: Marital Status and Health in Late Life
glossary.def: Research demonstrating that transitions in marital status (e.g., widowhood, divorce) have significant effects on physical and mental health outcomes in older adults. Bookwala, Marshall, and Manning (2014) found that married individuals generally have better health outcomes, though social support from friends can buffer some negative effects of being unmarried.

The influence of generational and cultural norms about masculinity on how men engage with their grandchildren. Many grandfathers report being more openly affectionate, light-hearted, and flexible with grandchildren than they were with their own children, partly because traditional occupational demands and masculine norms had previously restricted such emotional expression during their years as fathers.

A psychological construct referring to a person's sense of agency and perceived ability to influence events and outcomes in their own life. Research by Lachman (2004) found that midlife adults with a strong sense of mastery and control are most likely to enjoy a plateau of good health through midlife, particularly when combined with physical activity, nutrition monitoring, adequate sleep, and social engagement.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A cited researcher/source referenced in the context of older adult nutrition, supporting the claim that a healthy diet increases mental acuteness, resistance to illness, energy levels, immune system strength, recuperation speed, and effectiveness in managing chronic health problems.

The psychological process of finding significance, purpose, and coherence in one's life experiences during old age. A central task of Erikson's Integrity vs. Despair stage; failure to find meaning contributes to despair.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Integrity vs. Despair | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A concept introduced by Paul Baltes referring to cognitive abilities dependent on brain functioning, including speed and accuracy of processing. Analogous to fluid intelligence, mechanics of intelligence decline with age as neurological efficiency decreases.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The role of mass media in shaping perceptions of aging by promoting youthful beauty standards, advertising anti-aging products, and supporting cosmetic procedures. Gilleard & Higgs (2000) noted that media significantly impacts how women view their own aging, often fostering negative self-perceptions and driving consumption of anti-aging goods and services.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A federal health insurance program available to Americans aged 65 and older. It is an entitlement that serves as a key financial consideration in retirement timing, as individuals must reach age 65 to qualify.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A mindfulness-based stress management technique that, along with relaxation activities, helps individuals reduce physiological and psychological stress responses. Included among interventions designed to reduce aversive responses to duress, especially related to health outcomes.

A pigment produced by hair follicles that is responsible for hair color. As individuals age, hair follicles produce progressively less melanin, causing hair to lose its color and eventually turn gray and then white. The rate of melanin loss is largely genetically determined.

T-cells that have been programmed to recognize and fight a specific invader and can remain in the body for many decades. They are responsible for long-term immunity from vaccines and prior infections. Older adults produce fewer new T-cells, reducing their ability to respond to novel threats.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The period preceding menopause during which hormonal fluctuations occur, menstrual cycles become irregular, and symptoms such as hot flashes, sleep disturbances, and mood changes may emerge. Research (Bromberger et al., 2013) suggests increased risk for anxiety during this phase.

The natural biological process marking the end of a woman's menstrual cycles, typically occurring in midlife. Menopause can negatively affect both sleep duration and sleep quality, contributing to sleep disturbances in middle-aged women.

Clinically defined as 12 consecutive months without menstruation, after which a woman is no longer capable of reproduction. The average age of menopause is approximately 51, though symptoms often begin in a woman's 40s during perimenopause.

The permanent cessation of menstruation defined clinically as the absence of menstrual periods for at least 12 consecutive months. It marks the end of a woman's reproductive capacity and is caused by declining estrogen and progesterone levels. The average age of onset in the United States is approximately 51 years.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The biological cessation of menstruation marking the end of a woman's reproductive years, typically occurring in midlife. In Western society, postmenopausal women may be socially regarded as 'old' due to the traditional valuation of women for their reproductive capabilities.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The cessation of menstruation for 12 consecutive months, typically occurring in women in their late 40s to early 50s. Associated with hormonal changes including declining estrogen, and symptoms such as hot flashes, mood changes, and increased anxiety risk during the menopausal transition.

A hormonal transition in women associated with cessation of menstrual cycles, linked to increased prevalence of dry eye syndrome. Women who experience early menopause are at particularly elevated risk. Hormonal changes following menopause alter tear composition and production, increasing susceptibility to dry eye and potential ocular surface damage.

The sharpness and clarity of cognitive function, including memory, attention, and reasoning. A healthy diet in older adulthood is associated with maintained or improved cognitive performance and reduced risk of cognitive decline.

A measure of how efficiently the body processes and uses energy, including glucose and fat metabolism. Insulin resistance is considered an important indicator of metabolic health, and irregular sleep is associated with metabolic decline in midlife women.

The biological stress created by the life-sustaining activities of the body, including circulating blood, eliminating waste, controlling body temperature, and neuronal firing in the brain. Gerontologists consider metabolic stress alongside major life events when studying stress and aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A cluster of metabolic conditions including high blood pressure, high blood sugar, excess abdominal fat, and abnormal cholesterol levels that together increase the risk of heart disease, stroke, and type 2 diabetes. It is also a recognized risk factor for the development of gallstones.

A cluster of conditions including high blood pressure, elevated blood sugar, excess abdominal fat, and abnormal cholesterol levels that increase the risk of heart disease, stroke, and type 2 diabetes. Influenced by aerobic fitness and weight (Crist et al., 2012).

The process by which the body converts food and drink into energy. Calories consumed are combined with oxygen to release energy needed to function. People with more muscle mass have a higher metabolism because muscle burns more calories even at rest.
