Metabolism slows during midlife as muscle mass decreases and fat accounts for a greater proportion of body weight.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The process by which cancer cells break away from the primary tumor site and travel through the lymph system or bloodstream to form new (metastatic) tumors in other parts of the body. The metastatic tumor is the same type of cancer as the original primary tumor. Metastasis is a key feature that distinguishes malignant from benign tumors.

The developmental period of the lifespan between early adulthood and late adulthood, most commonly defined as ages 40-45 to 60-65. Also referred to as midlife, it is characterized by both developmental gains and losses, considerable individual differences, and is one of the least studied periods of the lifespan. Cultural factors influence the specific age boundaries and developmental tasks associated with this period.

A stage of the human lifespan typically spanning from approximately age 40 to 65. It is characterized by a range of physical changes including the onset of graying and thinning hair, increased skin wrinkling, the beginning of sarcopenia, and changes in lung capacity, many of which are influenced by both genetics and lifestyle factors.

The developmental life stage roughly spanning the ages of 40–65, during which sleep quality and duration often decline. This stage is associated with increased risks of metabolic disorders, sleep disturbances related to menopause, and reduced deep sleep.

The developmental period generally spanning ages 40 to 65, characterized by continued brain plasticity, improved emotional regulation, peak financial decision-making ability, and diverse cognitive trajectories including both gains and losses.

A stage of the lifespan typically associated with middle age, during which individuals often possess a substantial store of knowledge and experience that allows them to recognize familiar problems, ignore irrelevant information, and focus on important aspects of issues. Many people reach the peak of their careers during this period.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The developmental stage generally spanning ages 40 to 65, during which individuals experience peak career involvement, potential job satisfaction, physiological changes, and significant life transitions including preparation for retirement. This stage is characterized by both peak career performance and vulnerability to workplace challenges such as burnout and discrimination.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A life stage occurring approximately between ages 45-50 according to Levinson, during which individuals commit to new choices made during the midlife transition and direct their energies into these revised commitments. More broadly used to refer to the period between roughly 40-65 years of age.

The developmental period roughly spanning ages 40–65, during which Erikson's seventh psychosocial stage (Generativity vs. Stagnation) takes place. Individuals during this time are primarily concerned with leaving a positive legacy, raising families, advancing careers, and contributing to their communities.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The life stage roughly spanning ages 40–65 in which many individuals take on the role of grandparent in addition to maintaining relationships with their own children and aging parents. Becoming a grandparent is identified as a significant new relational role during this developmental period.

Period between early childhood and early adolescence, approximately ages 6 to 11. The HEALTHIEST years of the life span — fatal diseases or accidents are rare. Good childhood habits protect later adult health. Permanent teeth come in. Muscles become stronger. Children run faster and exercise longer. Children can master any motor skill that does not require an adult-sized body. ORAL HEALTH is important — gum disease can lead to cardiovascular and other systemic diseases. EMBODIED COGNITION: movement improves intellectual functioning through better cerebral blood flow and increased neurotransmitters.

The common accumulation of body fat in the abdominal region experienced by many adults during midlife, often described as a thickening around the midsection. Men tend to gain fat on the upper abdomen and back, while women tend to gain fat on the waist and upper arms. It is largely driven by a slowing metabolism and age-related muscle loss.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A subgroup within late adulthood generally referring to adults aged 75–84. This group tends to experience more chronic health conditions and may require increased assistance with daily living. In 2012, those aged 75–79 made up 2.4% and those 80–84 made up 1.8% of the U.S. population.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: An alternative term for middle adulthood, referring to the approximate middle portion of the human lifespan, typically spanning ages 40-45 to 60-65. It is a period marked by ongoing physical, cognitive, and psychosocial development, with both gains and losses occurring simultaneously.

The period of adult development roughly spanning the ages of 40 to 65, characterized by significant biological, psychological, and social changes. For women, midlife often includes menopause and increased concern about aging due to societal pressures related to youthful beauty and identity.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The period of adult development generally spanning ages 40–65, characterized by transitions in career, family roles, physical health, and psychological identity, including phenomena such as the midlife crisis, generativity, and the empty nest.

Individuals typically in their 50s and early 60s who are the most likely caregivers for their aging parents. In 2000, there were about 10 people aged 85 and older for every 100 persons between ages 50 and 64, a ratio projected to more than double by 2040.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A controversial concept suggesting that middle-aged adults experience a period of significant psychological turmoil, questioning, and identity re-evaluation triggered by awareness of aging and mortality. Its universality and severity are debated in developmental psychology.

A period of psychological distress and reevaluation that some adults experience during midlife, typically between the ages of 40-60. Characterized by questioning life choices, sense of urgency, and sometimes dramatic behavioral changes. Research suggests it is not universal or inevitable, occurring in only about 23% of midlife adults, and is more common among highly educated individuals triggered by major life events rather than fear of aging.

A cognitive reorientation that occurs during middle adulthood in which individuals shift from thinking about how long they have lived to thinking about how many years they have left to live. Identified by Neugarten (1968), this shift reflects a growing awareness of mortality and influences priorities and values.

A developmental stage identified by Levinson occurring approximately between ages 40-45, characterized by reevaluating previous commitments, making dramatic changes if necessary, expressing previously ignored talents or aspirations, and feeling increased urgency about life and its meaning.

A psychologist who developed the theory of flow (1990) and researched creativity in relation to optimal experience. He identified characteristics of creative people including curiosity, drive, value for intellectual endeavors, and ability to lose one's sense of self. He also argued that the 'tortured creative person' is a myth and that creative people tend to be very happy with their lives.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The participation of males and females in armed forces. According to the Department of Defense (2015), 83% of all officers and 85% of all enlisted service members were male in 2014, exposing men to substantially greater risk of injury and death associated with military service.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A DSM-5 diagnosis characterized by a modest cognitive decline from a previous level of performance in one or more cognitive domains that does not interfere with independent functioning. Represents a less severe form than Major Neurocognitive Disorder.

Membership in a racial, ethnic, or social group that is a numerical or political minority within a larger society. In the context of aging in the United States, older minority adults — including African Americans, Mexican Americans, and Asian Americans — face compounded disadvantages from both ageism and racism. In 2012, older minority adults comprised approximately 21% of the U.S. population, projected to reach 39% by 2050.

The concept that individuals belonging to stigmatized minority groups experience chronic stress as a result of prejudice, discrimination, and social stigma. For lesbian and gay elders, minority stress from multiple sources (sexual orientation, age, race, religion, geography) compounds to negatively affect health outcomes.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gay and Lesbian Elders | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A quorum of at least 10 Jewish men required for communal worship services in Orthodox Judaism. The requirement ensures that men have high rates of synagogue attendance in Orthodox communities, which is why Jewish men in Orthodox communities attend synagogue more often than Jewish women.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Mississippi had the lowest Healthy Life Expectancy among U.S. states, with individuals at age 65 expected to experience only 10.8 additional years of good health, as determined from 2007–2009 CDC data.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Cell organelles that use oxygen to produce energy from food in the form of adenosine triphosphate (ATP). When mitochondrial DNA is damaged, mitochondria become less efficient, generate less energy, and can lead to cellular death, contributing to biological aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Physical activity that raises the heart rate and causes increased breathing but still allows conversation. Examples include brisk walking. Guidelines recommend at least 150 minutes per week of moderate intensity aerobic activity for substantial health benefits, with sessions lasting at least 10 minutes, preferably spread throughout the week.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Exercise performed at a moderate intensity level, such as brisk walking or cycling. When incorporated into leisure time, moderate physical activity has been shown to provide additional physical health benefits beyond rest alone (Lee et al., 2015).

A lifestyle that includes physical activity equivalent to walking more than 1.5 to 3 miles per day at 3 to 4 miles per hour, in addition to the activities of independent living. Caloric needs are higher than for sedentary individuals; for example, a moderately active 36-40 year old male requires approximately 2,600 calories per day.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A peer-reviewed journal published by the U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics that covers topics including employment, wages, productivity, and occupational safety. It is cited in this content as the source for workforce participation projections by age, gender, and race through 2022.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The state of being diseased or having a health condition; in epidemiology and public health, it refers to the rate of disease or illness within a population and is factored into Healthy Life Expectancy calculations.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Any person or resource that possesses greater knowledge or skill than the learner on a specific task. The MKO is not necessarily a teacher or adult — it can be a peer, sibling, or technology tool (educational software, AI). The role is fluid: learners can become MKOs for others as their own competence grows. MKO guides learners through the ZPD via scaffolding.

The state of being subject to death; in public health, age-specific mortality refers to death rates within particular age groups and is a key factor used to calculate both life expectancy and healthy life expectancy.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The relationship between an individual's interest or relevance of material and their cognitive performance. Older adults tend to perform better on tasks involving meaningful, personally relevant information compared to arbitrary laboratory tasks (e.g., random word lists), partly explaining why cognitive losses are often overestimated in research settings.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: According to Socioemotional Selectivity Theory, the internal drives that guide social behavior. In younger people, goals focus on acquiring information and expanding social networks. In older adults, goals shift toward emotional regulation, seeking positive affect, and investing time in emotionally close relationships due to the perception of limited time remaining.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Social Networks in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: GROSS MOTOR SKILLS: physical abilities involving large body movements such as walking and jumping. Develop in cephalocaudal direction; normal walking range is roughly 9-15+ months. FINE MOTOR SKILLS: physical abilities involving small body movements, especially of the hands and fingers. The PINCER GRASP (controlling thumb and forefinger) is a key milestone enabling purposeful grasping and self-feeding. First movements are reflexes that become skills through practice and encouragement.

A household in which multiple generations of a family live together, such as middle-aged parents living with both their own aging parents and their adult children. Research suggests that living in a multigenerational household may serve as an economic safety net for young adults, as those living with parents are less likely to be living in poverty (10%) compared to those living independently (17%).

A living arrangement in which three or more generations of a family reside together under one roof. This dynamic is more common in Latin America, Asia, and Africa, but has been increasing in the United States. Grandparents in these households often play a greater role in the day-to-day activities of grandchildren.

A living arrangement in which three or more generations of a family reside together in the same home. In 2010, approximately 17% of the U.S. population lived in multigenerational households, with higher rates among Hispanic (22%), Black (23%), and Asian (25%) families compared to White families (13%).

An age-related reduction in the body's ability to convert dietary protein into muscle tissue energy and mass. This decline is one of the key mechanisms underlying sarcopenia, as the body becomes less efficient at rebuilding and maintaining muscle mass even when adequate protein is consumed.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Exercises of moderate or high intensity that work all major muscle groups, such as weight lifting and resistance training. Guidelines recommend performing these activities on two or more days per week. These exercises help maintain or increase muscle mass, which supports a higher metabolic rate and reduces age-related fat accumulation.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The proportion of muscle tissue to fat tissue in the body. During middle adulthood, this ratio changes for both men and women, with fat accumulating particularly in the stomach (abdominal) area while muscle mass declines, contributing to changes in body composition and metabolic health.

The fatty sheath that surrounds axons and enables rapid electrical signal transmission between neurons. Loss of white matter connections between brain areas in aging leads to slower neural conduction, impeding communication between brain regions and contributing to cognitive slowing.

The process by which nerve fibers are coated with a fatty white substance (myelin) that speeds neural transmission. In late adulthood, myelination thins as part of senescence, contributing to slower processing speed and cognitive decline.

The process by which nerve fibers are coated with a myelin sheath to speed neural transmission. Myelination thins with age during senescence, slowing the transmission of brain impulses and contributing to cognitive slowing.

The process by which axons become coated with myelin, a fatty substance that speeds the transmission of nerve impulses from neuron to neuron. The primary reason for faster thinking in early childhood. A gradual increase in myelination makes 5-year-olds much quicker than 3-year-olds, who are quicker than toddlers. The brain reaches 90% of adult weight by age 6.

A refractive error of the eye, commonly known as nearsightedness, in which distant objects appear blurry because light focuses in front of the retina rather than directly on it. Highly myopic individuals are at greater risk for floaters and retinal complications.

A dietary guidance tool developed by Tufts University that recommends older adults aim for 50% fruits and vegetables, 25% whole grains, and 25% protein-rich foods in their daily diet. It is designed to address the unique nutritional needs of the aging population.

Widely held but inaccurate beliefs about older adults, such as the assumption that all older adults are frail, cognitively impaired, asexual, or unable to learn new skills. These myths contribute to ageism and can negatively impact the health and wellbeing of older adults.

Researchers who described flow as the height of enjoyment, finding that individuals who experience flow more frequently judge their lives as more gratifying. Their work extended Csikszentmihalyi's theory of flow to examine its relationship with life satisfaction and well-being.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: National Council on Alcoholism and Drug Dependence (NCADD)
glossary.def: A U.S. national nonprofit organization that provides education, information, help, and hope regarding alcoholism and drug dependence. Cited in this context for 2015 statistics illustrating the scope and severity of substance abuse among older adults in America.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Substance Abuse and the Elderly | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A U.S. federal agency within the National Institutes of Health (NIH) that conducts and supports research on eye diseases and visual disorders. It is cited as a source for clinical information on presbyopia, floaters, and dry eye syndrome in the context of age-related sensory changes.

glossary.term: National Gay and Lesbian Task Force (2006)
glossary.def: An advocacy and research organization that projected the number of lesbian and gay older adults in the United States would rise to more than 7 million by 2025, underscoring the growing public health and policy significance of this demographic group.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gay and Lesbian Elders | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A United States federal agency that conducts and supports research on aging and the health of older adults. The NIA reported that there are 524 million people over 65 worldwide and projects this group will grow from 8% to 16% of the global population by 2050.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A U.S. federal agency that funds and conducts research on aging. The NIA identifies chronic illnesses, hormonal changes, physical disabilities, surgeries, and medications as factors that can adversely affect sexual functioning in older adults.

A nonprofit organization dedicated to improving sleep health. The National Sleep Foundation's Sleep in America polls have documented declining sleep duration among Americans and have provided data on sleep disorders, sleep debt, and the impact of lifestyle factors on sleep.

glossary.term: National Social Life, Health, and Aging Project (NSHAP)
glossary.def: A large-scale research study examining the social and health factors affecting the lives of older Americans, including sexuality. Data from this project indicated that 72% of men and 45.5% of women aged 52 to 72 reported being sexually active.

glossary.term: National Survey of Midlife in the United States (MIDUS)
glossary.def: A large-scale survey conducted in the United States used to study health and well-being across adulthood. The 2005 data from this survey were analyzed by Greenfield et al. (2009) to examine relationships between religiosity, spirituality, and psychological well-being.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: National Survey of Sexual Health and Behavior
glossary.def: A comprehensive survey of sexual behaviors among Americans that provided data indicating 20–30% of individuals remain sexually active well into their 80s, contradicting stereotypes about elderly asexuality.

glossary.term: National Survey of Sexual Health and Behavior (NSSHB)
glossary.def: A large-scale nationally representative survey conducted by the Center for Sexual Health Promotion (2010) that collected data on sexual behaviors, attitudes, and health across the lifespan, including middle and older adulthood. It provided data showing that a significant majority of adults aged 40–59 remained sexually active.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The evolutionary mechanism by which genes that enhance survival and reproduction are more likely to be transmitted to offspring. In the context of aging, natural selection does not act against genes that cause harm only after reproductive years have passed, allowing such genes to persist in the gene pool.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A neighborhood or apartment complex whose population is mostly retired people who moved to the location as younger adults and never left. NORCs develop organically rather than by design and are important for preserving social convoys and supporting aging in place.

Nature = the genetic influence on a person. Nurture = all non-genetic influences, from the mother's nutrition during pregnancy to national culture, family, school, community, and society. Nature always affects nurture, and nurture affects nature. The two are inseparable and interact continuously.

The experience of negative emotional states such as sadness, anxiety, and hostility. Higher levels of spirituality have been associated with lower levels of negative affect in research by Greenfield et al. (2009).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The intentional or unintentional refusal or failure of a caregiver to fulfill their caregiving obligations to an elder, including providing food, shelter, health care, or protection. Abandonment involves deserting an elder. An estimated 100,000 older adults with severe disabilities are abandoned in emergency rooms each year by family members.

A researcher who conducted a comprehensive review of the existing literature on ageism, concluding that ageism remains one of the most institutionalized forms of prejudice. Nelson found that older individuals who internalized their culture's negative stereotypes about aging experienced memory and cognitive decline, while those in cultures with positive views of aging (e.g., China) did not demonstrate comparable cognitive deficits.

An age-related decrease in the number and efficiency of motor neurons responsible for transmitting signals from the brain to skeletal muscles to initiate movement. This reduction in neural communication contributes to sarcopenia, loss of coordination, and decreased muscle strength in older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Bernice Neugarten, a developmental psychologist who noted that in midlife, adults no longer conceptualize life in terms of years lived since birth. Instead, they begin to think in terms of time remaining, reflecting a fundamental shift in temporal perspective that influences their goals and identity.

Early researchers in the field of grandparent roles who identified specific role functions including family historian and family advisor. Their work noted gender differences in role adoption, with grandfathers more likely to see themselves as family historians and advisors compared to grandmothers.

Synaptic links between neurons that form the structural basis of brain function, memory, and learning. White matter facilitates these connections, and their continued development into the 50s supports sustained cognitive functioning in middle adulthood.

The process by which the aging brain builds alternative neural connections to compensate for deteriorating or lost pathways due to neural atrophy. It is a form of neuroplasticity that enables older adults to maintain cognitive performance. Central to the Scaffolding Theory of Aging and Cognition.

The DSM-5 replacement term for dementia, referring to significant or modest cognitive decline from a previous level of performance in one or more cognitive domains. Divided into Major and Minor subtypes based on severity and impact on independent functioning.

A DSM-5 classification encompassing conditions characterized by significant decline in one or more cognitive domains. NCDs are classified as either major (previously called dementia) or mild (previously called mild cognitive impairment), depending on the severity of symptoms and impact on daily functioning.

The third most common neurocognitive disorder, affecting more than 1 million Americans. Caused by microscopic protein deposits (Lewy bodies) in neurons that affect brain chemicals related to thinking, movement, behavior, and mood. Typically begins at age 50 or older, lasts approximately 5 to 7 years, affects slightly more men than women, and presents with both cognitive and motor symptoms simultaneously, along with sleep disturbances and recurrent visual hallucinations.

Disorders characterized by significant cognitive decline from a previous level of performance in one or more cognitive domains, including memory, attention, and executive function. These disorders range from mild to major and can result from various underlying causes such as Alzheimer's disease or vascular damage.

The progressive deterioration and loss of function of neurons in the brain and nervous system. It is associated with free radical damage and is seen in disorders of advanced age, including Alzheimer's disease.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Twisted strands of malfunctioning tau protein that form inside neurons in Alzheimer's disease. These tangles disrupt the intracellular transport system, preventing nutrients and essential materials from moving through the cells, ultimately causing neuronal death.

The formation of new neurons in the adult brain, occurring particularly in the hippocampus and olfactory areas. In adulthood this process is slow and limited, providing cognitive adaptability but not sufficient to fully restore a younger brain state.

The process by which new neurons (nerve cells) are generated in the brain. Although brain cells lose some functioning with age, research including findings from the BLSA indicates that new neurons can be produced even in late adulthood, suggesting some capacity for brain plasticity persists throughout the lifespan.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The formation of new neurons in the adult brain, occurring particularly in the hippocampus and olfactory areas. This process provides cognitive adaptability to succeed in challenging and changing environments, though growth is slow, limited, and insufficient to restore the brain to its younger state.

glossary.term: Neuron Structure: Axon, Dendrite, Synapse, Neurotransmitter
glossary.def: AXON: fiber extending FROM a neuron that TRANSMITS electrochemical impulses to dendrites of other neurons. DENDRITE: fiber extending from a neuron that RECEIVES impulses transmitted from other neurons via their axons. SYNAPSE: intersection between the axon of one neuron and the dendrites of another — the gap where signals cross. NEUROTRANSMITTER: brain chemical that carries information across the synapse. MYELINIZATION: coating of axons with insulating substance that increases speed and efficiency of nerve transmission.

The loss of nerve cells in the brain, which is a hallmark of Alzheimer's disease. Caused by the accumulation of amyloid plaques, neurofibrillary tangles, inflammatory responses, and disrupted intracellular transport. Leads to brain shrinkage and impairment across all aspects of brain functioning.

The brain's ability to reorganize itself by forming new neural connections throughout life. Despite age-related brain changes, neuroplasticity allows for compensatory adaptation through practice, training, and environmental enrichment, enabling the brain to offset some effects of aging.

A personality trait characterized by emotional instability, anxiety, moodiness, and negative emotional reactivity. The middle-aged brain is described as less neurotic than younger brains, indicating improved emotional regulation with age.

One of the Big Five personality traits characterized by emotional instability, anxiety, moodiness, and negative affect. Generative adults were found to score low on neuroticism, suggesting that emotional stability is associated with successful generative functioning.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Chemical messengers that allow nerve impulses to jump across synaptic gaps between neurons. Key neurotransmitters reduced during senescence include glutamate, acetylcholine, serotonin, and especially dopamine, contributing to slower cognitive processing.

Episodes of excessive sweating during sleep, often associated with hot flashes during perimenopause and menopause. African American women were found in the SWAN study to experience higher rates of night sweats compared to other ethnic groups, though this finding varied across research sites.

An age-related deterioration in the ability to see adequately in low-light or nighttime conditions. It occurs because the pupil loses some of its ability to dilate fully in response to darkness, reducing the amount of light entering the eye. This increases sensitivity to glare from headlights and streetlights and reduces awareness of objects and movement in peripheral vision.

Cumulative hearing damage resulting from prolonged or repeated exposure to loud sounds. It is a major contributor to hearing loss in middle and older adulthood. Men are disproportionately affected due to higher rates of employment in loud occupational environments. Most such hearing loss is preventable by limiting exposure to intense noise levels.

Researcher cited in the context of strategic thinking among medical experts, noting that skilled clinicians generate more accurate diagnostic hypotheses than novices by discounting misleading symptoms and focusing on the most probable problem.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A developmental occurrence that is expected, anticipated, and commonly experienced at a particular life stage. The empty nest is considered a normative event because most parents expect their children to eventually become adults and leave home. Normative events tend to be less destabilizing than non-normative events because individuals can psychologically prepare for them.

An example of selective expertise in academic settings, where experienced students develop more efficient and strategic note-taking skills over time, such as writing only central ideas and developing personal shorthand, rather than transcribing everything verbatim as a novice might.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: An individual who is new to a particular domain or skill set and relies heavily on explicit rules and conscious processing. Novices lack the pattern recognition and intuitive decision-making that characterize experts.

An individual who has limited experience with a particular task or domain. Novices typically follow step-by-step rules and procedures when problem solving, and their actions are more formulaic and less efficient compared to experts.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The ability to perform arithmetic and mathematical calculations. The Seattle Longitudinal Study found that numerical computation declines in middle and late adulthood, alongside perceptual speed, contrasting with skills like vocabulary that improve with age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A long-term residential care facility that provides medical and personal care services to older adults who are unable to live independently. Nursing home placement becomes increasingly common with advancing age, particularly among those 85 and older.

Physical, emotional, financial, or neglectful abuse occurring within a residential care facility for elders. Estimated to occur in 25%-30% of nursing homes, and is more prevalent in facilities that are run down and understaffed.

Dietary habits and nutritional intake during the middle adult years, recognized as a key factor in promoting optimal health and preventing or mitigating secondary aging. Poor diet is a controllable factor that can accelerate age-related physical decline, while a balanced, nutritious diet supports physical health, cognitive functioning, and overall well-being during this developmental stage.

Flavor-enhancing strategies recommended by government health websites that substitute sodium-heavy seasoning with herbs, spices, citrus, and other natural flavorings. These alternatives help older adults maintain palatability of food without the cardiovascular risks associated with high sodium intake.

A medical condition characterized by excess body fat, typically defined by a body mass index (BMI) of 30 or higher. Obesity increases cholesterol levels in the bile, which raises the risk of gallstone development. It is one of the major modifiable risk factors for gallstones.

A health condition characterized by excessive body fat that significantly increases the risk of diseases including diabetes, high blood pressure, and heart disease. In midlife, obesity has also been linked to earlier onset of Alzheimer's disease. Obesity rates in the United States have increased by 25% over the 15-year period studied in the United Health Foundation's report.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The realization that objects or people continue to exist when they are no longer in sight. Piaget's findings: infants younger than 8 months do not search for a hidden object. At about 8 months, infants search immediately after hiding but not if they must wait. By 2 years, children fully understand object permanence.

A risk or danger inherent to a particular job or work environment. Males disproportionately work in high-risk occupations such as law enforcement, firefighting, and construction, increasing their exposure to injury and death and contributing to lower male life expectancy.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A U.S. government health office whose data indicated that approximately 25% of grandparents raising grandchildren are aged 65 and older, with the majority being between 55 and 64 years of age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Generativity in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A demographic situation in which a growing proportion of elderly individuals require support — financially, physically, or emotionally — from a smaller proportion of working-age adults, particularly their adult children. The ratio of people aged 85 and older to potential midlife caregivers (ages 50–64) is expected to more than double by 2040.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: One of three age subcategories within late adulthood, typically referring to individuals between the ages of 75 and 84. This group may begin to experience more significant health and functional challenges compared to the young-old.

One of three age subcategories within late adulthood, typically referring to individuals aged 85 and above. This is the fastest-growing segment of the older adult population and often requires the most healthcare and support services.

A demographic subgroup within late adulthood typically defined as individuals aged 85 and older. According to U.S. Census Bureau projections, this group represented 1.9% of the population in 2012 and is projected to grow to 2.5% by 2030, making it one of the fastest-growing demographic subgroups.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A subcategory of late adulthood encompassing individuals between the ages of 85 and 99, sometimes called the 'fourth age.' Characterized by more serious chronic ailments, higher rates of hospitalization, greater need for long-term care, and lower likelihood of being married or living independently compared to the young-old.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A demographic term referring to individuals in the most advanced stages of late adulthood, typically age 85 and older, among whom sensory declines and disability rates are most pronounced.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A demographic category typically referring to individuals aged 85 and older. Research by Lee, Steinman, and Tan (2011) found that even among the oldest-old, volunteers have a death rate that is half that of non-volunteers.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Generativity in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The sense of smell. Decreases more significantly with age than gustation. Olfactory cells located high in the nasal cavity are stimulated by two pathways: direct inhalation through the nose and via the nose-throat connection during chewing and digestion. Problems with olfaction are more common in men (1 in 4 males in their 60s) than women (1 in 10).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The sense of smell, which plays a critical role in flavor perception and appetite. Decline in olfactory function is common in older adults and can significantly reduce the enjoyment of food, contributing to poor dietary intake and nutritional deficiencies.

The branch of medicine that deals with the prevention, diagnosis, and treatment of cancer. Given the projected increase in older cancer patients, it is recommended that physicians have expertise in both oncology and geriatrics.

The use of internet-based platforms to seek romantic or companionate relationships. Montenegro (2003) surveyed over 3,000 singles aged 40–69, finding that almost half cited having someone to talk to or do things with as their most important reason for dating. Alterovitz and Mendelsohn (2013) found that romantic and sexual interests were mentioned at similar rates among middle-aged and young-old online daters, but less so among old-old individuals.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The use of internet platforms, including e-mail, chat rooms, and dedicated dating websites, by older adults to expand social networks and seek romantic relationships. Research shows that older adults mirror younger adults in online dating behavior, with men prioritizing physical attractiveness and offering status-related information.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: One of the Big Five personality traits characterized by intellectual curiosity, creativity, and receptivity to new ideas. Highly generative individuals scored high on this trait, reflecting the creative and idea-generating dimensions of generativity.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A personality trait characterized by curiosity, creativity, and a willingness to engage with new ideas and perspectives. Identified by Baltes and Kunzmann as one of the personality characteristics that provides the building blocks of wisdom, alongside generativity and other-focused experiences.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Reinforcement or punishment may be used to either increase or decrease the probability that a behavior will occur again. Positive consequences make behavior more likely to repeat (reinforcement); negative consequences make it less likely. Also called instrumental conditioning. What counts as punishment or reward is individualized.

A class of analgesic drugs derived from or chemically related to opium, prescribed for moderate to severe pain. Among the drugs of choice abused by older adults, often prescribed by family physicians to manage chronic pain rather than pursuing non-pharmacological alternatives such as therapy.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Substance Abuse and the Elderly | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Brain receptors that bind to opiate drugs to produce pain relief. Women have fewer opioid receptors than men, resulting in less effective pain relief from opiate medications in women, who also paradoxically report pain more frequently than men.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The nerve that transmits visual information from the retina to the brain. In glaucoma, increased intraocular pressure damages this nerve, beginning with its exterior portions that receive peripheral visual input and progressing inward.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A level of aging in late adulthood characterized by better-than-average health, social well-being, and functioning, representing the positive end of the spectrum of how individuals age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: Optimism and Positive Views of Aging
glossary.def: A psychological orientation in which older adults hold favorable and constructive attitudes about the aging process and their own capabilities. Research indicates that older adults who possess positive, optimistic views of aging are less likely to experience physical or mental health problems and are more likely to live longer than those with negative views.

Within the Selective Optimization with Compensation model, optimization refers to the process of making the best use of available internal and external resources to maintain or enhance functioning in selected goal areas. Examples include practicing a skill repeatedly or using assistive technologies to remain capable.

A form of sexual expression involving stimulation of the genitals with the mouth. It is one of several alternatives to vaginal intercourse that older couples may incorporate more frequently as age-related physical changes affect traditional sexual functioning.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD)
glossary.def: An intergovernmental economic organisation with 38 member countries, founded to stimulate economic progress and world trade. In the context of work at midlife, OECD data is cited to compare annual work hours across nations, showing that American workers work more hours per year than most European workers but fewer hours than Mexican workers.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A major branch of Judaism characterized by strict adherence to traditional Jewish law and practice. In Orthodox Judaism, a minyan of 10 men is required for communal prayer, which influences gender differences in worship attendance. Roughly 22% of Jewish adults in Israel self-identify as Orthodox.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A form of low blood pressure that occurs when a person stands up from a sitting or lying position. It is among the age-related conditions linked to hormonal and metabolic changes associated with the Hormonal Stress Theory of aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Authors of the U.S. Census Bureau report 'An Aging Nation: The Older Population in the United States,' which provided 2012 life expectancy data broken down by sex, race, and ethnicity used in lifespan development research.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who defined and described the young-old age category as comprising older adults between the ages of 65 and 84, contributing to the framework for subcategorizing late adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Authors of a United States Census Bureau report titled 'An Aging Nation: The Older Population in the United States,' which provided comparative data on population aging across developed nations and projections through 2050, noting that the U.S. is younger than most other developed countries despite its own aging trend.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The most common type of arthritis, occurring when the cartilage on the ends of bones wears away, causing bone to rub against bone. This results in pain, swelling, stiffness, and over time, chronic pain and loss of joint strength. Risk factors include genetics, obesity, age, previous injury, and other medical conditions.

An early-stage condition in which bones lose some of their mineral density and become less dense than normal but not to the degree classified as osteoporosis. It represents a transitional state between normal bone density and osteoporosis and increases fracture risk.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A condition characterized by decreased bone mass and density, leading to increased bone fragility and fracture risk. During menopause, the decline in estrogen—which plays a key role in bone formation—accelerates bone loss and increases a woman's risk of developing osteoporosis.

A disease that thins and weakens bones to the point that they become fragile and break easily. After age 50, 1 in 2 women and 1 in 4 men will experience an osteoporosis-related fracture. More than 53 million adults in the U.S. either already have osteoporosis or are at high risk due to low bone mass.

An advanced condition characterized by severely reduced bone mineral density, making bones fragile and highly susceptible to fractures. It is the later, more severe stage following osteopenia and is associated with aging, hormonal changes, and reduced calcium and mineral content in bones.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A skeletal disorder characterized by decreased bone density and mass, leading to fragile bones that are susceptible to fractures. It is particularly common in postmenopausal women and older adults generally. Risk factors include age, sex, low calcium intake, sedentary lifestyle, and hormonal changes.

Careers and life experiences that emphasize concern for others rather than self-interest. According to Baltes and Kunzmann (2004), engagement in other-focused roles provides the building blocks of wisdom by fostering empathy, broader perspective-taking, and concern for collective well-being.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The state of exceeding one's physical or cognitive limits, which becomes increasingly important to avoid as people age due to declining energy reserves and physical resilience. In the context of SOC theory, reorganizing daily routines is one compensatory strategy used to prevent over-exertion in older adults.

A researcher who noted that ageist stereotypes are reflected in everyday cultural artifacts including greeting cards, where jokes about aging serve as a socially acceptable means of expressing discomfort with growing older in a culture that does not revere age.

U.S. federal and state regulations requiring employers to pay additional compensation (typically 1.5 times the regular rate) to employees who work beyond a standard number of hours. Not all employees are covered under these laws, which contributes to longer work hours without additional compensation for many American workers.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A key biological mechanism of primary aging involving harm to cells and tissues caused by free radicals — unstable molecules that react with and damage DNA, proteins, and lipids. Oxidative damage accumulates over time and contributes to cellular aging, organ dysfunction, and increased susceptibility to age-related diseases.

Employer-provided compensated time away from work. In the United States, unlike many other developed nations, there is no federal law mandating paid vacation time for workers in many job sectors. Higher-paying jobs and unionized positions are more likely to include paid vacation and holidays.

Age-related alterations in the structure and function of brain regions involved in pain processing, as documented by Farrell (2012). Older adults may have altered pain thresholds and perception, which can complicate the assessment and management of pain in clinical settings.

Foods that are pleasant in taste, smell, and texture. In the context of older adult nutrition, palatability decreases due to sensory decline, prompting the need for healthy flavor-enhancing alternatives to salt and sugar.

Specialized cells in the pancreas responsible for producing and secreting insulin. In Type 2 diabetes, as insulin resistance increases, beta cells are forced to produce more insulin to compensate, and over time may gradually lose this capacity. In some Type 2 diabetics, beta cells cease functioning entirely, making insulin injections necessary.

The duct through which pancreatic enzymes flow from the pancreas to the small intestine. If a gallstone becomes lodged at the junction with the bile duct, it can obstruct pancreatic secretion, leading to serious complications such as pancreatitis.

A brain region involved in attention, spatial processing, and sensory integration. Older adults activate larger regions of their attentional and executive networks located in the parietal and prefrontal cortex during complex tasks, reflecting compensatory neural recruitment.

Researchers who studied the relationship between cognitive aging and everyday functioning, including driving and work performance. They found that while cognitive declines may pose limitations for driving under certain conditions, older workers rarely perform poorer on the job compared to younger workers.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who conducted a comprehensive Pew Research survey on the sandwich generation, finding that 47% of middle-aged adults are part of this group, 15% provide financial support to an older parent while also supporting children, 48% have supported adult children in the past year, and 27% are the primary source of support for their grown children.

A progressive neurodegenerative disorder characterized by motor tremors, loss of balance, poor coordination, rigidity, and difficulty moving. It affects approximately 1% of those over 60, is caused by deterioration of the substantia nigra, and involves loss of dopaminergic neurons projecting to the basal ganglia. Both genetic (28 chromosomal areas implicated) and environmental factors (toxins, head injury) contribute to its development.

A neurodegenerative movement disorder with motor symptoms including tremors and muscle rigidity that are similar to those seen in Neurocognitive Disorder with Lewy Bodies. A key distinction is that in Parkinson's disease, cognitive symptoms appear well after the motor symptoms, whereas in Lewy body disorder, cognitive and motor symptoms occur simultaneously.

The presence of a willing and able sexual partner, which is a critical factor in maintaining sexual activity in later life. Older heterosexual women are disproportionately affected by lack of partner availability due to the sex ratio imbalance and older men's tendency to pair with younger women after divorce or widowhood.

Age-related trends in romantic partner selection observed in online dating research by Alterovitz and Mendelsohn (2011). With advancing age, men increasingly desired women younger than themselves, while women desired older men until age 75+, at which point women also began seeking men younger than themselves, likely to avoid becoming caregivers or widowed again.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Developmental psychologist (with Staudinger and Lindenberger, 1999) who researched wisdom and lifespan development. Along with Scheibe and Kunzmann, contributed to understanding how crystallized intelligence and life experience contribute to wisdom in older adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A developmental psychologist who introduced the concepts of pragmatics and mechanics of intelligence, and who conducted extensive research on wisdom. He proposed that wisdom is rare, can emerge as early as late adolescence, and is more influenced by certain types of experiences and personality characteristics than by age alone.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

glossary.term: Peer Relationships: Selection, Facilitation, Deviancy Training
glossary.def: PEER PRESSURE: encouragement to conform to friends in behavior, dress, and attitude. SELECTION: teenagers select friends whose values and interests they share, abandoning friends who follow other paths. FACILITATION: peers facilitate both destructive AND constructive behaviors — it is easier to do both the wrong thing and the right thing. DEVIANCY TRAINING: destructive peer support in which one person shows another how to rebel against authority or social norms. ROMANCE SEQUENCE: same-sex groups → loose mixed crowd → small mixed-sex groups → couples with private intimacies. Sexual orientation is fluid during the teen years.

An employer-sponsored retirement savings plan providing workers with a defined benefit upon retirement. Historically one of three pillars of retirement income alongside Social Security and personal savings. Many pension plans lost significant value during the 2008 recession, contributing to delayed retirement among older workers.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The number of centenarians relative to the total population of a given country. While the U.S. has the most centenarians in absolute numbers, Japan and Italy have the highest per capita rates of centenarians (Stepler, 2016e).
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The ability to quickly and accurately perceive and compare visual information. According to the Seattle Longitudinal Study, perceptual speed declines in middle and late adulthood, representing one area where younger adults outperform older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Crystalized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Tests measuring the ability to solve problems quickly, often involving nonverbal tasks, spatial reasoning, and processing speed. Scores on performance tests decline with age, reflecting decreases in fluid intelligence and mechanics of intelligence.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The transitional phase before menopause during which a woman's ovaries gradually stop releasing eggs and the production of estrogen and progesterone decreases. This period can begin 2 to 8 years before menopause and is characterized by irregular periods and menopausal symptoms such as hot flashes and sleep disturbances.

The transitional phase leading up to menopause during which hormonal fluctuations cause irregular menstrual cycles and various symptoms such as hot flashes, mood changes, and vaginal dryness. It can last several years before menopause is officially reached.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A sleep disorder in which individuals involuntarily jerk and kick their legs every 20 to 40 seconds during sleep, disrupting sleep quality. It is distinct from restless legs syndrome and is more common in older adults.

In the Convoy Model, relationships located at the outer edges of the convoy, such as coworkers, neighbors, and acquaintances. These relationships are less stable than inner-circle relationships and are more vulnerable to changes in jobs, social roles, geographic location, and other life events. Their frequency, type, and reciprocity of social exchanges decrease with age.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Social Networks in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Individuals who commit elder abuse. Most commonly family members, including spouses/partners and adult children. Adult children who are abusive tend to be financially, housing, and emotionally dependent on their parents. Substance use, mental illness, and chronic unemployment increase this dependency. Also includes paid caregivers, guardians, and lawyers.

Hands-on physical assistance provided to aging parents, such as helping with bathing or getting dressed. In the United States, approximately 1 in 5 women report having provided personal care to aging parents in the past year, compared to 8% of men. In Germany, the figures are similar, but in Italy, men (25%) and women (26%) are equally likely to have provided personal care, reflecting cultural differences in caregiving norms.

A dimension of psychological well-being referring to an individual's sense of continued development, openness to new experiences, and realization of personal potential. Both spirituality and formal religious participation (among older adults) were associated with higher personal growth.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who studied the relationship between generativity and positive outcomes across adulthood. Their work demonstrated that generative adults possess good cultural knowledge and healthy adaptation to the world, and that women scoring high in generativity at age 52 were rated higher in positive personality characteristics, marital satisfaction, and successful aging at age 62.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Erikson: Generativity vs Stagnation | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: An American nonpartisan think tank that informs the public about issues, attitudes, and trends shaping the world. In the midlife work context, Pew Research data from 2010 is cited to show that 55% of adults experienced workplace difficulties during the recent recession and that middle-aged adults (especially ages 50–64) reported being hit hardest by the economic downturn.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A nonpartisan American fact tank that conducts public opinion polling, demographic research, and surveys on a wide range of topics including religion. Their 2016 report analyzed religious beliefs and practices of men and women in 84 countries between 2008 and 2015.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A research organization whose findings documented the increasing prevalence of multigenerational households in the United States, a trend more historically associated with Latin American, Asian, and African family structures. This increase reflects broader demographic and economic shifts influencing American family dynamics.

glossary.term: Pew Research Center Survey on Living Arrangements
glossary.def: A survey referenced in research on late adulthood living arrangements, finding that 33% of older adults living alone reported living comfortably versus 49% of those living with others, and that 12% living alone lacked money for basic needs versus 5% of those living with others.

A gradual transition into retirement in which a worker reduces their working hours over time rather than retiring abruptly. Approximately 10% of workers pursue this approach, though not all employers permit it due to pension regulations.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A subsystem of working memory responsible for maintaining and processing auditory and verbal information. It stores spoken or written material in a short-term phonological store and rehearses it through subvocal repetition. Age-related declines in this component are minimal compared to the central executive.

Premature aging of the skin caused by repeated and prolonged exposure to ultraviolet (UV) radiation from the sun. Effects include wrinkles, dryness, age spots, and increased risk of skin cancer, and are distinct from intrinsic (chronological) aging changes.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The use of physical force against an elder that results in bodily injury, physical pain, or impairment. Examples include hitting, pushing, restraining, or improper use of medication or physical restraints.

Research finding, particularly from Kramer and Erickson (2007), demonstrating that regular physical exercise can slow age-related declines in cognitive functioning across the adult life span. Exercise is considered a behavioral intervention that supports both physical and mental health in older adults.

A controllable lifestyle factor and contributor to secondary aging characterized by insufficient levels of physical exercise and movement. Physical inactivity in middle adulthood is associated with increased risk of cardiovascular disease, obesity, diabetes, and accelerated physical decline. Getting out of shape during middle adulthood is largely attributed to reduced physical activity rather than inevitable aging.

A tendency or prejudice held by medical professionals that influences clinical decision-making. In the context of elderly substance abuse, physicians may be biased against inquiring about drug or alcohol use in older patients, contributing to underdiagnosis and undertreatment of substance use disorders in this population.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Substance Abuse and the Elderly | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Researchers who found a link between volunteerism and mental health outcomes in older adults, specifically that volunteering and the associated social contact are linked to higher life satisfaction and lower rates of depression and anxiety.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Generativity in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A buildup of fat, cholesterol, calcium, and other blood substances on arterial walls that hardens over time and restricts blood flow. Relevant to erectile dysfunction as plaque restricts oxygenated blood flow to the penis. It is the pathological basis of atherosclerosis.

Fatty deposits that accumulate in the walls of arteries in atherosclerosis. Plaque buildup restricts blood flow and oxygen delivery, potentially causing heart attacks, strokes, or leg pain depending on the arteries affected.

Two complementary aspects: (1) human traits can be molded — people can and do change; (2) people maintain a certain durability of identity. Dynamic systems approach: human development is an ongoing, ever-changing interaction between the physical and emotional being and every aspect of the environment.

Five types of social play in developmental sequence: SOLITARY play (child plays alone, unaware of others). ONLOOKER play (child watches other children play). PARALLEL play (children play with similar toys in similar ways but not together). ASSOCIATIVE play (children interact, share materials, observe each other, but play is not yet mutual and reciprocal). COOPERATIVE play (children play together, creating and elaborating a joint activity or taking turns). Also: ROUGH-AND-TUMBLE play — mimics aggression with no intent to harm; common among young males; may positively affect prefrontal cortex development. SOCIODRAMATIC play — enables children to explore social rules, practice emotional regulation, develop self-concept.

Older adults (65+) are the most politically engaged age group relative to formal civic participation such as voting, writing to representatives, identifying with a political party, and staying informed. However, only about 7% volunteer for political, civic, international, or professional groups, and only 2% volunteer for political campaigns.

The simultaneous use of multiple medications by a single patient, which is common among older adults. This practice increases the risk of unpredictable and potentially dangerous drug interactions when combined with psychoactive substances such as alcohol, benzodiazepines, or opioids.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Substance Abuse and the Elderly | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A global demographic trend in which the median age of a population increases due to rising life expectancy and falling birth rates, resulting in a larger proportion of older adults relative to younger age groups.

A global and national demographic phenomenon whereby the proportion of older individuals in a population increases over time relative to younger cohorts. In the United States, this is projected to result in nearly one in four Americans being over 65 by 2050, driven by baby boomer aging and rising life expectancy.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The process by which the median age of a population increases and the proportion of older individuals grows relative to younger cohorts. It results from declining fertility rates and improvements in life expectancy and is a global trend affecting both developed and less developed nations.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The experience of positive emotional states such as joy, enthusiasm, and contentment. Higher levels of spirituality have been associated with higher levels of positive affect according to the 2005 National Survey of Midlife in the United States.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The tendency of older adults to focus more on positive information and less on negative information compared to younger adults. This includes better memory for positive images and reduced amygdala response to negative stimuli.

A key mechanism in Socioemotional Selectivity Theory whereby older adults actively prioritize interactions and relationships that maximize positive emotional experiences and minimize exposure to negative emotions. This leads to the characteristic pattern of older adults having smaller but more emotionally satisfying social networks.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Social Networks in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The tendency observed in elderly people to perceive, prefer, and remember positive images and experiences more than negative ones. It is considered an outgrowth of socioemotional selectivity theory and selective optimization with compensation, and it reflects the convergence of the ideal self and real self with age.

Ongoing pain that persists in the area where the shingles rash occurred after the rash itself has resolved. PHN is more likely to develop and be more severe in older individuals. It can interfere with daily activities such as dressing, cooking, and eating, and may cause depression, anxiety, and sleeplessness.

A term used interchangeably with 'empty nest' to describe the phase of parenting after children have grown up and left the home. This period is associated with changes in marital satisfaction, personal identity, and emotional well-being, and has been studied primarily in American samples, with different outcomes observed in Chinese populations.

A clinical illness and form of stress response that can result from exposure to extremely traumatic or threatening events. Listed in the source as an example of stress manifesting as clinical illness, distinct from everyday stressors or major life events.

The stage of life following the permanent cessation of menstrual cycles. In Western cultural contexts, postmenopausal women may be perceived as having lost social value tied to reproductive ability, contributing to heightened anxiety and identity concerns associated with aging.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The percentage of a given population whose income falls below the federally defined poverty threshold. Among older adults in the United States, poverty rates vary significantly by gender, marital status, race, and age. Women 65 and older are 70% more likely to be poor than men, and women aged 80 and above face even higher poverty rates.

A concept introduced by Paul Baltes referring to cultural exposure to facts and procedures that are maintained as one ages. Analogous to crystallized intelligence, pragmatics of intelligence show little decline and typically increase with age. They may also compensate for declines in mechanics of intelligence.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: A condition in which blood glucose levels are higher than normal but not yet high enough to be diagnosed as Type 2 diabetes. Approximately 1 in 3 American adults has prediabetes, and 9 in 10 of them are unaware. Without intervention, 15–30% of people with prediabetes will develop Type 2 diabetes within 5 years.

The anterior region of the frontal lobe responsible for planning, inhibiting unwanted responses, and coordinating thoughts. It is particularly vulnerable to age-related shrinkage and its decline contributes significantly to impaired executive function in late adulthood.

A region of the brain involved in pain processing and modulation. Age-related decreases in brain volume in this area, along with the brainstem and hippocampus, have been associated with changes in pain perception in older adults.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The anterior portion of the frontal lobe involved in executive functions, working memory, decision-making, and attention. In aging, older adults recruit larger regions of the prefrontal cortex during complex tasks, but this increased activation is associated with reduced performance compared to younger adults.

Between ages 2 and 6, neurological increases are especially notable in areas of the cortex where planning, thinking, social awareness, and language occur. Maturation gradually facilitates: focused attention, reduced impulsiveness, and less perseveration. PERSEVERATION: a child persists in or sticks to one thought or action, unable to quit — an opposite manifestation to impulsiveness, both caused by prefrontal immaturity. Neurological immaturity also underlies why adults must prevent childhood injury.

A condition in which blood pressure is elevated above normal but not yet at hypertensive levels. Defined as systolic pressure of 120–139 mmHg or diastolic pressure of 80–89 mmHg. Individuals with prehypertension are considered at risk for developing hypertension and cardiovascular disease.

The placement of an elder into a nursing home or care facility earlier than medically necessary, often as a direct consequence of abuse or neglect that has deteriorated the elder's health and functional capacity.

Piaget's second stage of cognitive development. Major accomplishment: SYMBOLIC THOUGHT — understanding that words can refer to things not seen and that an item can symbolize something else. Characteristics that obstruct logic: ANIMISM (belief that natural objects are alive), CENTRATION (focusing on one idea, excluding others), EGOCENTRISM (seeing world only from own perspective — not selfishness but inability to take another's viewpoint), FOCUS ON APPEARANCE (ignoring non-apparent attributes), STATIC REASONING (nothing changes), IRREVERSIBILITY (nothing can be undone), and CONSERVATION FAILURES (the amount of a substance remains the same when its appearance changes — not yet understood).

Age-related hearing loss that accumulates gradually over time, often resulting from years of exposure to loud noise, biological aging of the auditory system, or both. It commonly includes difficulty hearing high-frequency sounds and understanding speech in noisy environments. It is more common and progresses more noticeably in men than women, partly because men are more likely to work in noisy occupational environments.

A common, gradual, age-related hearing loss that affects both ears equally. It runs in families and increases in incidence with age. More prevalent in males than females. Affects nearly 1 in 4 adults aged 65–74 and 1 in 2 adults aged 75 and older.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: An age-related vision condition derived from the Latin for 'old vision,' characterized by a progressive loss of elasticity in the lens of the eye that makes it increasingly difficult to focus on nearby objects. It results from weakening of the tiny ciliary muscles surrounding the lens, which can no longer adequately bend the lens to focus light on the retina. Anyone over the age of 35 is at risk. Symptoms include difficulty reading small print, needing to hold reading material farther away than arm's length, headaches, and eyestrain.

Age-related loss of the ability to focus on close objects, caused by the gradual hardening and loss of flexibility of the eye's lens. Typically begins in middle adulthood and becomes more pronounced in late adulthood.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The age-related loss of the sense of smell. More common in men than women. Can lead to dietary changes, malnutrition, or overconsumption of sugar and salt as individuals try to compensate for diminished flavor perception.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: The use of prescription medications in a manner not intended by the prescribing physician, including taking someone else's prescription, taking higher doses than prescribed, or using medication for non-medical purposes. Particularly serious among older adults, who account for nearly 30% of all U.S. prescriptions despite being only 13% of the population.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Substance Abuse and the Elderly | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Actions taken by individuals to prevent illness, maintain health, and detect disease early, such as exercise, healthy diet, medical screenings, and vaccinations. Older adults who believe in negative aging stereotypes are less likely to engage in these behaviors, leading to worse health outcomes.

Healthcare practices aimed at preventing disease or detecting it early before symptoms become severe, rather than treating conditions after they develop. Exercise is considered a form of preventative health care. Midlife adults who engage in challenging physical and mental activity, monitor nutrition, get adequate sleep, and use social resources are most likely to maintain good health.
glossary.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development

glossary.def: Age-related physical changes that result from biological factors beyond individual control, including molecular and cellular changes and oxidative damage. Coined by Busse (1969), primary aging is universal and inevitable, occurring regardless of lifestyle choices. Examples include cellular senescence, collagen changes in skin, and age-related declines in organ function.

The gradual and inevitable biological process of bodily deterioration that occurs with advancing age, independent of disease or environmental influences. Includes changes such as decreased muscle mass, reduced sensory acuity, and slower processing speed.
