flashcards.back: Because the double standard of aging labels postmenopausal women as 'old' and less socially valued, women may feel compelled to counter this narrative by demonstrating productivity and value beyond reproductive roles (Bromberger, Kravitz, & Chang, 2013).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What factors moderate how women experience and perceive aging?
flashcards.back: Key moderating factors include race/ethnicity (Black and Latina women hold fewer stereotypes), culture (Asian women may gain status with age), sexual orientation (lesbians more positive about aging), and media exposure (which can increase pressure to look young). These factors all intersect with the broader societal double standard of aging.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the difference between religiosity and spirituality according to Greenfield, Vaillant, and Marks (2009)?
flashcards.back: Religiosity refers to engaging with a formal religious group's doctrines, values, traditions, and co-members. Spirituality refers to an individual's intrapsychic sense of connection with something transcendent and the feelings of awe, gratitude, compassion, and forgiveness that follow. Spirituality can exist independently of formal religious participation.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What psychological well-being outcomes are associated with higher spirituality according to the 2005 MIDUS data?
flashcards.back: Higher spirituality was associated with lower negative affect and higher levels of: positive affect, personal growth, purpose in life, positive relationships with others, self-acceptance, environmental mastery, and autonomy.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What psychological well-being outcomes are associated with formal religious participation according to Greenfield et al. (2009)?
flashcards.back: Formal religious participation was associated with higher levels of purpose in life and personal growth among older adults only, and with lower levels of autonomy. It was NOT associated with the broad range of well-being outcomes linked to spirituality.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What three factors explain why older individuals tend to be more religious and spiritual than younger individuals?
flashcards.back: 1) Religion and spirituality help older individuals cope with age-related losses. 2) They provide opportunities for socialization and social support in later life. 3) A cohort effect — older generations were socialized more strongly to be religious and spiritual than younger cohorts.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: According to the Pew Research Center (2016), what are the key gender differences in religious participation in the United States?
flashcards.back: Women are more likely than men to say religion is very important (60% vs. 47%), pray daily (64% vs. 47%), and attend religious services at least weekly (40% vs. 32%).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why might women be more religious and spiritual than men according to the theoretical explanations provided?
flashcards.back: Two main theories: 1) Women may benefit more from the social-relational aspects of religion/spirituality because social relationships more strongly influence women's mental health. 2) Women have been socialized to internalize behaviors linked with religious values such as cooperation and nurturance more than men.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is a minyan and how does it affect gender differences in Jewish worship attendance?
flashcards.back: A minyan is a quorum of at least 10 Jewish men required for communal worship services in Orthodox Judaism. This religious norm ensures high rates of synagogue attendance among men. In Israel, where ~22% of Jewish adults are Orthodox, a higher percentage of men than women report engaging in daily prayer — a rare exception to the global pattern of women being more religious.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How did Muslim gender differences in religiosity differ from Christian gender differences in the Pew Research Center 2016 survey?
flashcards.back: Among Christians, women reported higher rates of weekly church attendance and daily prayer than men. Among Muslims, men and women showed similar levels of religiousness overall, except that Muslim men attended mosque more frequently than Muslim women due to religious norms, not a difference in general religiosity.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What did Sawatzky, Ratner, and Chiu (2005) find about spirituality and quality of life?
flashcards.back: Spirituality was related to a higher quality of life for both individuals and societies, suggesting that the benefits of spiritual orientation extend beyond the individual level to broader societal well-being.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: According to Grzywacz and Keyes (2004), how does religious involvement relate to health?
flashcards.back: Involvement in religious-related activities is positively related to optimal health, similar to personal health behaviors like regular exercise, healthy weight, and not smoking. Both personal and social behaviors (including religious involvement) contribute to optimal health outcomes.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Is spirituality's relationship with psychological well-being dependent on formal religious participation?
flashcards.back: No. Research by Vaillant (2008) demonstrated a strong relationship between spirituality and psychological well-being irrespective of an individual's religious participation. One does not need to be formally religious to benefit psychologically from spirituality.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: In the Pew Research Center 2016 global survey, what was the overall gender gap in religious identification worldwide?
flashcards.back: Approximately 83% of women worldwide identified with a religion compared to 80% of men, equaling approximately 97 million more women than men identifying with a religion. There were no countries in which men were more religious than women by 2 percentage points or more.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does formal religious participation relate to autonomy compared to spirituality?
flashcards.back: Formal religious participation is associated with LOWER levels of autonomy (possibly due to adherence to group doctrines and norms), whereas spirituality — as an intrapsychic, individually-driven experience — is associated with HIGHER levels of autonomy.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What age-related factors explain increased religiosity and spirituality in older adulthood?
flashcards.back: Older adults face more age-related losses (health, loved ones, roles), making religion and spirituality important coping mechanisms. They also benefit from the social support and socialization opportunities religious communities provide. Additionally, a cohort effect means older generations were raised to be more religious.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the significance of the finding that spirituality benefits both religious and non-religious individuals?
flashcards.back: It means that the psychological benefits of spirituality — including lower negative affect, higher positive affect, personal growth, purpose, and well-being — are accessible to anyone who cultivates a personal sense of connection to something transcendent, regardless of institutional religious affiliation. This has implications for health promotion across diverse populations.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Compare the scope of psychological well-being benefits from spirituality vs. formal religious participation.
flashcards.back: Spirituality is broadly associated with 8 dimensions of well-being: lower negative affect, higher positive affect, personal growth, purpose in life, positive relationships, self-acceptance, environmental mastery, and autonomy. Formal religious participation has a much narrower association — only purpose in life and personal growth (and only among older adults), plus lower autonomy.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the 'gray divorce revolution' and who documented it?
flashcards.back: The gray divorce revolution refers to the rising divorce rate among adults aged 50 and older in the United States between 1990 and 2010. It was documented by Brown and Lin (2013) through the National Center for Family & Marriage Research at Bowling Green State University.

flashcards.front: What did Chang et al. (2016) find about midlife adiposity and Alzheimer's disease?
flashcards.back: Chang et al. (2016) found that midlife adiposity (obesity/overweight) predicts earlier onset of Alzheimer's dementia, neuropathology, and presymptomatic cerebral amyloid accumulation, suggesting that weight management in midlife may be a modifiable risk factor for Alzheimer's.

flashcards.front: Describe Erik Erikson's stage of generativity versus stagnation and when it occurs.
flashcards.back: Generativity vs. stagnation is Erikson's seventh psychosocial stage, occurring in middle adulthood (approximately ages 40–65). Generativity involves contributing to society through raising children, mentoring, and creative productivity. Failure results in stagnation — a sense of self-absorption and lack of meaningful contribution.

flashcards.front: What is the buffering hypothesis proposed by Cohen and Wills (1985)?
flashcards.back: The buffering hypothesis states that social support protects (buffers) individuals from the harmful psychological and physical effects of stress. Social support is especially protective during periods of high stress, acting as a moderator between stressors and negative health outcomes.

flashcards.front: How does Csikszentmihalyi define 'flow' and what are its key characteristics?
flashcards.back: Csikszentmihalyi (1990) defines flow as a state of optimal experience in which a person is completely absorbed in a challenging, intrinsically rewarding activity. Key characteristics include full concentration, loss of self-consciousness, distorted time perception, and deep enjoyment. It is associated with creativity and peak performance.

flashcards.front: What did Dew et al. (2003) and Ferrie et al. (2007) find about sleep and mortality in adults?
flashcards.back: Dew et al. (2003) found that healthy older adults' sleep quality predicted all-cause mortality at 4 to 19 years of follow-up. Ferrie et al. (2007) found that changes in sleep duration were associated with mortality in the Whitehall II cohort, with both short and long sleep durations linked to increased risk.

flashcards.front: What is the 'glass ceiling' versus the 'glass partition' in workplace research?
flashcards.back: The glass ceiling (Barreto, Ryan & Schmitt, 2009) refers to invisible barriers preventing women and minorities from reaching top organizational positions. The glass partition (Elsesser & Peplau, 2006) refers specifically to obstacles preventing cross-sex friendships in the workplace, such as concerns about sexual misinterpretation.

flashcards.front: What is kinkeeping and which demographic typically performs this role?
flashcards.back: Kinkeeping is the role of maintaining family connections, organizing family gatherings, facilitating communication among family members, and preserving intergenerational bonds. This role is most often assumed by women in midlife. It was studied by Bengtson (2001) and Brown & DeRycke (2010).

flashcards.front: What did Bromberger et al. (2013) find about anxiety and the menopausal transition?
flashcards.back: Bromberger et al. (2013), using data from the Study of Women's Health Across the Nation (SWAN), found evidence suggesting that risk for anxiety increases during the menopausal transition, indicating that hormonal changes during perimenopause may contribute to psychological vulnerability.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between problem-focused and emotion-focused coping?
flashcards.back: Problem-focused coping involves taking direct action to address or eliminate the source of stress (e.g., making a plan, seeking information). Emotion-focused coping involves managing the emotional distress caused by the stressor (e.g., seeking social support, reframing). Carver, Scheier & Weintraub (1989) developed a widely used measure of these coping strategies.

flashcards.front: What did Ahlborg, Misvaer & Möller (2009) find about marital quality and parenthood?
flashcards.back: Ahlborg, Misvaer & Möller (2009) conducted a follow-up study of parents when their firstborn was 4 years old and examined perceptions of marital quality. The study highlighted that having small children can strain marital satisfaction, with couples reporting declines in relationship quality during early parenthood.

flashcards.front: What does research say about the experience of parents when children leave home (empty nest)?
flashcards.back: Research presents mixed findings. Borland (1982) found empty nest syndrome varies by ethnicity. Bouchard (2013) found most parents adapt well. Dennerstein, Dudley & Guthrie (2002) found a 'revolving door' phenomenon where children leave and re-enter. Many parents, especially women, report improved wellbeing and marital satisfaction after children leave.

flashcards.front: What did Baltes, Staudinger & Lindenberger (1999) argue about lifespan psychology and intellectual functioning?
flashcards.back: Baltes, Staudinger & Lindenberger (1999) argued that intellectual functioning across the lifespan is characterized by multidirectionality (some abilities decline while others improve), plasticity (capacity for change), and embeddedness in historical and cultural context. They emphasized that development involves both gains and losses throughout life.

flashcards.front: What are the health risks associated with caregiver roles in midlife and beyond?
flashcards.back: Beach et al. (2000) found both negative and positive health effects of caregiving. Negative effects include increased stress, depression, and physical health decline. Positive effects can include sense of purpose and relationship closeness. Arai et al. (2000) found that in rural Japan, concern for others' opinions deterred caregivers from using public services, compounding burden.

flashcards.front: What did Carmichael, Reis & Duberstein (2015) find about social activity across adulthood?
flashcards.back: Carmichael, Reis & Duberstein (2015) found that in one's 20s, the quantity of social activity is more prognostically valuable, while in one's 30s, the quality of social relationships becomes more important for wellbeing outcomes over a 30-year follow-up period.

flashcards.front: What did the CDC (2014e) report about HIV among adults aged 50 and older?
flashcards.back: The CDC (2014e) reported that HIV diagnoses among adults aged 50 and older represent a significant and growing concern in the United States. Older adults are often underrepresented in prevention messaging and may be diagnosed later due to assumptions that they are not at risk, leading to worse health outcomes.

flashcards.front: What relationship did Avis, Stellato & Crawford (2001) examine regarding menopause?
flashcards.back: Avis, Stellato & Crawford (2001) examined whether a universal menopausal syndrome exists across racial and ethnic groups. They found that menopausal symptoms and their severity vary significantly by racial and ethnic background, suggesting that the menopausal experience is culturally and biologically diverse rather than uniform.

flashcards.front: What is the key developmental task of Erikson's integrity versus despair stage, and what are its two possible outcomes?
flashcards.back: The key task is a life review—reflecting on one's life and integrating experiences with a vision of community. Those who view their life positively and retain identity despite decline achieve integrity; those who feel regret or believe their life lacked meaning experience despair. Integrity is most likely for those who achieved identity earlier in life.

flashcards.front: How does socioemotional selectivity theory explain changes in social relationships in late adulthood?
flashcards.back: As older adults perceive their future time as limited, they prioritize emotional regulation and seek familiar, meaningful social contacts who reinforce generativity, pride, and joy. They actively reduce peripheral social contacts and focus on emotionally rewarding relationships, resulting in smaller but more satisfying social networks.

flashcards.front: What is the positivity effect, and how is it related to self-acceptance?
flashcards.back: The positivity effect is the tendency for elderly people to perceive, prefer, and remember positive images and experiences more than negative ones. It reflects the convergence of the ideal self and the real self with age—as self-acceptance increases, happiness follows. It is an outgrowth of socioemotional selectivity theory and selective optimization.

flashcards.front: What are the five ADLs, and why are they clinically significant?
flashcards.back: The five ADLs are eating, bathing, toileting, dressing, and transferring from a bed to a chair. They are clinically significant because inability to perform any of them is considered a sign of frailty. They serve as a standard measure of functional independence in older adults.

flashcards.front: How do IADLs differ from ADLs, and why may IADLs be more critical to self-sufficiency?
flashcards.back: ADLs are basic physical self-care tasks. IADLs are more complex tasks requiring intellectual competence and forethought, such as paying bills and driving. IADLs may be even more critical because they reflect the cognitive and organizational capacity needed to manage daily life independently, and their loss often precedes full dependence.

flashcards.front: What distinguishes disengagement theory from activity theory in explaining aging?
flashcards.back: Disengagement theory holds that aging naturally leads to mutual withdrawal between the individual and society—role relinquishment and passivity are seen as natural. Activity theory holds that elderly people want and need to stay active and that withdrawal happens only due to external forces like ageism, not by natural desire.

flashcards.front: Under what conditions is retirement most likely to negatively affect health?
flashcards.back: Retirement correlates with illness and decline only when it is precipitated by poor health or fading competence, not when it is voluntary. In most cases, retirement does not inherently cause health problems and can be associated with wellbeing when chosen freely.

flashcards.front: What risk factors increase the likelihood of elder abuse?
flashcards.back: Elder abuse is more likely when: (1) the caregiver suffers from emotional problems or substance abuse, (2) the care receiver is frail, confused, and demanding, and (3) the care location is isolated with few visitors. Resentment and social isolation in caregiving relationships also escalate the risk.

flashcards.front: What is a NORC and why is it developmentally significant?
flashcards.back: A NORC (naturally occurring retirement community) is a neighborhood or apartment complex where most residents are retirees who moved there as younger adults and never left. It is significant because it supports aging in place and preserves the social convoy—the long-term close relationships that are critical to wellbeing in late adulthood.

flashcards.front: How does religious involvement benefit elderly people, especially minority group members?
flashcards.back: Religious involvement correlates with better physical and emotional health through several mechanisms: health-promoting behavioral prohibitions, caring community relationships, meaning-making that reduces stress around life and death, and identity reinforcement. For minority group elders, religious identity often provides stronger community identification than national or ethnic background.

flashcards.front: What are the key differences between how sons and daughters relate to aging parents?
flashcards.back: Research shows that sons feel a stronger sense of obligation to care for aging parents, while daughters feel stronger affection. This difference affects the nature of caregiving and relationship quality. Additionally, frequency of contact is related to geographic proximity rather than the level of affection.

flashcards.front: What factors shape the grandparent-grandchild relationship?
flashcards.back: The nature of grandparent-grandchild relationships is influenced by: the grandparent's personality, ethnicity, national background, past family interactions, and the age and personality of the grandchild. Approximately 85% of adults over age 65 are grandparents.

flashcards.front: What is the beanpole family, and what social trends have contributed to its emergence?
flashcards.back: A beanpole family has multiple generations (sometimes four or five) but only a few members in each generation. It has emerged due to longer life expectancy combined with lower birth rates. Today some families span five generations, whereas in past centuries most adults died before their grandchildren were born.

flashcards.front: How do stratification theories differ from self theories in explaining late adulthood?
flashcards.back: Self theories focus on internal psychological processes—how individuals maintain identity and integrity. Stratification theories focus on external social structures, arguing that accumulated disadvantages based on gender, ethnicity, income, and age limit an individual's choices and functioning in late adulthood, regardless of personal effort.

flashcards.front: What psychological and social benefits does work provide for older adults?
flashcards.back: Work provides social support and status, boosts self-esteem, enables generativity, and gives a sense of purpose. Many of these benefits can also be obtained through volunteer work. Some older adults continue working past 65 not only for financial reasons but for social recognition and self-fulfillment.

flashcards.front: What is 'aging in place' and why is disruption of this particularly harmful for certain groups?
flashcards.back: Aging in place involves remaining in the same home and community in later life, adjusting as health fades rather than relocating. Disruption of social connections through relocation is especially harmful for women and the frailest older adults, who depend most heavily on established community and social networks.

flashcards.front: How does the real self versus ideal self dynamic change with age, and what is the psychological significance?
flashcards.back: Research shows that with age, the real self (how one perceives oneself) and the ideal self (what one hopes to be) converge and become more aligned. This convergence reflects increasing self-acceptance, which according to self theory leads to greater happiness and is associated with the positivity effect.

flashcards.front: Why might conflict be more common in emotionally close intergenerational relationships than in distant ones?
flashcards.back: Closer relationships involve greater mutual expectations, interdependence, and emotional investment, which increases the potential for ambivalence and conflict. Additionally, adult children may imagine parental disapproval even when it is not overtly expressed, and disagreements about caregiving, schedules, and decisions are more salient when relationships are emotionally charged.

flashcards.front: What role does volunteer work play in health outcomes for elderly adults, and what encourages volunteering?
flashcards.back: Volunteer work is linked to generativity, social connections, reduced depression, improved health, and may reduce death rates. Volunteering is increased by knowing someone else who volunteers, by explicit encouragement, and by specific suggestions for volunteer opportunities. Official statistics undercount elder volunteering because informal help to friends and family is not tracked.

flashcards.front: How does income stratification specifically affect older adults, and what mechanisms are involved?
flashcards.back: Income stratification directly affects older adults through accumulated lifetime disadvantages in employment, savings, and Social Security benefits. Poverty magnifies stratification by gender, ethnicity, and age. Stress and accumulating disadvantages become increasingly limiting as age advances, creating compounding inequalities in health, housing, and social resources.

flashcards.front: What makes a marriage protective in late adulthood, according to the research on partnerships?
flashcards.back: Spouses in strong marriages provide a buffer against old age problems and protect health. An exceedingly positive spousal portrayal facilitates discussion rather than argument and integration rather than domination. Together, couples can achieve selective optimization with compensation, helping each other manage age-related decline more effectively.

flashcards.front: What is filial responsibility and how does it vary?
flashcards.back: Filial responsibility is the obligation of adult children to care for their aging parents. It varies widely by culture and country. In the United States, older adults tend to value independence and often provide more help to their children than they receive. Globally, the degree and nature of expected filial care differs significantly based on cultural values and resources.

flashcards.front: What percentage of adults aged 75 and older have a chronic condition?
flashcards.back: 90% of adults aged 75 and older have a chronic condition, compared to less than 50% of adults aged 50-64.

flashcards.front: Which gender of older adults is more likely to have a chronic condition, and what are the respective percentages?
flashcards.back: Older women are more likely to have a chronic condition than older men (88% vs. 83%), according to CDC 2009 data.

flashcards.front: What percentage of newly diagnosed cancers and cancer deaths occur in persons over 65?
flashcards.back: Persons over 65 account for 60% of newly diagnosed cancers and 70% of all cancer deaths.

flashcards.front: What is atherosclerosis, and why is it not considered a normal part of aging?
flashcards.back: Atherosclerosis is the buildup of fatty plaques in artery walls that restricts blood flow. It is not a normal part of aging because an older heart can normally pump blood as strongly as a younger heart; reduced pumping ability is caused by disease, not age.

flashcards.front: What are the leading risk factors for developing COPD?
flashcards.back: The leading cause is cigarette smoking. Other causes include pipe or cigar smoking (especially if inhaled), long-term exposure to secondhand smoke, chemical fumes, dust, and the genetic condition alpha-1 antitrypsin (AAT) deficiency.

flashcards.front: What is alpha-1 antitrypsin deficiency, and how does it cause both lung and liver disease?
flashcards.back: AAT deficiency is a recessive genetic condition (affecting 1 in 1,600–5,000 people) where the AAT protein is misshapen and cannot leave the liver. This deprives the lungs of protection against harmful proteins (raising COPD risk) and causes excess protein accumulation in the liver, potentially leading to cirrhosis.

flashcards.front: What is post-herpetic neuralgia (PHN) and who is most at risk?
flashcards.back: PHN is ongoing pain in the area where a shingles rash occurred after the rash resolves. Older individuals are most at risk — the older a person is when they get shingles, the greater the chance of developing PHN, which can interfere with daily activities and cause depression, anxiety, and sleeplessness.

flashcards.front: How does shingles differ from chickenpox in terms of cause and presentation?
flashcards.back: Both are caused by the varicella-zoster virus (VZV). Chickenpox is the initial infection; shingles is a reactivation of dormant VZV in nerve cells. Unlike chickenpox, shingles typically affects only one side of the body or face in a limited area, producing pain, burning, blisters, and rash.

flashcards.front: What are the serious complications that can result from a shingles infection?
flashcards.back: Complications include infected or scarring blisters, eye damage or blindness (blisters near/in the eye), brief facial paralysis, hearing loss, and rarely encephalitis (brain swelling).

flashcards.front: Why is depression screening recommended for elderly cancer patients?
flashcards.back: Depression is associated with weight loss, failure to thrive, and reduced motivation to receive treatment. Depression has been associated with decreased survival rates in elderly cancer patients.

flashcards.front: What are the osteoporosis lifetime fracture statistics by sex after age 50?
flashcards.back: After age 50, 1 in 2 women and 1 in 4 men will experience an osteoporosis-related fracture in their lifetime, commonly affecting the hip, spine, and wrist.

flashcards.front: What are the recommended strategies to prevent bone mass loss and osteoporosis?
flashcards.back: Eating a healthy diet with adequate calcium and vitamin D, engaging in regular exercise, limiting alcohol consumption, and not smoking.

flashcards.front: What percentage of adults with arthritis are aged 65 and older, and what functional limitations do they face?
flashcards.back: Approximately 62% of adults with arthritis are aged 65 and older. Almost 1 in 2 older adults with arthritis experience some degree of mobility limitations, such as difficulty climbing stairs, walking, and grasping objects.

flashcards.front: How does chemotherapy affect cognitive function in elderly cancer patients?
flashcards.back: Chemotherapy has been found to compromise cognitive function in cancer patients ('chemo brain'), and in elderly patients it may further exacerbate existing dementia and age-related cognitive declines, complicating treatment decisions.

flashcards.front: What does the data from Table 9.7 reveal about major cardiovascular disease as a cause of death with increasing age?
flashcards.back: The percentage of deaths due to major cardiovascular disease increases consistently with age: 24.3% (ages 45-54), 26.5% (55-64), 27.7% (65-74), 31.6% (75-84), and 38.9% (85+), making it an increasingly dominant cause of death in very old age.

flashcards.front: Why are frail elderly individuals not considered candidates for toxic chemotherapy?
flashcards.back: Frail individuals are defined as having limited life expectancy and near-exhausted functional reserves. Their compromised organ systems and reduced resilience make it unlikely they can tolerate aggressive chemotherapy, and the risks outweigh potential benefits.

flashcards.front: What is kyphosis and what causes it in older adults?
flashcards.back: Kyphosis is a progressive spinal curvature resulting in a hunched-over posture. In older adults, it is caused by weakening and compression of spinal bones due to osteoporosis. Over time it can make walking and even sitting up difficult, and adults also gradually lose height.

flashcards.front: Is shingles contagious? Can someone catch shingles from an infected person?
flashcards.back: Shingles itself is not contagious from person to person. However, someone who has not had chickenpox can catch chickenpox (not shingles) from a person with an active shingles infection.

flashcards.front: What age range defines late adulthood?
flashcards.back: Late adulthood spans from approximately the mid-sixties (around age 65) until death. It is considered the longest developmental stage in the human lifespan.

flashcards.front: What are the three subcategories of age within late adulthood?
flashcards.back:
1. Young-Old: ages 65–74
2. Old-Old: ages 75–84
3. Oldest-Old: ages 85 and above. These distinctions help researchers and clinicians recognize the significant diversity in functioning and health needs within the late adulthood stage.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between life span and life expectancy?
flashcards.back: Life span refers to the maximum number of years any member of a species has been known to live (approximately 122 years for humans). Life expectancy is the statistical average number of years a person born in a given time and place is expected to live, and it varies by gender, ethnicity, geography, and socioeconomic conditions.

flashcards.front: Why do women generally outlive men in late adulthood?
flashcards.back: Women tend to outlive men due to a combination of biological factors (e.g., estrogen's protective cardiovascular effects), behavioral factors (e.g., men engaging in more risk-taking behaviors and being less likely to seek medical care), and social factors. This results in a higher proportion of women than men in late adulthood populations.

flashcards.front: What factors contribute to becoming a centenarian?
flashcards.back: Contributing factors include favorable genetics, healthy diet, regular physical activity, strong social connections, low chronic stress, positive outlook, non-smoking status, moderate alcohol use, and access to quality healthcare. Centenarians tend to cluster in specific geographic regions known as 'Blue Zones.'

flashcards.front: What is ageism and how does it affect older adults?
flashcards.back: Ageism is prejudice or discrimination based on age, typically targeting older adults. It can negatively impact healthcare quality, employment opportunities, mental health, and self-perception of older adults. Ageism is reinforced by cultural myths that portray older adults as uniformly frail, incompetent, or burdensome.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between primary and secondary aging?
flashcards.back: Primary aging refers to inevitable, universal biological changes that occur with age (e.g., reduced muscle mass, slower reaction time). Secondary aging refers to changes resulting from controllable factors like disease, poor diet, smoking, or inactivity. Secondary aging is largely preventable or modifiable.

flashcards.front: What is the Baby Boomer generation and why is it significant for late adulthood demographics?
flashcards.back: Baby Boomers are individuals born between 1946 and 1964. As this large cohort ages into late adulthood, it dramatically increases the proportion of older adults in the United States, creating significant demands on healthcare systems, Social Security, retirement programs, and community services.

flashcards.front: How do developmental psychologists describe the different ways people age?
flashcards.back: Developmental psychologists describe aging through multiple lenses: biological aging (physical changes), psychological aging (cognitive and emotional changes), and social aging (changes in roles and relationships). They also distinguish between primary vs. secondary aging and use frameworks like successful aging to capture variability in outcomes.

flashcards.front: Why is late adulthood considered the most diverse developmental stage?
flashcards.back: Older adults vary enormously in health, cognitive functioning, socioeconomic status, ethnicity, gender, culture, and life history. A 65-year-old marathon runner and an 85-year-old with dementia are both in 'late adulthood,' illustrating the vast heterogeneity that makes this stage uniquely complex.

flashcards.front: What is the fastest-growing age segment within late adulthood?
flashcards.back: The oldest-old (individuals aged 85 and above) is the fastest-growing segment of the older adult population, driven by advances in medicine, improved public health, and better management of chronic diseases.

flashcards.front: What is successful aging?
flashcards.back: Successful aging is a model emphasizing three components: (1) avoiding disease and disability, (2) maintaining high cognitive and physical functioning, and (3) sustained engagement in social and productive activities. Critics note it may not account for structural barriers or diverse cultural definitions of a good later life.

flashcards.front: What are some common myths associated with late adulthood?
flashcards.back: Common myths include: all older adults are cognitively impaired or have dementia; older adults cannot learn new skills; older adults are asexual; all older adults are lonely or depressed; and older adults are a homogeneous group. These myths are contradicted by research showing wide variability in functioning and wellbeing.

flashcards.front: What global implications does the increase in the older adult population carry?
flashcards.back: An aging global population increases demand for healthcare, long-term care, and pension systems; alters workforce composition; shifts political priorities; creates intergenerational equity debates; and requires new social policies. Developing nations face particular challenges as they age faster than developed nations did historically.

flashcards.front: What is gerontology and what disciplines does it incorporate?
flashcards.back: Gerontology is the multidisciplinary study of aging and older adults. It incorporates biology, psychology, sociology, medicine, public health, economics, and social work to understand and address the complexities of the aging process and the needs of older populations.

flashcards.front: How do ethnic differences influence aging outcomes in late adulthood?
flashcards.back: Racial and ethnic minority groups often experience disparities in life expectancy, chronic disease burden, and access to healthcare. However, the 'Hispanic paradox' illustrates that some minority groups (e.g., Hispanic Americans) have longer life expectancy than expected given their socioeconomic status, suggesting cultural protective factors such as strong family support systems.

flashcards.front: What age range defines late adulthood, and what makes this group demographically significant in the U.S.?
flashcards.back: Late adulthood encompasses individuals aged 65 and older. It is the fastest growing age division of the U.S. population (Gatz, Smyer, & DiGilio, 2016), currently representing about 1 in 7 Americans, with projections reaching nearly 1 in 4 by 2050.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How many baby boomers turn 65 every day, and when did the first baby boomers reach that age?
flashcards.back: Approximately 10,000 baby boomers turn 65 every day. The first baby boomers (born in 1946) turned 65 in 2011.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: According to the U.S. Census Bureau (2014), how many additional years can a person who turned 65 in 2015 expect to live, and how does this compare to 1950?
flashcards.back: A person turning 65 in 2015 can expect to live approximately 19 more years — 5.5 years longer than someone who turned 65 in 1950, reflecting major improvements in longevity.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the 'Graying of America' and what societal areas does it affect?
flashcards.back: The 'Graying of America' refers to the rapid increase in the proportion of older adults (65+) in the U.S. It significantly affects work, healthcare, housing, Social Security, caregiving, and adaptive technologies.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What percentage of the U.S. population was 65 or older in 2012, and what is it projected to be in 2030?
flashcards.back: In 2012, 13.7% of the U.S. population was 65 or older. By 2030, this is projected to rise to 20.3%, representing roughly 1 in 5 Americans.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What birth years define the baby boomer generation, and why are they central to demographic aging trends?
flashcards.back: Baby boomers were born between 1946 and 1964. Their large cohort size means that as they age into late adulthood, they dramatically increase the proportion of older adults in the U.S. population, driving the Graying of America.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: By 2050, what fraction of Americans is projected to be over age 65?
flashcards.back: By 2050, nearly one in four Americans (approximately 25%) is projected to be over the age of 65, up from about one in seven today.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What percentage of the U.S. population was aged 85 or older in 2012, and what is it projected to be in 2030?
flashcards.back: In 2012, 1.9% of the U.S. population was aged 85 or older (oldest-old). This is projected to grow to 2.5% by 2030, reflecting faster growth in this subgroup.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are the three broad subgroups within late adulthood and their approximate age ranges?
flashcards.back: Young-old (65–74), middle-old (75–84), and oldest-old (85+). These subgroups differ significantly in health status, independence, and care needs.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does the growth rate of the 65+ population compare to the general U.S. population?
flashcards.back: The 65+ age group is the fastest growing age division of the U.S. population (Gatz, Smyer, & DiGilio, 2016), outpacing the growth rate of younger age groups due to baby boomer aging and increased life expectancy.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are adaptive technologies in the context of aging, and why are they increasingly important?
flashcards.back: Adaptive technologies are devices and systems designed to help older adults maintain independence, such as hearing aids, mobility aids, and smart home systems. They are increasingly important as the older adult population grows, driving demand for assistive innovation.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What proportion of Americans was 65 or older as of the content's present reference point?
flashcards.back: As of the reference point in the content, approximately 1 in 7 Americans (about 14%) was 65 years of age or older.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the projected percentage of Americans aged 65–69 in 2030, compared to 2012?
flashcards.back: In 2012, adults aged 65–69 made up 4.5% of the U.S. population. By 2030, this is projected to increase to 5.6%, reflecting the continued aging of baby boomers through this age bracket.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why does population aging place strain on the Social Security system?
flashcards.back: As more Americans enter late adulthood and collect Social Security benefits, and as the ratio of working-age adults contributing to the system declines, the financial demands on Social Security increase, creating long-term funding challenges.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What does increased longevity mean for caregiving in the United States?
flashcards.back: Increased longevity means more Americans will live into advanced old age, potentially requiring extended periods of care. This increases demand for family caregivers, professional home care workers, assisted living, and nursing home services, creating significant social and economic challenges.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What source is cited for the statistic that late adulthood is the fastest growing age division of the U.S. population?
flashcards.back: Gatz, Smyer, & DiGilio (2016) is cited as the source for the claim that late adulthood (65+) is the fastest growing age division of the United States population.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does the projected percentage of adults aged 80–84 change from 2012 to 2030?
flashcards.back: The percentage of adults aged 80–84 is projected to grow from 1.8% in 2012 to 2.9% in 2030, representing a more than 60% relative increase in this age subgroup's share of the population.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Which country had the highest percentage of elderly population in 2012, and what was that percentage?
flashcards.back: Japan had the highest percentage of elderly population in 2012, with 24% of its population aged 65 and older. Japan is also projected to maintain the oldest population in 2030 (32.2%) and 2050 (40%).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are the two primary factors driving the global increase in the proportion of older adults?
flashcards.back: The two primary factors are: (1) declines in fertility rates, and (2) improvements in longevity (life expectancy). Together, these account for the growing percentage of people aged 65 and older worldwide.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the replacement-level fertility rate, and when did more developed countries fall below it?
flashcards.back: The replacement-level fertility rate is approximately 2 live births per woman (generally 2.1 in developed countries). More developed countries fell below this rate by the 1970s, down from nearly 3 children per woman around 1950.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How many people over 65 existed worldwide as reported by the NIA, and what is the projected global share by 2050?
flashcards.back: According to the National Institute on Aging (NIA, 2015b), there were 524 million people over 65 worldwide. This group is expected to grow from 8% to 16% of the global population by 2050.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: By what percentage is the older population in less developed countries projected to increase between 2010 and 2050, compared to developed countries?
flashcards.back: The older population in less developed countries is projected to increase more than 250% between 2010 and 2050, compared with only a 71% increase in developed countries.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the projected percentage of the U.S. population aged 65 and older in 2030 and 2050?
flashcards.back: The U.S. is projected to have 20.3% of its population aged 65 and older by 2030 and 22% by 2050, up from 13.7% in 2012.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does the United States compare to other developed nations in terms of population aging?
flashcards.back: The United States is still younger than most other developed countries. In 2012, the U.S. had 13.7% of its population aged 65+, while Germany and Italy each had 20%, and Japan had 24%. However, in total numbers, the U.S. is projected to have a larger older population than other developed nations, though smaller than China and India.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What was the ratio of people aged 85 and older to potential midlife caregivers (ages 50–64) in 2000, and how is it expected to change?
flashcards.back: In 2000, there were approximately 10 people aged 85 and older for every 100 persons between ages 50 and 64. This ratio is expected to more than double by the year 2040, according to He et al. (2005).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why are midlife adults (ages 50–64) significant in the context of global aging?
flashcards.back: Midlife adults aged 50–64 are the most likely care providers for their aging parents. As the elderly population grows relative to this group, families will need increasing external physical, emotional, and financial support for long-term care.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is projected about China's older population by 2050?
flashcards.back: By 2050, China's older population is projected to grow larger than the total current U.S. population, highlighting the extraordinary scale of demographic aging in the world's most populous nation.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How did fertility rates change in less developed countries between 1950 and 2005?
flashcards.back: In less developed countries, fertility rates fell from an average of 6 children per woman in 1950 to an average of 2 or 3 children per woman by 2005. By 2006, fertility was at or below the two-child replacement level in 44 less developed countries.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are the projected percentages of population aged 65+ for Japan in 2030 and 2050?
flashcards.back: Japan's population aged 65 and older is projected to be 32.2% in 2030 and 40% in 2050, maintaining its status as the country with the oldest population among developed nations.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Compare Germany and Italy's aging statistics in 2012, 2030, and 2050.
flashcards.back: In 2012, both Germany and Italy had 20% of their population aged 65+. By 2030, Germany is projected at 27.9% and Italy at 25.5%. By 2050, Germany is projected at 30% and Italy at 31%, showing Italy slightly surpassing Germany in the long term.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How many less developed countries had fertility at or below the two-child replacement level by 2006?
flashcards.back: By 2006, fertility was at or below the two-child replacement level in 44 less developed countries, indicating that population aging is not limited to developed nations but is a truly global phenomenon.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What external supports will families need as the elderly population grows?
flashcards.back: As the ratio of elderly individuals to midlife caregivers grows, families will need external physical support (healthcare and caregiving services), emotional support (mental health and counseling resources), and financial support (social programs, pensions, and insurance) to manage the demands of caring for aging parents.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the significance of the year 2040 in the context of elder care demographics?
flashcards.back: By 2040, the number of older adults requiring support from their children (midlife caregivers) is expected to more than double compared to 2000 levels, based on projections by He, Sengupta, Velkoff, and DeBarros (2005), raising serious concerns about caregiver availability and long-term care infrastructure.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What did Ortman, Velkoff, and Hogan (2014) conclude about the United States in relation to other developed nations?
flashcards.back: Ortman, Velkoff, and Hogan (2014) concluded that the United States is still younger than most other developed countries despite its aging trend. They also noted that by 2050, the U.S. will have a larger older population in total numbers than other developed nations, but smaller than China and India.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: The “Graying” of the World | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the difference between lifespan and life expectancy?
flashcards.back: Lifespan (maximum lifespan) is the greatest age reached by any member of a species — for humans, 120–125 years. Life expectancy is the average number of years members of a population actually live — globally 71.4 years at birth in 2015.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What was the global life expectancy at birth in 2015 according to the WHO, broken down by sex?
flashcards.back: Global life expectancy was 71.4 years overall: females averaged 73.8 years and males averaged 69.1 years. The gap between sexes has remained unchanged since 1990.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Which WHO region had the highest life expectancy in 2015, and which had the lowest?
flashcards.back: The WHO European Region had the highest life expectancy at 76.8 years. The WHO African Region had the lowest at 60.0 years.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: By how many years did global life expectancy increase between 2000 and 2015, and which region saw the greatest increase?
flashcards.back: Global life expectancy increased by 5 years between 2000 and 2015. The WHO African Region saw the greatest increase at 9.4 years, primarily due to improved child survival and expanded access to antiretroviral medication for HIV.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What was the global Healthy Life Expectancy in 2015, and how does it differ from regular life expectancy?
flashcards.back: The global Healthy Life Expectancy (HLE) in 2015 was 63.1 years (up from 58.5 in 2000). Unlike standard life expectancy, HLE accounts for age-specific mortality, morbidity, and disability risks, measuring years of full health rather than total years lived.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Which U.S. demographic group (by race/ethnicity) had the highest life expectancy in 2012, and what were their figures?
flashcards.back: Hispanic Americans had the highest life expectancy: females at 83.7 years and males at 78.9 years, compared to the all-groups average of 81.97 years for females and 77.32 years for males.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Which U.S. demographic group had the lowest life expectancy in 2012?
flashcards.back: Non-Hispanic Black and American Indian or Alaskan Native individuals had the lowest life expectancy: females at 78.0 years and males at 71.7 years.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What U.S. state had the highest Healthy Life Expectancy for individuals at age 65, and what was the value?
flashcards.back: Hawaii had the highest HLE, with individuals at age 65 expected to have 16.2 additional years of good health (based on 2007–2009 CDC data).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What U.S. state had the lowest Healthy Life Expectancy for individuals at age 65, and what was the value?
flashcards.back: Mississippi had the lowest HLE at 10.8 additional years of good health for individuals at age 65. Overall, southern states had the lowest HLE values.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What does the Healthy Life Expectancy metric measure, and which WHO region had the highest and lowest values in 2015?
flashcards.back: HLE measures equivalent years of full health expected for a newborn, accounting for mortality, morbidity, and disability. The WHO Western Pacific Region had the highest HLE (68.7 years); the WHO African Region had the lowest (52.3 years).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What warning did former U.S. Surgeon General Richard Carmona issue in 2007 regarding life expectancy?
flashcards.back: Carmona warned that due to increasing rates of obesity, unhealthy eating habits, and physical inactivity, the current generation of American children may be the first to have a shorter life expectancy than their parents.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What health problems are associated with childhood obesity?
flashcards.back: Childhood obesity is associated with high blood pressure, type 2 diabetes, elevated blood cholesterol levels, low self-esteem, negative body image, and depression. Excess weight also increases risk of obesity-related diseases and earlier death.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How did the rate of childhood obesity in the U.S. change between 1971 and 2011?
flashcards.back: The rate of childhood obesity tripled from 1971 to 2011. As of 2014, one in three American children was overweight or obese, according to the American Heart Association.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Where does the United States rank globally in life expectancy, and what is its overall life expectancy?
flashcards.back: The U.S. ranks 43rd in the world for life expectancy (CIA, 2016). The overall U.S. life expectancy is 79.7 years.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why did life expectancy in the WHO African Region increase by 9.4 years between 2000 and 2015?
flashcards.back: The increase was primarily due to improvements in child survival rates and expanded access to antiretroviral medication for the treatment of HIV.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Are there racial exceptions to the rule that whites have higher HLE than blacks in the U.S.?
flashcards.back: Yes. While HLE was greater for whites than blacks in D.C. and most states, Nevada and New Mexico were exceptions where this pattern did not hold.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why is chronological age considered insufficient when studying late adulthood?
flashcards.back: Chronological age does not capture variation in health and functional status. Some individuals in late adulthood age optimally with excellent health and social well-being, while others experience poor health and greater dependence — making functional assessments more meaningful.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does the U.S. HLE study define its starting point, and what CDC data were used?
flashcards.back: The U.S. HLE study evaluated factors from 2007–2009 to determine how long an individual currently at age 65 would continue to experience good health. This was based on CDC data from that period.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why are females considered to have greater genetic and developmental stability than males?
flashcards.back: Females (XX) can select the 'better' X chromosome from either parent and inactivate the 'worse' one. Males (XY) can only express the single X chromosome inherited from their mother and have no such selection mechanism, leaving them more vulnerable to genetic disturbances and developmental disorders.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the role of the SRY gene in sex determination and how does it contribute to male developmental vulnerability?
flashcards.back: The SRY gene on the Y chromosome initiates molecular events that redirect development from the default female pathway to male development. Because this requires many different factors and mechanisms to work correctly at precise developmental stages, males experience a greater number of disturbances and developmental disorders than females.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does estrogen contribute to female longevity?
flashcards.back: Estrogen has two key protective effects: (1) it protects the heart and circulatory system, reducing cardiovascular disease risk, and (2) it acts as an antioxidant, neutralizing free radicals that damage cell components, cause mutations, and drive the aging process.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does testosterone contribute to shorter male lifespan?
flashcards.back: Higher testosterone levels in men are associated with more frequent cardiovascular and immune disorders, increased aggression and violence, and riskier behavioral patterns, all of which elevate mortality risk and shorten average male lifespan.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does the frontal lobe contribute to the gender gap in life expectancy?
flashcards.back: The frontal lobe, which governs judgment and evaluation of consequences, develops more slowly in boys and young men. This delayed development contributes to poorer decision-making and increased risk-taking behaviors, leading to higher male mortality from smoking, excessive alcohol use, accidents, drunk driving, and violence.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What percentage of U.S. homicide victims in 2014 were male, according to the FBI?
flashcards.back: 77% of the 11,961 U.S. homicides recorded in 2014 were male victims, illustrating the dramatically greater exposure of men to lethal violence.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How much more likely are males to die by suicide compared to females?
flashcards.back: Males are more than three times as likely as females to commit suicide, according to CDC data (2016), making suicide a major contributor to the gender gap in life expectancy.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What proportion of U.S. military officers and enlisted service members were male in 2014?
flashcards.back: According to the Department of Defense (2015), 83% of all officers and 85% of all enlisted service members were male in 2014, reflecting significantly greater male exposure to military-related injury and death.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does healthcare utilization differ between men and women, and what are the consequences?
flashcards.back: Men are less likely than women to have health insurance, establish a regular relationship with a doctor, or seek treatment for medical conditions. This results in delayed diagnoses, untreated conditions, and poorer overall health outcomes that contribute to shorter male lifespans.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does immune system aging differ between males and females?
flashcards.back: Males experience significantly faster deterioration of cellular immunity with age and are more likely to contract viral and bacterial infections. Females' immune systems deteriorate more slowly, though women are slightly more prone to autoimmune and inflammatory diseases such as rheumatoid arthritis.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between social contact frequency and the gender gap in longevity?
flashcards.back: Social isolation is a health hazard. Nearly 20% of men over 50 see friends less than once a month, compared to only 12% of women. Men's greater social isolation contributes to loneliness-related health risks and shorter lifespans.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why are women considered the 'default sex' in developmental biology?
flashcards.back: In the absence of specific molecular signals (particularly those triggered by the SRY gene on the Y chromosome), a human embryo will develop as female. Producing a male requires an additional sequence of molecular events, making female development the biological baseline or default pathway.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does religiosity relate to the gender gap in life expectancy?
flashcards.back: Women are more religious than men on average, and religiosity is associated with healthier behaviors. Greater female religious participation contributes to healthier lifestyles, which in turn supports longer female life expectancy (Greenfield, Vaillant & Marks, 2009).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How do occupational differences between men and women contribute to the gender life expectancy gap?
flashcards.back: Men disproportionately work in dangerous occupations such as law enforcement, firefighting, and construction, exposing them to greater risks of injury and death. This occupational hazard differential is a significant lifestyle factor in shorter male lifespan.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are free radicals and how do they relate to the aging process and gender differences in longevity?
flashcards.back: Free radicals are unstable molecules that damage cell components and cause mutations, contributing to aging. Estrogen in females acts as an antioxidant that neutralizes free radicals, giving women a cellular protective advantage that slows aging and contributes to longer female life expectancy.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the significance of males being heterogametic vs. females being homogametic for health outcomes?
flashcards.back: Males (XY) are heterogametic and can only express their single X chromosome from their mother, with no ability to compensate for defective X-linked genes. Females (XX) are homogametic and can inactivate the inferior X chromosome and rely on the superior one, providing a genetic buffer against X-linked disorders and developmental instability.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Summarize the two broad categories of reasons why women live longer than men.
flashcards.back: (1) Biological factors: including X chromosome advantages, SRY gene-related developmental complexity, slower immune deterioration, and estrogen's cardioprotective and antioxidant effects. (2) Lifestyle/behavioral factors: including working in more dangerous jobs, higher rates of violence and suicide, greater military service, less healthcare utilization, lower religiosity, and greater social isolation.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are the three subcategories of late adulthood used in this chapter, and what ages do they span?
flashcards.back: Young-old (65-84), oldest-old (85-99), and centenarians (100+). These divisions are based on biological, psychological, social, and chronological differences in aging.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why is the young-old period (65-84) often considered the 'golden years'?
flashcards.back: Individuals typically have fewer responsibilities than in earlier life stages. When combined with adequate finances and good health, they can pursue leisure and self-fulfillment. They also report better emotional well-being than at any other period of adulthood.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does the young-old group compare cognitively to those 85 and older?
flashcards.back: The young-old show strong performance in attention, memory, and crystallized intelligence. They retain knowledge, expertise, and adaptive flexibility in daily living, and are more similar cognitively to midlife adults than to those 85 and older.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What percentage of the U.S. population did the oldest-old represent in 2015, and what is the projected figure for 2050?
flashcards.back: In 2015, the oldest-old represented about 2% of the U.S. population (14% of the older adult population). By 2050, they are projected to reach nearly 18 million people, or about 4.5% of the U.S. population — a projected increase of more than 300%.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are the most common reasons for hospitalization among the oldest-old (85+)?
flashcards.back: Congestive heart failure, pneumonia, urinary tract infections, septicemia, stroke, and hip fractures. Notably, hospitalizations for urinary tract infections and septicemia have increased in recent years for this age group.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What proportion of the oldest-old require some assistance with activities of daily living?
flashcards.back: Almost 50% of the oldest-old (85 and older) require some assistance with daily living activities (APA, 2016).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What distinguishes centenarians from the general oldest-old population in terms of health trajectory?
flashcards.back: Centenarians tend to have been healthier than peers throughout aging, often delaying serious disease or disability until their 90s. They typically experience rapid terminal decline near death rather than prolonged gradual decline, and 25% reach age 100 with no serious chronic illnesses.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is a supercentenarian, and who defined the term?
flashcards.back: A supercentenarian is an individual aged 110 or older. The term was defined by Wilcox, Wilcox, and Ferrucci (2008).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Who is Jeanne Louise Calment, and why is she significant?
flashcards.back: Jeanne Louise Calment from France is documented by Guinness World Records (2016) as the longest-living person on record, having lived 122 years and 164 days.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What gender disparity exists in the oldest-old population?
flashcards.back: Females comprise more than 60% of those 85 and older, but they also suffer from more chronic illnesses and disabilities than older males (Gatz et al., 2016).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Although the oldest-old account for only 2% of the U.S. population, what proportion of all hospitalizations do they account for?
flashcards.back: The oldest-old account for 9% of all hospitalizations in the U.S. (Levant, Chari & DeFrances, 2015), despite being only 2% of the population.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Which countries have the most centenarians in absolute numbers vs. per capita?
flashcards.back: The United States has the most centenarians in absolute numbers. Japan and Italy have the highest number of centenarians per capita (Stepler, 2016e).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the 'third age' concept introduced by Laslett (1989)?
flashcards.back: The third age refers to the post-employment period of life (roughly 65-84) that follows childhood (first age) and work/parenting (second age). It is characterized by opportunities for leisure and self-fulfillment before age-related limitations begin around age 80-85.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What chronic diseases are most common in the young-old, and why are they considered manageable?
flashcards.back: Cardiovascular disease, hypertension, and cancer are most common. Because they are largely linked to lifestyle choices, they can typically be prevented, lessened, or managed with appropriate interventions.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What researchers documented emotional well-being in the young-old period?
flashcards.back: Carstensen, Fung, & Charles (2003); George (2009); and Robins & Trzesniewski (2005) all found that those in the young-old category experience better happiness and emotional well-being than at any other period of adulthood.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How many centenarians existed worldwide in 2015, and what is the projection for 2050?
flashcards.back: In 2015, there were nearly half a million centenarians worldwide. This number is projected to grow to almost 3.7 million by 2050.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What percentage of centenarians reach age 100 without serious chronic illnesses such as dementia, heart disease, or depression?
flashcards.back: 25% of centenarians reach age 100 with no serious chronic illnesses such as depression, osteoporosis, heart disease, respiratory illness, or dementia (Ash et al., 2015).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: In what way is the young-old an 'unusual' age category according to Rubinstein (2002)?
flashcards.back: The young-old is unusual because people in this category are simultaneously considered both in old age and not in old age — they are old enough to be classified as older adults but functionally similar to midlife adults.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the Hayflick limit and what number is associated with it?
flashcards.back: The Hayflick limit is the maximum number of times a normal cell can divide before it stops. Cells in vitro divide approximately 40 to 60 times before ceasing replication, forming the biological basis of the Cellular Clock Theory of aging.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are the three outcomes when a telomere becomes too short?
flashcards.back: 1) Cellular senescence — the cell turns itself off but remains alive; 2) Apoptosis — the cell stops dividing by dying; 3) Abnormal division — as seen in cancer development, the cell continues dividing and becomes abnormal.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does the Evolutionary Theory explain why Alzheimer's disease has not been eliminated by natural selection?
flashcards.back: Alzheimer's disease and similar aging-related genetic disorders do not manifest until after an individual's primary reproductive years. Since natural selection only eliminates genes that reduce survival or reproduction before offspring are produced, these damaging genes are not selected against and persist in the gene pool.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What distinguishes 'naïve' T-cells from 'memory' T-cells?
flashcards.back: Naïve T-cells have not yet been programmed to fight a specific invader. Memory T-cells have been previously activated and retain the ability to recognize and fight a specific pathogen for decades, providing long-term immunity. As adults age, they produce fewer naïve T-cells, reducing their ability to fight new threats.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why does the shingles vaccine work well in older adults but new vaccines work less effectively?
flashcards.back: The shingles vaccine works as a booster because older adults already have existing memory T-cells against the varicella virus from prior exposure or childhood vaccination. New vaccines work less effectively in older adults because they produce fewer naïve T-cells to be programmed against novel antigens.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the mechanism by which free radicals cause a 'snowball effect' of cellular damage?
