flashcards.back: Free radicals are missing an electron and steal electrons from surrounding molecules. The molecule that lost an electron then becomes a free radical itself and steals from another molecule, creating a chain reaction. This disrupts cells and causes abnormal cellular behavior, contributing to cancer, atherosclerosis, cataracts, and neurodegeneration.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does cortisol relate to the Hormonal Stress Theory of aging?
flashcards.back: Unlike most hormones that decline with age, cortisol levels remain elevated or increase with chronic stress. Excess cortisol damages the hypothalamus over time, impairing its ability to regulate other hormones and leading to metabolic problems such as diabetes, thyroid dysfunction, osteoporosis, and orthostatic hypotension.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the difference between Programmed Theories and Damage/Error Theories of aging?
flashcards.back: Programmed Theories suggest aging follows a genetically predetermined biological timetable based on gene expression changes. Damage or Error Theories propose that aging results from cumulative environmental damage to biological structures like DNA, mitochondria, and cells over time.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why are senescent cells potentially harmful in older age despite being protective in youth?
flashcards.back: In youth, senescent cells may reduce cancer risk by halting the division of potentially malignant cells. However, as we age, these non-dividing but metabolically active cells continue to interact with surrounding tissues, promoting inflammation and increasing the risk of diseases including cancer.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What environmental factors are known to damage DNA and accelerate aging?
flashcards.back: Ultraviolet (UV) radiation, cigarette smoking, and exposure to hydrocarbons (such as auto exhaust and coal) are identified factors that cause DNA damage. When this damage cannot be repaired, cells deteriorate and malfunction, contributing to the aging process.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does mitochondrial damage lead to cellular death?
flashcards.back: When mitochondrial DNA is damaged, mitochondria become less efficient at converting oxygen into ATP (adenosine triphosphate). With reduced energy production, cells cannot sustain normal functions, which can ultimately lead to cellular death and contribute to tissue and organ deterioration associated with aging.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What role do genetics play in longevity according to current research?
flashcards.back: Genetic makeup influences longevity, but no single gene is responsible. Some genes promote longer life (e.g., by equipping individuals to survive disease), while others accelerate aging. Researchers believe it is most likely a combination of many genes. Studies have identified variants associated with blood fat levels and cholesterol as risk factors for early death.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What components make up the innate vs. adaptive immune systems?
flashcards.back: Innate immune system: skin, mucous membranes, cough reflex, stomach acid, and alert cells. Adaptive immune system: tonsils, spleen, bone marrow, thymus, circulatory system, and lymphatic system — produces and transports T-cells (lymphocytes) that provide targeted immune responses and immunological memory.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is metabolic stress and why is it relevant to aging theories?
flashcards.back: Metabolic stress refers to the biological stress caused by the body's life-sustaining processes, including circulating blood, eliminating waste, regulating body temperature, and neuronal activity. Gerontologists include metabolic stress alongside major life events when studying aging, as all activities that keep the body alive also create cumulative biological wear.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why is antioxidant supplementation studied in the context of aging, and what does current research conclude?
flashcards.back: Antioxidants are studied because they can donate electrons to neutralize free radicals, potentially reducing oxidative cellular damage associated with aging and diseases like cancer and atherosclerosis. However, research on the effectiveness of dietary antioxidants in slowing aging or preventing disease is not conclusive.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does the shortening of telomeres connect the Cellular Clock Theory to cancer development?
flashcards.back: Telomeres shorten with each cell division. When telomeres become critically short, most cells undergo senescence or apoptosis. However, in some cases cells bypass these checkpoints and continue dividing abnormally — this uncontrolled division is a hallmark of cancer development, linking telomere biology to oncogenesis.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What does genome-wide scanning reveal about the genetics of longevity?
flashcards.back: Researchers scan the entire genome to compare genetic variants in long-lived individuals versus those with average or shorter lifespans. For example, NIH studies have identified genes possibly associated with blood fat levels and cholesterol — both risk factors for coronary disease and early death — suggesting that longevity is influenced by a combination of many genetic variants.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the Baltimore Longitudinal Study on Aging (BLSA) and what are its key findings about aging?
flashcards.back: The BLSA began in 1958 and has followed approximately 1,400 people aged 20–90. Key findings include: aging varies significantly between individuals and organ systems; heart muscles thicken with age; arteries become less flexible; lung capacity diminishes; kidneys become less efficient; the bladder loses storage capacity; and brain cells lose some function but new neurons can be produced. Many changes are influenced by genetics, lifestyle, and disease.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is sarcopenia and what factors contribute to its development?
flashcards.back: Sarcopenia is the age-related loss of skeletal muscle mass and strength. Contributing factors include: (1) reduced nerve cell signaling from brain to muscles, (2) decreased ability to convert protein into energy, and (3) insufficient caloric or protein intake. Inactive individuals may lose 3–5% of muscle mass per decade after age 30. It typically accelerates around age 75 but can begin between ages 65–80, and leads to weakness, frailty, and increased fall/fracture risk.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the difference between osteopenia and osteoporosis?
flashcards.back: Both involve reduced bone mineral density. Osteopenia is the early stage, where bones are less dense than normal but fracture risk is moderate. Osteoporosis is the advanced stage, where bone density is severely reduced, making bones highly fragile and significantly increasing fracture risk. Both are associated with aging, tissue water loss, and mineral depletion from bones.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does body fat distribution change with aging, and why is this clinically significant?
flashcards.back: After age 30, body fat increases steadily, and older adults may have nearly one-third more fat than when younger. Fat redistributes toward the center of the body and around internal organs (visceral fat). This central redistribution is clinically significant because visceral fat is associated with increased risk of cardiovascular disease, metabolic syndrome, and type 2 diabetes.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What skin changes occur with normal aging and what are their causes?
flashcards.back: Aging skin becomes thinner, less elastic, and loses subcutaneous fat, causing it to look less plump and smooth. Veins and bones become more visible. Healing of cuts and bruises slows. Sun exposure causes wrinkles, dryness, age spots, and skin cancer risk. Gravity causes sagging and wrinkling. Smoking accelerates skin wrinkling. Skin tags become more common, especially in women. Age spots (flat, brown, harmless) result from cumulative sun exposure.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How do men and women differ in weight changes across late adulthood?
flashcards.back: Men typically gain weight until about age 55, then begin losing weight, partly due to declining testosterone levels. Women generally gain weight until about age 65, then begin to lose weight. In both sexes, later weight loss is partly because fat replaces lean muscle tissue, and fat weighs less than muscle, resulting in an overall lower body weight despite increased fat percentage.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the pattern and rate of height loss in aging adults?
flashcards.back: Height loss occurs universally across all races and both sexes. After age 40, people lose approximately one-half inch every 10 years. After age 70, the rate of height loss accelerates. Total lifetime height loss from aging is typically 1 to 3 inches. Height loss is caused by age-related changes in bones (compression), muscles, and joints, particularly in the vertebral column.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What exercises are recommended to counteract sarcopenia and muscle atrophy in aging?
flashcards.back: Weight-lifting (resistance training), walking, swimming, and other cardiovascular exercises can help strengthen muscles, prevent atrophy, and slow the progression of sarcopenia. Maintaining strong leg and heart muscles is particularly important for preserving independence in older adults. Both aerobic and resistance exercises are beneficial.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why are strong leg and heart muscles particularly important in late adulthood?
flashcards.back: Strong leg muscles are essential for balance, mobility, and preventing falls and fractures, which are major causes of disability and mortality in older adults. Strong heart muscles support cardiovascular efficiency and endurance. Together, these muscle groups are directly linked to maintaining independence and quality of life in late adulthood.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What neurological changes occur in the aging brain, and is brain cell loss absolute?
flashcards.back: Brain cells lose some functioning with age, and there is a reduction in nerve cells responsible for sending movement signals from the brain to muscles. However, aging does not result in complete or absolute loss — new neurons can be produced (neurogenesis) even in late adulthood. Many brain changes are influenced by genetics, lifestyle, and disease rather than being solely determined by chronological age.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are age spots (liver spots) and how do they differ from skin tags?
flashcards.back: Age spots are flat, brown, harmless areas of hyperpigmentation caused by years of sun (UV) exposure. They were previously called 'liver spots.' Skin tags are small, usually flesh-colored, raised growths of skin that become more common with aging, especially in women. Both are benign, but age spots are caused by photoaging while skin tags are structural growths unrelated to sun exposure.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What happens to the body's water content with aging and why?
flashcards.back: The body's water content decreases with age because lean tissue (muscle, organ cells) is progressively lost after age 30. Lean tissue contains a high proportion of water, so as it is replaced by fat tissue (which contains much less water), total body water declines. This affects drug distribution, temperature regulation, and hydration status in older adults.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does arterial stiffness in aging affect cardiovascular health?
flashcards.back: As arteries lose flexibility and become stiffer with age, the heart must generate greater pressure to circulate blood through the body. This contributes to elevated systolic blood pressure (isolated systolic hypertension), increased workload on the heart, and higher risk of heart disease, stroke, and heart failure. Heart muscle thickening (compensatory hypertrophy) also occurs as a result.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What role does genetics vs. lifestyle play in physical aging changes?
flashcards.back: Both genetics and lifestyle significantly influence the aging process. Genetics determines baseline rates of cell aging, bone density, and predisposition to conditions like osteoporosis. Lifestyle factors — including diet, exercise, sun exposure, smoking, and disease management — can accelerate or slow many aging changes. The BLSA found that aging varies significantly between individuals, partly because of these interacting factors.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What changes in hair occur with aging and what causes them?
flashcards.back: Nearly everyone experiences hair loss with aging as many hair follicles stop producing new hairs and the rate of hair growth slows. The loss of melanocyte function in hair follicles, which began in middle adulthood, continues in late adulthood, leading to progressive graying. These are considered normal intrinsic aging changes rather than pathological conditions.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does the rate of wound healing change with aging and what factors influence it?
flashcards.back: Wound healing slows significantly with age. Cuts, scratches, and bruises take longer to heal in older adults due to reduced skin elasticity, thinner epidermis, decreased blood flow, and slower immune response. Additionally, some medications and illnesses can further impair healing and increase bruising susceptibility, compounding the intrinsic aging effects on skin repair.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What percentage of adults aged 75 and older will have problems with cataracts?
flashcards.back: 70% of adults by age 75 will have problems with cataracts. More people develop cataract problems after age 60.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why are cataracts the leading cause of blindness in developing nations but not in the United States?
flashcards.back: In developed countries like the U.S., cataracts can be effectively treated with surgery to remove and replace the lens with a synthetic one. In developing nations, access to this surgery is limited, so cataracts go untreated and cause blindness.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the difference between dry and wet macular degeneration?
flashcards.back: Dry macular degeneration (most common, ~90% of cases) is caused by drusen (fatty protein deposits) forming beneath the retina, leading to gradual thinning of the macula. Wet macular degeneration (~10% of cases) is caused by abnormal blood vessel growth beneath the retina; these vessels may leak fluid or blood, causing more rapid and severe central vision loss. Only wet type has treatment options, with only 25% seeing improvement.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does glaucoma damage vision, and in what order does visual loss progress?
flashcards.back: Glaucoma most commonly results from increased intraocular fluid pressure that damages the optic nerve. Damage begins at the exterior of the optic nerve, which receives peripheral visual input, so peripheral vision is lost first. As glaucoma progresses, the visual field loss moves toward the center, potentially resulting in total blindness in advanced stages.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are the two main jobs of the auditory system?
flashcards.back: 1) To enable hearing by detecting sound frequencies and intensities. 2) To help maintain balance by transmitting information from hair cells in the inner ear to the brain about the position and orientation of the body.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why do older adults often incorrectly attribute flavor loss to a decline in taste rather than smell?
flashcards.back: Because taste bud loss is very gradual and taste ages relatively well, the more significant loss is usually in olfaction (smell). Two pathways stimulate olfactory cells — direct nasal inhalation and the nose-throat connection during chewing — and decline in these pathways reduces perceived flavor, mimicking taste loss.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the sex difference in disorders of smell, and what is the clinical significance?
flashcards.back: Almost 1 in 4 males in their 60s have a smell disorder compared to only 1 in 10 women of the same age. Men have higher rates of both presbyosmia and tinnitus. Loss of smell can affect diet, causing malnutrition (eating too little) or unhealthy eating (adding excess sugar/salt to compensate for blandness).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What percentage of adults over 65 report chronic pain, and which conditions are the most common causes?
flashcards.back: Approximately 60–75% of adults over 65 report at least some chronic pain, with higher rates in nursing home residents. The most common causes are arthritis, cancer, diabetes, joint pain, sciatica, and shingles.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why is decreased pain sensitivity in older adults a clinical concern despite seeming advantageous?
flashcards.back: Pain serves as an important indicator that something is medically wrong. Decreased pain sensitivity in older adults can mask or conceal illnesses or injuries requiring medical attention, leading to delayed diagnosis and treatment.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What brain structures show age-related volume decreases related to pain processing?
flashcards.back: The prefrontal cortex, brainstem, and hippocampus show age-related decreases in brain volume in structures involved in pain, based on neuroimaging research (Farrell, 2012).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between chronic pain in older adults and physical activity, weight, mood, and sleep?
flashcards.back: Unmanaged chronic pain causes older adults to restrict movement, leading to decreased activity, further disability, weight gain, and obesity. It is also associated with sleep disorders and depression (mood disorders).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does glaucoma risk differ by race and age?
flashcards.back: African Americans over age 40 are at higher risk for glaucoma. For all other racial groups, the higher risk begins at age 60. Diabetes and family history also increase risk regardless of race.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are the risk factors for cataracts beyond normal aging?
flashcards.back: Diabetes, high blood pressure, obesity, smoking, prolonged UV sunlight exposure, previous eye trauma, long-term use of steroid medication, and family history of cataracts.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the smoking-related risk increase for macular degeneration?
flashcards.back: Smoking doubles the risk of developing age-related macular degeneration.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does touch sensitivity change with age, and what variability exists?
flashcards.back: Pressure detection declines with age, becoming more pronounced by the 6th decade and worsening further with advanced age. However, approximately 40% of older adults show pressure sensitivity comparable to younger adults. The ability to detect roughness/smoothness or hardness/softness shows no appreciable change with age.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the concern regarding addiction to pain medication in older adults, and what is the evidence-based response?
flashcards.back: Some older adults fear developing addiction to pain medications and therefore delay or avoid treatment. If medication is taken exactly as prescribed, addiction should not be a concern. Undertreating pain leads to greater disability, depression, and reduced quality of life.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What treatments are available for wet macular degeneration, and how effective are they?
flashcards.back: Medications that slow the growth of abnormal blood vessels, and surgery such as laser treatment to destroy abnormal blood vessels, may be used. Only 25% of those with wet macular degeneration see improvement with these procedures.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does a head cold demonstrate the two olfactory pathways?
flashcards.back: The two pathways are: (1) inhalation through the nose, and (2) the connection between the nose and throat during chewing and digestion. A head cold blocks the second pathway, explaining why foods like chocolate or coffee seem tasteless — the olfactory contribution from the nose-throat route is disrupted.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How much weight does the brain lose between ages 20 and 90, and what structural changes cause this?
flashcards.back: The brain loses 5–10% of its weight between ages 20 and 90. This is due to shrinkage of neurons, a lower number of synapses, and shorter axon lengths, all of which reduce overall brain volume.

flashcards.front: What is the Scaffolding Theory of Aging and Cognition, and who proposed it?
flashcards.back: Proposed by Park and Reuter-Lorenz (2009), the Scaffolding Theory of Aging and Cognition states that the aging brain adapts to neural atrophy by building alternative neural connections called scaffolding. This compensatory mechanism allows older adults to maintain high cognitive performance despite structural brain decline.

flashcards.front: How does brain lateralization differ between younger and older adults performing memory tasks?
flashcards.back: Younger adults tend to use only the right prefrontal cortex for memory tasks (greater lateralization), while older adults who perform equally well use both prefrontal areas (decreased lateralization). This bilateral activation appears to assist older adults in maintaining cognitive performance (Cabeza et al., 2002).

flashcards.front: What were the key findings and limitations of the ACTIVE study?
flashcards.back: The ACTIVE study (1999–2001) with 2,802 participants aged 65–94 found that 10 group training sessions plus 4 follow-up sessions in memory, reasoning, and processing speed improved cognitive functioning for at least 5 years. However, improvements were specific to trained tasks with no generalization to other abilities, and the program did not delay or slow Alzheimer's disease progression.

flashcards.front: What are the most effective ways to improve brain functioning in older adults, and what do they build?
flashcards.back: Physical exercise, learning new skills, and socializing are the most effective ways to train the brain. These activities build cognitive reserve, which helps minimize the effects of primary aging of the brain.

flashcards.front: What is the pathological mechanism of Parkinson's disease, and what are its motor symptoms?
flashcards.back: Parkinson's disease results from deterioration of the substantia nigra, whose neurons normally send dopamine-releasing axons to the basal ganglia to regulate motor activity. Loss of this dopaminergic input causes the hallmark symptoms: motor tremors, loss of balance, poor coordination, rigidity, and difficulty moving.

flashcards.front: How does head injury increase the risk of Parkinson's disease?
flashcards.back: Being knocked unconscious once increases the risk of Parkinson's disease by 32%, while being knocked unconscious multiple times increases the risk by 174%, according to Garrett (2015). This indicates that traumatic brain injury is a significant environmental risk factor for Parkinson's.

flashcards.front: How does levodopa (L-dopa) treat Parkinson's disease?
flashcards.back: L-dopa is a dopamine precursor that, unlike dopamine itself, can cross the blood-brain barrier. Once in the brain, it is converted into dopamine, partially restoring the dopamine levels lost due to substantia nigra degeneration and thereby improving motor symptoms.

flashcards.front: What is advanced sleep phase syndrome and what causes it in older adults?
flashcards.back: Advanced sleep phase syndrome is a pattern in which older adults go to sleep earlier and wake up earlier than younger adults. It is caused by age-related changes in circadian rhythms and is considered a normal part of aging rather than a disorder.

flashcards.front: What are the health consequences of untreated sleep apnea?
flashcards.back: Untreated sleep apnea can lead to impaired daytime functioning, high blood pressure, headaches, stroke, and memory loss. It involves repeated pauses in breathing during sleep that reduce blood oxygen levels, and snoring is a common symptom that often worsens with age.

flashcards.front: How does periodic limb movement disorder differ from restless legs syndrome?
flashcards.back: Restless legs syndrome involves uncomfortable waking sensations (tingling, crawling, pins and needles) in the legs that are worse at night and disrupt sleep onset. Periodic limb movement disorder causes involuntary jerking and kicking of the legs every 20–40 seconds during sleep, disrupting sleep quality without necessarily causing waking sensations.

flashcards.front: What role do glutamate and GABA play in age-related cognitive decline?
flashcards.back: Glutamate (excitatory) and GABA (inhibitory) are neurotransmitters whose receptor activity declines with normal aging. Reduced activity of these receptors contributes to the cognitive decline seen in aging, specifically affecting memory storage, synaptic plasticity, and overall neural communication efficiency.

flashcards.front: Why does loss of myelin/white matter matter for aging cognitive function?
flashcards.back: Myelin sheaths enable rapid signal conduction along axons. Loss of white matter connections between brain areas in aging leads to slower neural conduction and causes neurons to impede each other's actions, contributing to slower information processing and cognitive decline.

flashcards.front: What amount of weekly exercise improves sleep quality in older adults, and what types are recommended?
flashcards.back: 150 minutes per week of exercise is recommended to improve sleep quality in older adults. Aerobic activity, weight training, and balance programs are all recommended. Even light exercise such as stretching and short walks can improve sleep, particularly for those in assisted living facilities. High-intensity activity is not required.

flashcards.front: What are the environmental risk factors for Parkinson's disease?
flashcards.back: Environmental risk factors for Parkinson's disease include brain injury (being knocked unconscious), toxins, industrial chemicals, carbon monoxide, herbicides, and pesticides (Olanow & Tatton, 1999). These factors, combined with genetic predispositions across 28 chromosomal areas, contribute to the disease's development.

flashcards.front: What brain regions show increased activation in older adults during complex tasks, and what does this suggest?
flashcards.back: Older adults activate larger regions of their attentional and executive networks in the parietal and prefrontal cortex during complex tasks. This increased activation correlates with reduced performance on executive tasks and working memory tests compared to younger adults, suggesting less efficient neural processing that requires broader recruitment.

flashcards.front: What medical conditions are known to disrupt sleep in older adults?
flashcards.back: Medical conditions that disrupt sleep in older adults include gastroesophageal reflux disease, diabetes mellitus, renal failure, respiratory diseases (e.g., asthma), immune disorders, Parkinson's disease, multiple sclerosis, and Alzheimer's disease. Alzheimer's patients may wake repeatedly, wander, and yell, significantly altering sleep duration.

flashcards.front: What are the consequences of sleep problems in older adults?
flashcards.back: Both minor and significant sleep problems in older adults can lead to increased risk of accidents and falls, chronic fatigue, decreased quality of life, cognitive decline, reduced immune function, and depression (Buman, 2013).

flashcards.front: What percentage of men aged 52–72 reported being sexually active according to the National Social Life, Health, and Aging Project?
flashcards.back: 72% of men aged 52–72 reported being sexually active (Karraker, DeLamater, & Schwarz, 2011).

flashcards.front: What percentage of women aged 52–72 reported being sexually active according to NSHAP data?
flashcards.back: 45.5% of women aged 52–72 reported being sexually active (Karraker, DeLamater, & Schwarz, 2011).

flashcards.front: According to the National Survey of Sexual Health, what percentage of individuals remain sexually active into their 80s?
flashcards.back: 20–30% of individuals remain sexually active well into their 80s (Schick et al., 2010).

flashcards.front: How does a change in marital status (e.g., widowhood) differentially affect sexual behavior in older men vs. older women?
flashcards.back: A change in marital status does not result in a decline in sexual behavior for men aged 57–85, but it does result in a significant decline for women of the same age group (Karraker et al., 2011).

flashcards.front: What is the sex ratio of women to men at age 85, and why does this matter for older women's sexuality?
flashcards.back: By age 85, women outnumber men approximately 2 to 1. This limits the pool of available partners for older heterosexual women, negatively affecting their opportunity to remain sexually active.

flashcards.front: What are four advantages older couples may have in their sex lives compared to when they were younger?
flashcards.back: Fewer distractions, more time and privacy, no concerns about pregnancy, and greater intimacy with a lifelong partner (NIA, 2013).

flashcards.front: Which chronic illness is specifically associated with erectile dysfunction in older men?
flashcards.back: Diabetes is specifically associated with erectile dysfunction, likely due to vascular and nerve damage caused by chronically elevated blood glucose.

flashcards.front: Which chronic illness is associated with difficulty achieving orgasm in both sexes?
flashcards.back: Heart disease is associated with difficulty achieving orgasm for both men and women.

flashcards.front: Which chronic illness is associated with inappropriate sexual behavior in older adults?
flashcards.back: Dementia is associated with inappropriate sexual behavior, due to disinhibition and impaired executive function.

flashcards.front: If a heterosexual older couple experiences a decline in sexual activity, what is typically the primary cause according to Erber & Szuchman (2015)?
flashcards.back: The decline is typically due to a decline in the male partner's physical health, not the female partner's.

flashcards.front: How can internalized ageism affect an older adult's sexuality?
flashcards.back: Older adults who internalize the cultural stereotype that elderly people are asexual may have difficulty accepting their own sexual feelings and desires, reducing their sexual activity and satisfaction.

flashcards.front: Why might older women experience a reduced pool of available male partners beyond the simple sex ratio imbalance?
flashcards.back: In addition to the 2:1 female-to-male ratio at age 85, older men who become widowed or divorced tend to pair with younger women, further reducing the number of available male partners for older heterosexual women.

flashcards.front: Name five lifestyle changes that can promote longer and more active lives in older adulthood.
flashcards.back: Regular exercise, stress reduction, smoking cessation, limiting alcohol consumption, and eating more fruits and vegetables (He et al., 2005).

flashcards.front: How does societal emphasis on youthful beauty affect older women's sexuality?
flashcards.back: Older women who are unhappy with their changing appearance may believe their partners no longer find them attractive. This negative body image can interfere with sexual performance and enjoyment.

flashcards.front: What is the trend in smoking rates and obesity rates over the last 40 years as it relates to healthy aging?
flashcards.back: Over the last 40 years, smoking rates have decreased (a positive trend), but obesity has increased and physical activity has only modestly increased, representing ongoing public health challenges for active aging.

flashcards.front: What did Kane (2008) identify regarding how society views older adults in terms of gender and sexuality?
flashcards.back: Kane (2008) identified that older men and women are often viewed as genderless and asexual, and that when elderly individuals do engage in sexual activity, it is sometimes perceived as offensive or inappropriate.

flashcards.front: What role does stress reduction play in late adulthood health, according to the content?
flashcards.back: Stress reduction, both in late adulthood and earlier in life, is crucial for promoting active life expectancy and reducing the burden of societal stressors on older adults' health and quality of life.

flashcards.front: Which type of memory is most consistently preserved in older adults?
flashcards.back: Implicit memory (including procedural memory and priming) is most consistently preserved in older adults. Semantic memory is also well-maintained and may improve. These forms require little conscious effort to retrieve.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does episodic memory change with aging, and why?
flashcards.back: Episodic memory declines significantly with aging due to reduced encoding efficiency, slower processing speed, decreased inhibitory control, and frontal lobe changes that impair source memory. Free recall of personal events is especially affected, while recognition is relatively better preserved.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the difference between fluid and crystallized intelligence, and how does each change with age?
flashcards.back: Fluid intelligence (novel problem solving, logical reasoning) declines with age starting in early adulthood due to reduced processing speed and working memory. Crystallized intelligence (accumulated knowledge, vocabulary) is maintained or increases with age. This divergence is known as the classic aging pattern of intelligence.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the general slowing hypothesis and what cognitive changes does it explain?
flashcards.back: The general slowing hypothesis posits that a universal reduction in neural processing speed underlies most age-related cognitive declines. It explains slower reaction times, reduced working memory capacity, poorer episodic recall, and diminished divided attention, as slower processing creates cascading effects across cognitive tasks.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does divided attention differ from selective attention in the context of aging?
flashcards.back: Divided attention (simultaneously processing multiple tasks) shows significant age-related decline because it requires substantial cognitive resources that diminish with age. Selective attention (focusing on one stimulus while ignoring others) shows only modest decline. Sustained attention is relatively preserved in older adults.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is source memory and why is it particularly vulnerable to aging?
flashcards.back: Source memory is the ability to recall the context in which information was learned (when, where, how). It is particularly vulnerable to aging because it relies heavily on frontal lobe functioning and effortful encoding strategies, both of which decline with age. Older adults may remember facts but forget where they learned them.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does stereotype threat affect cognitive performance in older adults?
flashcards.back: Stereotype threat occurs when older adults are reminded of negative aging stereotypes (e.g., memory loss), which increases anxiety and cognitive load, impairing performance on memory and cognitive tests. This means that some apparent age-related cognitive decline measured in research settings may be artificially inflated by stereotype threat effects.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Compare time-based and event-based prospective memory in aging.
flashcards.back: Time-based prospective memory (remembering to act at a specific time) is more impaired in older adults because it requires self-initiated monitoring without external cues. Event-based prospective memory (remembering to act when a specific external cue occurs) is better preserved because the environment provides a retrieval trigger, reducing reliance on self-monitoring.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What role does inhibitory control play in age-related cognitive decline?
flashcards.back: Inhibitory control, the ability to suppress irrelevant information in working memory, declines with age. This leads to greater susceptibility to distraction, difficulty filtering irrelevant information, increased intrusion errors in memory tasks, and impaired performance on complex attention and reasoning tasks requiring mental focus.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does processing speed relate to other cognitive changes in aging?
flashcards.back: Processing speed is considered a foundational cognitive resource. Its decline with age creates downstream effects on working memory (less time to rehearse information), episodic memory encoding (shallower processing), divided attention (tasks compete for limited time), and problem solving (slower integration of information). It is the single most well-documented age-related cognitive change.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What cognitive abilities represent strengths in older adulthood?
flashcards.back: Older adults show relative strengths in semantic memory (vocabulary, world knowledge), crystallized intelligence, wisdom (practical judgment, emotional insight), procedural memory, priming effects, and event-based prospective memory. These represent areas where life experience and accumulated knowledge offset age-related processing limitations.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How do older adults compensate for cognitive decline in everyday problem solving?
flashcards.back: Older adults often compensate by relying on crystallized knowledge and expertise, using external memory aids (lists, calendars), selecting familiar and well-practiced strategies, reducing cognitive load through environmental structuring, and leveraging wisdom and experience to make efficient decisions without engaging in exhaustive novel analysis.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is wisdom and why is it associated with older adulthood?
flashcards.back: Wisdom is a multidimensional quality encompassing deep insight, good judgment, emotional regulation, recognition of uncertainty, and understanding of the human condition. It tends to increase with age because it is built through accumulated life experience, perspective-taking, and reflection — qualities that standard cognitive tests do not measure.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does encoding change with aging and what are the consequences?
flashcards.back: Older adults tend to use shallower, less elaborative encoding strategies, process information more slowly, and are less likely to spontaneously organize material for easier retrieval. These changes result in weaker memory traces, poorer subsequent free recall, and greater difficulty remembering contextual details such as source information.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the difference between recognition memory and free recall in older adults, and what does this reveal about memory storage vs. retrieval?
flashcards.back: Older adults perform significantly worse on free recall (unaided retrieval) than on recognition (choosing from options), suggesting that memory storage is often intact but retrieval processes are impaired. This distinction indicates that much of age-related memory decline may reflect retrieval failure rather than complete loss of stored information.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does working memory decline affect complex cognition in older adults?
flashcards.back: Reduced working memory capacity limits the amount of information that can be actively held and manipulated simultaneously. This impairs complex reasoning, multi-step problem solving, language comprehension of complex sentences, and the ability to keep track of multiple elements in a conversation or task — all of which depend on efficient working memory.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why do stereotypes about memory loss in older adults not fully reflect the research evidence?
flashcards.back: Research shows that memory is not a unitary system — many memory types (implicit, semantic, procedural) are well-preserved in aging. Declines are selective (primarily episodic and working memory) and influenced by non-cognitive factors such as stereotype threat, motivation, health, and testing conditions. Stereotypes exaggerate decline and ignore significant preserved capacities and gains such as wisdom.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Which component of working memory is most negatively impacted by aging?
flashcards.back: The central executive is most negatively impacted by aging. It oversees working memory, allocates attentional resources, and monitors cognitive strategy effectiveness. Tasks requiring dual-task management or divided attention show the largest age-related deficits.

flashcards.front: What did Göthe, Oberauer, and Kliegl (2007) find about dual-task learning in older vs. younger adults?
flashcards.back: Younger adults eventually learned and performed two tasks simultaneously without loss of speed or efficiency (with considerable practice). None of the older adults could achieve simultaneous dual-task performance, though older adults could match young adult performance when each task was learned individually, indicating the deficit is specific to central executive demands.

flashcards.front: Why does episodic memory decline more with age than semantic memory?
flashcards.back: Episodic memory requires encoding and retrieving at least two types of information: the event itself and its spatiotemporal context (when and where it occurred). Semantic memory only requires encoding or retrieving the knowledge itself, without any contextual timeline. The additional complexity makes episodic memory more vulnerable to age-related cognitive slowing.

flashcards.front: What did Spaniol, Madden, and Voss (2006) find about retrieval speed for semantic vs. episodic memory?
flashcards.back: Retrieval of semantic information was considerably faster for both younger and older adults than retrieval of episodic information. There was little difference between age groups for semantic memory retrieval. As task difficulty increased, the performance gap between age groups widened more for episodic memory than for semantic memory.

flashcards.front: Does priming ability decline significantly with age?
flashcards.back: No. Priming, as a form of implicit memory, shows minimal decline with normal aging. Studies (Schacter, Church, & Osowiecki, 1994) confirm that most older adults retain intact priming effects, meaning prior exposures continue to influence subsequent behavior without conscious effort.

flashcards.front: How do older adults compare to younger adults on recognition vs. recall memory tasks?
flashcards.back: On recognition tasks, older adults perform at roughly equivalent levels to younger adults because recognition requires less cognitive energy. On recall tasks, older adults show deficits compared to younger adults, with declines beginning around age 40 and increasing with age.

flashcards.front: What is a tip-of-the-tongue (TOT) event and how does its frequency change with age?
flashcards.back: A TOT event is a temporary failure to retrieve information (especially words) that a person is certain they know. Older adults experience TOT events more frequently than younger adults, even though their semantic and lexical knowledge may be equal to or greater than that of younger adults.

flashcards.front: What are the two subtypes of prospective memory and which shows greater age-related decline?
flashcards.back: The two subtypes are time-based prospective memory (remembering to do something at a specific future time) and event-based prospective memory (remembering to do something when a specific event occurs). Time-based prospective memory shows greater age-related decline. Event-based prospective memory is more resilient, and older adults may perform comparably or slightly better than younger adults in real-world settings.

flashcards.front: Why does prospective memory decline when competing tasks are present?
flashcards.back: Prospective memory relies heavily on the central executive of working memory. When the central executive is occupied managing other demanding tasks, resources available to maintain and monitor future intentions are reduced, causing prospective memories to be more likely forgotten.

flashcards.front: How does the digit span test reflect age-related changes in working memory?
flashcards.back: The digit span test primarily engages the phonological loop, not the central executive. Because age-related working memory decline is concentrated in the central executive, older adults perform at par with younger adults on digit span tests, demonstrating that not all working memory components decline equally.

flashcards.front: On what types of memory tasks do older adults frequently outperform younger adults?
flashcards.back: Older adults often outperform younger adults on semantic memory tasks (e.g., vocabulary, lexical memory, general knowledge of politics and history), reflecting decades of accumulated knowledge. They also outperform younger adults in domain-specific expertise tasks, such as chess strategy efficiency and reading-ahead typing compensation.

flashcards.front: How did older expert typists compensate for age-related slowing, according to Salthouse (1984)?
flashcards.back: Older expert typists compensated for slower motor speed by looking farther ahead at the printed text, allowing them to anticipate and prepare for upcoming keystrokes. This strategy effectively neutralized the speed disadvantage and demonstrates how expertise can offset age-related declines.

flashcards.front: How do older chess experts differ from younger chess players in cognitive strategy?
flashcards.back: According to Charness (1981), older chess experts focus on a smaller set of possible moves than younger players, resulting in greater cognitive efficiency. This selective focus compensates for slower processing speed and allows older experts to perform at high levels.

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between task difficulty and the age gap in episodic memory performance?
flashcards.back: As episodic memory task difficulty increases, the performance gap between older and younger adults widens. Older adults' poorer performance is attributed to slower information processing and the greater demands placed on encoding and retrieval of contextual details in difficult tasks.

flashcards.front: In what setting do older adults show the most resilience in prospective memory, and why?
flashcards.back: Older adults show the most resilience—and sometimes superior performance—in real-world prospective memory settings. This is because older adults tend to be more selective and motivated about where they direct mental and social energy, making personally meaningful future tasks (like doctor appointments) higher priority than laboratory tasks (like pressing a space bar when 'tiger' appears).

flashcards.front: What does the concept of 'accrued knowledge advantage' mean for older adult decision-making?
flashcards.back: Research (Tentori et al., 2001) found that older adults' accumulated real-world knowledge, such as awareness of everyday grocery prices, allows them to make better consumer decisions than younger adults. This illustrates how semantic and experiential knowledge can compensate for processing speed or working memory declines.

flashcards.front: How does implicit memory compare to explicit memory in terms of age-related decline?
flashcards.back: Implicit memory (procedural skills, priming, classical conditioning) shows few declines with age and remains largely intact in older adults. Explicit memory (episodic and semantic) shows more age-related decline, with episodic memory showing the greatest decline of the two explicit memory types.

flashcards.front: According to Jefferies et al. (2015), what happens to older adults' selective attention when they are given double the time to complete attention tasks?
flashcards.back: When given double the time, older adults can perform at young adult levels on selective attention tasks. This suggests that the deficit is related to speed rather than an absolute inability to selectively attend.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the Processing Speed Theory of cognitive aging, and who proposed it?
flashcards.back: Proposed by Salthouse (1996, 2004), the Processing Speed Theory argues that as the nervous system slows with advanced age, the ability to process information declines, which explains age-related differences across many cognitive tasks including working memory.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the Inhibition Theory of cognitive aging, and what research paradigm provides key evidence for it?
flashcards.back: Proposed by Hasher and Zacks (1988), Inhibition Theory argues that older adults have difficulty suppressing attention to irrelevant information. Key evidence comes from directed forgetting research, in which older adults recall more words from a 'forget' list than younger adults do.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does directed forgetting research demonstrate deficits in inhibitory functioning in older adults?
flashcards.back: In directed forgetting tasks, participants are told to forget a memorized word list and learn a new one. Older adults recall significantly more words from the 'forget' list than younger adults, showing they cannot effectively suppress or inhibit previously encoded information.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What did Salthouse and Babcock (1991) find about processing speed and working memory across ages 18–82?
flashcards.back: Salthouse and Babcock found that processing speed accounted for all but 1% of age-related differences in working memory, strongly supporting the idea that slowing of processing speed is the primary driver of age-related cognitive decline.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why might cross-sectional research overestimate cognitive decline in older adults?
flashcards.back: Cross-sectional research compares different age cohorts at one point in time and does not account for cohort differences (education, historical factors, health). Longitudinal research, which follows the same individuals over time, shows that cognitive loss is less dramatic than cross-sectional studies suggest.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are four reasons cognitive losses in late adulthood are often exaggerated in research?
flashcards.back: 1) Laboratory tasks are often meaningless and unmotivating for older adults. 2) Research is often cross-sectional rather than longitudinal. 3) Loss may reflect lack of practice rather than true decline. 4) Uncontrolled sensory deficits (vision, hearing) may be mistaken for cognitive decline.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How do older adults compensate for cognitive decline in workplace settings?
flashcards.back: Older workers develop more efficient strategies and rely on accumulated expertise to compensate for slowing cognitive processes. Research (Park & Gutchess, 2000) rarely finds that older individuals perform poorer on the job compared to younger workers.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What did Blanchard-Fields (2007) find about older adults and interpersonal problem solving?
flashcards.back: Blanchard-Fields found that older adults use more effective strategies than younger adults when navigating social and emotional problems, suggesting that real-world wisdom and experience can offset declines in processing speed for interpersonal challenges.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does motivation influence cognitive performance in older adults?
flashcards.back: Older adults are less motivated to remember meaningless information (e.g., random word lists) but perform better on tasks involving personally relevant or meaningful material. This motivational difference partly explains why laboratory studies overestimate cognitive decline.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are the implications of attentional changes in late adulthood for driving safety?
flashcards.back: While older adults have more driving experience, cognitive declines in reaction time and attentional processes may pose limitations under certain conditions (e.g., complex intersections, night driving). Park and Gutchess (2000) noted these circumstances as potential areas of limitation.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What did Schaie (1994) find when statistically removing the effects of speed from cognitive performance data?
flashcards.back: Schaie found that statistically removing the effects of processing speed significantly diminished observed performance declines in older adults, indicating that much of what appears to be broad cognitive decline is actually attributable to slowing rather than true loss of cognitive ability.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does the type of problem-solving task affect age-related performance differences?
flashcards.back: Tasks requiring rapid processing of non-meaningful information show clear age-related declines. In contrast, real-life problems that rely on social input, expertise, and experience show preserved or superior performance in older adults, highlighting the context-dependence of cognitive aging.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What did Baltes and Lindenberger (1997) propose about sensory functioning and cognitive aging?
flashcards.back: Through longitudinal research, Baltes and Lindenberger proposed that declines in sensory functioning (vision and hearing) explain age differences in a variety of cognitive abilities, linking perceptual decline to broader cognitive aging.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How do older adults differ from younger adults in their approach to making important decisions?
flashcards.back: Older adults are less likely than younger adults to delay making decisions on important matters such as medical care (Strough et al., 2003; Meegan & Berg, 2002). They also resolve everyday problems by relying more on input from family and friends.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What does Tales, Muir, Bayer, and Snowden (2002) research reveal about attentional shifting in older adults?
flashcards.back: Their research found that older adults have greater difficulty shifting attention between objects or locations compared to younger adults, contributing to broader attentional and cognitive challenges in late adulthood.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: When does cognitive decline NOT significantly impair performance in older adults?
flashcards.back: When speed is not imperative to the task, healthy older adults perform as competently as younger adults. Salthouse (1996) noted that given sufficient time, older adults can match young adult performance across many cognitive tasks.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Attention and Problem Solving | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the difference between crystallized and fluid intelligence in terms of aging?
flashcards.back: Crystallized intelligence (vocabulary, knowledge-based tasks) shows minimal or no decline with age and may increase. Fluid intelligence (logical reasoning, processing speed, spatial ability) declines with age. This mirrors the distinction between Baltes's pragmatics and mechanics of intelligence.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are Baltes's two types of intelligence introduced to reflect cognitive changes in aging?
flashcards.back: Pragmatics of intelligence (analogous to crystallized intelligence — cultural facts and procedures, maintained or increased with age) and Mechanics of intelligence (analogous to fluid intelligence — brain-dependent processing, declines with age).
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How can pragmatics of intelligence compensate for mechanics of intelligence?
flashcards.back: As processing speed and other brain-dependent abilities decline (mechanics), accumulated cultural knowledge, experience, and procedural know-how (pragmatics) can offset functional limitations, allowing older adults to perform effectively despite neurological declines.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: According to research, at what life stage can wisdom first emerge?
flashcards.back: Wisdom can emerge as early as late adolescence and young adulthood (Staudinger & Gluck, 2011). There are few additional gains in wisdom over the course of adulthood, suggesting age is not the primary driver of wisdom.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What factors are stronger determinants of wisdom than age alone?
flashcards.back: According to Baltes & Kunzmann (2004), other-focused occupations and experiences, personality characteristics such as openness to experience, and generativity are stronger determinants of wisdom than age alone.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What did Botwinick (1984) find about verbal skills versus performance test scores in aging?
flashcards.back: Botwinick found that verbal skills show minimal or no age-related decline, while performance test scores — which measure solving problems quickly — decline with age. This mirrors the crystallized/fluid intelligence distinction.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Is wisdom a common outcome of aging?
flashcards.back: No. According to Baltes and colleagues, wisdom is rare. While aging brings more experience, it does not automatically produce wisdom. Specific types of experience, personality traits, and attitudes toward others are required.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What types of tasks measure crystallized intelligence?
flashcards.back: Vocabulary tests, solving number problems, and understanding texts. These tasks rely on accumulated knowledge and experience rather than real-time cognitive processing speed.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What types of tasks measure fluid intelligence?
flashcards.back: Logical reasoning, remembering lists, spatial ability, and reaction time. These tasks reflect information processing efficiency and are dependent on neurological functioning.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Define wisdom according to the content and explain why living longer does not guarantee it.
flashcards.back: Wisdom is the ability to use accumulated knowledge about practical matters for sound judgment and decision making. Living longer brings more experience, but wisdom also requires specific personality traits (openness to experience, generativity) and other-focused experiences — factors not guaranteed by age alone.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How does the profile of cognitive aging differ between verbal and performance tests?
flashcards.back: Verbal skills (crystallized/pragmatics) are largely preserved or improved with age. Performance tests measuring speed-dependent problem solving (fluid/mechanics) decline. This means cognitive aging is not global but domain-specific.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the relationship between generativity and wisdom?
flashcards.back: Generativity — concern for guiding and contributing to the next generation — is one of the personality characteristics identified by Baltes & Kunzmann (2004) as a building block of wisdom. It reflects an other-focused orientation that supports wise decision-making.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: Why are global cognitive declines not considered typical in normal aging?
flashcards.back: Because different cognitive domains age differently. Crystallized intelligence and pragmatics of intelligence are preserved or increase, while fluid intelligence and mechanics decline. Additionally, compensation mechanisms help offset some losses, especially in processing speed.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is cognitive compensation and how does it relate to aging?
flashcards.back: Cognitive compensation refers to using preserved or enhanced abilities to offset declining ones. In aging, pragmatics of intelligence (knowledge, experience) can compensate for declines in mechanics (processing speed, reasoning speed), maintaining functional competence.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What personality traits does Baltes & Kunzmann identify as contributing to wisdom?
flashcards.back: Openness to experience and generativity. These traits, combined with other-focused occupations and experiences and age, provide the building blocks necessary for wisdom to develop.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How do mechanics and pragmatics of intelligence relate to fluid and crystallized intelligence respectively?
flashcards.back: Mechanics of intelligence are analogous to fluid intelligence — both are brain-dependent and decline with age. Pragmatics of intelligence are analogous to crystallized intelligence — both reflect accumulated cultural knowledge and experience and are maintained or increase with age.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What does a wise person possess according to the definition in this content?
flashcards.back: A wise person is insightful, possesses accumulated knowledge about practical matters, can exercise sound judgment and decision making, and can apply that knowledge to overcome obstacles in living.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the key difference between Major and Minor Neurocognitive Disorder?
flashcards.back: Major Neurocognitive Disorder involves significant cognitive decline that interferes with independent functioning. Minor Neurocognitive Disorder involves modest cognitive decline that does NOT interfere with independent functioning. Both require decline in one or more cognitive domains from a previous level of performance.

flashcards.front: How many Americans were estimated to have Alzheimer's disease in 2016, and what is the projected number by 2050?
flashcards.back: In 2016, approximately 5.4 million Americans were diagnosed with Alzheimer's disease (about 1 in 9 people aged 65 and older). By 2050, the number is projected to reach 13.8 million if no medical breakthroughs occur.

flashcards.front: Why is Alzheimer's disease considered unique among the top 10 causes of death in America?
flashcards.back: Alzheimer's disease is the only one among the top 10 causes of death in America that cannot be prevented, cured, or even slowed by current medical interventions.

flashcards.front: What are the progressive stages of symptom development in Alzheimer's disease?
flashcards.back: Stage 1: Subtle personality changes and memory loss different from normal aging. Stage 2: Confusion, difficulty with change, deterioration in language, problem-solving, and personality. Stage 3: Loss of physical coordination, inability to complete everyday tasks including self-care. Stage 4: Loss of ability to respond to environment, carry on conversation, and control movement.

flashcards.front: What is the role of tau protein in Alzheimer's disease?
flashcards.back: Normally, tau maintains the brain's intracellular transport system. In Alzheimer's disease, tau malfunctions and forms twisted strands called neurofibrillary tangles that disrupt this transport system. As a result, nutrients and supplies cannot move through neurons, causing the cells to die.

flashcards.front: What is beta-amyloid and how does it contribute to Alzheimer's disease?
flashcards.back: Beta-amyloid is a sticky protein fragment derived from a larger protein in the fatty membrane surrounding nerve cells. It accumulates into amyloid plaques between neurons, blocking cell-to-cell communication and triggering an inflammatory immune response that leads to further neuronal death and cognitive decline.

flashcards.front: Why is memory loss typically one of the earliest symptoms of Alzheimer's disease?
flashcards.back: Because the hippocampus — the brain region most critically involved in learning and memory formation — is one of the first areas to be damaged by Alzheimer's disease pathology, including amyloid plaques and neurofibrillary tangles.

flashcards.front: How does Vascular Neurocognitive Disorder differ from Alzheimer's disease in terms of onset, mechanism, and personality effects?
flashcards.back: Vascular NCD: comes on more abruptly, caused by blockage of cerebral blood vessels affecting a specific brain region, does not significantly affect personality, affects more males than females, and has a shorter course before death. Alzheimer's: gradual onset, involves diffuse neuronal death throughout the brain, significantly affects personality, and affects both sexes more equally.

flashcards.front: What are the distinguishing clinical features of Neurocognitive Disorder with Lewy Bodies compared to Parkinson's disease?
flashcards.back: Both involve similar motor symptoms (tremors, muscle rigidity). The key distinction is timing: in Lewy body disorder, cognitive and motor symptoms occur simultaneously. In Parkinson's disease, cognitive symptoms appear well after motor symptoms. Lewy body disorder also involves sleep disturbances, recurrent visual hallucinations, and increased fall risk.

flashcards.front: What is familial Alzheimer's disease and at what age do symptoms typically appear?
flashcards.back: Familial Alzheimer's disease is caused by deterministic (inherited) genes. People who inherit these rare genes typically develop symptoms in their 30s, 40s, and 50s. It accounts for a portion of early-onset cases and represents about 5% of all Alzheimer's diagnoses occurring in individuals under age 65.

flashcards.front: What percentage of all neurocognitive disorder cases are estimated to be caused by Alzheimer's disease?
flashcards.back: Approximately 50% of all identified neurocognitive disorder cases are caused by Alzheimer's disease, making it by far the most common neurocognitive disorder.

flashcards.front: What is the typical survival time after an Alzheimer's diagnosis, and what factors influence this?
flashcards.back: On average, people with Alzheimer's survive eight years after diagnosis, but some may live up to 20 years. The disease course depends on the individual's age at diagnosis and the presence of other health conditions.

flashcards.front: What are the prevalence rates of Vascular Neurocognitive Disorder across age groups?
flashcards.back: Vascular Neurocognitive Disorder affects approximately 0.2% of individuals in the 65-70 age group and rises to 16% of individuals aged 80 and older, demonstrating a dramatic increase in prevalence with advancing age.

flashcards.front: What are the motor symptoms of Neurocognitive Disorder with Lewy Bodies and why are falls a concern?
flashcards.back: Motor symptoms include tremors and muscle rigidity, similar to Parkinson's disease. These movement disturbances, combined with cognitive impairment, significantly impair balance and coordination, placing individuals at increased risk for falling and associated injuries.

flashcards.front: What are the modifiable and non-modifiable risk factors for Alzheimer's disease?
flashcards.back: Non-modifiable: Age (greatest risk factor), genetics/familial genes, sex. Modifiable: Traumatic brain injury, obesity, hypertension, high cholesterol, and diabetes. Addressing modifiable risk factors may help reduce overall risk.

flashcards.front: How does the DSM-5 differ from previous editions in its classification of cognitive decline disorders?
flashcards.back: The DSM-5 replaced the historical term 'dementia' with the term 'neurocognitive disorder,' and created two subtypes — Major Neurocognitive Disorder (significant decline interfering with independence) and Minor Neurocognitive Disorder (modest decline not interfering with independence) — providing a more nuanced diagnostic framework.

flashcards.front: Where in the brain do Lewy bodies deposit, and what symptoms result from each location?
flashcards.back: Lewy bodies can deposit in both the cortex and the brain stem. Cortical deposits result in cognitive symptoms (thinking, memory, behavior, mood). Brain stem deposits result in motor symptoms (tremors, muscle rigidity). The simultaneous presence in both regions distinguishes it from Parkinson's disease.

flashcards.front: What percentage of workers transition directly to bridge jobs rather than retiring outright?
flashcards.back: Approximately 60% of workers transition straight to bridge jobs, which are often part-time positions between a primary career and full retirement.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What is the difference between a bridge job and an encore career?
flashcards.back: A bridge job is any transitional, often part-time job between a career and full retirement. An encore career specifically involves work in a different field from one's primary career and is typically pursued after full retirement.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: At what age can Americans born after 1959 receive full Social Security benefits?
flashcards.back: Americans born after 1959 must wait until age 67 to receive full Social Security benefits. Those who retire at age 62 receive only 75% of their monthly benefits.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What did the 1986 amendment to the Age Discrimination in Employment Act (ADEA) establish?
flashcards.back: The 1986 amendment eliminated mandatory retirement for most U.S. workers, allowing them to continue working as long as they choose and are able. Exceptions include pilots, air traffic controllers, federal law enforcement, national park rangers, and firefighters.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are the three historical pillars of retirement income in the United States?
flashcards.back: The three historical pillars of retirement income are: (1) Social Security, (2) a pension plan, and (3) individual savings. The 2008 recession significantly reduced the value of pension plans for many workers.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What did Wu et al. (2016) find about delaying retirement by one year?
flashcards.back: Wu et al. (2016) found that a one-year delay in retirement was associated with a decreased risk of death from all causes, looking at both healthy and unhealthy retirees.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What are the six stages of Atchley's retirement model in order?
flashcards.back: (1) Remote pre-retirement phase, (2) Immediate pre-retirement phase, (3) Actual retirement, (4) Honeymoon phase, (5) Disenchantment phase, (6) Reorientation phase. Not all individuals experience every stage.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: What did Kaskie et al. (2008) find about retirees not involved in productive activities?
flashcards.back: Kaskie et al. (2008) found that 70% of retirees not involved in productive activities spent most of their time watching television, which was correlated with negative affect and reduced well-being.
flashcards.source: courses.lumenlearning.com: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development

flashcards.front: How has the average age of retirement in the U.S. changed over time?
