- White families: 13% living in multigenerational households
- Conclusion: With the exception of some cultural groups, the MAJORITY of older adults wish to live INDEPENDENTLY for as long as they are able

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

DO THOSE IN LATE ADULTHOOD MOVE AFTER RETIREMENT?
- Answer: NO
- The MAJORITY of those in late adulthood remain in the SAME LOCATION, and often in the SAME HOUSE, where they lived before retiring (Erber & Szuchman, 2015)

AGE-RELATED RELOCATION PATTERNS:
- YOUNGER late adults (65–74 years): may relocate to WARMER CLIMATES
- OLDER late adults (75–84 years): often RETURN to their home states to be closer to adult children
  • Source: Stoller & Longino, 2001

RECENT HOUSING CRISIS IMPACT:
- The recent housing crisis has kept those in late adulthood in their current SUBURBAN LOCATIONS
  • Because they are UNABLE TO SELL their homes
  • Source: Erber & Szuchman, 2015

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

DO THOSE IN LATE ADULTHOOD PRIMARILY LIVE IN INSTITUTIONS?
- Answer: NO
- Only a SMALL PORTION (3.2%) of adults older than 65 lived in an institution in 2015
  • Source: United States Department of Health and Human Services, 2015

AGE-BASED BREAKDOWN OF INSTITUTIONAL LIVING (2015):
- Ages 65–74: 1% lived in an institution
- Ages 75–84: 3% lived in an institution
- Ages 85 and older: 10% lived in an institution
- Note: As individuals INCREASE IN AGE, the percentage living in institutions (e.g., nursing homes) also INCREASES

FUTURE PROJECTIONS FOR LONG-TERM CARE:
- Due to the INCREASING NUMBER OF BABY BOOMERS reaching late adulthood:
  • Number of people dependent on long-term care expected to rise from:
    - 12 MILLION in 2010
    - 27 MILLION in 2050
  • Source: United States Senate Commission on Long-Term Care, 2013

SHIFT IN CARE PHILOSOPHY:
- Focus on LEAST RESTRICTIVE CARE ALTERNATIVES
- Resulting in a shift TOWARD home and community-based care
- AWAY FROM placement in a nursing home
- Source: Gatz et al., 2016

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. In 2014: 72% of older MEN and 46% of older WOMEN lived with their spouse (Vespa & Schondelmyer, 2015)
2. The number of older adults living alone INCREASED from 1900–1990 (due to improved health/longevity), then DECLINED after 1990
3. Older adults living ALONE are more financially strained: only 33% comfortable vs. 49% of those with others; 12% lack basic needs vs. 5% of those with others
4. Multigenerational household rates: Asian (25%) > Black (23%) > Hispanic (22%) > White (13%)
5. MAJORITY of older adults stay in the SAME HOME/LOCATION after retirement
6. Younger retirees (65–74) may move to warmer climates; older retirees (75–84) often return home to be near adult children (Stoller & Longino, 2001)
7. Only 3.2% of adults 65+ lived in an institution in 2015 (USDHHS, 2015)
8. Institutional living by age: 1% (65–74), 3% (75–84), 10% (85+)
9. Long-term care need projected to grow from 12 million (2010) to 27 million (2050) — US Senate Commission on Long-Term Care, 2013
10. Current trend: SHIFT TOWARD home and community-based care as the LEAST RESTRICTIVE ALTERNATIVE to nursing homes (Gatz et al., 2016)

notes.title: Ageism | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
AGEISM — OVERVIEW
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Stereotypes of people in late adulthood lead many to assume that aging automatically brings poor physical health and mental decline
- These stereotypes are reflected in: everyday conversations, the media, and greeting cards (Overstreet, 2006)
- Birthday cards that joke about getting older are one example of how people seek relief from age-related anxiety
- Age is NOT revered in the United States
- DEFINITION — AGEISM: prejudice based on age; negative attitudes people have about those in late adulthood
- The term 'ageism' was FIRST USED IN 1969
- According to Nelson (2016): ageism remains one of the most INSTITUTIONALIZED forms of prejudice today

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
EFFECTS OF NEGATIVE STEREOTYPES ON COGNITIVE AND PHYSICAL PERFORMANCE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Nelson (2016) reviewed research on ageism and concluded:
  • When older individuals BELIEVED their culture's negative stereotypes about aging → their memory and cognitive skills DECLINED
  • Older individuals in cultures with MORE POSITIVE views on aging (e.g., China) did NOT demonstrate cognitive deficits
- DEFINITION — SELF-FULFILLING PROPHECY: the belief in one's ability results in actions that make it come true; when one agrees with the stereotype, it becomes self-fulfilling
- DEFINITION — STEREOTYPE THREAT: being the target of stereotypes can adversely affect individuals' performance on tasks because they worry they will CONFIRM cultural stereotypes
  • Originally used to explain RACE AND GENDER differences in academic achievement (Gatz et al., 2016)
  • Stereotype threat research (Levy, 2009) demonstrated that older adults who INTERNALIZE aging stereotypes will exhibit:
    — Worse MEMORY performance
    — Worse PHYSICAL performance
    — Reduced SELF-EFFICACY

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
EFFECTS OF NEGATIVE STEREOTYPES ON HEALTH BEHAVIORS AND OUTCOMES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Those who believe in negative stereotypes are (Nelson, 2016):
  • Less likely to engage in PREVENTATIVE HEALTH BEHAVIORS
  • Less likely to RECOVER from illnesses
  • More likely to feel STRESS AND ANXIETY → adversely affects immune functioning and cardiovascular health
- Individuals who ATTRIBUTE their health problems to their AGE had a HIGHER DEATH RATE
- Doctors who believe illnesses are just a natural consequence of aging are:
  • Less likely to have older adults PARTICIPATE IN CLINICAL TRIALS
  • Less likely to provide LIFE-SUSTAINING TREATMENT
- In CONTRAST — older adults who possess POSITIVE AND OPTIMISTIC views of aging:
  • Less likely to have physical or mental health problems
  • More likely to LIVE LONGER
- Removing societal stereotypes about aging AND helping older adults reject those notions is another way to PROMOTE HEALTH AND LIFE EXPECTANCY among the elderly

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
MINORITY STATUS AND AGEISM
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Older minority adults accounted for approximately 21% of the U.S. population in 2012
- Expected to reach 39% of the population by 2050 (U.S. Census Bureau, 2012)
- Racism is a FURTHER CONCERN for minority elderly already suffering from ageism
- Older adults who are African American, Mexican American, and Asian American experience psychological problems often associated with DISCRIMINATION by the White majority (Youdin, 2016)
- Ethnic minorities are MORE LIKELY to become sick but LESS LIKELY to receive medical intervention
- DEFINITION — TRIPLE JEOPARDY: older minority women can face ageism, racism, AND sexism simultaneously (Hinze, Lin, & Andersson, 2012)
  • This can adversely affect their life in late adulthood

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
POVERTY RATES AMONG OLDER ADULTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- According to Quinn and Cahill (2016), poverty rate for older adults varies based on:
  • GENDER
  • MARITAL STATUS
  • RACE
  • AGE
- Women aged 65 or older were 70% MORE LIKELY TO BE POOR than men
- Older women aged 80 and above have HIGHER LEVELS OF POVERTY than younger elderly women
- MARRIED COUPLES are less likely to be poor than nonmarried men and women
- Poverty is more prevalent among OLDER RACIAL MINORITIES
- 2012 POVERTY RATES (specific statistics):
  • White older MEN: 5.6%
  • White older WOMEN: 9.6%
  • Black older MEN: 14%
  • Black older WOMEN: 21%
  • Hispanic older MEN: 19%
  • Hispanic older WOMEN: 22%

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ KEY EXAM POINTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. AGEISM = prejudice based on age; term first coined in 1969; identified by Nelson (2016) as one of the most institutionalized forms of prejudice
2. SELF-FULFILLING PROPHECY = when belief in a stereotype causes actions that confirm it; key mechanism through which ageism harms older adults
3. STEREOTYPE THREAT = fear of confirming cultural stereotypes impairs performance; originally applied to race and gender (Gatz et al., 2016); extended to aging by Levy (2009)
4. Levy (2009): older adults who internalize aging stereotypes show worse memory, worse physical performance, and reduced self-efficacy
5. Negative stereotypes → less preventative health behavior, lower recovery rates, higher stress/anxiety → impairs immune and cardiovascular health
6. Attributing health problems to age = HIGHER DEATH RATE
7. Doctors with ageist views = less likely to include older adults in clinical trials or provide life-sustaining treatment
8. POSITIVE views on aging = better physical and mental health outcomes + longer life
9. China example: cultures with positive views on aging → no cognitive deficits observed in elderly
10. TRIPLE JEOPARDY = ageism + racism + sexism faced by older minority women (Hinze, Lin, & Andersson, 2012)
11. Minority elderly: 21% of U.S. population in 2012 → projected 39% by 2050
12. Ethnic minorities: MORE likely to get sick, LESS likely to receive medical intervention
13. Women 65+ are 70% MORE LIKELY to be poor than men; women 80+ have even higher poverty
14. Poverty rates (2012): White men 5.6%, White women 9.6%, Black men 14%, Black women 21%, Hispanic men 19%, Hispanic women 22%
15. Married couples are LESS likely to be poor than nonmarried individuals

notes.title: Work and Retirement | Lifespan Development (Lumen Learning)
notes.body:
WORK AND RETIREMENT — LIFESPAN DEVELOPMENT
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

OLDER ADULTS IN THE WORKPLACE
- Older adults are just as capable as younger adults in the workplace
- Jobs that require social skills, accumulated knowledge, and relevant experiences FAVOR older adults (Erber & Szuchman, 2015)
- Older adults demonstrate:
  • Lower rates of absenteeism
  • Greater investment in their work
- In 2015: 8.8 million adults aged 65 or older were employed or actively seeking employment
- This constitutes approximately 5.6% of the U.S. labor force (AOA, 2016)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

TRANSITIONING INTO RETIREMENT
- For most Americans, retirement is a PROCESS, not a one-time event (Quinn & Cahill, 2016)
- 60% of workers transition straight to BRIDGE JOBS
  • Bridge jobs are often part-time
  • Bridge jobs occur BETWEEN a career and full retirement
- About 15% of workers get another job AFTER being fully retired
  • Reasons: inadequate finances after retirement OR not enjoying retirement
  • Some of these jobs may be in ENCORE CAREERS — work in a different field from the one in which they retired
- Approximately 10% of workers begin PHASING INTO RETIREMENT by reducing their hours
  • Not all employers will allow this due to pension regulations

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

RETIREMENT AGE CHANGES
- Average age of retirement DECLINED from more than 70 in 1910 to age 63 in the early 1980s
- This trend has REVERSED — current average age of retirement is now 65
- 18.5% of those over age 65 continue to work (US Department of Health and Human Services, 2012)
  • Compare: only 12% continued working in 1990 (U.S. Government Accountability Office, 2011)
- With individuals living longer, the average amount of time a retired worker collects Social Security is approximately 17–18 years (James, Matz-Costa, & Smyer, 2016)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

WHEN TO RETIRE
- Laws often influence when someone decides to retire
- In 1986: the AGE DISCRIMINATION IN EMPLOYMENT ACT (ADEA) was amended
  • Mandatory retirement was ELIMINATED for most workers (Erber & Szuchman, 2015)
  • Exceptions — enforced retirement ages still apply to:
    ◦ Pilots
    ◦ Air traffic controllers
    ◦ Federal law enforcement
    ◦ National park rangers
    ◦ Fire fighters
  • For most workers: they can continue to work if they choose and are able
- SOCIAL SECURITY BENEFITS also play a role in retirement timing:
  • Born before 1938: full benefits at age 65
  • Born between 1943 and 1954: must wait until age 66 for full benefits
  • Born after 1959: must wait until age 67 for full benefits (Social Security Administration, 2016)
  • Extra months are added for those born in years between (e.g., born in 1957 → must wait until 66 years and 6 months)
  • The longer one waits to receive Social Security, the more money will be paid out
  • Those retiring at age 62 receive only 75% of their monthly benefits
- MEDICARE health insurance is another entitlement not available until age 65

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

DELAYED RETIREMENT
- Older adults primarily choose to delay retirement due to ECONOMIC REASONS (Erber & Szuchman, 2015)
- Financial reasons to continue working:
  • Added income
  • Does not dip into retirement savings which may not be sufficient
- Historically: THREE PARTS to retirement income:
  1. Social Security
  2. Pension plan
  3. Individual savings
  (Quinn & Cahill, 2016)
- With the 2008 RECESSION:
  • Pension plans lost value for most workers
  • Many older workers had to work later in life to compensate for absent or minimal pension plans and personal savings
- Social Security was never intended to replace full income; benefits may not cover all expenses
- Vulnerable populations who are especially unable to secure later employment:
  • Persons with disabilities
  • Single women
  • The oldest-old
  • Individuals with intermittent work histories
- PSYCHOLOGICAL REASONS to delay retirement:
  • Health benefits
  • Social contacts
- Recent research: Delaying retirement has been associated with living longer
  • Looking at both healthy and unhealthy retirees: a ONE-YEAR DELAY in retiring was associated with a decreased risk of death from all causes (Wu, Odden, Fisher, & Stawski, 2016)
- When individuals are FORCED to retire (due to health concerns or downsizing): more likely to have negative physical and psychological consequences (Erber & Szuchman, 2015)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

RETIREMENT STAGES — ATCHLEY (1994)
- Atchley (1994) identified several phases that individuals go through when they retire:

  1. REMOTE PRE-RETIREMENT PHASE
     • Includes fantasizing about what one wants to do in retirement

  2. IMMEDIATE PRE-RETIREMENT PHASE
     • When concrete plans are established

  3. ACTUAL RETIREMENT
     • The event of retiring

  4. HONEYMOON PHASE
     • Retirees travel and participate in activities they could not do while working

  5. DISENCHANTMENT PHASE
     • Retirees experience an emotional let-down

  6. REORIENTATION PHASE
     • Retirees attempt to adjust to retirement by making less hectic plans and getting into a regular routine

- Not everyone goes through every stage
- This model demonstrates that retirement is a PROCESS

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

POST-RETIREMENT
- Those who look MOST FORWARD to retirement and have plans are those who anticipate adequate income (Erber & Szuchman, 2015)
  • This is especially true for males who have worked consistently and have a pension and/or adequate savings
- Staying ACTIVE and SOCIALLY ENGAGED is important after retirement
- Ways to stay engaged:
  • Volunteering
  • Caregiving
  • Informal helping
- Kaskie, Imhof, Cavanaugh and Culp (2008) found:
  • 70% of retirees who are NOT involved in productive activities spent most of their time watching TV
  • Watching TV is correlated with NEGATIVE AFFECT
  • In contrast, being PRODUCTIVE improves well-being

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

ELDER EDUCATION
- Attending college is not just for the young
- Many reasons why someone in late adulthood chooses to attend college
- PNC Financial Services surveyed retirees aged 70 and over:
  • 58% indicated they had retired BEFORE they had planned (Holland, 2014)
  • Many chose to pursue additional training to improve skills to return to work in a second career
  • Others look to take their career in a new direction
  • For some older students no longer focused on financial reasons: returning to school is intended to enable personally fulfilling work
- Attending college in late adulthood is a great way to stay young and keep minds sharp
- Even if an elder chooses NOT to attend college for a degree: many continuing education programs are available on topics of interest

ELDERHOSTEL / ROAD SCHOLAR:
- In 1975: a nonprofit educational travel organization called ELDERHOSTEL began in New Hampshire
  • Started with five programs for several hundred retired participants (DiGiacomo, 2015)
  • Combined college classroom time with travel tours and experiential learning experiences
- In 2010: organization changed its name to ROAD SCHOLAR
  • Now serves 100,000 people per year in the U.S. and in 150 countries
  • Courses offered include:
    ◦ Academic courses
    ◦ Computer classes
    ◦ Foreign languages
    ◦ Budgeting
    ◦ Holistic medicines
    ◦ Other practical skills
- Older adults with HIGHER LEVELS OF EDUCATION are more likely to take continuing education
- Offering educational experiences to a DIVERSE GROUP of older adults — including those institutionalized in nursing homes — can enhance quality of life

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

LEARNING OBJECTIVES: PSYCHOSOCIAL DEVELOPMENT IN LATE ADULTHOOD
(Listed for course context — topics covered in associated sections)
- Explain the stereotypes of those in late adulthood and how it impacts their lives
- Summarize Erikson's eighth psychosocial task of integrity vs. despair
- Explain how self-concept and self-esteem affect those in late adulthood
- Identify sources of despair and regret
- Describe paths to integrity, including the activity, socioemotional selectivity, and convoy theories
- Describe the continuation of generativity in late adulthood
- Describe the relationships those in late adulthood have with their children and other family members
- Describe singlehood, marriage, widowhood, divorce, and remarriage in late adulthood
- Describe the different types of residential living in late adulthood
- Describe friendships in late life
- Explain concerns experienced by those in late adulthood, such as abuse and mental health issues

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS

1. Older adults have LOWER absenteeism and GREATER investment in work than younger adults; favor jobs requiring social skills and accumulated knowledge (Erber & Szuchman, 2015)
2. In 2015: 8.8 million adults 65+ employed or seeking employment = 5.6% of U.S. labor force (AOA, 2016)
3. Retirement is a PROCESS, not a one-time event (Quinn & Cahill, 2016)
4. 60% of workers go to BRIDGE JOBS; 15% get a new job after full retirement; 10% phase in by reducing hours
5. ENCORE CAREERS = work in a different field from the one in which they retired
6. Average retirement age: declined from 70+ (1910) → 63 (early 1980s) → reversed to 65 today
7. 18.5% of those over 65 continue to work (2012) vs. only 12% in 1990
8. Average time collecting Social Security after retirement = 17–18 years (James, Matz-Costa, & Smyer, 2016)
9. 1986 ADEA amendment: mandatory retirement eliminated for most workers; exceptions = pilots, air traffic controllers, federal law enforcement, national park rangers, fire fighters
10. Social Security full benefits: before 1938 = age 65; 1943–1954 = age 66; after 1959 = age 67
11. Retiring at age 62 = only 75% of monthly Social Security benefits; Medicare begins at age 65
12. THREE pillars of retirement income: Social Security, pension plan, individual savings
13. 2008 recession caused pension plan losses → forced older workers to delay retirement
14. Vulnerable groups unable to secure later employment: persons with disabilities, single women, oldest-old, those with intermittent work histories
15. One-year delay in retirement = decreased risk of death from ALL causes (Wu, Odden, Fisher, & Stawski, 2016)
16. FORCED retirement (health/downsizing) → negative physical and psychological consequences
17. ATCHLEY (1994) six retirement stages: Remote Pre-retirement → Immediate Pre-retirement → Actual Retirement → Honeymoon → Disenchantment → Reorientation
18. 70% of retirees NOT in productive activities spend most time watching TV → correlated with negative affect (Kaskie et al., 2008)
19. PNC survey: 58% of retirees 70+ retired BEFORE they planned (Holland, 2014)
20. ELDERHOSTEL (1975, New Hampshire) → renamed ROAD SCHOLAR (2010) → serves 100,000/year in U.S. and 150 countries
21. Higher education level in older adults = more likely to participate in continuing education

notes.title: Neurocognitive Disorders | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
NEUROCOGNITIVE DISORDERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

HISTORICAL TERMINOLOGY & DSM-5 CHANGES
- Historically, the term DEMENTIA referred to difficulties with: memory, language, abstract thinking, reasoning, decision making, and problem-solving (Erber & Szuchman, 2015)
- In the DSM-5 (American Psychiatric Association, 2013), the term dementia was REPLACED by NEUROCOGNITIVE DISORDER

DEFINITIONS: MAJOR VS. MINOR NEUROCOGNITIVE DISORDER
- MAJOR Neurocognitive Disorder: diagnosed as a SIGNIFICANT cognitive decline from a previous level of performance in one or more cognitive domains AND interferes with INDEPENDENT FUNCTIONING
- MINOR Neurocognitive Disorder: diagnosed as a MODEST cognitive decline from a previous level of performance in one or more cognitive domains and does NOT interfere with independent functioning

GENERAL NOTES ON DIAGNOSIS
- Several different neurocognitive disorders are typically demonstrated in late adulthood
- Determining the exact type can be difficult because symptoms may OVERLAP with each other
- Diagnosis often includes:
  • Medical history
  • Physical exam
  • Laboratory tests
  • Changes noted in behavior
- Three disorders discussed: Alzheimer's disease, Vascular Neurocognitive Disorder, Neurocognitive Disorder with Lewy bodies

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE

PREVALENCE & STATISTICS
- Probably the most well-known and most COMMON neurocognitive disorder for older individuals
- 2016: estimated 5.4 MILLION Americans diagnosed with Alzheimer's disease (Alzheimer's Association, 2016)
- Approximately ONE IN NINE aged 65 and over
- By 2050: projected 13.8 MILLION people age 65 and older with Alzheimer's disease (if no medical breakthroughs to prevent or cure)
- Alzheimer's disease is the 6TH LEADING CAUSE OF DEATH in the United States overall
- 5TH LEADING CAUSE for those 65 and older
- Among the top 10 causes of death in America, Alzheimer's disease is the ONLY ONE that CANNOT be prevented, cured, or even slowed
- Current estimates: Alzheimer's disease affects approximately 50% of those identified with a neurocognitive disorder (Cohen & Eisdorfer, 2011)

DISEASE PROGRESSION
- GRADUAL ONSET
- Stage 1 (earliest): subtle personality changes and memory loss (differs from normal age-related memory problems)
- Stage 2: confusion, difficulty with change, deterioration in language, problem-solving skills, and personality
- Stage 3 (later stages): individual loses physical coordination; unable to complete everyday tasks including self-care and personal hygiene (Erber & Szuchman, 2015)
- Stage 4 (last stage): individuals lose ability to respond to their environment, carry on a conversation, and eventually to control movement (Alzheimer's Association, 2016)
- Average survival: EIGHT YEARS after diagnosis
- Some may live up to 20 YEARS
- Disease course often depends on individual's age and other health conditions
- NOTE: Visit https://www.wesleylife.org/memory-care/get-the-facts/ to view a timeline of how Alzheimer's disease progresses

RISK FACTORS
- GREATEST risk factor: AGE
- Genetic factors
- Environmental factors
- Some forms are HEREDITARY
- EARLY ONSET TYPE: several rare genes have been identified that directly cause Alzheimer's
  • People who inherit these genes develop symptoms in their 30s, 40s, and 50s
  • 5% of those identified with Alzheimer's disease are YOUNGER THAN AGE 65
- When caused by deterministic genes: called FAMILIAL ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE (Alzheimer's Association, 2016)
- Traumatic brain injury (Carlson, 2011)
- Obesity (Carlson, 2011)
- Hypertension (Carlson, 2011)
- High cholesterol (Carlson, 2011)
- Diabetes (Carlson, 2011)

NEUROPATHOLOGY: PLAQUES AND TANGLES
- Problems occur due to: "death of neurons, the breakdown of connections between them, and the extensive formation of plaques and tau, which interfere with neuron functioning and neuron survival" (Erber & Szuchman, 2015, p. 50)

PLAQUES:
- Abnormal formations of protein pieces called BETA-AMYLOID
- Beta-amyloid comes from a larger protein found in the FATTY MEMBRANE surrounding nerve cells
- Because beta-amyloid is STICKY, it builds up into plaques (Alzheimer's Association, 2016)
- Plaques appear to BLOCK CELL COMMUNICATION
- May also trigger an INFLAMMATORY RESPONSE in the immune system → leads to further neuronal death

TAU / TANGLES:
- Tau is an important protein that helps maintain the brain's TRANSPORT SYSTEM
- When tau MALFUNCTIONS, it changes into TWISTED STRANDS called TANGLES
- Tangles DISRUPT the transport system
- Consequence: nutrients and other supplies cannot move through the cells → cells eventually die

BRAIN CHANGES:
- Death of neurons leads to the BRAIN SHRINKING, affecting all aspects of brain functioning
- HIPPOCAMPUS is involved in learning and memory
- Brain cells in the hippocampus are OFTEN THE FIRST TO BE DAMAGED
- This is why MEMORY LOSS is often one of the earliest symptoms of Alzheimer's disease
- Reference: Figure 9.30 (Plaques and Tangles) and Figure 9.31 (comparison of Alzheimer's brain vs. healthy brain)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

VASCULAR NEUROCOGNITIVE DISORDER

PREVALENCE
- SECOND MOST COMMON neurocognitive disorder
- Affects 0.2% in the 65–70 years age group
- Affects 16% of individuals 80 years and older (American Psychiatric Association, 2013)

CHARACTERISTICS
- Associated with a BLOCKAGE OF CEREBRAL BLOOD VESSELS
- Affects ONE PART of the brain (rather than a general loss of brain cells as seen with Alzheimer's)
- Personality is NOT AS AFFECTED as in Alzheimer's disease
- MORE MALES diagnosed than females (Erber & Szuchman, 2015)
- Comes on MORE ABRUPTLY than Alzheimer's disease
- Has a SHORTER COURSE before death compared to Alzheimer's

RISK FACTORS
- Smoking
- Diabetes
- Heart disease
- Hypertension
- History of strokes

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

NEUROCOGNITIVE DISORDER WITH LEWY BODIES

DEFINITION & PATHOLOGY
- Lewy bodies are MICROSCOPIC PROTEIN DEPOSITS found in neurons; seen POSTMORTEM (National Institute on Aging, 2015a)
- They affect chemicals in the brain that can lead to difficulties in:
  • Thinking
  • Movement
  • Behavior
  • Mood

PREVALENCE & DEMOGRAPHICS
- THIRD MOST COMMON form of neurocognitive disorder
- Affects MORE THAN 1 MILLION Americans
- Typically begins at AGE 50 OR OLDER
- Appears to affect SLIGHTLY MORE MEN than women

DISEASE COURSE
- Lasts approximately 5 TO 7 YEARS from time of diagnosis to death
- Can range from 2 TO 20 YEARS depending on individual's age, health, and severity of symptoms

SYMPTOMS
- Lewy bodies can occur in BOTH THE CORTEX AND BRAIN STEM → results in COGNITIVE AS WELL AS MOTOR symptoms (Erber & Szuchman, 2015)
- Movement symptoms similar to PARKINSON'S DISEASE:
  • Tremors
  • Muscle rigidity
- KEY DISTINCTION from Parkinson's disease: Motor disturbances occur AT THE SAME TIME as cognitive symptoms
  • In Parkinson's disease: cognitive symptoms occur WELL AFTER the motor symptoms
- Additional symptoms:
  • Sleep disturbances
  • Recurrent VISUAL HALLUCINATIONS
  • At risk for FALLING

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS

1. DSM-5 replaced the term 'dementia' with 'neurocognitive disorder'
2. MAJOR = significant decline + interferes with independent functioning; MINOR = modest decline + does NOT interfere with independent functioning
3. Alzheimer's disease = MOST COMMON neurocognitive disorder; ~50% of all neurocognitive disorder cases (Cohen & Eisdorfer, 2011)
4. 5.4 million Americans with Alzheimer's in 2016; projected 13.8 million by 2050
5. Alzheimer's is the 6th leading cause of death overall; 5th for those 65+; ONLY top-10 cause that cannot be prevented, cured, or slowed
6. Average survival with Alzheimer's = 8 years; range up to 20 years
7. Earliest symptom of Alzheimer's = MEMORY LOSS (hippocampus is first region damaged)
8. Plaques = BETA-AMYLOID protein buildup (sticky); block cell communication, trigger inflammation
9. Tangles = TWISTED TAU protein strands; disrupt transport system; cells die without nutrients
10. Familial Alzheimer's disease = caused by deterministic genes; early onset in 30s–50s; 5% of Alzheimer's cases are under age 65
11. Greatest risk factor for Alzheimer's = AGE; others include TBI, obesity, hypertension, high cholesterol, diabetes
12. Vascular Neurocognitive Disorder = 2ND most common; caused by BLOCKAGE of cerebral blood vessels; more abrupt onset; more males; shorter course
13. Vascular prevalence: 0.2% (ages 65–70) → 16% (age 80+)
14. Neurocognitive Disorder with Lewy Bodies = 3RD most common; affects 1 million+ Americans; onset age 50+; slightly more men
15. Lewy bodies course = 5–7 years average (range 2–20 years)
16. KEY DISTINCTION: In Lewy body disorder, motor and cognitive symptoms appear SIMULTANEOUSLY; in Parkinson's, cognitive symptoms appear WELL AFTER motor symptoms
17. Lewy body symptoms: tremors, rigidity, sleep disturbances, recurrent VISUAL HALLUCINATIONS, fall risk

notes.title: Intelligence and Wisdom | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
INTELLIGENCE AND WISDOM
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

INTELLIGENCE AND AGING — OVERVIEW
- Traditional intelligence tests reveal a SPLIT pattern of aging:
  • Tasks measuring VERBAL SKILLS → show minimal or NO age-related declines
  • PERFORMANCE TESTS (measuring solving problems quickly) → DECLINE with age
  • Source: Botwinick (1984)
- This split profile mirrors the distinction between CRYSTALLIZED and FLUID intelligence

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

CRYSTALLIZED INTELLIGENCE
- Encompasses abilities that draw upon EXPERIENCE and KNOWLEDGE
- Measures of crystallized intelligence include:
  • Vocabulary tests
  • Solving number problems
  • Understanding texts
- Generally MAINTAINED or increases with age

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

FLUID INTELLIGENCE
- Refers to INFORMATION PROCESSING abilities
- Includes:
  • Logical reasoning
  • Remembering lists
  • Spatial ability
  • Reaction time
- DECLINES with age

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

BALTES'S TWO ADDITIONAL TYPES OF INTELLIGENCE (1993)
- Paul Baltes (1993) introduced two additional types to reflect cognitive changes in aging:

1. PRAGMATICS OF INTELLIGENCE
   - Definition: Cultural exposure to facts and procedures that are MAINTAINED as one ages
   - Similar to: CRYSTALLIZED intelligence
   - Age trajectory: Shows LITTLE DECLINE and typically INCREASES with age
   - Additional function: May COMPENSATE for declines that occur in mechanics of intelligence

2. MECHANICS OF INTELLIGENCE
   - Definition: Dependent on BRAIN FUNCTIONING
   - Similar to: FLUID intelligence
   - Age trajectory: DECLINES with age

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SUMMARY ON COGNITIVE AGING
- Global cognitive declines are NOT TYPICAL as one ages
- Individuals compensate for some cognitive declines, ESPECIALLY processing speed
- Pragmatics of intelligence serve a compensatory function for declining mechanics

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

WISDOM — DEFINITION
- Wisdom = the ability to use ACCUMULATED KNOWLEDGE about practical matters that allows for SOUND JUDGMENT and DECISION MAKING
- A wise person is:
  • Insightful
  • Possesses knowledge that can be used to OVERCOME OBSTACLES in living

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

DOES AGING BRING WISDOM?
- Living longer brings EXPERIENCE, but does NOT always bring WISDOM
- Paul Baltes and colleagues (Baltes & Kunzmann, 2004; Baltes & Staudinger, 2000) suggest:
  • Wisdom is RARE
  • Emergence of wisdom can be seen in LATE ADOLESCENCE and YOUNG ADULTHOOD
  • There are FEW GAINS in wisdom over the course of adulthood (Staudinger & Gluck, 2011)
  • This suggests that factors OTHER THAN AGE are STRONGER DETERMINANTS of wisdom

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

FACTORS THAT CONTRIBUTE TO WISDOM
- Occupations and experiences that emphasize OTHERS RATHER THAN SELF
- Personality characteristics, including:
  • OPENNESS TO EXPERIENCE
  • GENERATIVITY
- These factors are MORE LIKELY to provide the building blocks of wisdom (Baltes & Kunzmann, 2004)
- Conclusion: AGE combined with CERTAIN TYPES OF EXPERIENCE and/or PERSONALITY brings wisdom

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

KEY RESEARCHERS AND CITATIONS
- Botwinick (1984) — verbal vs. performance test patterns in aging
- Baltes (1993) — pragmatics and mechanics of intelligence
- Baltes & Kunzmann (2004) — wisdom is rare; personality and experience as determinants
- Baltes & Staudinger (2000) — wisdom appears in late adolescence/young adulthood
- Staudinger & Gluck (2011) — few gains in wisdom across adulthood

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
1. Verbal skills show MINIMAL/NO age-related decline; performance tests DECLINE with age (Botwinick, 1984)
2. Crystallized intelligence = experience/knowledge-based; maintained or increases with age
3. Fluid intelligence = information processing; DECLINES with age
4. Baltes (1993) introduced PRAGMATICS (like crystallized; maintained/increases) and MECHANICS (like fluid; declines) of intelligence
5. Pragmatics of intelligence can COMPENSATE for declines in mechanics of intelligence
6. Global cognitive decline is NOT TYPICAL; individuals compensate especially for processing speed
7. Wisdom = using accumulated knowledge for sound judgment and decision making
8. Wisdom is RARE (Baltes & Kunzmann, 2004; Baltes & Staudinger, 2000)
9. Wisdom emerges in LATE ADOLESCENCE and YOUNG ADULTHOOD — NOT primarily gained through old age
10. Few gains in wisdom across adulthood (Staudinger & Gluck, 2011)
11. Strongest determinants of wisdom: OTHER-FOCUSED experience/occupation + openness to experience + generativity
12. Age ALONE does not bring wisdom — it must be combined with certain experiences and/or personality traits

notes.title: Attention and Problem Solving in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
ATTENTION AND PROBLEM SOLVING
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

CHANGES IN ATTENTION IN LATE ADULTHOOD
• Changes in sensory functioning and speed of processing information in late adulthood often translates into changes in attention (Jefferies et al., 2015)
• Research has shown that older adults are LESS ABLE TO SELECTIVELY FOCUS on information while ignoring distractors (Jefferies et al., 2015; Wascher, Schneider, Hoffman, Beste, & Sänger, 2012)
• EXCEPTION: Jefferies and colleagues found that when given DOUBLE TIME, older adults could perform at YOUNG ADULT LEVELS
• Other studies found that older adults have GREATER DIFFICULTY SHIFTING THEIR ATTENTION between objects or locations (Tales, Muir, Bayer, & Snowden, 2002)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

COGNITIVE ABILITY AND EVERYDAY LIFE IN LATE ADULTHOOD
• Researchers have studied cognition in the context of several different everyday activities

DRIVING:
• Older adults often have more years of driving experience
• Cognitive declines related to REACTION TIME or ATTENTIONAL PROCESSES may pose limitations under certain circumstances (Park & Gutchess, 2000)

INTERPERSONAL PROBLEM SOLVING:
• Older adults use MORE EFFECTIVE STRATEGIES than younger adults to navigate through social and emotional problems (Blanchard-Fields, 2007)

WORK CONTEXT:
• Researchers rarely find that older individuals perform POORER ON THE JOB (Park & Gutchess, 2000)
• Older workers may develop more efficient strategies and rely on EXPERTISE to compensate for cognitive decline
• Similar compensation pattern to everyday problem solving

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

PROBLEM SOLVING
• Problem solving tasks that require processing NON-MEANINGFUL INFORMATION QUICKLY (e.g., laboratory experiments on mental processes) DECLINES WITH AGE
• Many real-life challenges facing older adults do NOT rely on:
  - Speed of processing
  - Making choices on one's own
• Older adults resolve everyday problems by RELYING ON INPUT FROM OTHERS (family and friends)
• Older adults are LESS LIKELY THAN YOUNGER ADULTS to delay making decisions on important matters such as medical care (Strough, Hicks, Swenson, Cheng & Barnes, 2003; Meegan & Berg, 2002)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

THEORIES EXPLAINING COGNITIVE DEFICITS WITH AGE

PROCESSING SPEED THEORY (Salthouse, 1996, 2004):
• Proposed by: SALTHOUSE (1996, 2004)
• As the nervous system SLOWS with advanced age, ability to process information DECLINES
• Slowing of processing speed may explain age differences on MANY DIFFERENT COGNITIVE TASKS
• As we age, WORKING MEMORY becomes less efficient (Craik & Bialystok, 2006)
• Older adults need LONGER TIME to complete mental tasks or make decisions
• KEY FINDING: When given SUFFICIENT TIME, older adults perform AS COMPETENTLY AS YOUNG ADULTS (Salthouse, 1996)
• Conclusion: When speed is NOT IMPERATIVE to the task, healthy older adults do NOT show cognitive declines

INHIBITION THEORY (Hasher & Zacks, 1988):
• Proposed by: HASHER & ZACKS (1988)
• Argues older adults have difficulty with INHIBITORY FUNCTIONING
• Inhibitory functioning = the ability to FOCUS ON CERTAIN INFORMATION while SUPPRESSING ATTENTION to less pertinent information
• Evidence comes from DIRECTED FORGETTING RESEARCH

DIRECTED FORGETTING:
• Definition: People are asked to forget or ignore some information, but not other information
• Example: Memorize a list of words → told researcher made a mistake and gave wrong list → asked to "forget" this list → given a second list to memorize
• Most people do well at forgetting the first list
• OLDER ADULTS are more likely to recall MORE WORDS from the "forget-to-recall" list than younger adults (Andrés, Van der Linden, & Parmentier, 2004)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

COGNITIVE LOSSES EXAGGERATED
• While there ARE information processing losses in late adulthood, overall loss has been EXAGGERATED (Garrett, 2015)
• Four key reasons why losses may be overstated:

REASON 1 – MEANINGLESS TASKS:
• The type of tasks people are tested on tend to be MEANINGLESS
• Older individuals are NOT motivated to remember a random list of words in a study
• They ARE motivated for MORE MEANINGFUL MATERIAL related to their life
• Consequently perform BETTER on meaningful tests

REASON 2 – CROSS-SECTIONAL RESEARCH DESIGN:
• Research is often CROSS-SECTIONAL
• When age comparisons occur LONGITUDINALLY, the amount of loss DIMINISHES (Schaie, 1994)

REASON 3 – LACK OF OPPORTUNITY / PRACTICE:
• Loss may be due to a LACK OF OPPORTUNITY in using various skills
• When older adults PRACTICED SKILLS, they performed as well as they had previously
• Diminished PERFORMANCE SPEED is especially noteworthy in the elderly
• Schaie (1994) found that statistically REMOVING THE EFFECTS OF SPEED diminished the individual's performance declines SIGNIFICANTLY
• Salthouse & Babcock (1991): Processing speed accounted for ALL BUT 1% of age-related differences in working memory when testing individuals from ages 18 to 82

REASON 4 – SENSORY DECLINE:
• It is well established that HEARING and VISION decline as we age
• Longitudinal research proposed that DEFICITS IN SENSORY FUNCTIONING explain age differences in a variety of cognitive abilities (Baltes & Lindenberger, 1997)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
• Jefferies et al. (2015): Older adults less able to selectively focus; but with DOUBLE TIME can match young adult performance
• Tales et al. (2002): Older adults have greater difficulty SHIFTING ATTENTION between objects/locations
• Blanchard-Fields (2007): Older adults use MORE EFFECTIVE STRATEGIES for social/emotional problem solving
• Park & Gutchess (2000): Older adults rarely perform poorer at work; driving may be affected by reaction time/attention declines
• Strough et al. (2003); Meegan & Berg (2002): Older adults LESS LIKELY to DELAY important decisions (e.g., medical care)
• PROCESSING SPEED THEORY – Salthouse (1996, 2004): Nervous system slowing → cognitive decline; given sufficient time, older adults perform as well as young adults
• INHIBITION THEORY – Hasher & Zacks (1988): Difficulty suppressing irrelevant information; evidenced by directed forgetting studies
• Andrés et al. (2004): Older adults recall MORE words from "forget" list than younger adults
• Craik & Bialystok (2006): Working memory becomes less efficient with age
• Schaie (1994): Longitudinal studies show LESS loss than cross-sectional; removing speed effects diminishes declines significantly
• Salthouse & Babcock (1991): Processing speed accounted for ALL BUT 1% of age-related working memory differences (ages 18–82)
• Baltes & Lindenberger (1997): Sensory functioning deficits explain age differences across cognitive abilities
• Garrett (2015): Overall cognitive loss in late adulthood has been EXAGGERATED
• Four reasons cognitive losses are overstated: (1) meaningless tasks, (2) cross-sectional design, (3) lack of practice/opportunity, (4) sensory decline

notes.title: Memory Across the Lifespan | Lifespan Development (Lumen Learning)
notes.body:
MEMORY

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHANGES IN WORKING MEMORY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

- Working memory = the more active, effortful part of our memory system
- Composed of THREE major systems:
  1. PHONOLOGICAL LOOP — maintains information about auditory stimuli
  2. VISUOSPATIAL SKETCHPAD — maintains information about visual stimuli
  3. CENTRAL EXECUTIVE — oversees working memory; allocates resources where needed; monitors whether cognitive strategies are effective
  (Source: Schwartz, 2011)

- The CENTRAL EXECUTIVE is most negatively impacted by age (Schwartz)
- In tasks requiring allocation of attention between different stimuli, OLDER adults fare WORSE than younger adults

- KEY STUDY: Göthe, Oberauer, & Kliegl (2007)
  • Older and younger adults asked to learn TWO tasks simultaneously
  • Young adults eventually managed to learn and perform each task without loss in speed and efficiency (required considerable practice)
  • NONE of the older adults were able to achieve this
  • However: older adults COULD perform at young adult levels if asked to learn each task individually
  • Conclusion: Learning and performing both tasks together was too taxing for the central executive in older adults

- Working memory tasks that do NOT require much input from the central executive:
  • Example: DIGIT SPAN TEST (uses predominantly the phonological loop)
  • Result: Older adults perform ON PAR with young adults (Dixon & Cohen, 2003)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHANGES IN LONG-TERM MEMORY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

- Long-term memory is divided into:
  1. SEMANTIC MEMORY — knowledge of facts
  2. EPISODIC MEMORY — events
  3. IMPLICIT MEMORY — procedural skills, classical conditioning, and priming

- Semantic and episodic memory = part of the EXPLICIT MEMORY system
  • Requires conscious effort to create and retrieve

- Several studies consistently reveal: EPISODIC memory shows GREATER age-related declines than SEMANTIC memory (Schwartz, 2011; Spaniol, Madden, & Voss, 2006)

- WHY episodic memory declines more:
  • Episodic memories may be harder to encode and retrieve because they contain AT LEAST TWO different types of memory: the event ITSELF + when and where the event took place
  • Semantic memories are NOT tied to any particular timeline — only the knowledge needs to be encoded or retrieved (Schwartz, 2011)

- Spaniol et al. (2006) findings:
  • Retrieval of semantic information was considerably FASTER for BOTH younger and older adults than retrieval of episodic information
  • LITTLE DIFFERENCE between the two age groups for semantic memory retrieval
  • Older adults' poorer performance on episodic memory appeared related to: slower processing of information + difficulty of the task
  • As the task became increasingly difficult, the GAP between age groups' performance increased MORE for episodic memory than for semantic memory

- Studies testing GENERAL KNOWLEDGE (semantic memory) — e.g., politics, history (Dixon, Rust, Feltmate, & See, 2007), or VOCABULARY/LEXICAL memory (Dahlgren, 1998):
  • Often find older adults OUTPERFORM younger adults

- HOWEVER: Older adults experience more "BLOCKS" at retrieving information they know
  • They experience MORE TIP-OF-THE-TONGUE (TOT) EVENTS than younger adults (Schwartz, 2011)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
IMPLICIT MEMORY AND PRIMING
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

- Implicit memory:
  • Requires LITTLE conscious effort
  • Often involves SKILLS or more habitual patterns of behavior
  • Shows FEW DECLINES with age

- Many studies assessing implicit memory measure the effects of PRIMING
- PRIMING defined: Changes in behavior as a result of FREQUENT or RECENT experiences

- Example of priming:
  • Shown pictures of food → asked to rate appearance → later asked to complete words such as s_ _ p
  • More likely to write SOUP than SOAP or SHIP
  • The images of food "primed" memory for words connected to food

- Does implicit memory/priming change with age?
  • Answer: Typically "NO" for most older adults (Schacter, Church, & Osowiecki, 1994)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PROSPECTIVE MEMORY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

- PROSPECTIVE MEMORY defined: Remembering things we need to do in the FUTURE
  • Examples: remembering a doctor's appointment next week, remembering to take medication before bedtime

- Described as "the flip-side of episodic memory" (Schwartz, 2011, p. 119)
  • Episodic memories = recall of events in our PAST
  • Prospective memories = focus on events in our FUTURE

- Humans are fairly good at prospective memory if they have LITTLE ELSE TO DO in the meantime
- When COMPETING tasks demand attention → prospective memory rapidly DECLINES
  • Explanation: This form of memory draws on the CENTRAL EXECUTIVE of working memory; when central executive is absorbed in other tasks, remembering to do something in the future is more likely to slip (Schwartz, 2011)

- TWO types of prospective memory:
  1. TIME-BASED prospective memories — remembering to do something at a FUTURE TIME
  2. EVENT-BASED prospective memories — remembering to do something when a CERTAIN EVENT OCCURS

- Age-related declines are MORE LIKELY to be:
  • TIME-BASED (rather than event-based)
  • Found in LABORATORY SETTINGS (rather than real-world settings)

- In REAL-WORLD settings:
  • Older adults can show COMPARABLE or SLIGHTLY BETTER prospective memory performance (Henry, MacLeod, Phillips & Crawford, 2004; Luo & Craik, 2008)

- WHY this makes sense:
  • Older adults tend to be MORE SELECTIVE in where they place physical, mental, and social energy
  • Remembering a doctor's appointment is of GREATER CONCERN than remembering to hit a space-bar on a computer every time "tiger" is displayed (lab task example)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
RECALL VERSUS RECOGNITION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

- Memory performance often depends on whether older adults are asked to:
  • RECOGNIZE previously learned material, OR
  • RECALL material on their own

- RECOGNITION tasks are EASIER for all humans — require LESS cognitive energy
- Older adults show ROUGHLY EQUIVALENT memory to young adults when assessed with a RECOGNITION task (Rhodes, Castel, & Jacoby, 2008)

- With RECALL measures:
  • Older adults show MEMORY DEFICITS compared to younger adults
  • The effect is initially NOT that large
  • Starting at AGE 40 — adults begin to show declines in recall memory compared to younger adults (Schwartz, 2011)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
THE AGE ADVANTAGE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

- FEWER age differences are observed when:
  • Memory CUES are available (e.g., recognition memory tasks)
  • Individuals can draw upon ACQUIRED KNOWLEDGE or EXPERIENCE

- Older adults often perform AS WELL IF NOT BETTER than young adults on:
  • Tests of WORD KNOWLEDGE or VOCABULARY

- With age often comes EXPERTISE — research points to areas where aging experts perform quite well:

  1. OLDER TYPISTS (Salthouse, 1984):
     • Compensated for age-related declines in SPEED by looking FARTHER AHEAD at printed text

  2. OLDER CHESS EXPERTS (Charness, 1981):
     • Focus on a SMALLER SET of possible moves compared to younger players
     • Leads to GREATER COGNITIVE EFFICIENCY

  3. EVERYDAY TASK KNOWLEDGE (Tentori, Osheron, Hasher, & May, 2001):
     • Accrued knowledge of everyday tasks (e.g., GROCERY PRICES) helps older adults make BETTER DECISIONS than young adults

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ KEY EXAM POINTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1. The THREE components of working memory: phonological loop, visuospatial sketchpad, central executive — the CENTRAL EXECUTIVE is MOST negatively impacted by age (Schwartz, 2011)
2. Göthe, Oberauer, & Kliegl (2007): Older adults COULD NOT learn two tasks simultaneously but COULD learn each task individually — central executive overloaded
3. Digit span test (phonological loop) — older adults perform ON PAR with younger adults; no central executive demand
4. EPISODIC memory declines MORE with age than SEMANTIC memory (Schwartz, 2011; Spaniol et al., 2006)
5. Reason episodic memory is harder: encodes BOTH the event AND time/place — dual encoding burden
6. Semantic memory: older adults often OUTPERFORM younger adults on general knowledge, vocabulary (Dixon et al., 2007; Dahlgren, 1998)
7. Older adults experience MORE TIP-OF-THE-TONGUE (TOT) events than younger adults
8. IMPLICIT MEMORY (priming, procedural skills) shows FEW declines with age (Schacter, Church, & Osowiecki, 1994)
9. Prospective memory = "flip-side of episodic memory" (Schwartz, 2011, p. 119) — age declines more for TIME-BASED and LAB settings, not real-world
10. RECOGNITION memory — older adults perform EQUIVALENTLY to young adults (Rhodes, Castel, & Jacoby, 2008)
11. RECALL memory declines begin at AGE 40 (Schwartz, 2011)
12. Age ADVANTAGES: older typists look farther ahead (Salthouse, 1984); older chess experts are more cognitively efficient (Charness, 1981); older adults make better decisions about grocery prices (Tentori et al., 2001)
13. Fewer age differences when memory CUES are available or when expertise/experience can be applied

notes.title: How Does Aging Affect Information Processing? | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
HOW DOES AGING AFFECT INFORMATION PROCESSING?
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Numerous stereotypes exist regarding older adults as being forgetful and confused
- Research on memory and cognition in late adulthood reveals a more nuanced picture
- Memory comes in MANY types:
  • Working memory
  • Episodic memory
  • Semantic memory
  • Implicit memory
  • Prospective memory
- There are MANY PROCESSES involved in memory
- It should NOT be a surprise that:
  • There are DECLINES in some types of memory and memory processes
  • Other areas of memory are MAINTAINED with age
  • Some areas of memory may even show IMPROVEMENT with age

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
TOPICS COVERED IN THIS SECTION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Changes in MEMORY
- Changes in ATTENTION
- Changes in PROBLEM SOLVING
- Changes in INTELLIGENCE
- Changes in WISDOM
- The EXAGGERATION OF LOSSES stereotyped in the elderly
  • Focus includes examination of how stereotypes overstate or misrepresent actual cognitive decline

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ KEY EXAM POINTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ Not all memory declines with age — some types are maintained or improve
★ Five key types of memory: working, episodic, semantic, implicit, and prospective
★ Cognitive aging affects memory, attention, problem solving, intelligence, AND wisdom
★ Stereotypes of older adults as universally forgetful and confused are EXAGGERATED and not fully supported by research
★ The section emphasizes a NUANCED view: decline in some areas, stability or growth in others

notes.title: Sexuality in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
SEXUALITY IN LATE ADULTHOOD
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

AGEIST STEREOTYPES ABOUT OLDER ADULTS AND SEXUALITY
- According to Kane (2008), older men and women are often viewed as GENDERLESS and ASEXUAL
- There is a stereotype that elderly individuals NO LONGER engage in sexual activity
- When older adults DO engage in sexual activity, they are perceived to have committed some kind of OFFENSE
- These ageist myths can become INTERNALIZED, making it harder for older people to accept their own sexuality (Gosney, 2011)
- Additionally, some older women indicate they no longer worry about sexual concerns once they are PAST THE CHILD BEARING YEARS

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

THE REALITY OF SEXUAL ACTIVITY IN OLDER ADULTS
- In reality, many older couples find GREATER SATISFACTION in their sex life than when they were younger
- Reasons for greater satisfaction include:
  • Fewer distractions
  • More time and privacy
  • No worries about getting pregnant
  • Greater intimacy with a lifelong partner (NIA, 2013)
- National Social Life Health, and Aging Project results (Karraker, DeLamater, & Schwarz, 2011):
  • 72% of MEN aged 52–72 reported being sexually active
  • 45.5% of WOMEN aged 52–72 reported being sexually active
- National Survey of Sexual Health data (Schick et al., 2010):
  • 20%–30% of individuals remain sexually active well into their 80s

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

CAUSES OF SEXUAL PROBLEMS IN OLDER ADULTS
- According to the National Institute on Aging (NIA, 2013), chronic illnesses can adversely affect sexual functioning:
  • ARTHRITIS → joint pain
  • DIABETES → erectile dysfunction
  • HEART DISEASE → difficulty achieving orgasm for BOTH sexes
  • STROKE → paralysis
  • DEMENTIA → inappropriate sexual behavior
- Additional factors that can affect sexual ability and enjoyment:
  • Hormonal changes
  • Physical disabilities
  • Surgeries
  • Medicines
- Psychological/emotional factors:
  • A woman unhappy about her appearance as she ages may think her partner will no longer find her attractive
  • A societal focus on youthful physical beauty for women may get in the way of her enjoyment of sex
  • Most men have a problem with ERECTILE DYSFUNCTION (ED) once in a while
  • Some men may fear that ED will become a MORE COMMON problem as they age
- For heterosexual couples, if there is a DECLINE IN SEXUAL ACTIVITY, it is typically due to a DECLINE IN THE MALE'S PHYSICAL HEALTH (Erber & Szuchman, 2015)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

FACTORS AFFECTING PARTNER AVAILABILITY FOR OLDER WOMEN
- The BEST way to experience a healthy sex life in later life is to KEEP SEXUALLY ACTIVE while aging
- However, lack of an available PARTNER can affect heterosexual women's participation in sexual relationships
- Population gender ratio statistics:
  • Beginning at AGE 40: more women than men in the population
  • At AGE 85: ratio becomes 2 TO 1 (women to men) (Karraker et al., 2011)
- Older men tend to PAIR WITH YOUNGER WOMEN when they become widowed or divorced → decreases the pool of available men for older women (Erber & Szuchman, 2015)
- Change in MARITAL STATUS effects (Karraker et al., 2011):
  • Does NOT result in a decline in sexual behavior of MEN aged 57–85
  • DOES result in a decline in sexual behavior for similar-aged WOMEN

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

CONCLUDING THOUGHTS: QUALITY OF LIFE IN LATE ADULTHOOD
- Key players in improving quality of life among older adults will be THOSE ADULTS THEMSELVES
- Behaviors that promote longer, more active lives (He et al., 2005):
  • Exercising
  • Reducing stress
  • Stopping smoking
  • Limiting use of alcohol
  • Consuming more fruits and vegetables
- Stress reduction — both in LATE ADULTHOOD and EARLIER IN LIFE — is also crucial
- Reduction of SOCIETAL STRESSORS can promote ACTIVE LIFE EXPECTANCY
- Trends in the last 40 years:
  • SMOKING RATES have DECREASED
  • OBESITY has INCREASED
  • PHYSICAL ACTIVITY has only MODESTLY INCREASED

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

LEARNING OBJECTIVES: COGNITIVE DEVELOPMENT IN LATE ADULTHOOD
(Topics to be covered in subsequent sections)
- Describe how MEMORY CHANGES for those in late adulthood
- Describe the THEORIES for why memory changes occur
- Describe how COGNITIVE LOSSES in late adulthood are exaggerated
- Explain the PRAGMATICS AND MECHANICS of intelligence
- Define what is a NEUROCOGNITIVE DISORDER
- Explain ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE and other neurocognitive disorders
- Describe WORK AND RETIREMENT in late adulthood
- Explain how those in late adulthood use STRATEGIES TO COMPENSATE FOR LOSSES

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- Kane (2008): older adults viewed as genderless and asexual — an ageist myth that can become internalized (Gosney, 2011)
- NIA (2013): older couples may find GREATER sexual satisfaction due to fewer distractions, more privacy, no pregnancy concerns, greater intimacy
- STAT: 72% of men and 45.5% of women aged 52–72 are sexually active (Karraker et al., 2011)
- STAT: 20%–30% remain sexually active into their 80s (Schick et al., 2010)
- Chronic illness effects on sex: arthritis=pain, diabetes=ED, heart disease=orgasm difficulty, stroke=paralysis, dementia=inappropriate behavior (NIA, 2013)
- Decline in sexual activity in heterosexual couples → typically due to MALE's physical health decline (Erber & Szuchman, 2015)
- At age 85: gender ratio is 2:1 women to men (Karraker et al., 2011)
- Marital status change affects WOMEN's sexual behavior but NOT men's (aged 57–85) (Karraker et al., 2011)
- He et al. (2005): exercise, stress reduction, no smoking, limit alcohol, eat fruits/vegetables = longer, more active lives
- Last 40 years: smoking ↓, obesity ↑, physical activity only modestly ↑

notes.title: Brain Functioning – Lifespan Development (Lumen Learning)
notes.body:
BRAIN FUNCTIONING
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

BRAIN CHANGES WITH AGE
- The brain loses 5% to 10% of its weight between ages 20 and 90 (Fjell & Walhovd, 2010)
- Decrease in brain volume is due to:
  • Shrinkage of neurons
  • Lower number of synapses
  • Shorter length of axons
- Normal cognitive decline across the lifespan is associated with (Garrett, 2015):
  • Reduced activity of genes involved in memory storage
  • Synaptic pruning
  • Reduced plasticity
  • Changes in glutamate and GABA (neurotransmitter) receptors
- Loss in white matter connections between brain areas
- Without myelin: neurons show slower conduction and impede each other's actions
- Loss of synapses in specific brain areas:
  • Hippocampus (involved in memory)
  • Basal forebrain region
- Older individuals activate LARGER regions of their attentional and executive networks (parietal and prefrontal cortex) when performing complex tasks
- This increased activation correlates with REDUCED performance on:
  • Executive tasks
  • Tests of working memory
  (compared to younger individuals; Kolb & Whishaw, 2011)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

BRAIN PLASTICITY AND COMPENSATION
- Despite age-related changes, the brain exhibits CONSIDERABLE PLASTICITY
- Through practice and training, the brain can compensate for age-related changes (Erber & Szuchman, 2015)

SCAFFOLDING THEORY OF AGING AND COGNITION
- Proposed by: Park and Reuter-Lorenz (2009)
- States: The brain adapts to neural atrophy (dying of brain cells) by building ALTERNATIVE CONNECTIONS, referred to as SCAFFOLDING
- Scaffolding allows older brains to retain high levels of performance
- Brain compensation is especially noted in the additional neural effort shown by those aging well
- Example (Cabeza, Anderson, Locantore, & McIntosh, 2002):
  • Older adults who performed JUST AS WELL as younger adults on a memory task used BOTH prefrontal areas
  • Younger participants used only the RIGHT prefrontal cortex
  • This DECREASE IN BRAIN LATERALIZATION appears to assist older adults with cognitive skills

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

CAN WE IMPROVE BRAIN FUNCTIONING?

ACTIVE STUDY (Advanced Cognitive Training for Independent and Vital Elderly)
- Conducted: 1999 to 2001
- Sample: 2,802 individuals aged 65 to 94
- Sample was racially diverse
- Participants received:
  • 10 group training sessions
  • 4 follow-up sessions
  • Tasks focused on: memory, reasoning, and speed of processing
- Results: Mental workouts improved cognitive functioning even 5 YEARS LATER
- Many participants believed improvement was seen in EVERYDAY TASKS as well (Tennstedt et al., 2006)

LIMITATIONS OF BRAIN TRAINING PROGRAMS
- Programs for the elderly on memory, reading, and processing speed training show:
  • Improvement on the SPECIFIC TASKS TRAINED
  • NO GENERALIZATION to other abilities (Jarrett, 2015)
- These programs have NOT been shown to delay or slow the progression of ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE
- Although not harmful, the most effective ways to train the brain remain (p. 207):
  1. Physical exercise
  2. Learning new skills
  3. Socializing
- These activities appear to BUILD A RESERVE to minimize the effects of primary aging of the brain

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

PARKINSON'S DISEASE

SYMPTOMS
- Motor tremors
- Loss of balance
- Poor coordination
- Rigidity
- Difficulty moving
(Garrett, 2015)

EPIDEMIOLOGY
- Affects approximately 1% of those over age 60
- Appears more frequently in family members in a little less than 10% of cases
- 28 chromosomal areas have been implicated

ENVIRONMENTAL FACTORS
- Brain injury:
  • Being knocked unconscious ONCE increases risk by 32%
  • Being knocked out SEVERAL TIMES increases risk by 174% (Garrett, 2015)
- Other environmental influences (Olanow & Tatton, 1999):
  • Toxins
  • Industrial chemicals
  • Carbon monoxide
  • Herbicides and pesticides

NEUROLOGICAL BASIS
- Symptoms are due to deterioration of the SUBSTANTIA NIGRA
  • Located in the midbrain
  • Neurons send dopamine-releasing axons to the BASAL GANGLIA
  • Basal ganglia affects motor activity

TREATMENT
- Medication: LEVODOPA (L-DOPA)
  • Crosses the blood-brain barrier
  • Converted into dopamine in the brain
- DEEP BRAIN STIMULATION:
  • Involves inserting an electrode into the brain
  • Provides electrical stimulation
  • Has resulted in improved motor functioning (Garrett, 2015)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SLEEP IN OLDER ADULTS

GENERAL SLEEP PATTERNS
- Older adults need between 7 to 9 hours of sleep per night (same as other adults)
- Tend to go to sleep EARLIER and get up EARLIER than younger people
- This pattern is called ADVANCED SLEEP PHASE SYNDROME
  • Based on changes in CIRCADIAN RHYTHMS (National Sleep Foundation, 2009)

INSOMNIA
- Most common sleep problem in those 60 and older (NIA, 2016)
- Characterized by trouble falling asleep and staying asleep
- Causes in older people include:
  • Certain medications
  • Being in pain
  • Medical or psychiatric conditions
  • Worrying about not being able to sleep (pre-sleep anxiety)
- Over-the-counter sleep aids or medication may only work short-term
- Sleep problems should be discussed with a health care professional

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SLEEP DISORDERS IN OLDER ADULTS (NIA, 2016)

SLEEP APNEA
- Repeated short pauses in breathing while asleep
- Can lead to reduced oxygen in the blood
- Snoring is a common symptom; worsens with age
- Untreated sleep apnea can lead to:
  • Impaired daytime functioning
  • High blood pressure
  • Headaches
  • Stroke
  • Memory loss

RESTLESS LEGS SYNDROME
- Feels like tingling, crawling, or pins and needles in one or both legs
- This feeling is worse at night

PERIODIC LIMB MOVEMENT DISORDER
- Causes people to jerk and kick their legs every 20 to 40 seconds during sleep

RAPID EYE MOVEMENT (REM) SLEEP BEHAVIOR DISORDER
- Occurs when muscles CAN MOVE during REM sleep
- Sleep is disrupted

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

MEDICAL CONDITIONS THAT AFFECT SLEEP
- According to National Sleep Foundation (2009):
  • Gastroesophageal reflux disease
  • Diabetes mellitus
  • Renal failure
  • Respiratory diseases (e.g., asthma)
  • Immune disorders
  • Parkinson's disease
  • Multiple sclerosis
- ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE and sleep:
  • Individuals may wake up many times during the night
  • May wander when up
  • May yell — altering total sleep time

CONSEQUENCES OF SLEEP PROBLEMS IN OLDER ADULTS (Buman, 2013)
- Increased risk of accidents
- Falls
- Chronic fatigue
- Decreased quality of life
- Cognitive decline
- Reduced immune function
- Depression

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

EXERCISE AND SLEEP QUALITY IN OLDER ADULTS
- Research examined whether exercise can improve sleep quality in late adulthood
- Results: 150 MINUTES PER WEEK of exercise can improve sleep quality (Buman, 2013)
- This amount of exercise also lowers risk for:
  • Heart disease
  • Diabetes
  • Some cancers
- Recommended types of activity:
  • Aerobic activity
  • Weight training
  • Balance programs
- For those in ASSISTED LIVING FACILITIES:
  • Even LIGHT EXERCISE (stretching and short walks) can improve sleep
  • High intensity activity is NOT necessary
- Effects of exercise on sleep may be EVEN LARGER for older adults because their baseline sleep quality may not be ideal

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
1. Brain loses 5%–10% of its weight between ages 20 and 90 (Fjell & Walhovd, 2010); due to neuron shrinkage, fewer synapses, shorter axons
2. Cognitive decline linked to reduced gene activity for memory storage, synaptic pruning, plasticity, and glutamate/GABA receptor changes (Garrett, 2015)
3. Loss of myelin → slower neural conduction; synaptic loss occurs in hippocampus and basal forebrain
4. Older adults activate LARGER prefrontal/parietal networks for complex tasks → correlates with reduced performance (Kolb & Whishaw, 2011)
5. SCAFFOLDING THEORY (Park & Reuter-Lorenz, 2009): brain builds alternative connections (scaffolding) to compensate for neural atrophy
6. Cabeza et al. (2002): aging-well older adults use BOTH prefrontal areas (vs. right only in young) → decreased lateralization aids cognition
7. ACTIVE Study: 2,802 adults, ages 65–94, 1999–2001; improvements in memory, reasoning, speed of processing lasted 5 years; but NO generalization to untrained tasks
8. Best brain training: physical exercise, learning new skills, socializing — NOT commercial brain training programs
9. PARKINSON'S: due to substantia nigra degeneration → loss of dopamine → basal ganglia affected; treated with L-dopa and deep brain stimulation
10. Parkinson's risk: 1% over age 60; concussion once = +32% risk; multiple times = +174% risk; 28 chromosomal areas implicated
11. ADVANCED SLEEP PHASE SYNDROME: older adults sleep/wake earlier due to circadian rhythm changes
12. INSOMNIA: most common sleep problem in those 60+ (NIA, 2016)
13. Sleep disorders in older adults: sleep apnea, restless legs syndrome, periodic limb movement disorder, REM sleep behavior disorder
14. Untreated sleep apnea → high BP, stroke, memory loss, headaches, impaired daytime functioning
15. 150 minutes/week of exercise improves sleep quality in older adults (Buman, 2013); even light exercise helps those in assisted living

notes.title: Sensory Changes in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
SENSORY CHANGES IN LATE ADULTHOOD
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

OVERVIEW
- In late adulthood, ALL senses show signs of decline, especially among the oldest-old
- Changes that began in middle adulthood (presbyopia, dry eyes, difficulty in dim light) become much more common
- Three serious eye diseases are more common in older adults: Cataracts, Macular Degeneration, and Glaucoma
- Only cataracts can be effectively cured in most people

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

VISION

CATARACTS
- Definition: A clouding of the lens of the eye
- The lens is made up of mostly water and protein; protein is precisely arranged to keep the lens clear
- With age, some protein starts to clump; as more protein clumps together, clarity of the lens is reduced
- In middle adulthood: some cloudiness may occur, but area affected is usually too small to interfere with vision
- After age 60: more people have problems with cataracts (NIH, 2014b)
- By age 75: 70% of adults will have problems with cataracts (Boyd, 2014)
- Cataracts also cause discoloration of the lens — tinting it more yellow, then brown
  - This interferes with ability to distinguish colors such as: black, brown, dark blue, dark purple

Risk Factors for Cataracts:
- Age (primary factor)
- Certain health problems: diabetes, high blood pressure, obesity
- Behavioral factors: smoking
- Environmental factors: prolonged exposure to ultraviolet sunlight
- Previous trauma to the eye
- Long-term use of steroid medication
- Family history of cataracts
- Sources: NEI, 2016a; Boyd, 2014

Treatment of Cataracts:
- Treated by removing and replacing the lens with a synthetic lens
- In developed countries (e.g., United States): easily treated with surgery
- In developing countries: access to operations is limited
  - Cataracts are the leading cause of blindness in late adulthood in Third World nations (Resnikoff, Pascolini, Mariotti & Pokharel, 2004)
  - In areas with limited medical treatment: more than 990 out of 100,000 people have a shortened lifespan due to disability caused by cataracts (darkest red areas on Figure 9.15 map)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

AGE-RELATED MACULAR DEGENERATION
- Definition: Loss of clarity in the center field of vision due to deterioration of the macula (center of the retina)
- Does NOT usually cause total vision loss
- Loss of central field of vision can greatly impair day-to-day functioning

Two Types:
1. DRY TYPE (most common form)
   - Occurs when tiny pieces of a fatty protein called drusen form beneath the retina
   - Eventually the macula becomes thinner and stops working properly (Boyd, 2016)
2. WET TYPE (about 10% of cases)
   - Causes more damage to central field of vision than dry form
   - Caused by abnormal development of blood vessels beneath the retina
   - These vessels may leak fluid or blood, causing more rapid loss of vision than dry form

Risk Factors for Macular Degeneration:
- Smoking — doubles your risk (NIH, 2015a)
- Race — more common among Caucasians than African Americans or Hispanics/Latinos
- High cholesterol
- Family history of macular degeneration (Boyd, 2016)
- At least 20 different genes have been related to this eye disease
- No simple genetic test to determine risk, despite claims by some genetic testing companies (NIH, 2015a)

Treatment of Macular Degeneration:
- DRY TYPE: No effective treatment currently exists
  - Some research suggests certain patients may benefit from a cocktail of antioxidant vitamins and minerals — but results are mixed at best
  - Not a cure; will not restore lost vision
  - This cocktail can slow progression of visual loss in some people (Boyd, 2016; NIH, 2015a)
- WET TYPE:
  - Medications that slow the growth of abnormal blood vessels
  - Surgery such as laser treatment to destroy abnormal blood vessels
  - Only 25% of those with the wet version may see improvement with these procedures (Boyd, 2016)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

GLAUCOMA
- Definition: Loss of peripheral vision, frequently due to a buildup of fluid in the eye that damages the optic nerve
- As age increases, pressure in the eye may increase, causing damage to the optic nerve
- The exterior of the optic nerve receives input from retinal cells on the periphery
- As glaucoma progresses, more and more of the peripheral visual field deteriorates toward the central field of vision
- In advanced stages: person can lose their sight
- Fortunately: Glaucoma tends to progress slowly (NEI, 2016b)
- Glaucoma is the most common cause of blindness in the U.S. (NEI, 2016b)

Risk Factors for Glaucoma:
- African Americans over age 40
- Everyone else over age 60
- Diabetes
- Family history of glaucoma
- Source: Owsley et al., 2015

Treatment/Management of Glaucoma:
- No cure for glaucoma
- Rate of progression can be slowed, especially with early diagnosis
- Routine eye exams to measure eye pressure and examination of the optic nerve can detect both risk and presence of glaucoma (NEI, 2016b)
- Those with elevated eye pressure are given medicated eye drops
- Reducing eye pressure lowers risk of developing glaucoma or slows its progression

NOTE: Figure 9.16 illustrates Normal Vision vs. Cataracts, Macular Degeneration, and Glaucoma side by side for comparison

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

HEARING
- Hearing declines in both:
  1. Frequencies of sound detectable
  2. Intensity of sound needed to hear
- These changes continue and worsen in late adulthood
- Almost 1 in 4 adults aged 65 to 74 have disabling hearing loss
- Almost 1 in 2 adults aged 75 and older have disabling hearing loss
- Source: NIH, 2016
- Table 9.4 lists common signs of hearing loss

PRESBYCUSIS
- Definition: A common form of hearing loss in late adulthood resulting in a gradual loss of hearing
- Runs in families
- Affects hearing in BOTH ears (NIA, 2015c)

TINNITUS
- Definition: A ringing, hissing, or roaring sound in the ears
- Exact cause is unknown
- Can be related to hypertension and allergies
- May come and go, or persist and get worse over time (NIA, 2015c)
- Incidence of both presbycusis and tinnitus increase with age
- Males have higher rates of both around the world (McCormak, Edmondson-Jones, Somerset, & Hall, 2016)

BALANCE AND THE INNER EAR
- Auditory system has TWO jobs:
  1. Help you hear
  2. Help you maintain balance
- Balance is controlled by the brain receiving information from the shifting of hair cells in the inner ear about position and orientation of the body
- With age, this function of the inner ear declines
- Can lead to problems with balance when sitting, standing, or moving (Martin, 2014)
- NOTE: Table 9.5 is referenced in this section

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

TASTE AND SMELL

TASTE (GUSTATION)
- Taste and smell are part of our chemical sensing system
- Our sense of taste appears to age WELL compared to other senses
- Normal taste occurs when molecules released by chewing food stimulate taste buds along:
  - The tongue
  - The roof of the mouth
  - The lining of the throat
- These cells send messages to the brain where specific tastes are identified
- After age 50: we begin to lose some of these sensory cells
- Most people do not notice changes in taste until their 60s (NIH: Senior Health, 2016b)
- Loss of taste buds is very gradual
- In late adulthood, many people are surprised that their apparent loss of taste is most likely the result of a loss of SMELL rather than taste

SMELL (OLFACTION)
- Our sense of smell decreases MORE with age than taste
- Problems with smell are more common in men than women
- Almost 1 in 4 males in their 60s have a disorder with the sense of smell
- Only 1 in 10 women in their 60s have a disorder with the sense of smell (NIH: Senior Health, 2016b)
- Loss of smell due to aging is called: PRESBYOSMIA
- Olfactory cells are located in a small area high in the nasal cavity
- Stimulated by TWO pathways:
  1. When we inhale through the nose
  2. Via the connection between the nose and the throat when we chew and digest food
- The SECOND pathway explains why foods like chocolate or coffee seem tasteless when we have a head cold
- Total loss of smell = ANOSMIA — extremely rare

Health Implications of Smell/Taste Loss:
- Problems with chemical senses can be linked to serious medical conditions:
  - Parkinson's disease
  - Alzheimer's disease
  - Multiple sclerosis
  - Source: NIH: Senior Health, 2016a
- Any sudden change in taste or smell should be checked out
- Loss of smell can change a person's diet in two ways:
  1. Loss of enjoyment of food → eating too little → poor/unbalanced nutrition
  2. Adding sugar and salt to foods that are becoming blander → unhealthy dietary changes

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

TOUCH
- With age, people may experience reduced or changed sensations of:
  - Vibration
  - Cold
  - Heat
  - Pressure
  - Pain
  - Source: Martin, 2014
- Many of these changes are aligned with medical conditions more common among the elderly (e.g., diabetes)
- However, changes in touch sensations also occur among HEALTHY older adults

Specific Findings:
- Ability to detect changes in pressure declines with age
  - More pronounced by the 6th decade (60s)
  - Diminishes further with advanced age (Bowden & McNelty, 2013)
  - Yet considerable variability exists: almost 40% show sensitivity comparable to younger adults (Thornbury & Mistretta, 1981)
- Ability to detect roughness/smoothness or hardness/softness of an object shows NO appreciable change with age (Bowden & McNulty, 2013)
- Those showing increasing insensitivity to pressure, temperature, or pain are at risk for injury (Martin, 2014)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

PAIN

Prevalence:
- Approximately 60%–75% of people over the age of 65 report at least some chronic pain (Molton & Terrill, 2014)
- Rate is even higher for those living in nursing homes
- Although presence of pain increases with age, older adults are LESS SENSITIVE to pain than younger adults (Harkins, Price, & Martinelli, 1986)

Neurological Basis:
- Farrell (2012) examined research using neuroimaging techniques in both healthy older people and those experiencing painful disorders
- Results: Age-related decreases in brain volume in structures involved in pain
- Especially noteworthy changes in:
  - Prefrontal cortex
  - Brainstem
  - Hippocampus

Gender Differences in Pain:
- Women are more likely to identify feeling pain than men (Tsang et al., 2008)
- Women have FEWER opioid receptors in the brain
- Women also receive LESS relief from opiate drugs (Garrett, 2015)

Concern About Decreased Pain Sensitivity:
- Pain serves as an important indicator that something is wrong
- Decreased sensitivity to pain in older adults can conceal illnesses or injuries requiring medical attention

Causes of Chronic Pain in Older Adults:
- Arthritis
- Cancer
- Diabetes
- Joint pain
- Sciatica
- Shingles
- Source: Molton & Terrill, 2014

Cancer and Pain — Special Concern:
- "Breakthrough pain": a severe pain that comes on quickly while a patient is already medicated with a long-acting painkiller
- Can be very upsetting; after one attack many people worry it will happen again

Concerns About Pain Medication:
- Some older individuals worry about developing an addiction to pain medication
- If medicine is taken exactly as prescribed, addiction should NOT be a concern (NIH, 2015b)
