- Side effects from pain medicine may include:
  - Constipation
  - Dry mouth
  - Drowsiness
  - These can adversely affect the elder's quality of life

Barriers to Treatment:
- Some older individuals put off going to the doctor because they think pain is just part of aging and nothing can help — THIS IS NOT TRUE
- Managing pain is crucial to ensure feelings of well-being for the older adult

Consequences of Unmanaged Chronic Pain:
- Individuals will restrict movement for fear of feeling pain or injuring themselves further
- Lack of activity leads to:
  - More restriction
  - Further decreased participation
  - Greater disability (Jensen, Moore, Bockow, Ehde, & Engel, 2011)
- Decline in physical activity due to pain is also associated with weight gain and obesity (Strine, Hootman, Chapman, Okoro, & Balluz, 2005)
- Sleep disorders can occur
- Mood disorders, such as depression, can also occur (Molton & Terrill, 2014)

Management Recommendations:
- Learning to cope effectively with pain is an important consideration in late adulthood
- Working with one's primary physician or a pain specialist is recommended (NIH, 2015b)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS

1. THREE major eye diseases of late adulthood: Cataracts, Macular Degeneration, Glaucoma — only CATARACTS can be effectively cured.
2. By age 75, 70% of adults will have cataracts (Boyd, 2014); cataracts are the leading cause of blindness in Third World nations.
3. Cataracts: Protein clumping in the lens; lens discoloration (yellow → brown); risk factors include diabetes, smoking, UV exposure, steroids.
4. Macular degeneration: Dry (most common, drusen protein deposits) vs. Wet (10%, abnormal blood vessels); smoking DOUBLES risk; no effective treatment for dry type; only 25% improve with wet type treatment.
5. Glaucoma: Loss of PERIPHERAL vision; buildup of fluid damages optic nerve; most common cause of blindness in the U.S.; no cure but slowed with eye drops and early diagnosis.
6. African Americans over 40 and everyone else over 60 have higher glaucoma risk.
7. Hearing: 1 in 4 adults aged 65–74 and 1 in 2 aged 75+ have disabling hearing loss; presbycusis = gradual hearing loss, both ears, runs in families.
8. Tinnitus: Ringing/hissing/roaring in ears; linked to hypertension and allergies; males have higher rates worldwide.
9. Balance is controlled by hair cells in the inner ear; inner ear function declines with age → balance problems.
10. Taste (gustation) ages WELL; most changes not noticed until 60s; apparent taste loss is usually due to loss of SMELL.
11. Smell (olfaction) declines more than taste; presbyosmia = age-related smell loss; 1 in 4 men vs. 1 in 10 women in their 60s affected; total loss of smell (anosmia) is extremely rare.
12. Problems with chemical senses can signal Parkinson's, Alzheimer's, or multiple sclerosis.
13. Touch: Pressure detection declines (especially by 6th decade); roughness/smoothness/hardness/softness detection does NOT change appreciably; ~40% of older adults retain sensitivity comparable to young adults.
14. Pain: 60%–75% of those over 65 report chronic pain; older adults are LESS sensitive to pain; brain volume decreases in prefrontal cortex, brainstem, hippocampus (Farrell, 2012).
15. Women: More likely to report pain, fewer opioid receptors, less relief from opiate drugs.
16. Unmanaged chronic pain leads to movement restriction → decreased activity → greater disability, weight gain/obesity, depression, sleep disorders.
17. Pain addiction concern: If medication is taken as prescribed, addiction should NOT be a concern.
18. Causes of chronic pain in older adults: Arthritis, cancer, diabetes, joint pain, sciatica, shingles.
19. Breakthrough pain = severe pain that comes on quickly even while already medicated with long-acting painkillers — a special cancer concern.

notes.title: Physical Changes of Aging | Lifespan Development (Lumen Learning)
notes.body:
PHYSICAL CHANGES OF AGING
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

BALTIMORE LONGITUDINAL STUDY ON AGING (BLSA)
- Source: NIA, 2011b
- Began: 1958
- Sample: Traced aging process in 1,400 people from age 20 to 90
- Key finding: Aging process varies significantly from individual to individual AND from one organ system to another
- Key generalizations from BLSA:
  • Heart muscles thicken with age
  • Arteries become less flexible
  • Lung capacity diminishes
  • Kidneys become less efficient in removing waste from the blood
  • Bladder loses its ability to store urine
  • Brain cells lose some functioning, BUT new neurons can also be produced
- Determinants of these changes: genetics, lifestyle, and disease

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

BODY CHANGES
- Source: National Library of Medicine, 2014
- Everyone's body shape changes naturally as they age
- After age 30: people tend to lose lean tissue
- Cells of muscles, liver, kidney, and other organs are lost
- Tissue loss reduces the amount of water in the body
- Bones may lose some of their minerals and become less dense:
  • Early stage = OSTEOPENIA
  • Later stage = OSTEOPOROSIS
- Body fat increases steadily after age 30
- Older individuals may have almost ONE THIRD MORE FAT compared to when they were younger
- Fat tissue builds up toward the CENTER of the body, including around internal organs

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SKIN AND HAIR
- Source: NIA, 2015f

SKIN CHANGES:
- Becomes thinner
- Becomes less elastic
- Loses fat
- No longer looks plump and smooth
- Veins and bones can be seen more easily
- Scratches, cuts, and bumps take longer to heal
- Years of sun exposure may lead to: wrinkles, dryness, age spots, and cancer
- Older people may bruise more easily
- Bruises take longer to heal
- Some medicines or illnesses may also cause bruising
- Gravity causes skin to sag and wrinkle
- Smoking can wrinkle the skin

AGE SPOTS:
- Previously called 'liver spots'
- Appear as flat, brown spots
- Often caused by years of sun exposure
- Harmless

SKIN TAGS:
- Small, usually flesh-colored growths of skin
- Have a raised surface
- Become common as people age, especially for women
- Harmless

HAIR CHANGES:
- Nearly everyone has hair loss as they age
- Rate of hair growth slows down
- Many hair follicles stop producing new hairs
- Loss of pigment and graying (begun in middle adulthood) continues into late adulthood

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SARCOPENIA
- Source: WebMD, 2016
- Definition: The loss of muscle tissue as a natural part of aging
- Most noticeable in: MEN
- Rate of muscle loss:
  • Physically inactive people can lose 3% to 5% of muscle mass each decade after age 30
  • Even active individuals experience some muscle loss
- Symptoms:
  • Loss of stamina
  • Weakness
  • Decreased physical activity → further muscle shrinkage (cycle)
- Typical onset/acceleration:
  • Typically happens faster around age 75
  • May speed up as early as age 65 or as late as age 80
- Factors involved in sarcopenia:
  1. Reduction in nerve cells responsible for sending signals from the brain to muscles to begin moving
  2. Decrease in the ability to turn protein into energy
  3. Not receiving enough calories or protein to sustain adequate muscle mass
- Consequences of muscle loss:
  • Lessens strength and mobility
  • Factor in frailty
  • Increases likelihood of falls and fractures in older adults
- Importance of maintaining strong muscles:
  • Strong leg and heart muscles are important for independence
- Prevention/treatment:
  • Weight-lifting
  • Walking
  • Swimming
  • Other cardiovascular exercises
  • These can help strengthen muscles and prevent atrophy

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

HEIGHT AND WEIGHT
- Source: National Library of Medicine, 2014
- Referenced figure: Figure 9.14 Bone Growth Changes

HEIGHT:
- Becoming shorter with age occurs among ALL races and BOTH sexes
- Height loss related to aging changes in the bones, muscles, and joints
- Rate of loss: almost ONE-HALF INCH every 10 years after age 40
- Height loss is even MORE RAPID after age 70
- Total height lost with aging: 1 to 3 inches

WEIGHT:
- Changes in body weight vary for men and women
- MEN:
  • Gain weight until about age 55
  • Then begin to lose weight later in life
  • Possible cause: drop in male sex hormone testosterone
- WOMEN:
  • Gain weight until age 65
  • Then begin to lose weight
- Weight loss in later life occurs partly because fat replaces lean muscle tissue, and fat weighs less than muscle
- Diet and exercise are important factors in weight changes in late adulthood

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
1. The BLSA began in 1958, studied 1,400 people ages 20–90, and found aging varies by individual and organ system.
2. Key BLSA findings: heart muscles thicken, arteries less flexible, lung capacity diminishes, kidneys less efficient, bladder loses storage ability, brain cells lose function but new neurons can be produced.
3. After age 30: lean tissue is lost, body fat increases steadily; older adults may have ~1/3 more fat than when younger.
4. Bone density loss stages: early = OSTEOPENIA; later = OSTEOPOROSIS.
5. Skin changes include thinning, loss of elasticity, slower healing, bruising more easily, age spots (flat, brown, harmless), and skin tags (flesh-colored, raised, harmless).
6. SARCOPENIA = loss of muscle tissue as a natural part of aging; most noticeable in men; inactive people lose 3–5% of muscle mass per decade after age 30.
7. Sarcopenia typically accelerates around age 75 (range: 65–80).
8. Sarcopenia causes: fewer nerve signals to muscles, decreased protein-to-energy conversion, insufficient caloric/protein intake.
9. Sarcopenia consequences: frailty, falls, fractures; prevention via weight-lifting, walking, swimming, cardiovascular exercise.
10. Height loss: ~1/2 inch per decade after age 40; more rapid after 70; total loss of 1–3 inches; occurs in all races and both sexes.
11. Men gain weight until ~55, then lose; women gain until ~65, then lose; weight loss linked to fat replacing muscle (fat weighs less than muscle).
12. Fat accumulates toward the center of the body and around internal organs with age.
13. Hair follicles stop producing hairs with age; graying continues from middle adulthood into late adulthood.

notes.title: Theories of Aging | Lifespan Development (Lumen Learning)
notes.body:
THEORIES OF AGING — OVERVIEW
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Researchers still do NOT fully understand what factors contribute to the human lifespan (Jin, 2010)
- Research on aging is constantly evolving and includes: genetics, biochemistry, animal models, and human longitudinal studies (NIA, 2011a)
- According to Jin (2010), modern biological theories of human aging involve TWO categories:
  1. PROGRAMMED THEORIES — follow a biological timetable, possibly a continuation of childhood development; depend on 'changes in gene expression that affect the systems responsible for maintenance, repair, and defense responses' (Jin, 2010, p. 72)
  2. DAMAGE OR ERROR THEORIES — emphasize environmental factors that cause cumulative damage in organisms

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
GENETICS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Genetic make-up certainly plays a role in longevity, but scientists are still attempting to identify WHICH genes are responsible
- Based on animal models:
  • Some genes PROMOTE longer life
  • Other genes LIMIT longevity
- Longevity may be due to genes that better equip someone to SURVIVE A DISEASE
- Some genes may ACCELERATE the rate of aging; others DECREASE the rate
- Researchers SCAN THE ENTIRE GENOME and compare genetic variants in those who live longer vs. those with average or shorter lifespan
- Example: A National Institutes of Health (NIH) study identified genes possibly associated with BLOOD FAT LEVELS and CHOLESTEROL — both risk factors for coronary disease and early death (NIA, 2011a)
- Researchers believe it is most likely a COMBINATION OF MANY GENES that affect the rate of aging
- Category: Programmed Theory

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
EVOLUTIONARY THEORY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Evolutionary psychology emphasizes the importance of NATURAL SELECTION
- Genes that allow one to SURVIVE AND REPRODUCE will be more likely to be transmitted to offspring
- Genes associated with aging (e.g., Alzheimer's Disease) do NOT appear until AFTER the individual has passed their main REPRODUCTIVE YEARS
- Consequently, natural selection has NOT eliminated these damaging disorders from the gene pool
- If detrimental disorders occurred EARLIER in the development cycle, they may have been eliminated already (Gems, 2014)
- Category: Programmed Theory

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CELLULAR CLOCK THEORY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Biological aging is due to the fact that NORMAL CELLS CANNOT DIVIDE INDEFINITELY
- Known as the HAYFLICK LIMIT (Bartlett, 2014)
- Evidenced in cells studied in TEST TUBES: cells divide about 40–60 TIMES before they stop
- Mechanism: TELOMERES
  • Located at the END OF EACH CHROMOSOMAL STRAND
  • A sequence of DNA that does NOT code for any particular protein
  • PROTECTS the rest of the chromosome
  • With EACH REPLICATION, the telomere gets SHORTER
  • Once too short, the cell does ONE OF THREE THINGS:
    1. CELLULAR SENESCENCE — stops replicating by TURNING ITSELF OFF
    2. APOPTOSIS — stops replicating by DYING
    3. CANCER — continues to divide and becomes ABNORMAL
- Senescent cells and their effects:
  • They are turned off but NOT dead
  • Still interact with other cells in the body
  • Can lead to INCREASED RISK OF DISEASE
  • YOUNG: senescent cells may REDUCE risk of serious diseases such as cancer
  • OLD AGE: senescent cells INCREASE risk of such problems (NIA, 2011a)
- Understanding why cellular senescence changes from BENEFICIAL to DETRIMENTAL is still under investigation
- The answer may lead to important clues about the aging process
- Figure 9.10: Telomeres and Cellular Senescence — Adapted from National Institute on Aging
- Category: Programmed Theory

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
DNA DAMAGE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Over time, DNA (which contains the GENETIC CODE for all organisms) accumulates damage
- Usually NOT a concern — cells are capable of REPAIRING DAMAGE throughout life
- Some damage is HARMLESS
- However, some damage CANNOT BE REPAIRED and remains in DNA
- Scientists believe that this damage and the body's INABILITY TO FIX ITSELF is an important part of aging (NIA, 2011a)
- As DNA damage accumulates with increasing age, it can cause cells to DETERIORATE and MALFUNCTION (Jin, 2010)
- Factors that can damage DNA include:
  • ULTRAVIOLET RADIATION
  • CIGARETTE SMOKING
  • Exposure to HYDROCARBONS (e.g., auto exhaust, coal) (Dollemore, 2006)
- Category: Damage/Error Theory

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
MITOCHONDRIAL DAMAGE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Damage to MITOCHONDRIAL DNA can lead to DECAYING of the mitochondria
- Mitochondria = cell organelle that uses OXYGEN to produce energy from food
- Mitochondria convert oxygen to ADENOSINE TRIPHOSPHATE (ATP) — provides energy for the cell
- When damaged:
  • Mitochondria become LESS EFFICIENT
  • Generate LESS ENERGY for the cell
  • Can lead to CELLULAR DEATH (NIA, 2011a)
- Category: Damage/Error Theory

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
FREE RADICALS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- When mitochondria use oxygen to produce energy, they also produce potentially harmful byproducts called OXYGEN FREE RADICALS (NIA, 2011a)
- Free radicals are MISSING AN ELECTRON
- Create instability in surrounding molecules by TAKING ELECTRONS from them
- SNOWBALL EFFECT: A takes from B, then B takes from C, etc. — creates MORE free radicals which disrupt the cell and cause it to BEHAVE ABNORMALLY
- Some free radicals ARE HELPFUL — can destroy bacteria and other harmful organisms
- For the most part they cause DAMAGE in cells and tissue
- Free radicals are identified with disorders seen in those of ADVANCED AGE, including:
  • CANCER
  • ATHEROSCLEROSIS
  • CATARACTS
  • NEURODEGENERATION
- ANTIOXIDANTS:
  • Some research has supported adding antioxidants to diet to COUNTER the effects of free radical damage
  • Antioxidants can DONATE AN ELECTRON that neutralizes damaged molecules
  • Research on effectiveness of antioxidants is NOT CONCLUSIVE (Harvard School of Public Health, 2016)
- Figure 9.11: Free Radicals
- Category: Damage/Error Theory

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
IMMUNE AND HORMONAL STRESS THEORIES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Example: U.S. presidents visibly age rapidly — before and after photos reveal how STRESS plays a role in aging
- Figure 9.12: President Obama (illustrative example)
- Gerontologists studying stress consider:
  • MAJOR LIFE EVENTS (unemployment, death of loved one, birth of a child)
  • METABOLIC STRESS — life-sustaining activities of the body:
    - Circulating the blood
    - Eliminating waste
    - Controlling body temperature
    - Neuronal firing in the brain
  • All activities that keep the body alive also create BIOLOGICAL STRESS

INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM:
- Made up of: SKIN, MUCOUS MEMBRANES, COUGH REFLEX, STOMACH ACID, and specialized cells that alert the body of an impending threat
- With age: these cells LOSE their ability to COMMUNICATE AS EFFECTIVELY
- Result: harder for the body to MOBILIZE ITS DEFENSES

ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM:
- Includes: TONSILS, SPLEEN, BONE MARROW, THYMUS, CIRCULATORY SYSTEM, and LYMPHATIC SYSTEM
- Works to PRODUCE and TRANSPORT T-CELLS
- T-CELLS (lymphocytes): fight bacteria, viruses, and other foreign threats
- T-cells begin in a 'NAÏVE' STATE before being programmed to fight an invader
- After programming: become 'MEMORY CELLS'
  • Remember how to fight a certain infection if the body encounters the invader again
  • Can remain in the body for MANY DECADES
  • Example: MEASLES VACCINE received as a child still protects from the virus today
- Aging and T-cells:
  • Older adults produce FEWER NEW T-CELLS to be programmed
  • Less able to fight off NEW THREATS
  • New vaccines work LESS EFFECTIVELY in older adults
  • Exception: SHINGLES VACCINE works well in older adults because they already have EXISTING MEMORY CELLS against the VARICELLA VIRUS — the shingles vaccine acts as a BOOSTER (NIA, 2011a)

HORMONAL STRESS THEORY (Neuroendocrine Theory of Aging):
- Also known as: NEUROENDOCRINE THEORY OF AGING
- Suggests that as we age, the ability of the HYPOTHALAMUS to regulate hormones begins to DECLINE, leading to METABOLIC PROBLEMS (AFAR, 2011)
- Linked to EXCESS of the stress hormone CORTISOL
- While MANY of the body's hormones DECREASE with age, CORTISOL DOES NOT (NIH, 2014a)
- More stress → more CORTISOL released → more HYPOTHALAMIC DAMAGE
- Changes in hormones have been linked to several metabolic and hormone-related problems that INCREASE WITH AGE:
  • DIABETES (AFAR, 2011)
  • THYROID PROBLEMS (NIH, 2013)
  • OSTEOPOROSIS
  • ORTHOSTATIC HYPOTENSION (NIH, 2014a)
- Category: Damage/Error Theory

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
REFERENCES CITED IN SOURCE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Jin, 2010
- NIA (National Institute on Aging), 2011a
- Bartlett, 2014
- Gems, 2014
- Dollemore, 2006
- Harvard School of Public Health, 2016
- AFAR (American Federation of Aging Research), 2011
- NIH, 2013
- NIH, 2014a

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ KEY EXAM POINTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. TWO MAIN CATEGORIES of biological aging theories: PROGRAMMED THEORIES and DAMAGE/ERROR THEORIES (Jin, 2010)
2. HAYFLICK LIMIT: Normal cells can only divide ~40–60 times before stopping; basis of Cellular Clock Theory
3. TELOMERES: Protective DNA sequences at chromosome ends that SHORTEN with each replication; when too short → senescence, apoptosis, or cancer
4. THREE outcomes when telomere is too short: CELLULAR SENESCENCE (turns off), APOPTOSIS (dies), CANCER (divides abnormally)
5. Young senescent cells = REDUCE cancer risk; Old senescent cells = INCREASE disease risk
6. FREE RADICALS: Missing an electron, created as byproduct of mitochondrial energy production; linked to cancer, atherosclerosis, cataracts, neurodegeneration; antioxidants may help but evidence is NOT CONCLUSIVE
7. MITOCHONDRIA: Convert oxygen to ATP; damaged mitochondria → less energy → cellular death
8. DNA DAMAGE causes: UV radiation, cigarette smoking, hydrocarbons (auto exhaust, coal)
9. EVOLUTIONARY THEORY: Alzheimer's genes survive because they appear AFTER reproductive years — natural selection cannot eliminate them
10. INNATE IMMUNE SYSTEM declines with age → less effective communication of defense cells
11. ADAPTIVE IMMUNE SYSTEM: T-cells become memory cells; older adults produce fewer new T-cells → less effective against NEW threats; shingles vaccine works as a BOOSTER because memory cells for varicella already exist
12. NEUROENDOCRINE/HORMONAL STRESS THEORY: Hypothalamus declines with age → excess CORTISOL (cortisol does NOT decrease with age unlike other hormones) → diabetes, thyroid problems, osteoporosis, orthostatic hypotension
13. GENETICS: Combination of many genes most likely affects aging rate; NIH study linked genes to blood fat/cholesterol levels
14. Measles vaccine example: Memory cells last DECADES — explains lifelong immunity from childhood vaccination

notes.title: Age Categories in Late Adulthood | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
AGE CATEGORIES IN LATE ADULTHOOD
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

OVERVIEW & FRAMEWORK
- Late adulthood is divided into THREE categories for comparison:
  1. Young-old: ages 65–84
  2. Oldest-old: ages 85–99
  3. Centenarians: ages 100+
- Categories based on conceptions of aging including: BIOLOGICAL, PSYCHOLOGICAL, SOCIAL, and CHRONOLOGICAL differences
- Categories also reflect the INCREASE IN LONGEVITY of individuals living to this latter stage
- Source: Ortman et al., 2014

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

YOUNG-OLD (Ages 65–84)

DEFINITION & TERMINOLOGY
- Older adults between ages 65 and 84 (Ortman et al., 2014)
- Also called the "THIRD AGE" — identified by Laslett (1989)
  - First age = childhood
  - Second age = work and parenting
  - Third age = young-old (post-employment)
- According to Barnes (2011a): spans post-employment years until approximately 80–85, when age-related limitations occur in physical, emotional, and cognitive development
- Also referred to as the "GOLDEN YEARS" of adulthood
- Unusual age category: people are considered BOTH in old age AND not in old age (Rubinstein, 2002)

POSITIVE CHARACTERISTICS
- Fewer responsibilities than in previous life stages
- When combined with adequate finances and good health → can pursue LEISURE and SELF-FULFILLMENT opportunities
- Strong performance in: ATTENTION, MEMORY, and CRYSTALLIZED INTELLIGENCE
- Sense of HAPPINESS and EMOTIONAL WELL-BEING better than at ANY other period of adulthood (Carstensen, Fung, & Charles, 2003; George, 2009; Robins & Trzesniewski, 2005)

COMPARISON TO 85+ (FOURTH AGE)
- Compared with those above 85, young-old experience:
  - Relatively good health and social engagement (Smith, 2000)
  - Knowledge and expertise (Singer, Verhaeghen, Ghisletta, Lindenberger, & Baltes, 2003)
  - Adaptive flexibility in daily living (Riediger, Freund, & Baltes, 2005)
- Young-old are MORE SIMILAR TO MIDLIFE ADULTS than to those 85 and older

SOCIAL & LIFESTYLE CHARACTERISTICS
- LESS LIKELY to:
  - Require long-term care
  - Be dependent or poor
- MORE LIKELY to:
  - Be married
  - Work for PLEASURE rather than income
  - Live INDEPENDENTLY

HEALTH CONSIDERATIONS
- Most common chronic diseases (especially later in this period):
  - Cardiovascular disease
  - Hypertension
  - Cancer
- These are linked to LIFESTYLE CHOICES → typically can be PREVENTED, LESSENED, or MANAGED (Barnes, 2011b)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

OLDEST-OLD (Ages 85–99)

DEFINITION & TERMINOLOGY
- Sometimes called the "FOURTH AGE"
- Often includes people with MORE SERIOUS CHRONIC AILMENTS among the older adult population

DEMOGRAPHICS & STATISTICS
- In the U.S., oldest-old represented 14% of the older adult population in 2015 (He, Goodkind, & Kowal, 2016)
- One of the FASTEST GROWING age groups WORLDWIDE
- Projected to INCREASE MORE THAN 300% over current levels (NIA, 2015b)
- Projected to be NEARLY 18 MILLION by 2050 (~4.5% of U.S. population)
- Currently less than 2% of the U.S. population
- FEMALES comprise more than 60% of those 85 and older
  - BUT females also suffer from MORE CHRONIC ILLNESSES and DISABILITIES than older males (Gatz et al., 2016)

HOSPITALIZATION DATA
- Accounts for only 2% of U.S. population BUT 9% of ALL HOSPITALIZATIONS (Levant, Chari & DeFrances, 2015)
- Those 85 and up are:
  - LESS LIKELY to be discharged
  - MORE LIKELY to die in hospital
- Most common reasons for hospitalization (oldest-old):
  1. Congestive heart failure
  2. Pneumonia
  3. Urinary tract infections (UTIs)
  4. Septicemia
  5. Stroke
  6. Hip fractures
- In recent years: hospitalizations for many of these conditions have been REDUCED
- EXCEPTION: Hospitalization for URINARY TRACT INFECTIONS and SEPTICEMIA has INCREASED for those 85 and older
- NOTE: Figure 9.6 — Hospitalizations by age: United States 2000–2010

LIVING & CARE ARRANGEMENTS
- MORE LIKELY to require LONG-TERM CARE and to be in NURSING HOMES than young-old
- ALMOST 50% of oldest-old require some assistance with DAILY LIVING ACTIVITIES (APA, 2016)
- However, MOST still live in the COMMUNITY rather than in a nursing home (Stepler, 2016b)
- LESS LIKELY to be married and living with a spouse compared to the majority of young-old (APA, 2016; Stepler, 2016c)
- GENDER is an important factor in likelihood of being married or living with one's spouse
- NOTE: Figure 9.7 illustrates living arrangements by gender

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

CENTENARIANS (Ages 100+)

DEFINITION & SUBTYPES
- Centenarians: 100 years and older
- Supercentenarians: 110 years and older (Wilcox, Wilcox & Ferrucci, 2008)
- Centenarians are a SEGMENT of the oldest-old

GLOBAL STATISTICS
- In 2015: nearly HALF A MILLION centenarians worldwide
- Estimated to GROW to almost 3.7 MILLION by 2050
- The U.S. has the MOST CENTENARIANS in total number
- JAPAN and ITALY have the most centenarians PER CAPITA (Stepler, 2016e)

HEALTH PROFILE
- Most centenarians tended to be HEALTHIER than many of their peers as they grew older
- Often a DELAY in onset of any serious disease or disability until their 90s
- 25% reached 100 with NO SERIOUS CHRONIC ILLNESSES — including:
  - Depression
  - Osteoporosis
  - Heart disease
  - Respiratory illness
  - Dementia
  (Ash et al., 2015)
- More likely to experience RAPID TERMINAL DECLINE in later life (rather than prolonged illness)
  - Meaning: for most of their adulthood and older adult years, they are RELATIVELY HEALTHY in comparison to many other older adults (Ash et al., 2015; Wilcox et al., 2008)

NOTABLE RECORD
- JEANNE LOUISE CALMENT (France) — documented to be the LONGEST LIVING PERSON at 122 YEARS and 164 DAYS OLD (Guinness World Records, 2016)
- NOTE: Figure 9.8 — Photo of Jeanne Louise Calment

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

LEARNING OBJECTIVES: PHYSICAL DEVELOPMENT IN LATE ADULTHOOD
- Describe DIFFERENT THEORIES OF AGING
- Describe the CHANGES IN PHYSICAL APPEARANCE in late adulthood
- Describe the SENSORY CHANGES in late adulthood
- Describe CHRONIC HEALTH CONDITIONS during late adulthood
- Describe the importance of NUTRITION AND EXERCISE in late adulthood
- Describe the PHYSICAL AND FUNCTIONAL CHANGES IN THE BRAIN during late adulthood
- Explain what happens in PARKINSON'S DISEASE
- Explain how SLEEP PATTERNS CHANGE in late adulthood
- Explain how SEXUALITY CHANGES in late adulthood

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS

1. THREE CATEGORIES of late adulthood: Young-old (65–84), Oldest-old (85–99), Centenarians (100+)
2. YOUNG-OLD also called the "THIRD AGE" (Laslett, 1989) and the "GOLDEN YEARS"
3. Young-old are MORE SIMILAR TO MIDLIFE than to those 85+
4. Young-old report HIGHEST EMOTIONAL WELL-BEING of any adult period (Carstensen et al., 2003; George, 2009; Robins & Trzesniewski, 2005)
5. Young-old strengths: attention, memory, crystallized intelligence, adaptive flexibility, knowledge/expertise
6. OLDEST-OLD = 14% of older adult population in U.S. (2015); projected 300%+ increase (NIA, 2015b)
7. FEMALES = more than 60% of those 85+, but suffer MORE chronic illnesses/disabilities than males
8. Oldest-old = only 2% of U.S. population but 9% of ALL hospitalizations
9. Top hospitalization causes (oldest-old): congestive heart failure, pneumonia, UTIs, septicemia, stroke, hip fractures
10. UTI and septicemia hospitalizations have INCREASED (not decreased) for 85+
11. Nearly 50% of oldest-old require assistance with DAILY LIVING ACTIVITIES
12. Most oldest-old still live in COMMUNITY (not nursing homes)
13. U.S. = most centenarians in number; JAPAN & ITALY = most PER CAPITA
14. 25% of centenarians reached 100 with NO serious chronic illnesses (Ash et al., 2015)
15. Centenarians tend to experience RAPID TERMINAL DECLINE rather than prolonged illness
16. SUPERCENTENARIANS = 110+ years old
17. JEANNE LOUISE CALMENT = longest lived person on record: 122 years and 164 days (Guinness, 2016)
18. Centenarians estimated to grow to 3.7 MILLION worldwide by 2050 (from ~500,000 in 2015)

notes.title: Gender Differences in Life Expectancy | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
GENDER DIFFERENCES IN LIFE EXPECTANCY

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

BIOLOGICAL EXPLANATIONS

• Biological differences in sex chromosomes and different patterns of gene expression are theorized as one reason why females live longer (Chmielewski, Boryslawski, & Strzelec, 2016)

• Males are HETEROGAMETIC (XY); females are HOMOGAMETIC (XX) with respect to sex chromosomes

• Males can only express their X chromosome genes that come from the mother
• Females have an advantage by selecting the 'better' X chromosome from either mother or father, while INACTIVATING the 'worse' X chromosome
• This process of selection for 'better' genes is IMPOSSIBLE in males
• Result: greater genetic and developmental stability of females

• In terms of developmental biology, WOMEN are the 'DEFAULT' sex
  - Creation of a male individual requires a SEQUENCE OF EVENTS at the molecular level
  - These events are initiated by the activity of the SRY GENE located on the Y chromosome
  - This activity and change in the direction of development results in a GREATER NUMBER OF DISTURBANCES AND DEVELOPMENTAL DISORDERS
  - Normal course of development requires many different factors and mechanisms, each of which must work properly and at a specific stage of development (Chmielewski et al., 2016, p. 134)

IMMUNITY:
• Men are more likely to contract VIRAL AND BACTERIAL INFECTIONS
• Immunity at the cellular level decreases SIGNIFICANTLY FASTER with age in men
• Women are slightly more prone to AUTOIMMUNE AND INFLAMMATORY DISEASES (e.g., rheumatoid arthritis)
• Gradual deterioration of the immune system is SLOWER in women (Caruso, Accardi, Virruso, & Candore, 2013; Hirokawa et al., 2013)

HORMONES:
• ESTROGEN levels in women appear to have a PROTECTIVE EFFECT on the heart and circulatory systems (Viña, Borrás, Gambini, Sastre, & Pallardó, 2005)
• Estrogens also have ANTIOXIDANT PROPERTIES that protect against harmful effects of FREE RADICALS
  - Free radicals damage cell components, cause mutations, and are partly responsible for the aging process
• TESTOSTERONE levels are higher in men than in women
  - Related to more frequent CARDIOVASCULAR and IMMUNE DISORDERS
  - Responsible in part for male behavioral patterns, including increased levels of AGGRESSION and VIOLENCE (Martin, Poon, & Hagberg, 2011; Borysławski & Chmielewski, 2012)

FRONTAL LOBE DEVELOPMENT:
• The FRONTAL LOBE controls judgment and consideration of an action's consequences
• Develops MORE SLOWLY in boys and young men
• This lack of judgment affects lifestyle choices
• Consequently, many more boys and men die by:
  - Smoking
  - Excessive drinking
  - Accidents
  - Drunk driving
  - Violence
  (Shmerling, 2016)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

LIFESTYLE FACTORS

• Not ALL reasons women live longer than men are biological
• Male behavioral patterns and lifestyle play a SIGNIFICANT ROLE in shorter male lifespans

DANGEROUS JOBS & VIOLENCE:
• Males work in more dangerous jobs including:
  - Police
  - Fire fighters
  - Construction
• Males are more exposed to violence
• According to the FBI (2014): there were 11,961 HOMICIDES in the U.S. in 2014 (last year for full data)
  - 77% of homicide victims were MALES
• Males are MORE THAN THREE TIMES as likely to commit SUICIDE (CDC, 2016a)
• Males serve in the military in MUCH LARGER NUMBERS than females
  - According to the Department of Defense (2015), in 2014:
    → 83% of all OFFICERS in the Services (Navy, Army, Marine Corps, and Air Force) were MALE
    → 85% of all ENLISTED SERVICE MEMBERS were MALE

HEALTH-SEEKING BEHAVIOR:
• Men are LESS LIKELY than women to:
  - Have health insurance
  - Develop a regular relationship with a doctor
  - Seek treatment for a medical condition
  (Scott, 2015)

RELIGION:
• Women are MORE RELIGIOUS than men (as mentioned in the middle adulthood chapter)
• Greater religiosity is ASSOCIATED WITH HEALTHIER BEHAVIORS (Greenfield, Vaillant & Marks, 2009)

SOCIAL CONTACT & LONELINESS:
• Loneliness is considered a HEALTH HAZARD
• Nearly 20% of men over 50 have contact with their friends LESS THAN ONCE A MONTH
• Only 12% of women see friends that infrequently (Scott, 2015)
• Men have significantly LESS social contact than women

OVERALL CONCLUSION:
• Men's lower life expectancy appears to be due to BOTH BIOLOGICAL AND LIFESTYLE FACTORS

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS

1. Males are HETEROGAMETIC (XY); females are HOMOGAMETIC (XX) — females can choose the 'better' X chromosome, males cannot
2. Women are the biological 'DEFAULT' sex; male development requires the SRY GENE on the Y chromosome to initiate a sequence of molecular events
3. Male cellular immunity decreases FASTER with age; women decline more slowly
4. ESTROGEN = protective for the heart and has ANTIOXIDANT properties against free radicals
5. TESTOSTERONE = associated with cardiovascular/immune disorders, aggression, and violence
6. The FRONTAL LOBE (controls judgment) develops MORE SLOWLY in males, contributing to risky behavior
7. FBI (2014): 77% of 11,961 U.S. homicide victims were MALE
8. Males are MORE THAN 3x as likely to commit SUICIDE (CDC, 2016a)
9. DoD (2015): 83% of officers and 85% of enlisted service members were MALE
10. ~20% of men over 50 see friends less than once a month vs. only 12% of women (Scott, 2015)
11. Women are more religious → associated with healthier behaviors (Greenfield, Vaillant & Marks, 2009)
12. Men are less likely to have health insurance, see a doctor regularly, or seek medical treatment
13. BOTH BIOLOGICAL AND LIFESTYLE FACTORS explain men's lower life expectancy

notes.title: Life Expectancy vs Lifespan | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
LIFE EXPECTANCY VS LIFESPAN
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

LIFESPAN (MAXIMUM LIFESPAN)
- Definition: The greatest age reached by any member of a given population (or species)
- For humans, the current lifespan is between 120 and 125 years

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

LIFE EXPECTANCY
- Definition: The average number of years that members of a population (or species) live
- Source: World Health Organization (WHO), 2016
- Global life expectancy at birth in 2015: 71.4 years
  • Females: 73.8 years
  • Males: 69.1 years
- Women live longer than men around the world
- The gap between the sexes has remained the same since 1990
- Range of global life expectancy:
  • Lowest: WHO African Region — 60.0 years
  • Highest: WHO European Region — 76.8 years
- Global life expectancy increased by 5 years between 2000 and 2015
- Largest increase: WHO African Region — increased by 9.4 years
  • Primary causes: improvements in child survival AND access to antiretroviral medication for treatment of HIV
- According to the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA, 2016): The United States ranks 43rd in the world for life expectancy

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

WORLD HEALTHY LIFE EXPECTANCY
- Context: A better way to appreciate diversity of people in late adulthood is to go beyond chronological age and examine how well the person is aging
  • Many in late adulthood enjoy better health and social well-being than average → aging at an OPTIMAL level
  • Others experience poor health and dependence to a greater extent than considered normal
- Definition: The WHO (2016) measures how many equivalent years of FULL HEALTH on average a newborn baby is expected to have
  • Also called: The Healthy Life Expectancy (HLE)
  • Takes into account: current age-specific mortality, morbidity, and disability risks
- Global Healthy Life Expectancy in 2015: 63.1 years (up from 58.5 years in 2000)
- Regional breakdown:
  • Lowest: WHO African Region — 52.3 years
  • Highest: WHO Western Pacific Region — 68.7 years

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

LIFE EXPECTANCY IN AMERICA
- Overall U.S. life expectancy: 79.7 years
- Life expectancies vary by: sex, race, and ethnicity
- Source: Ortman et al. (2014) — Table 9.3: 2012 U.S. Life Expectancy by Sex, Race, and Ethnic Origin (in Years)

TABLE 9.3 — 2012 U.S. LIFE EXPECTANCY BY SEX, RACE, AND ETHNIC ORIGIN

  Demographic                                              | Female | Male
  ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────|────────|──────
  All Groups                                               | 81.97  | 77.32
  Non-Hispanic White and Asian or Pacific Islander         | 81.7   | 77.1
  Non-Hispanic Black and American Indian or Alaskan Native | 78.0   | 71.7
  Hispanic                                                 | 83.7   | 78.9

- Source: An Aging Nation: The Older Population in the United States. United States Census Bureau.
- Key findings from table:
  • Females enjoy a longer life expectancy across all demographic groups
  • Overall, Hispanics have the HIGHEST life expectancy of all groups listed

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

AMERICAN HEALTHY LIFE EXPECTANCY
- Data evaluated: 2007–2009 (CDC, 2013)
- Focus: How long an individual currently at age 65 will continue to experience good health
- Highest HLE in the U.S.: Hawaii — 16.2 years of additional good health
- Lowest HLE in the U.S.: Mississippi — only 10.8 years of additional good health
- Overall: The lowest HLE was among SOUTHERN STATES
- Sex differences: Females had a GREATER HLE than males at age 65 in EVERY state and DC
- Race differences: HLE was GREATER for WHITES than for BLACKS in DC and all states with available data
  • Exceptions: Nevada and New Mexico (HLE was NOT greater for whites vs. blacks in these two states)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

THREAT TO FUTURE LIFE EXPECTANCY: CHILDHOOD OBESITY AND SEDENTARY LIFESTYLE
- Despite improvements in overall life expectancy, children born in the United States today may be the FIRST GENERATION to have a SHORTER LIFESPAN than their parents
- Primary attributed cause: increase in sedentary lifestyle and obesity
- American Heart Association (2014) statistic: Currently 1 in 3 American children is overweight or obese
- Rate of childhood obesity TRIPLED from 1971 to 2011
- Health problems associated with childhood obesity:
  • High blood pressure
  • Type 2 diabetes
  • Elevated blood cholesterol levels
  • Psychological concerns: low self-esteem, negative body image, and depression
- Excess weight is associated with an earlier risk of obesity-related diseases and death
- Quote — Former U.S. Surgeon General Richard Carmona (2007): "Because of the increasing rates of obesity, unhealthy eating habits and physical inactivity, we may see the first generation that will be less healthy and have a shorter life expectancy than their parents" (p. 1)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- LIFESPAN (maximum) for humans = 120–125 years; LIFE EXPECTANCY = average years lived
- Global life expectancy (2015, WHO): 71.4 years overall; females 73.8, males 69.1
- Largest increase in life expectancy 2000–2015: WHO African Region (+9.4 years) — due to child survival improvements and antiretroviral HIV treatment
- U.S. ranks 43rd in the world for life expectancy (CIA, 2016)
- Healthy Life Expectancy (HLE) accounts for mortality, morbidity, AND disability risks
- Global HLE (2015): 63.1 years; lowest = WHO African Region (52.3); highest = WHO Western Pacific Region (68.7)
- U.S. overall life expectancy: 79.7 years
- Highest U.S. life expectancy group: Hispanic females (83.7), Hispanic males (78.9)
- Lowest U.S. life expectancy group: Non-Hispanic Black and American Indian/Alaskan Native males (71.7)
- U.S. HLE highest in Hawaii (16.2 yrs after 65), lowest in Mississippi (10.8 yrs after 65)
- Exceptions to white > black HLE: Nevada and New Mexico
- Childhood obesity rate TRIPLED 1971–2011; 1 in 3 U.S. children overweight or obese
- Surgeon General Carmona (2007) warned current U.S. children may be the first generation with SHORTER life expectancy than their parents
- Females consistently outlive males globally and in the U.S.; sex gap in global life expectancy unchanged since 1990

notes.title: The 'Graying' of the World | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
THE 'GRAYING' OF THE WORLD
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

U.S. AGE RELATIVE TO OTHER DEVELOPED COUNTRIES
- Even though the United States is aging, it is still YOUNGER than most other developed countries (Ortman, Velkoff, & Hogan, 2014)
- Germany, Italy, and Japan all had at least 20% of their population aged 65 and over in 2012
- Japan had the HIGHEST percentage of elderly among developed nations in 2012
- Between 2012 and 2050, the proportion aged 65 and over is projected to INCREASE in ALL developed countries
- Japan is projected to continue to have the OLDEST population in both 2030 and 2050

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

TABLE 9.2 — PERCENTAGE OF CITIZENS 65 YEARS AND OLDER IN SIX DEVELOPED COUNTRIES

| Country   | 2012  | 2030  | 2050  |
|-----------|-------|-------|-------|
| America   | 13.7% | 20.3% | 22%   |
| Japan     | 24%   | 32.2% | 40%   |
| Germany   | 20%   | 27.9% | 30%   |
| Italy     | 20%   | 25.5% | 31%   |
| Canada    | 16.5% | 25%   | 26.5% |
| Russia    | 13%   | 20%   | 26%   |

Source: Compiled from data from An Aging Nation: The Older Population in the United States. United States Census Bureau. http://www.census.gov/prod/2014pubs/p25-1140.pdf

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

GLOBAL AGING STATISTICS (National Institute on Aging — NIA, 2015b)
- There are 524 MILLION people over 65 worldwide
- This number is expected to increase from 8% to 16% of the global population by 2050
- Between 2010 and 2050, the number of older people in LESS DEVELOPED countries is projected to increase more than 250%
- Compared with only a 71% increase in DEVELOPED countries

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

CAUSES OF POPULATION AGING
- DECLINES IN FERTILITY and IMPROVEMENTS IN LONGEVITY account for the percentage increase for those 65 years and older
- In MORE DEVELOPED countries:
  - Fertility fell below the replacement rate of TWO live births per woman by the 1970s
  - Down from nearly THREE children per woman around 1950
- In LESS DEVELOPED countries:
  - Fertility rates fell from an average of SIX children in 1950
  - To an average of TWO OR THREE children in 2005
  - In 2006, fertility was at or below the two-child replacement level in 44 less developed countries (NIA, 2015d)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

U.S. OLDER POPULATION IN GLOBAL CONTEXT
- In total NUMBER, the United States is projected to have a LARGER older population than other developed nations
- BUT a SMALLER older population compared with China and India — the world's two most populous nations (Ortman et al., 2014)
- By 2050, CHINA's older population is projected to grow LARGER than the TOTAL U.S. POPULATION TODAY

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

LONG-TERM CARE AND THE AGING POPULATION
- As the population ages, concerns grow about WHO WILL PROVIDE for those requiring long-term care
- In 2000, there were about 10 people aged 85 and older for every 100 persons between ages 50 and 64
- Midlife adults (ages 50–64) are the MOST LIKELY CARE PROVIDERS for their aging parents
- The number of old requiring support from their children is expected to MORE THAN DOUBLE by the year 2040 (He, Sengupta, Velkoff, & DeBarros, 2005)
- These families will certainly need EXTERNAL PHYSICAL, EMOTIONAL, AND FINANCIAL SUPPORT in meeting this challenge

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

KEY RESEARCHERS AND SOURCES CITED
- Ortman, Velkoff, & Hogan, 2014 — U.S. aging relative to developed nations; China and India projections
- National Institute on Aging (NIA, 2015b) — 524 million over 65 worldwide; global 8% to 16% increase by 2050
- NIA, 2015d — fertility rates in less developed countries; 44 nations at or below replacement level in 2006
- He, Sengupta, Velkoff, & DeBarros, 2005 — caregiving burden doubling by 2040

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
1. The U.S. is YOUNGER than most other developed countries despite its own aging trend
2. Japan had the HIGHEST percentage of elderly (24%) in 2012 and is projected to remain oldest (40% by 2050)
3. There are 524 MILLION people over 65 worldwide (NIA, 2015b)
4. Global elderly population expected to go from 8% to 16% of world population by 2050
5. Less developed countries will see a 250%+ increase in older population (2010–2050) vs. only 71% in developed countries
6. Fertility DECLINES and LONGEVITY IMPROVEMENTS are the two main drivers of population aging
7. Replacement fertility rate = TWO live births per woman
8. Developed countries fell below replacement rate by the 1970s (from ~3 children in 1950)
9. Less developed countries went from average of 6 children (1950) to 2–3 children (2005)
10. By 2050, China's older population will exceed TODAY'S ENTIRE U.S. POPULATION
11. In 2000: 10 people aged 85+ for every 100 people aged 50–64
12. Number of elderly needing child support expected to MORE THAN DOUBLE by 2040
13. Midlife adults (50–64) are the MOST LIKELY caregivers for aging parents
14. Families will need external physical, emotional, AND financial support

notes.title: Late Adulthood in America | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
LATE ADULTHOOD IN AMERICA
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

DEFINITION AND SCOPE
- Late adulthood includes those aged 65 years and above
- Late adulthood is the FASTEST GROWING age division of the United States population
- Source: Gatz, Smyer, & DiGilio, 2016

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

CURRENT STATISTICS
- Currently, ONE IN SEVEN Americans is 65 years of age or older
- The first of the baby boomers turned 65 in 2011
- Baby boomers were born from 1946–1964
- Approximately 10,000 baby boomers turn 65 EVERY DAY

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

FUTURE PROJECTIONS
- By the year 2050, almost ONE IN FOUR Americans will be over 65
- Future generations are expected to live LONGER than previous generations
- According to the U.S. Census Bureau (2014b): a person who turned 65 in 2015 can expect to live another 19 YEARS
- This is 5.5 YEARS LONGER than someone who turned 65 in 1950

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

"GRAYING OF AMERICA"
- The increasingly aged population has been referred to as the "Graying of America"
- This "graying" is ALREADY having significant effects on the nation in many areas, including:
  • Work
  • Health care
  • Housing
  • Social security
  • Caregiving
  • Adaptive technologies

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

TABLE 9.1 — PERCENT OF UNITED STATES POPULATION 65 YEARS AND OLDER
(Compiled from U.S. Census Bureau data: An Aging Nation: The Older Population in the United States)

AGE GROUP | 2012 | 2020 | 2030
65 Years and Older (Total) | 13.7% | 16.8% | 20.3%
65–69 | 4.5% | 5.4% | 5.6%
70–74 | 3.2% | 4.4% | 5.2%
75–79 | 2.4% | 3.0% | 4.1%
80–84 | 1.8% | 1.9% | 2.9%
85 Years and Older | 1.9% | 2.0% | 2.5%

Source: U.S. Census Bureau. http://www.census.gov/prod/2014pubs/p25-1140.pdf

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- Late adulthood = 65 years and older; FASTEST growing age division in the U.S.
- Researchers to know: Gatz, Smyer, & DiGilio (2016)
- 1 in 7 Americans is currently 65+; by 2050 it will be 1 in 4
- Baby boomers born 1946–1964; first turned 65 in 2011; 10,000/day turning 65
- A person turning 65 in 2015 expected to live 19 more years — 5.5 years longer than a person who turned 65 in 1950 (U.S. Census Bureau, 2014b)
- "Graying of America" = term for the increasingly aged U.S. population
- Key areas affected by graying: work, health care, housing, social security, caregiving, adaptive technologies
- 2030 projection: 20.3% of U.S. population will be 65+ (more than 1 in 5)
- Fastest-growing subgroup within late adulthood by 2030: 75–79 age group shows one of the largest percentage increases (2.4% → 4.1%)
- Table 9.1 data source: U.S. Census Bureau, An Aging Nation report

notes.title: Lifespan Development: Introduction to Late Adulthood – Lumen Learning
notes.body:
INTRODUCTION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Late adulthood spans the time when we reach our mid-sixties until death
- This is the LONGEST developmental stage across the entire lifespan
- Chapter will cover:
  • Growth in numbers for those in late adulthood
  • How that number is expected to change in the future
  • Implications for both the United States and worldwide
  • Several theories of human aging
  • Physical changes that occur in this population
  • Cognitive changes that occur in this population
  • Socioemotional changes that occur in this population
  • The vast diversity among those in this developmental stage
  • Ageism and many of the myths associated with those in late adulthood

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
LEARNING OBJECTIVES: LATE ADULTHOOD DEFINITION AND DEMOGRAPHICS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Describe the increase in the number of individuals who are currently identified as late adults
- Describe the increase in late adulthood worldwide
- Explain gender and ethnic differences in the number of individuals identified as late adults
- Explain the different ways developmental psychologists describe aging
- Explain the difference between life span and life expectancy
- Define the three age categories for late adulthood
- Explain what factors contribute to becoming a centenarian

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
KEY THEMES OF THE CHAPTER
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- POPULATION GROWTH: Examination of how many people are currently in late adulthood and projections for future growth
- GLOBAL IMPLICATIONS: Impact of aging population on the United States AND worldwide
- THEORIES OF AGING: Multiple theoretical frameworks used to understand the aging process
- DOMAINS OF CHANGE: Physical, cognitive, and socioemotional changes all addressed
- DIVERSITY: Recognition of vast diversity among individuals in this developmental stage
- AGEISM: Exploration of age-based discrimination and myths surrounding late adulthood

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ KEY EXAM POINTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ Late adulthood begins at mid-sixties and extends until death
★ Late adulthood is the LONGEST developmental stage across the lifespan
★ Key demographic topics include: current numbers, future projections, gender differences, and ethnic differences among late adults
★ Students must be able to distinguish between LIFE SPAN and LIFE EXPECTANCY
★ There are THREE age categories for late adulthood (to be defined in subsequent sections)
★ Factors that contribute to becoming a centenarian (age 100+) are a testable learning objective
★ Developmental psychologists describe aging in MULTIPLE ways — students must know these different frameworks
★ Ageism and myths about late adulthood are explicit topics of study in this chapter
★ Physical, cognitive, AND socioemotional changes are all covered — all three domains are testable
★ Diversity within the late adulthood population is emphasized as a major theme

notes.title: Chronic Conditions | Lifespan Development – Complete Study Notes
notes.body:
CHRONIC CONDITIONS – OVERVIEW
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Chronic illnesses: ongoing, generally incurable conditions requiring continuing medical attention and affecting daily life
- As individuals live longer, diseases affecting older individuals become more prevalent
- Burden of chronic illness grows with age
- Less than 50% of adults aged 50–64 have a chronic condition (Cohen, 2011)
- 90% of adults aged 75 and up have a chronic condition (Cohen, 2011)
- Older women are MORE likely to have a chronic condition than older men: 88% vs. 83% (CDC, 2009)
- Table 9.6: Lists percentage of older adults with certain chronic illnesses (National Health Survey, 2014)
- Other studies place diabetes in older adults at 26% (CDC, 2014)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CANCER AND MAJOR CARDIOVASCULAR DISEASE – OVERVIEW
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Cancer and cardiovascular disease are the overall leading causes of death
- Especially high reasons for death in middle and late adults
- Table 9.7: Death percentages by cause for selected age groups (2013, most recent year for data)
  Source: Xu, Murphy, Kochanek, & Bastian (2016)

  2013 CAUSES OF DEATH TABLE:
  | Age Group | Cancer | Major Cardiovascular Disease |
  |-----------|--------|------------------------------|
  | 45–54     | 6.4%   | 24.3%                        |
  | 55–64     | 13.7%  | 26.5%                        |
  | 65–74     | 24.9%  | 27.7%                        |
  | 75–84     | 24.5%  | 31.6%                        |
  | 85+       | 12%    | 38.9%                        |

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CANCER
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Advancing age is a significant risk factor for cancer
- Persons over 65 account for:
  • 60% of newly diagnosed cancers (Berger et al., 2006)
  • 70% of all cancer deaths (Berger et al., 2006)
- More than 70% of mortality associated with many cancers occur in patients 65 and older, including:
  • Prostate, bladder, colon, uterus, pancreas, stomach, rectum, and lung cancers
- Other conditions that affect elderly and can occur WITH cancer (comorbidities):
  • Anemia
  • Coronary artery diseases
  • Congestive heart failure
  • Chronic obstructive pulmonary diseases (COPD)
  • Renal insufficiency
  • Cerebrovascular diseases
  • Neurovascular complications of diabetes mellitus
  • Arthritis that restricts mobility
  Source: Balducci & Extermann (2000)
- Comorbidity will complicate treatment

BALDUCCI AND EXTERMANN (2000) – CANCER TREATMENT CONCERNS IN THE ELDERLY:
- With aging, there is a decline in multiple organ systems that can adversely affect the ability of medications to treat cancer
- Chemotherapy has been found to compromise cognitive function in those being treated for cancer
- Chemotherapy may further exacerbate dementia and elderly cognitive declines
- Frail individuals (defined as having limited life expectancy and near-to-exhausted functional reserves) are NOT considered candidates for more toxic forms of chemotherapy
- Prevalence and risk of malnutrition are higher with cancer
- Diminished visual and hearing function makes elderly cancer patients more susceptible to environmental injury
- Screening for depression is recommended because:
  • Depression is associated with weight loss
  • Depression associated with failure to thrive
  • Depression may reduce motivation to receive treatment
  • Depression has been associated with decreased survival rates in the elderly
- Due to projected increase in total number of older patients with cancer, it is recommended that physicians and caretakers have expertise in BOTH oncology and geriatrics (Berger et al., 2006)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
HEART DISEASE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Age-related changes to the heart that may increase risk of heart disease include:
  • Stiffening blood vessels and valves
  • May result in leaks or problems pumping blood out of the heart (NIA, 2012)
- Heart disease is the leading cause of death for those in late adulthood (CDC, 2016b)
- Most common type: Atherosclerosis – buildup of fatty deposits or plaques in walls of arteries
  • As plaque builds up, blood is unable to flow normally and bring oxygen throughout the body, including to the heart
  • Depending on where buildup is, atherosclerosis can cause:
    – Heart attack
    – Leg pain
    – Stroke
- IMPORTANT DISTINCTION: Atherosclerosis is NOT part of normal aging
- Many problems older people have with heart and blood vessels are caused by DISEASE, not by aging
- Example: An older heart can normally pump blood as strongly as a younger heart; less ability to pump is caused by disease
- Leading a heart-healthy lifestyle is most important to keeping one's heart strong in late adulthood

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ARTHRITIS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Arthritis and other rheumatic conditions are the MOST COMMON cause of disability among US adults
- Have been the most common cause of disability among US adults for the past 15 years (NIH: National Institute of Arthritis and Musculoskeletal and Skin Diseases, 2014)
- Approximately 62% of adults with arthritis are 65 years old and up (NIH)
- Almost 1 in 2 older adults with arthritis have some degree of mobility limitations, including:
  • Climbing stairs
  • Walking
  • Grasping objects
- Pain and limitations of arthritis can increase risk of depression and other forms of mental distress

OSTEOARTHRITIS:
- Most common type of arthritis
- Definition: When the cartilage (the slick, cushioning surface on the ends of bones) wears away, bone rubs against bone, causing pain, swelling, and stiffness. Over time, joints can lose strength and pain may become chronic. (Arthritis Foundation, 2017)
- Common risk factors for osteoarthritis:
  • Genetics
  • Obesity
  • Age
  • Previous injury
  • Other medical conditions

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
OSTEOPOROSIS AND KYPHOSIS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Osteoporosis: A disease that thins and weakens bones to the point that they become fragile and break easily
- After age 50:
  • 1 in 2 women will experience an osteoporosis-related fracture in their lifetime
  • 1 in 4 men will experience an osteoporosis-related fracture in their lifetime
  • Often leading to hip, spine, and wrist fractures (Dailey & Cravedi, 2006)
- Broken hips are a very serious problem with aging
  • They greatly increase the risk of death, especially during the year after they break (NIH Senior Health, 2015)
- In the U.S., more than 53 million adults either already have osteoporosis or are at high risk due to low bone mass (NIH Senior Health, 2015)

KYPHOSIS:
- As bones weaken in the spine, adults gradually lose height and posture becomes hunched over → called Kyphosis
- Over time, a bent spine can make it hard to walk or even sit up

PREVENTION OF BONE MASS LOSS:
- Eating a healthy diet with enough calcium and vitamin D
- Regularly exercising
- Limiting alcohol
- Not smoking
  Source: National Osteoporosis Foundation (2016)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHRONIC OBSTRUCTIVE PULMONARY DISEASE (COPD)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Definition: A progressive lung disease in which the airways become damaged, making it difficult to breathe
- COPD includes: Emphysema and chronic bronchitis (NIH Senior Health, 2013)
- COPD kills more than 120,000 people every year → one of the leading causes of death
- COPD develops slowly; people may not notice early signs
- Early symptoms may be attributed to age or lack of physical exercise
- Most people are NOT diagnosed until midlife or late adulthood
- There is NO CURE; damage cannot be reversed
- Treatments aim at slowing further damage

CAUSES OF COPD:
- Cigarette smoking is the LEADING cause of COPD
- Other tobacco smoking (pipe, cigar) can cause COPD, especially if smoke is inhaled
- Heavy or long-term exposure to secondhand smoke can also lead to COPD (NIH Senior Health, 2013)
- Long-term exposure to other environmental irritants:
  • Chemical fumes
  • Dust from environment and workplace

GENETIC CAUSE – ALPHA-1 ANTITRYPSIN (AAT) DEFICIENCY:
- About 1 in every 1,600 to 5,000 people have a risk for COPD due to AAT deficiency
- AAT deficiency is a recessive genetic condition (NIH, 2011)
- AAT is a protein made in the LIVER that protects organs, especially the lungs, from the effects of other harmful proteins
- In those with the genetic defect:
  • The AAT protein created is the WRONG SHAPE and cannot leave the liver
  • This leads to heightened risk for lung disease
  • Excess AAT protein can lead to CIRRHOSIS (disease in which liver becomes scarred and does not function properly) → risk for liver disease
- Some people with AAT deficiency are not affected and live a normal life
- COPD is more likely in AAT deficiency individuals if their lungs are exposed to environmental irritants

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SHINGLES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Source: National Institute on Aging (2015e)
- Definition: A disease that affects the nerves
- Caused by the same virus as chicken pox: the varicella-zoster virus (VZV)
- After recovery from chickenpox, the virus continues to live in some nerve cells
- Usually inactive; most adults live with VZV in their body and NEVER get shingles
- However, the virus becomes active in 1 in 3 adults
- Instead of causing chickenpox again, it produces shingles

RISK FACTORS FOR SHINGLES:
- Advanced age: People have a harder time fighting off infections as they get older
- About HALF of all shingles cases are in adults age 60 or older
- The chance of getting shingles becomes much greater by age 70
- Other factors that weaken ability to fight infections:
  • Cancer
  • HIV infections
  • Other medical conditions

SYMPTOMS OF SHINGLES:
- Pain, burning, tingling, or itching in the affected area
- A rash and blisters
- Typically develops on ONLY ONE SIDE of the body or face
- Develops in a SMALL AREA rather than all over
- Most cases last 3 to 5 weeks

POST-HERPETIC NEURALGIA (PHN):
- After shingles rash goes away, some people may be left with ongoing pain called post-herpetic neuralgia (PHN) in the area where the rash had been (NIA, 2015e)
- The OLDER one is when getting shingles, the greater the chance of developing PHN
- PHN effects:
  • Hard to perform daily activities (dressing, cooking, eating)
  • Depression, anxiety, and sleeplessness
- Medicines can help with pain
- PHN will usually disappear

COMPLICATIONS OF SHINGLES:
- Blisters may become infected or leave a scar
- Blisters near or in the eye can cause lasting eye damage or blindness
- Brief paralysis of the face
- Hearing loss
- Very rarely, swelling of the brain (encephalitis)

SHINGLES VACCINE AND CONTAGION:
- Shingles vaccine is recommended for those aged 60 and older
- Shingles is NOT contagious
- However, one CAN catch chickenpox from someone with shingles

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ KEY EXAM POINTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. Less than 50% of adults 50–64 have a chronic condition; 90% of adults 75+ do (Cohen, 2011)
2. Older women are MORE likely than older men to have a chronic condition (88% vs. 83%) (CDC, 2009)
3. Persons over 65 account for 60% of newly diagnosed cancers and 70% of all cancer deaths (Berger et al., 2006)
4. Chemotherapy can compromise cognitive function and may exacerbate dementia in elderly patients
5. Frail individuals are NOT candidates for more toxic forms of chemotherapy
6. Depression in elderly cancer patients is associated with decreased survival rates
7. Atherosclerosis is NOT part of normal aging — it is caused by disease
8. Arthritis/rheumatic conditions = MOST COMMON cause of disability among US adults for the past 15 years
9. 62% of adults with arthritis are aged 65 and up; almost 1 in 2 have mobility limitations
10. Osteoporosis: After age 50, 1 in 2 women and 1 in 4 men will experience a related fracture
11. More than 53 million US adults have osteoporosis or are at high risk from low bone mass
12. Kyphosis = hunched posture caused by weakening spine bones (consequence of osteoporosis)
13. COPD is progressive, incurable, and kills more than 120,000 people/year
14. Cigarette smoking = leading cause of COPD
15. AAT deficiency: recessive genetic condition affecting 1 in 1,600–5,000 people; wrongly shaped AAT protein cannot leave liver, increasing COPD and cirrhosis risk
16. Shingles caused by varicella-zoster virus (VZV); affects 1 in 3 adults; half of cases in adults 60+
17. Shingles: develops on only ONE SIDE of body; lasts 3–5 weeks
18. Post-herpetic neuralgia (PHN): ongoing pain after shingles rash — more common the older one is
19. Shingles vaccine recommended at age 60+; shingles is NOT contagious but CAN transmit chickenpox
20. Major cardiovascular disease death percentage increases with age: from 24.3% (age 45–54) to 38.9% (age 85+)

notes.title: Chapter 24: Late Adulthood – Cognitive Development (Berger, 11e)
notes.body:
CHAPTER 24: LATE ADULTHOOD — COGNITIVE DEVELOPMENT
The Developing Person Through the Life Span | Kathleen Stassen Berger | Eleventh Edition

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
THE BRAIN DURING ADULTHOOD
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• NEW BRAIN CELLS
  - Neurons form and dendrites grow in adulthood, particularly in the hippocampus.
  - New neurons provide cognitive adaptability to succeed in the context of challenging and changing environments.
  - Growth of the brain is slow, limited, and not sufficient to restore itself to its younger state.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ATROPHY RANKING
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
NOTE: Not All Average — A team of neuroscientists in Scotland (Farrell et al., 2009) published images of the brains of healthy 65- to 70-year-olds. The images show normal brain loss from the lowest (5th percentile) to the highest (95th percentile).

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ONGOING DEVELOPMENT
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• NEW CELLS
  - New neurons appear in the adult brain particularly in the hippocampus (memory center) and the olfactory (smell) areas.
  - Dendrite growth is also variable and crucial.
  - New dendrites are evidence of lifelong plasticity.

NOTE: Senescence reduces the production of neurotransmitters — including glutamate, acetylcholine, serotonin, and especially dopamine — that allow a nerve impulse to jump quickly across the synaptic gap from one neuron to another. Neural fluid decreases, myelination thins, and cerebral blood circulates more slowly. Some experts believe that processing speed is a basic element of the g. Shrinkage is particularly notable in the hippocampus and the areas of the prefrontal cortex that are needed for planning, inhibiting unwanted responses, and coordinating thoughts in every part of the brain. The volume of gray matter (crucial for processing new experiences) is reduced, in part because the cortex becomes thinner with every decade of adulthood.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SENESCENCE AND THE BRAIN
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• SLOWER THINKING
  - Attributed to overall slowdown in reaction time, movement, speech, and thought.
  - Neurotransmitter production is reduced.
  - Neural fluid decreases, myelination thins, and cerebral blood circulates more slowly.

• SMALLER BRAINS
  - Total volume of the brain becomes smaller.
  - Gray matter volume is reduced in every part of the brain and white-matter lesions form.
  - Transmission of brain impulses is disrupted.

NOTE: Sensation precedes perception, which precedes comprehension. Some information never reaches sensory memory in older people because the senses never detect the stimuli. The brain automatically fills in missed sights and sounds. Older people may believe that they see and hear whatever is important, but vital information may be distorted or lost without the person realizing it. People can compensate for some sensory decline, which would improve input.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
INFORMATION PROCESSING AFTER AGE 65 (PART 1): INPUT
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Sensory loss may be undetected as the brain fills in missed sights and sounds.
• Interpretation of facial expressions, especially anger and sadness, may be impaired.
• Understanding of speech may be hampered by reduced hearing and vision acuity.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
INFORMATION PROCESSING AFTER AGE 65 (PART 2): MEMORY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Stereotype threat impedes memorial processes; suspecting memory loss can impact memory.
• Memory loss can be normal and pathological, but generally explicit memory loss is greater than implicit memory loss.
• Source amnesia may contribute to less analysis of information when elders cannot remember the origin of a fact.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
INFORMATION PROCESSING AFTER AGE 65 (PART 3): PROSPECTIVE MEMORY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Includes the ability to quickly shift mentally among tasks.
• Involves remembering to perform a future task.
• Fades notably with age.
• May be aided through routines.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
INFORMATION PROCESSING AFTER AGE 65 (PART 4): CONTROL PROCESSES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Are involved in the underlying impairment of cognition in late adulthood, especially impaired retrieval.
• Include executive function of the brain: Selective attention, strategic judgment, and then appropriate action.
• Shift as analysis and forethought give way to reliance on prior knowledge, general principles, and rules of thumb.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
OUTPUT (PART 1)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Gradual decline in output of primary mental abilities (e.g., verbal meaning, spatial orientation, inductive reasoning, number ability, word fluency) is normal.
• In daily life, output is usually verbal.
• Two important modifiers are health and training.

NOTE: Ecological validity: The idea that cognition should be measured in settings that are realistic and that the abilities measured should be those needed in real life. This may be key to accurately measuring cognition in the elderly. Fundamental ecological issue: Addresses the question: What should be assessed — pure, abstract thinking or practical, contextual thought, depersonalized abilities, or everyday actions?

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
OUTPUT (PART 2): COGNITIVE TESTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Measured output of five primary mental abilities show decline, beginning around age 60 (Seattle Longitudinal Study).
• Tests are normed and validated on younger adult output.
• Awareness of ecological validity may help restructure memory research.

NOTE: When they were together, younger partners were more accurate than older ones at knowing their partner's emotions, but older partners were as good as younger ones when they were apart. This study used 'smartphone experience sampling,' buzzing both partners simultaneously to ask how they and their partner felt. Interestingly, differences were found with age but not length of relationship — 5, 10, 20, or 30 years of togetherness did not necessarily increase empathy when apart, but men who were in their 70s were better at absent mood assessment than men in their 20s.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ALWAYS ON MY MIND
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
NOTE: DSM-5: Neurocognitive disorders (NCDs) — Either major neurocognitive disorder or mild neurocognitive disorder, depending upon the severity of symptoms.
• Mild NCD, formerly called mild cognitive impairment (MCI)
  - Older adults who have significant problems with memory, but who still function well at work and home.
  - Forgetfulness and loss of verbal fluency that often comes before the first stage of AD.
  - About half will become demented, but some stabilize with mild impairment and others regain their cognitive abilities.
  - Measuring mild loss: biological indicators (biomarkers); clinical judgment of professionals.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
NEUROCOGNITIVE DISORDERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• AGEISM OF WORDS
  - Lines between normal age-related problems, mild disorder, and major disorder are not clearly defined, and the symptoms vary depending on the specifics of brain loss and context.
  - Caution is advised in the use of words to describe cognitive decline in the elderly.
  - Older terms: Senile, dementia.
  - Current classification: DSM-5.

NOTE: See Table 24.1 for additional information about stages of Alzheimer's disease.
• Plaques: Clumps of a protein called beta-amyloid, found in brain tissues surrounding the neurons.
• Tangles: Twisted masses of threads made of a protein called tau within the neurons of the brain.
• Alzheimer's disease (AD): Most common cause of NCD, characterized by gradual deterioration of memory and personality and marked by the formation of plaques of beta-amyloid protein and tangles of tau protein in the brain.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
THE PREVALENCE OF NCDs
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• How many people suffer from neurocognitive disorders in their older years?
  - 50 million worldwide are affected.
  - 60 percent in low-income nations.
  - Approximately 9 percent of the world's population over age 65.
• Decline in prevalence has been found in England, Wales, and Sweden.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
THE MANY NEUROCOGNITIVE DISEASES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Alzheimer's disease
• Vascular disease
• Frontotemporal NCDs
• Other disorders

NOTE: Hopeful Brains — Even the brain without symptoms (a) might eventually develop Alzheimer's disease, but people with a certain dominant gene definitely will. They have no symptoms in early adulthood (b), some symptoms in middle adulthood (c), and stage-five Alzheimer's disease (d) before old age. Research finds early brain markers (such as those shown here) that predict the disease. This is not always accurate, but it may soon lead to early treatment that halts AD, not only in those genetically vulnerable but in everyone.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE (AD)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Alzheimer's disease (AD) is diagnosed in every large nation.
• Severe and worsening memory loss is the main symptom.
• Most cases begin much later and many genes have some impact (e.g., SORL1 and ApoE4).
• Diagnosis is not definitive until autopsy.
• Five stages of Alzheimer's disease.

NOTE: Vascular disease is more common than Alzheimer's disease for those over age 90 but not for the young-old. Vascular disorders correlate with the ApoE4 allele (Cramer & Procaccio, 2012) and, for some of the elderly, are caused by surgery that requires general anesthesia. This may cause a ministroke, which, added to reduced cognitive reserve, damages the brain.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
MAJOR AND MINOR IMPAIRMENT (PART 1)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Computer graphic shows a vertical slice through a brain ravaged by Alzheimer's disease (left) compared with a similar slice of a normal brain (right).
• The diseased brain is shrunken because neurons have degenerated.
• The red indicates plaques and tangles.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PREVALENCE OF NCD (PART 2): VASCULAR DISEASE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Formerly called vascular or multi-infarct dementia.
• Characterized by sporadic, and progressive, loss of intellectual functioning caused by repeated infarcts, or temporary obstructions of blood vessels, which prevent sufficient blood from reaching the brain.
• Correlates with the ApoE4 allele and, for some of the elderly, is caused by surgery that requires general anesthesia.
• May cause transient ischemic attacks (TIAs).

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PREVALENCE OF NCD (PART 3): FRONTOTEMPORAL NCD
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Also called frontotemporal lobar degeneration.
• Is deterioration of the frontal lobes and the amygdala.
• May be a cause of 15 percent of all major NCDs.
• Tends to occur before age 70.
• Characterized by emotional and personality changes in which compassion, self-awareness, and judgment fade.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
THE PROGRESSION OF ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE AND VASCULAR DISEASE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• The pattern of decline for each disease is different.
• Victims of AD show steady, gradual decline.
• Those who suffer from vascular disease get suddenly worse, improve somewhat, and then experience another serious loss (stepwise/sporadic pattern).

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
OTHER DISORDERS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• PARKINSON'S DISEASE
  - Does not always lead to NCD.
  - Starts with rigidity or tremor of the muscles as neurons that produce dopamine degenerate.
  - Younger adults with Parkinson's disease may avoid cognitive problems for years.

• LEWY BODY DISEASE
  - Named after round deposits of protein (Lewy bodies) in the neuron.
  - Numerous and dispersed throughout the brain.
  - Motor movements and cognition are impacted.
  - Main symptom is loss of inhibition.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CAUSES OF NCDs
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Causes for types of NCDs include:
  - Huntington's disease
  - Multiple sclerosis
  - Severe head injury
  - Last stages of syphilis
  - AIDS
  - Chronic traumatic encephalopathy (CTE)
  - Bovine spongiform encephalopathy (BSE, mad cow disease)

NOTE: The average elderly person in the United States sees a doctor eight times a year.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PREVENTING IMPAIRMENT
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Severe brain damage cannot be reversed, but the rate of decline and some symptoms can be treated.
• Declining risk for NCD incidence or prevalence found in high-income countries.
• Education, exercise, medication, and good health may ameliorate mild losses and prevent worse ones.
• Avoidance of specific pathogens and toxins is critical.
• Early, accurate diagnosis leads to more effective treatment.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
REVERSIBLE IMPAIRMENT (PART 1)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Accurate diagnosis is crucial when a person is wrongly thought to have NCD.
• The most common reversible cause of NCD symptoms is depression.
• Malnutrition, dehydration, alcohol, brain tumors, physical illness, and overmedication can cause NCD-like symptoms.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
REVERSIBLE IMPAIRMENT (PART 2)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• With age, bodies become less efficient at digesting food and using its nutrients.
• POLYPHARMACY
  - When the elderly are prescribed several drugs and the side effects can cause NCD symptoms.
  - Some drug combinations can produce confusion and psychotic behavior.
  - Underuse of medication may also have adverse effects.
