NOTE: The elderly may have an advantage in developing wisdom, particularly if they have: (1) dedicated their lives to the 'understanding of life,' (2) learned from their experiences, and (3) become more mature and integrated.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
NEW COGNITIVE DEVELOPMENT: ERIKSON AND MASLOW
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• INTEGRITY VERSUS DESPAIR
  - The final stage in Erikson's model.
  - Older people gain interest in the arts, in children, and in human experience as a whole.

• SELF-ACTUALIZATION
  - The final stage in Maslow's hierarchy of needs.
  - Characterized by aesthetic, creative, philosophical, and spiritual understanding.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
AESTHETIC SENSE AND CREATIVITY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• Elderly artists with extraordinary talents did not feel their abilities had been impaired by age.
• Examples:
  - Michelangelo and the Sistine Chapel — age 75
  - Verdi and Falstaff — age 80
  - Frank Lloyd Wright and the Guggenheim Museum design — age 91
• Artistic expression may aid social skills, resilience, and even brain health.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
LOOKING BACK: LIFE REVIEW AND WISDOM
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
• LIFE REVIEW
  - An examination of one's own role in the history of human life, engaged in by many elderly people.
  - Results are almost always positive.

• WISDOM
  - Expert knowledge system dealing with the conduct and understanding of life.
  - Life review, self-actualization, and integrity are considered parts of wisdom.
  - Some elderly people are unusually wise.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ KEY EXAM POINTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. New neurons form primarily in the HIPPOCAMPUS and OLFACTORY areas; new dendrites demonstrate lifelong plasticity.
2. Senescence reduces key neurotransmitters: glutamate, acetylcholine, serotonin, and especially DOPAMINE.
3. Brain changes in aging: reduced gray matter, white-matter lesions, thinning myelination, slower cerebral blood flow.
4. Information processing sequence: Sensation → Perception → Comprehension; older brains fill in missed sights/sounds.
5. Types of memory affected: EXPLICIT memory declines more than IMPLICIT memory; SOURCE AMNESIA is common.
6. PROSPECTIVE MEMORY (future task remembering) fades notably with age; aided by routines.
7. CONTROL PROCESSES: include executive function — selective attention, strategic judgment, appropriate action.
8. PRIMARY MENTAL ABILITIES decline begins ~age 60 (Seattle Longitudinal Study); include verbal meaning, spatial orientation, inductive reasoning, number ability, word fluency.
9. ECOLOGICAL VALIDITY: cognition should be measured in realistic settings relevant to real-life abilities.
10. DSM-5 classification: MAJOR NCD vs. MILD NCD (formerly MCI); about half of mild NCD cases progress to dementia.
11. ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE: most common NCD; characterized by plaques (beta-amyloid) and tangles (tau protein); diagnosed definitively only at AUTOPSY; genes include SORL1 and ApoE4; five stages; steady, gradual decline.
12. VASCULAR DISEASE: sporadic/stepwise decline; caused by repeated infarcts; correlates with ApoE4; can cause TIAs; more common than AD in those over age 90.
13. FRONTOTEMPORAL NCD: deterioration of frontal lobes and amygdala; ~15% of all major NCDs; tends to occur BEFORE AGE 70; personality and emotional changes (loss of compassion, self-awareness, judgment).
14. LEWY BODY DISEASE: round protein deposits (Lewy bodies) throughout brain; main symptom = loss of inhibition; affects motor and cognition.
15. PARKINSON'S DISEASE: dopamine-producing neurons degenerate; starts with rigidity/tremor; does not always lead to NCD.
16. REVERSIBLE NCD CAUSES: most common = DEPRESSION; also malnutrition, dehydration, alcohol, brain tumors, physical illness, overmedication (polypharmacy).
17. POLYPHARMACY: multiple prescribed drugs causing NCD-like symptoms; some drug combinations produce confusion and psychotic behavior.
18. NCD PREVALENCE: 50 million worldwide; 9% of world population over 65; 60% in low-income nations; declining in England, Wales, and Sweden.
19. PREVENTION: education, exercise, medication, good health, avoidance of pathogens/toxins, early accurate diagnosis.
20. ERIKSON's final stage: INTEGRITY vs. DESPAIR; MASLOW's final stage: SELF-ACTUALIZATION (aesthetic, creative, philosophical, spiritual).
21. WISDOM = expert knowledge system about life; includes life review, self-actualization, and integrity.
22. Famous elderly creators: Michelangelo (age 75), Verdi (age 80), Frank Lloyd Wright (age 91).
23. Stereotype threat: merely suspecting memory loss can negatively impact memory performance in elders.
24. Farrell et al. (2009): Scottish neuroscientists documented normal brain atrophy range (5th–95th percentile) in healthy 65–70-year-olds.

notes.title: Chapter 25: Late Adulthood – Psychosocial Development (Berger, 11th Ed.)
notes.body:
CHAPTER 25: LATE ADULTHOOD – PSYCHOSOCIAL DEVELOPMENT
The Developing Person Through the Life Span | Kathleen Stassen Berger | Eleventh Edition

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
THEORIES OF LATE ADULTHOOD (PART 1) — SELF THEORIES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Self theories emphasize the core self, or the search to maintain one's integrity and identity.
- Integrity versus despair:
  • Manifests itself in a life review.
  • Older adults seek to integrate their unique experiences with their vision of community (Erikson).
  • Accepting one's whole life and reflecting on that life in a positive manner is critical.
  • Retaining identity despite life changes and decline is critical.

NOTE: As self theory contends, identity achievement echoes lifelong, with integrity attained for those who achieve identity, and despair (Erikson's opposite course) likely for those who never achieved identity. The critical need in late adulthood is "retaining a sense of identity despite decline."

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
THEORIES OF LATE ADULTHOOD (PART 2) — HOLDING ON TO THE SELF
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Most older people feel their personalities and attitudes have remained stable over their life span, even as they recognize the physical changes of their bodies.
- Objects and places become more precious, as a way to hold on to identity.

NOTE: Trash or Treasure? Tryphona Flood, threatened with eviction, admitted she's a hoarder and got help from Megan Tolen, shown here discussing what in this four-room apartment can be discarded. Flood sits on the only spot of her bed that is not covered with stuff. This photo was taken midway through a three-year effort to clean out the apartment—the clutter was worse a year earlier.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
THEORIES OF LATE ADULTHOOD (PART 3) — SOCIOEMOTIONAL SELECTIVITY THEORY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Socioemotional selectivity theory:
  • Older people prioritize regulation of their own emotions.
  • Seek familiar social contacts who reinforce generativity, pride, and joy.
- Positivity effect:
  • Tendency for elderly people to perceive, prefer, and remember positive images and experiences more than negative ones.

NOTE: Positivity effect is an outgrowth of socioemotional activity and selective optimization. Research on what people hope for themselves (the ideal self) and how they perceive themselves (the real self) finds that, with age, the two selves come closer together. As self theory contends, self-acceptance leads to happiness.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
THEORIES OF LATE ADULTHOOD (PART 4) — STRATIFICATION THEORIES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Stratification theories:
  • Emphasize that social forces limit individual choices and affect a person's ability to function in late adulthood.
  • Past stratification continues to limit life in various ways.
  • Every stereotype adds to stratification.

NOTE: Ageism takes many forms. Some cultures are youth-oriented, devaluing the old, while others are the opposite. These twin sisters are lucky to be alive: They were born in rural China in 1905, a period when most female twins died. Now, at age 103, they are fortunate again, venerated because they have lived so long.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
THEORIES OF LATE ADULTHOOD (PART 5) — STRATIFICATION BY GENDER
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Irrational, gender-based fear may limit women's independence throughout life.
- Young women typically marry older men.
- Men seek medical help less than women.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
THEORIES OF LATE ADULTHOOD (PART 6) — ETHNIC STRATIFICATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Stratification theory contends that factors such as education, health, employment, and place of residence create large discrepancies in income by old age.
- Immigrant elders often face multiple challenges related to:
  • Cultural differences in care by children
  • Housing
  • Stereotypes

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
THEORIES OF LATE ADULTHOOD (PART 7) — INCOME STRATIFICATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Direct effects of poverty magnify stratification of gender, ethnicity, and age.
- Employment is directly and indirectly related to income in late adulthood (e.g., Social Security benefits).
- Stress and accumulating disadvantages are increasingly limiting as age advances.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
THEORIES OF LATE ADULTHOOD (PART 8) — AGE STRATIFICATION: OPPOSING THEORIES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Disengagement theory:
  • Aging makes a person's social sphere increasingly narrow.
  • Results in role relinquishment, withdrawal, and passivity.
- Activity theory:
  • Elderly people want and need to remain active in a variety of social spheres—with relatives, friends, and community groups.
  • Become withdrawn only unwillingly, as a result of ageism.

NOTE: Industrialized nations segregate elderly people, gradually shunting them out of the mainstream of society as they grow older. Segregation by age harms everyone because it creates socialization deficits for members of all age groups.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ACTIVITIES IN LATE ADULTHOOD (PART 1) — WORKING
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- The activities of older people are intense and varied.
- The psychological benefits of work can be obtained through volunteer work.
- Work provides social support and status, boosting self-esteem.
- For many people, employment allows generativity.

NOTE: Not Ready to Quit — An increasing number of elders are still in the labor force. Why? Do they enjoy the work? Do they need the money? Are they unable to retire?

notes.title: Chapter 24: Late Adulthood – Cognitive Development (Berger, 11th Edition)
notes.body:
CHAPTER 24: LATE ADULTHOOD: COGNITIVE DEVELOPMENT
The Developing Person Through the Life Span | Kathleen Stassen Berger | Eleventh Edition

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

THE BRAIN DURING ADULTHOOD
- New brain cells: Neurons form and dendrites grow in adulthood, particularly in the hippocampus.
- New neurons provide cognitive adaptability to succeed in the context of challenging and changing environments.
- Growth of the brain is slow, limited, and not sufficient to restore itself to its younger state.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

ATROPHY RANKING
NOTE: Not All Average — A team of neuroscientists in Scotland (Farrell et al., 2009) published images of the brains of healthy 65- to 70-year-olds. The images show normal brain loss from the lowest (5th percentile) to the highest (95th percentile).

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

ONGOING DEVELOPMENT
- New cells: New neurons appear in the adult brain particularly in the hippocampus (memory center) and the olfactory (smell) areas.
- Dendrite growth is also variable and crucial.
- New dendrites are evidence of lifelong plasticity.

NOTE: Senescence reduces the production of neurotransmitters—including glutamate, acetylcholine, serotonin, and especially dopamine—that allow a nerve impulse to jump quickly across the synaptic gap from one neuron to another. Neural fluid decreases, myelination thins, and cerebral blood circulates more slowly. Some experts believe that processing speed is a basic element of the g. Shrinkage is particularly notable in the hippocampus and the areas of the prefrontal cortex that are needed for planning, inhibiting unwanted responses, and coordinating thoughts. In every part of the brain, the volume of gray matter (crucial for processing new experiences) is reduced, in part because the cortex becomes thinner with every decade of adulthood.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SENESCENCE AND THE BRAIN
- Slower thinking is attributed to overall slowdown in reaction time, movement, speech, and thought.
- Neurotransmitter production is reduced.
- Neural fluid decreases, myelination thins, and cerebral blood circulates more slowly.
- Smaller brains:
  • Total volume of the brain becomes smaller.
  • Gray matter volume is reduced in every part of the brain and white-matter lesions form.
  • Transmission of brain impulses is disrupted.

NOTE: Sensation precedes perception, which precedes comprehension. Some information never reaches sensory memory in older people because the senses never detect the stimuli. The brain automatically fills in missed sights and sounds. Older people may believe that they see and hear whatever is important, but vital information may be distorted or lost without the person realizing it. People can compensate for some sensory decline, which would improve input.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

INFORMATION PROCESSING AFTER AGE 65 (PART 1) — INPUT
- Sensory loss may be undetected as the brain fills in missed sights and sounds.
- Interpretation of facial expressions, especially anger and sadness, may be impaired.
- Understanding of speech may be hampered by reduced hearing and vision acuity.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

INFORMATION PROCESSING AFTER AGE 65 (PART 2) — MEMORY
- Stereotype threat impedes memorial processes; suspecting memory loss can impact memory.
- Memory loss can be normal and pathological, but generally explicit memory loss is greater than implicit memory loss.
- Source amnesia may contribute to less analysis of information when elders cannot remember the origin of a fact.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

INFORMATION PROCESSING AFTER AGE 65 (PART 3) — PROSPECTIVE MEMORY
- Includes the ability to quickly shift mentally among tasks.
- Involves remembering to perform a future task.
- Fades notably with age.
- May be aided through routines.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

INFORMATION PROCESSING AFTER AGE 65 (PART 4) — CONTROL PROCESSES
- Are involved in the underlying impairment of cognition in late adulthood, especially impaired retrieval.
- Include executive function of the brain: Selective attention, strategic judgment, and then appropriate action.
- Shift as analysis and forethought give way to reliance on prior knowledge, general principles, and rules of thumb.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

OUTPUT (PART 1)
- Gradual decline in output of primary mental abilities (e.g., verbal meaning, spatial orientation, inductive reasoning, number ability, word fluency) is normal.
- In daily life, output is usually verbal.
- Two important modifiers are health and training.

NOTE: Ecological validity: The idea that cognition should be measured in settings that are realistic and that the abilities measured should be those needed in real life. This may be key to accurately measuring cognition in the elderly. Fundamental ecological issue: Addresses the question — What should be assessed—pure, abstract thinking or practical, contextual thought, depersonalized abilities, or everyday actions?

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

OUTPUT (PART 2)
- Cognitive tests: Measured output of five primary mental abilities show decline, beginning around age 60 (Seattle Longitudinal Study).
- Tests are normed and validated on younger adult output.
- Awareness of ecological validity may help restructure memory research.

NOTE: When they were together, younger partners were more accurate than older ones at knowing their partner's emotions, but older partners were as good as younger ones when they were apart. This study used smartphone experience sampling, buzzing both partners simultaneously to ask how they and their partner felt. Interestingly, differences were found with age but not length of relationship—5, 10, 20, or 30 years of partnership.

notes.title: Religion and Spirituality | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
RELIGION AND SPIRITUALITY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

HEALTH AND RELIGION/SPIRITUALITY — OVERVIEW
• Grzywacz and Keyes (2004): In addition to personal health behaviors (regular exercise, healthy weight, not smoking), social behaviors — including involvement in religious-related activities — have been shown to be POSITIVELY related to optimal health.
• It is not only those involved in a specific religion who benefit — those identified as being SPIRITUAL also benefit.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

DEFINITIONS (Greenfield, Vaillant, and Marks, 2009)
• RELIGIOSITY: Engaging with a formal religious group's doctrines, values, traditions, and co-members.
• SPIRITUALITY: An individual's intrapsychic sense of connection with something transcendent (that which exists apart from and not limited by the material universe) and the subsequent feelings of:
  - Awe
  - Gratitude
  - Compassion
  - Forgiveness

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SPIRITUALITY AND PSYCHOLOGICAL WELL-BEING
• Vaillant (2008): Research demonstrates a STRONG relationship between spirituality and psychological well-being, IRRESPECTIVE of an individual's religious participation.
• Sawatzky, Ratner, & Chiu (2005): Spirituality was related to a HIGHER QUALITY OF LIFE for both individuals AND societies.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

NATIONAL SURVEY OF MIDLIFE IN THE UNITED STATES (2005) — FINDINGS
• Source: Greenfield et al. (2009), based on reports from the 2005 National Survey of Midlife in the United States.
• Higher levels of SPIRITUALITY were associated with:
  - Lower levels of NEGATIVE AFFECT
  - Higher levels of POSITIVE AFFECT
  - Personal growth
  - Purpose in life
  - Positive relationships with others
  - Self-acceptance
  - Environmental mastery
  - Autonomy
• Higher levels of FORMAL RELIGIOUS PARTICIPATION were associated with:
  - Higher levels of PURPOSE IN LIFE — only among OLDER ADULTS
  - PERSONAL GROWTH — only among OLDER ADULTS
  - LOWER levels of AUTONOMY
• Summary: Formal religious participation and spirituality relate DIFFERENTLY to an individual's overall psychological well-being.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

AGE DIFFERENCES IN RELIGION/SPIRITUALITY
• Beit-Hallahmi & Argyle (1998): OLDER individuals identify religion/spirituality as being MORE important in their lives than YOUNGER individuals.
• Explanations for this age difference (Greenfield et al., 2009):
  1. Religion and spirituality assist older individuals in COPING WITH AGE-RELATED LOSSES.
  2. Provide OPPORTUNITIES FOR SOCIALIZATION and social support in later life.
  3. COHORT EFFECT: Older individuals were socialized MORE to be religious and spiritual than those who are younger.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

GENDER DIFFERENCES IN RELIGION/SPIRITUALITY (UNITED STATES)
• de Vaus & McAllister (1987): In the United States, WOMEN report identifying as being MORE religious and spiritual than MEN.
• Pew Research Center (2016) — U.S. Statistics:
  - Women more likely to say religion is VERY IMPORTANT: 60% (women) vs. 47% (men)
  - Women more likely to say they PRAY DAILY: 64% (women) vs. 47% (men)
  - Women more likely to ATTEND RELIGIOUS SERVICES at least once a week: 40% (women) vs. 32% (men)
• Theories to explain gender difference (Greenfield et al., 2009):
  1. Women may benefit MORE from the SOCIAL-RELATIONAL aspects of religion/spirituality because social relationships more strongly influence women's mental health.
  2. Women have been SOCIALIZED to internalize the behaviors linked with religious values — such as COOPERATION and NURTURANCE — more than males.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

WORLDWIDE GENDER DIFFERENCES IN RELIGION/SPIRITUALITY
• Pew Research Center (2016): Conducted surveys in 84 COUNTRIES between 2008 and 2015 to measure religious beliefs and practices of men and women worldwide.
• Overall: An estimated 83% of WOMEN worldwide identified with a religion compared with 80% of MEN.
  - This equaled 97 MILLION MORE WOMEN than men identifying with a religion.
  - There were NO countries in which men were more religious than women by 2 percentage points or more.
• CHRISTIANS:
  - Women reported HIGHER rates of weekly church attendance.
  - Women reported HIGHER rates of daily prayer.
• MUSLIMS:
  - Muslim women and Muslim men showed SIMILAR levels of religiousness, EXCEPT frequency of attendance at worship services.
  - Because of RELIGIOUS NORMS, Muslim MEN worshiped at a mosque MORE OFTEN than Muslim women.
• JEWISH:
  - Jewish MEN attended a synagogue MORE OFTEN than Jewish women.
  - In ORTHODOX JUDAISM, communal worship services cannot take place unless a MINYAN (a quorum of at least 10 JEWISH MEN) is present — thus ensuring men will have HIGH rates of attendance.
  - Only in ISRAEL — where roughly 22% of all Jewish adults self-identify as ORTHODOX — did a higher percentage of MEN than women report engaging in DAILY PRAYER.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
1. Grzywacz & Keyes (2004): Religious activity is positively related to optimal health, alongside personal health behaviors.
2. RELIGIOSITY = formal group engagement (doctrines, values, traditions, co-members).
3. SPIRITUALITY = intrapsychic connection to something TRANSCENDENT + feelings of awe, gratitude, compassion, forgiveness.
4. Vaillant (2008): Spirituality → psychological well-being regardless of religious participation.
5. Sawatzky, Ratner, & Chiu (2005): Spirituality → higher quality of life for individuals AND societies.
6. 2005 National Survey of Midlife (Greenfield et al., 2009): Spirituality associated with 7 positive psychological outcomes; formal religion only linked to purpose in life and personal growth in OLDER ADULTS and lower autonomy.
7. Age: Older adults value religion/spirituality more — due to coping with loss, socialization, and cohort effects (Beit-Hallahmi & Argyle, 1998; Greenfield et al., 2009).
8. Gender (U.S.): Women > Men on all measures of religiosity (60% vs. 47% importance; 64% vs. 47% daily prayer; 40% vs. 32% weekly attendance) — Pew 2016.
9. Worldwide (Pew 2016, 84 countries, 2008–2015): 83% women vs. 80% men identify with religion = 97 million more women.
10. No countries found where men are more religious than women by ≥2 percentage points.
11. Exception: Muslim men attend mosque MORE than Muslim women (religious norms); Jewish men attend synagogue MORE than Jewish women (MINYAN requirement — quorum of 10 men).
12. Israel (22% Orthodox Jewish adults): Men report MORE daily prayer than women — rare exception to global pattern.

notes.title: Women in Midlife | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
WOMEN IN MIDLIFE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

AGING AND WOMEN IN WESTERN SOCIETY
- In Western society, aging for women is MUCH MORE STRESSFUL than for men
- Society emphasizes youthful beauty and attractiveness for women (Slevin, 2010)
- The description that aging men are viewed as "distinguished" and aging women are viewed as "old" is referred to as the DOUBLE STANDARD OF AGING (Teuscher & Teuscher, 2006)
- Women have traditionally been valued for their REPRODUCTIVE CAPABILITIES → may be considered old once they are POSTMENOPAUSAL
- Men have traditionally been valued for their ACHIEVEMENTS, COMPETENCE, and POWER → not considered old until they are PHYSICALLY UNABLE TO WORK (Carroll, 2016)
- Consequently, women experience MORE FEAR, ANXIETY, and CONCERN about their identity as they age
- Women may feel pressure to prove themselves as productive and valuable members of society (Bromberger, Kravitz, & Chang, 2013)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

VARIATIONS IN ATTITUDES ABOUT AGING: RACE, CULTURE, AND SEXUAL ORIENTATION
- Attitudes about aging vary by RACE, CULTURE, and SEXUAL ORIENTATION
- In SOME CULTURES, aging women gain GREATER SOCIAL STATUS
  • Example: As Asian women age, they attain GREATER RESPECT and have GREATER AUTHORITY in the household (Fung, 2013)
- Compared to WHITE WOMEN, BLACK and LATINA WOMEN possess FEWER STEREOTYPES about aging (Schuler et al., 2008)
- LESBIANS are MORE POSITIVE about aging and looking older than HETEROSEXUALS (Slevin, 2010)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

ROLE OF MEDIA IN WOMEN'S ATTITUDES TOWARD AGING
- The impact of MEDIA plays a significant role in how women view aging
- Media promotes anti-aging by:
  • Selling ANTI-AGING PRODUCTS
  • Supporting COSMETIC SURGERIES to look younger
- Source: Gilleard & Higgs, 2000

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

KEY RESEARCHERS AND CITATIONS
- Slevin (2010) — aging more stressful for women; lesbians more positive about aging
- Teuscher & Teuscher (2006) — double standard of aging
- Carroll (2016) — men valued for achievements/competence/power; not considered old until physically unable to work
- Bromberger, Kravitz, & Chang (2013) — women's fear, anxiety, concern about identity with aging
- Fung (2013) — Asian women gain respect and household authority with age
- Schuler et al. (2008) — Black and Latina women have fewer stereotypes about aging than white women
- Gilleard & Higgs (2000) — media's role in selling anti-aging products and cosmetic surgery

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
1. DOUBLE STANDARD OF AGING: Aging men = "distinguished"; aging women = "old" — coined/referenced by Teuscher & Teuscher (2006)
2. Women traditionally valued for REPRODUCTIVE CAPABILITIES → considered old after MENOPAUSE
3. Men traditionally valued for ACHIEVEMENTS, COMPETENCE, POWER → considered old only when PHYSICALLY UNABLE TO WORK (Carroll, 2016)
4. Women experience MORE FEAR and ANXIETY about aging due to societal pressures (Bromberger, Kravitz, & Chang, 2013)
5. ASIAN WOMEN gain greater respect and household authority as they age (Fung, 2013)
6. BLACK and LATINA women hold FEWER aging stereotypes compared to WHITE women (Schuler et al., 2008)
7. LESBIANS are MORE POSITIVE about aging and looking older than HETEROSEXUALS (Slevin, 2010)
8. MEDIA contributes to negative aging views through anti-aging products and promotion of cosmetic surgery (Gilleard & Higgs, 2000)
9. Aging stress for women is largely tied to Western society's emphasis on YOUTHFUL BEAUTY and ATTRACTIVENESS

notes.title: Friendships Across Adulthood | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
FRIENDSHIPS — OVERVIEW
- Adults of all ages who reported having a confidante or close friend with whom they could share personal feelings and concerns believed these friends contributed to a sense of belonging, security, and overall wellbeing (Dunér & Nordstrom, 2007)
- Having a close friend is a factor in significantly lower odds of psychiatric morbidity including depression and anxiety (Harrison, Barrow, Gask, & Creed, 1999; Newton et al., 2008)
- The availability of a close friend has also been shown to lessen the adverse effects of stress on health (Kouzis & Eaton, 1998; Hawkley et al., 2008; Tower & Kasl, 1995)
- Poor social connectedness in adulthood is associated with a larger risk of premature mortality than cigarette smoking, obesity, and excessive alcohol use (Holt-Lunstad, Smith, & Layton, 2010)

━━━

FEMALE FRIENDSHIPS AND SOCIAL SUPPORT AT MIDLIFE
- Female friendships and social support networks at midlife contribute significantly to a woman's feeling of life satisfaction and well-being (Borzumato-Gainey, Kennedy, McCabe, & Degges-White, 2009)
- Degges-White and Myers (2006) found that women who have supportive people in their life experience greater life satisfaction than do those who live a more solitary life
- A friendship network or the presence of a confidant have both been identified for their importance to women's mental health (Baruch & Brooks-Gunn, 1984)
- With numerous caretaking responsibilities at home, it may be difficult for women to find time and energy to enhance the friendships that provide an increased sense of life satisfaction (Borzumato-Gainey et al., 2009)

━━━

MALE FRIENDSHIPS AND ALCOHOL AT MIDLIFE
- Emslie, Hunt and Lyons (2013) found that for men in midlife, the shared consumption of alcohol was important to creating and maintaining male friendships
- Drinking with friends was justified as a way for men to:
  • Talk to each other
  • Provide social support
  • Relax
  • Improve mood
- Although the social support provided when men drink together can be helpful, the role of alcohol in male friendships can lead to health damaging behavior from excessive drinking

━━━

SOCIAL RELATIONSHIPS: EARLY TO MIDDLE ADULTHOOD
- The importance of social relationships begins in early adulthood by laying down a foundation for strong social connectedness and facilitating comfort with intimacy (Erikson, 1959)
- Carmichael, Reis, and Duberstein (2015) assessed individuals at age 50 on measures of:
  • Social connection (types of relationships and friendship quality)
  • Psychological outcomes (loneliness, depression, psychological well-being)
- Key findings:
  • The QUANTITY of social interactions at age 20 predicted midlife social interactions
  • The QUALITY (not quantity) of social interaction at age 30 predicted midlife social interactions
  • High levels of social information seeking (quantity) at age 20 followed by less quantity but greater emotional closeness (quality) in one's 30s resulted in positive psychosocial adjustment at midlife
  • Continuing to socialize widely in one's 30s appeared to NEGATIVELY affect the development of intimacy, and consequently resulted in WORSE psychological outcomes at age 50

━━━

INTERNET FRIENDSHIPS
- People use the Internet with the goal of meeting and making new friends (Fehr, 2008; McKenna, 2008)
- Researchers have questioned whether not being face-to-face reduces the authenticity of relationships, or if the Internet allows people to develop deep, meaningful connections
- Research has demonstrated that virtual relationships are often AS INTIMATE as in-person relationships
- Bargh and colleagues found that online relationships are sometimes MORE INTIMATE than in-person relationships (Bargh, McKenna, & Fitsimons, 2002)
- Individuals who are more socially anxious and lonely are more likely to turn to the Internet to find new and meaningful relationships (McKenna, Green, & Gleason, 2002)
- McKenna and colleagues suggest that for people who have a hard time meeting and maintaining relationships due to shyness, anxiety, or lack of face-to-face social skills, the Internet provides a safe, nonthreatening place to develop and maintain relationships
- Benford (2008) found that for HIGH-FUNCTIONING AUTISTIC INDIVIDUALS, the Internet:
  • Facilitated communication and relationship development with others
  • Would have been more difficult in face-to-face contexts
  • Led to the conclusion that Internet communication could be empowering for those who feel frustrated when communicating face to face

━━━

WORKPLACE FRIENDSHIPS
- Friendships often take root in the workplace due to the fact that people are spending as much, or more, time at work than they are with their family and friends (Kaufman & Hotchkiss, 2003)
- Benefits of workplace friendships:
  • People receive mentoring
  • Obtain social support and resources
- Potential downsides:
  • Conflicts
  • Potential for misinterpretation when sexual attraction is an issue
- Elsesser and Peplau (2006) found that many workers reported:
  • Friendships grew out of collaborative work projects
  • These friendships made their days more pleasant
- Riordan and Griffeth (1995) found that people who worked in an environment where friendships could develop and be maintained were more likely to report:
  • Higher levels of job satisfaction
  • Higher job involvement
  • Greater organizational commitment
  • Less likely to leave that job
- Gallup Poll finding: Employees who had close friends at work were almost 50% MORE SATISFIED with their jobs than those who did not (Armour, 2007)

━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- Holt-Lunstad, Smith, & Layton (2010): Poor social connectedness = greater premature mortality risk than cigarette smoking, obesity, and excessive alcohol use
- Dunér & Nordstrom (2007): Close friends contribute to belonging, security, and overall wellbeing across all adult ages
- Carmichael, Reis, & Duberstein (2015): QUANTITY of social interaction matters at age 20; QUALITY (not quantity) matters at age 30 for predicting positive midlife psychosocial adjustment; wide socializing in one's 30s leads to WORSE outcomes at 50
- Erikson (1959): Social relationships in early adulthood lay foundation for social connectedness and intimacy
- Bargh, McKenna, & Fitsimons (2002): Online relationships can be MORE intimate than face-to-face relationships
- Benford (2008): Internet is empowering for high-functioning autistic individuals in relationship-building
- Gallup Poll (Armour, 2007): Close workplace friendships = nearly 50% higher job satisfaction
- Riordan & Griffeth (1995): Workplace friendships linked to higher job satisfaction, job involvement, organizational commitment, and lower turnover
- Emslie, Hunt & Lyons (2013): Shared alcohol consumption important to creating and maintaining male friendships at midlife — but can lead to health-damaging behavior
- Degges-White & Myers (2006): Women with supportive people in their lives have greater life satisfaction than those living more solitary lives
- Baruch & Brooks-Gunn (1984): Friendship networks and confidants are important to women's mental health
- McKenna, Green, & Gleason (2002): Socially anxious and lonely individuals more likely to seek meaningful relationships online

notes.title: Grandparents | Lifespan Development – Complete Study Notes
notes.body:
GRANDPARENTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Many people in middle adulthood take on the role of grandparent, in addition to maintaining relationships with their children AND aging parents.
- The role of grandparent varies around the world.
- In multigenerational households, grandparents may play a greater role in the day-to-day activities of their grandchildren.
- Multigenerational households are more common in: Latin America, Asia, and Africa.
- Multigenerational household dynamic has been on the increase in the U.S. (Pew Research Center, 2010).

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
PROXIMITY AND TECHNOLOGY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- The degree of grandparent involvement depends on the proximity of the grandparents' home to the grandchildren.
- In developed nations, greater mobility of society means grandparents may live long distances from their grandchildren.
- Technology has brought grandparents and their more distant grandchildren together.
- Sorenson and Cooper (2010) found that many of the grandfathers they interviewed would TEXT, EMAIL, or SKYPE with their grandchildren in order to stay in touch.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
THREE STYLES OF GRANDPARENTS (CHERLIN AND FURSTENBERG, 1986)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Cherlin and Furstenberg (1986) describe three styles of grandparents:

  1. REMOTE
     - 30% of grandparents rarely see their grandchildren.
     - Usually live far away from grandchildren, but may also have a distant relationship (not just geographic).
     - Contact is typically made on special occasions, such as holidays or birthdays.

  2. COMPANIONATE
     - 55% of grandparents were described as "companionate" — the MOST COMMON style.
     - These grandparents do things WITH the grandchild but have LITTLE AUTHORITY or CONTROL over them.
     - They prefer to spend time with grandchildren WITHOUT interfering in parenting.
     - They are more like FRIENDS to their grandchildren.

  3. INVOLVED
     - 15% of grandparents were described as "involved."
     - These grandparents take a VERY ACTIVE ROLE in their grandchild's life.
     - The children might even LIVE WITH the grandparent.
     - The involved grandparent has FREQUENT CONTACT WITH and AUTHORITY OVER the grandchild.
     - GRANDMOTHERS, more so than grandfathers, play this role.
     - In contrast, more GRANDFATHERS than grandmothers saw their role as FAMILY HISTORIAN and FAMILY ADVISOR (Neugarten and Weinstein, 1964).

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHANGING GRANDPARENT ROLES (BENGTSON, 2001; HAYSLIP ET AL., 2003)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Bengtson (2001) suggests that grandparents adopt DIFFERENT STYLES WITH DIFFERENT GRANDCHILDREN.
- Over time, grandparents may CHANGE STYLES as circumstances in the family change.
- Today, more grandparents are the SOLE CARE PROVIDERS for grandchildren, or may step in at times of CRISIS.
- With these changes, grandparents are REDEFINING how they see their role in the family.
- FEWER grandparents are adopting a more FORMAL ROLE (Hayslip, Henderson & Shore, 2003).

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
GRANDFATHERS — RESEARCH AND UNIQUE CONSIDERATIONS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Early research on grandparents routinely FOCUSED ON GRANDMOTHERS.
- Grandfathers have often become INVISIBLE MEMBERS OF THE FAMILY (Sorensen & Cooper, 2010).
- Yet, grandfathers stress the importance of their relationships with their grandchildren AS STRONGLY AS do grandmothers (Waldrop et al., 1999).
- For some men, the grandparent role provides the OPPORTUNITY TO ENGAGE IN ACTIVITIES that their occupations, as well as their generation's views of FATHERHOOD AND MASCULINITY, kept them from engaging in with their OWN CHILDREN (Sorenson & Cooper, 2010).
- Many of the grandfathers in Sorenson and Cooper's study felt that being a grandfather was EASIER and a lot MORE ENJOYABLE.
- Even among grandfathers who took on a more involved role, there was still a greater sense that they could be more LIGHT-HEARTED and FLEXIBLE in their interactions with their grandchildren.
- Many grandfathers reported that they were MORE OPENLY AFFECTIONATE with their grandchildren than they had been with their own children.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ KEY EXAM POINTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ Grandparent role is taken on during MIDDLE ADULTHOOD.
★ Multigenerational households are more common in Latin America, Asia, and Africa; increasing in the U.S. (Pew Research Center, 2010).
★ Sorenson & Cooper (2010) — grandfathers use text, email, Skype to stay connected; also found grandfathers are often INVISIBLE in early research; found grandparenting feels EASIER and MORE ENJOYABLE than parenting.
★ Cherlin & Furstenberg (1986) — THREE grandparenting styles: Remote (30%), Companionate (55%), Involved (15%).
★ COMPANIONATE is the MOST COMMON style (55%).
★ INVOLVED grandparents = frequent contact + authority; more likely to be GRANDMOTHERS.
★ Neugarten & Weinstein (1964) — GRANDFATHERS more likely to be FAMILY HISTORIAN and FAMILY ADVISOR.
★ Bengtson (2001) — grandparents can have DIFFERENT styles with DIFFERENT grandchildren and CHANGE styles over time.
★ Hayslip, Henderson & Shore (2003) — fewer grandparents adopting FORMAL roles; more serving as SOLE CARE PROVIDERS.
★ Waldrop et al. (1999) — grandfathers value grandparent relationships AS STRONGLY as grandmothers.
★ Grandfathers often MORE OPENLY AFFECTIONATE with grandchildren than they were with their own children, due to generational norms around masculinity and fatherhood.

notes.title: Middle Adult Lifestyles | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
MIDDLE ADULT LIFESTYLES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SINGLEHOOD
- According to a recent Pew Research study (Wang & Parker, 2014):
  • 16 per 1,000 adults age 45–54 have NEVER married in the U.S.
  • 7 per 1,000 adults age 55 and older have NEVER married in the U.S.
- Some in these groups may be living with a partner
- Some singles at midlife may be single through DIVORCE or WIDOWHOOD
- Bella DePaulo (2014) challenged the idea that singles — especially always-single individuals — fare worse emotionally and in health compared to those who are married
- DePaulo argues there is a BIAS in how studies examine the benefits of marriage:
  • Most studies compare only MARRIED vs. NOT MARRIED
  • This does NOT include a separate comparison between those who have ALWAYS been single vs. those who are single due to divorce or widowhood
- Her research (and that of others) found:
  • Married individuals may be more satisfied with life than the DIVORCED or WIDOWED
  • BUT there is LITTLE DIFFERENCE between married and always-single individuals
  • Especially when comparing recently married with those married 4+ years
  • Once the initial "honeymoon blush" wears off, married individuals are no happier or healthier than those who remained single
- This also suggests problems with treating the "married" category as one homogeneous group

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

ONLINE DATING
- Montenegro (2003) surveyed over 3,000 singles aged 40–69:
  • Almost HALF reported their most important reason for dating was to have SOMEONE TO TALK TO OR DO THINGS WITH
  • SEXUAL FULFILLMENT was also identified as an important goal for many
- Alterovitz & Mendelsohn (2013) reviewed online personal ads for men and women over age 40:
  • Romantic activities and sexual interests were mentioned at SIMILAR RATES among middle-age and young-old age groups
  • Mentioned LESS frequently in the old-old age group

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

MARRIAGE
- Changes in marriage rates due to:
  • More people cohabitating
  • More people deciding to stay single
  • More people getting married at a later age
- Statistics (Figure 8.32):
  • 48% of adults age 45–54 are married:
    - 22% in their FIRST marriage
    - 26% have REMARRIED
  • Marriage is the MOST COMMON relationship status for middle-aged adults in the U.S.
- Marital satisfaction tends to INCREASE for many couples in midlife as children leave home (Landsford, Antonucci, Akiyama, & Takahashi, 2005)
- Counter-argument (Umberson, Williams, Powers, Chen, & Campbell, 2005):
  • Those unhappy with their marriage are likely to have ALREADY divorced by midlife
  • This makes the quality of marriages in later life only APPEAR more satisfactory (selection effect)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

DIVORCE
- Livingston (2014): 27% of adults age 45–54 were divorced (Figure 8.32)
- 57% of divorced adults were WOMEN
  • Reflects the fact that MEN are more likely to REMARRY than women
- TWO-THIRDS of divorces are initiated by WOMEN (AARP, 2009)
- Most divorces take place within the first 5–10 YEARS of marriage
  • Reflects initial attempts to salvage the relationship
  • After a few years of limited success, the couple may decide to end the marriage
- Divorce after 20+ years used to be rare, but is now INCREASING
- Brown and Lin (2013) — "GRAYING OF DIVORCE":
  • While the overall U.S. divorce rate has declined since the 1990s
  • The rate among those 50 and OLDER has DOUBLED
  • Reasons for "graying of divorce":
    1. Less STIGMA attached to divorce today
    2. Some older women are OUT-EARNING their spouses, making them more financially capable of supporting themselves (especially with grown children)
    3. Given INCREASES IN HUMAN LONGEVITY, the prospect of living many more years with an incompatible spouse may prompt divorce

- Gottman and Levenson (2000) on timing of divorce:
  • EARLY ADULTHOOD divorces: more ANGRY and CONFLICTUAL; partners blame each other
  • MIDLIFE divorces: more about having GROWN APART or a COOLING OFF of the relationship

- AARP (2009) Survey — Reasons for divorce by gender:
  • WOMEN cited:
    - Verbal/physical abusiveness of partner: 23%
    - Drug/alcohol abuse: 18%
    - Infidelity: 17%
  • MEN cited:
    - Fallen out of love: 17%
    - No longer shared interests or values: 14%
    - Infidelity: 14%
  • BOTH genders felt the marriage had been over LONG BEFORE the decision to divorce
  • Many stayed together because they were still RAISING CHILDREN
  • Only 1 IN 4 regretted their decision to divorce

- EFFECTS OF DIVORCE:
  • Overall, YOUNG ADULTS struggle more with consequences of divorce than those at midlife
    - Higher risk of DEPRESSION and problems with psychological adjustment (Birditt & Antonucci, 2013)
  • Divorce at midlife is MORE STRESSFUL FOR WOMEN:
    - 44% of middle-aged women reported FINANCIAL PROBLEMS after divorcing (AARP, 2009)
    - Only 11% of men reported such difficulties
  • Many women who divorce in midlife report feeling a great RELEASE from day-to-day unhappiness
  • Hetherington (Hetherington & Kelly, 2002) identified groups of divorcees:
    1. ENHANCERS — used the experience to better themselves and seek more productive intimate relationships
    2. COMPETENT LONERS — used the divorce experience to grow emotionally, but chose to stay single
    • Overwhelming majority in both groups were WOMEN

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

DATING POST-DIVORCE
- Most divorced adults have dated by ONE YEAR after filing for divorce (Anderson et al., 2004; Anderson & Greene, 2011)
- 1 in 4 recent filers report having been in or were currently in a SERIOUS RELATIONSHIP
- OVER HALF were in a serious relationship by one year after filing
- YOUNGER ADULTS were more likely to be dating than middle-aged or older adults (larger pool of potential partners)
- Teachman (2008): MORE THAN TWO-THIRDS of women under age 45 had COHABITED with a partner between their first and second marriages

- DATING WITH CHILDREN challenges:
  • Courtships are SHORTER in remarriage than in first marriages
  • Less going out; more time spent at HOME or with CHILDREN
  • Couple gets LESS TIME TOGETHER to focus on the relationship
  • Anxiety or memories of past relationships can interfere
  • Talmudic scholar quote: "When a divorced man marries a divorced woman, FOUR GO TO BED." (Secombe & Warner, 2004)

- GATEKEEPING — post-divorce parents regulate the flow of information about new romantic partners to their children
  • Balancing own needs for romance with children's needs and reactions
  • Anderson et al. (2004) found THREE approaches:
    1. GRADUAL INTRODUCTION (47%): Gradually introduce children to dating partner; gives both children and partner time to adjust
       - Avoids children meeting someone new repeatedly until the relationship is clearer
       - Allows adult relationship to be on firmer ground before children's potential pushback
    2. OPEN AND TRANSPARENT (40%): Open about the new relationship from the outset with children
    3. DELAYED DISCLOSURE (13%): Do not reveal the relationship until cohabitation and/or remarriage is likely
  • Practical matters influence which gatekeeping method is used:
    - Availability of reliable CHILDCARE
    - AGE and TEMPERAMENT of the child
    - Concerns about the reaction of the EX-SPOUSE

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

RATES OF REMARRIAGE
- The rate of remarriage, like the rate of marriage, has been DECLINING OVERALL
- In 2013: remarriage rate was approximately 28 per 1,000 adults 18 and older
  • Represents a 44% DECLINE since 1990
  • Represents a 16% DECLINE since 2008 (Payne, 2015)
- Brown and Lin (2013): Rate of remarriage dropped MORE for younger adults than for middle-aged and older adults
- Livingston (2014): As we age we are MORE LIKELY to have remarried (Figure 8.33)
  • In 2013:
    - 67% of adults age 55–64 had remarried (up from 55% in 1960)
    - 50% of adults age 65 and older had remarried (up from 34% in 1960)
  • Unlike those younger than 55, those 55 and OLDER are remarrying at a HIGHER rate than in the past

- GENDER DIFFERENCES in remarriage:
  • Men have a HIGHER rate of remarriage at every age group starting at age 25 (Payne, 2015)
  • Livingston (2014) — In 2013:
    - 64% of divorced or widowed MEN had remarried
    - 52% of divorced or widowed WOMEN had remarried
  • However, this GENDER GAP has NARROWED over time:
    - Men are remarrying at a SLOWER rate
    - Women are remarrying MORE today than they did in 1980
    - Gap has mostly closed among YOUNG and MIDDLE-AGED adults
    - Gap still PERSISTS among those 65 and older

- RACE AND ETHNICITY differences:
  • In 2012, WHITES who were previously married were MORE LIKELY to remarry than other racial/ethnic groups (Livingston, 2014)
  • Rate of remarriage has INCREASED among Whites
  • Rate of remarriage has DECLINED for other racial/ethnic groups
  • Increase driven by WHITE WOMEN, whose rate of remarriage has increased
  • Rate for White MALES has declined

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SUCCESS OF REMARRIAGE
- Reviews are MIXED regarding happiness and success of remarriages
- Some remarriages ARE more successful, especially if divorce motivated the adult to engage in SELF-IMPROVEMENT and PERSONAL GROWTH (Hetherington & Kelly, 2002)
- A number of divorced adults end up in VERY SIMILAR MARRIAGES the second or third time around (Hetherington & Kelly, 2002)
- UNIQUE CHALLENGES in remarriage (not found in first marriages):
  • General LACK OF CLARITY in family roles and expectations
  • Difficulty incorporating new kin into the family structure
  • Even determining APPROPRIATE TERMS for new kin and their roles is a challenge
  • Partners must navigate carefully their ROLE WITH PARTNER'S CHILDREN
  • May lead to greater DISSATISFACTION and RESENTMENT among family members
- Even though remarried couples tend to have MORE REALISTIC EXPECTATIONS, they are LESS WILLING to stay in unhappy situations
- Rate of DIVORCE AMONG REMARRIAGES is HIGHER than among first marriages (Payne, 2015)
  • Can add additional burdens, especially when children are involved

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

CHILDREN'S INFLUENCE ON REPARTNERING
- Goldscheider and Sassler (2006): Children residing with their MOTHERS reduces the mother's likelihood of marriage — BUT ONLY with respect to marrying a MAN WITHOUT CHILDREN
- Stewart, Manning, & Smock (2003): Having children in the home appears to INCREASE single men's likelihood of marrying a WOMAN WITH CHILDREN
- Goldscheider and Kaufman (2006): Having experienced FAMILY DIVORCE as a child is associated with a GREATER WILLINGNESS to marry a partner with children
  • Individuals who participated in a stepfamily while growing up may feel better PREPARED for stepfamily living as adults

- TWO TYPES OF PARENTS regarding child influence on repartnering (Greene, Anderson, Hetherington, Forgatch, & DeGarmo, 2003):
  1. CHILD-FOCUSED PARENT: Allows the child's views, reactions, and needs to INFLUENCE the repartnering decision
  2. ADULT-FOCUSED PARENT: Expects the child to ADAPT and accommodate to parental wishes

- Anderson and Greene (2011) findings about ADULT-FOCUSED custodial mothers:
  • Tended to be OLDER, more EDUCATED, EMPLOYED, and more likely to have been MARRIED LONGER
  • Reported having LESS RAPPORT with their children
  • Spent LESS TIME in joint activities with their children
  • Child reported LOWER RAPPORT with their mothers
  • When child and partner were in conflict, adult-focused mothers responded more to the concerns of the PARTNER
  • Child-focused mothers responded more to the concerns of the CHILD
- Understanding these two perspectives can assist parents in their attempts to repartner

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
1. Singlehood stats: 16/1,000 (age 45–54) and 7/1,000 (age 55+) have never married (Wang & Parker, 2014)
2. DePaulo (2014): Challenges marriage bias — always-single individuals are nearly as happy/healthy as married; main difference is married vs. divorced/widowed, not married vs. always-single
3. Montenegro (2003): Top reason for dating among 40–69-year-olds = companionship (someone to talk to/do things with)
4. 48% of adults age 45–54 are married (22% first marriage, 26% remarried) — marriage is most common relationship status at midlife
5. 27% of adults age 45–54 are divorced; 57% of divorced adults are women; 2/3 of divorces initiated by women
6. Brown & Lin (2013): Divorce rate doubled among those 50+ — "graying of divorce" — reasons: less stigma, women's financial independence, increased longevity
7. Gottman & Levenson (2000): Early adulthood divorces = angry/conflictual; midlife divorces = growing apart/cooling off
8. AARP (2009): Women cite abuse (23%), substance abuse (18%), infidelity (17%); Men cite falling out of love (17%), values/interests (14%), infidelity (14%); Only 1 in 4 regret divorce
9. Women face more financial hardship post-divorce (44% vs. 11% of men); many women feel emotional release
10. Hetherington's groups: Enhancers and Competent Loners — majority were women
11. Remarriage rate = 28/1,000 in 2013; down 44% since 1990; men remarry more than women at all ages 25+
12. Whites remarry more than other racial/ethnic groups; increase driven by White women
13. Remarriage divorce rate is HIGHER than first marriage divorce rate
14. Gatekeeping: 47% gradual introduction, 40% open/transparent, 13% delayed disclosure
15. Child-focused vs. Adult-focused parents: Adult-focused mothers are older, more educated, employed, married longer, less rapport with children
16. Goldscheider & Sassler (2006): Children reduce mothers' likelihood of marrying a man WITHOUT children; men with children more likely to marry women with children

notes.title: Midlife Relationships | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
MIDLIFE RELATIONSHIPS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

THE SANDWICH GENERATION
- Definition: Adults who have at least one parent age 65 or older AND are either raising their own children OR providing support for grown children
- Source: Pew Research survey (Parker & Patten, 2013)
- 47% of middle-aged adults are part of the sandwich generation
- 15% of middle-aged adults are providing financial support to an older parent while raising or supporting their own children
- 48% (almost half) of middle-aged adults have supported their adult children in the past year
- 27% are the primary source of support for their grown children

SANDWICH GENERATION — DEMOGRAPHICS
- 71% of the sandwich generation is age 40–59
- 19% were younger than 40
- 10% were age 60 or older
- Hispanics: 31% have parents 65 or older AND a dependent child
- Whites: 24% in the same situation
- Blacks: 21% in the same situation
- Hispanics are more likely than whites or blacks to find themselves supporting two generations
- Source: Parker & Patten, 2013

SANDWICH GENERATION — GENDER AND CAREGIVING
- Women are more likely to take on the role of care provider for older parents in the U.S. and Germany (Pew Research, 2015)
- About 1 in 5 women (approx. 20%) say they have helped with personal care (e.g., getting dressed, bathing) of aging parents in the past year
- Comparison: 8% of men in the U.S. and 4% of men in Germany provided personal care
- Exception — Italy: Men (25%) are just as likely as women (26%) to have provided personal care

SANDWICH GENERATION — WELL-BEING AND FINANCIAL STRAIN
- Almost 1 in 3 sandwich-generation adults were more likely to say they always feel rushed
- Only 23% of other (non-sandwich) adults said they always feel rushed
- Those supporting both parents and children reported being JUST AS HAPPY as those not in the sandwich generation (Parker & Patten, 2013)
- However, sandwich-generation adults did report GREATER FINANCIAL STRAIN:
  - Only 28% of sandwich-generation adults reported living comfortably
  - 41% of non-sandwich adults reported living comfortably
  - Almost one third of sandwich-generation adults were just making ends meet
  - 17% of non-sandwich adults were just making ends meet

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

KINKEEPING
- Definition: At midlife, adults may find themselves as a kinkeeper — a person (or persons) who keeps the family connected and promotes solidarity and continuity in the family
- Source: Brown & DeRycke, 2010
- Kinkeeper roles include:
  - Organizing family gatherings
  - Knowing the history of the family
  - Being the person people turn to for advice and support
  - Working to strengthen bonds between family members
- Kinkeepers are USUALLY WOMEN (Leach & Braithwaite, 1996; Brown & DeRycke, 2010)

KINKEEPING — KEY RESEARCH FINDINGS
- Leach & Braithwaite: 86% of respondents named a WOMAN as their family's kinkeeper
- Brown & DeRycke: Mothers, maternal grandmothers, and paternal grandmothers were more likely to be a family's kinkeeper than fathers, young adult children, and grandfathers COMBINED
- Brown & DeRycke also found: Among young adults, women were more likely to be a kinkeeper than young adult men

KINKEEPING — STRESS AND DISTRESS
- Kinkeeping can be a source of distress when it interferes with other obligations (Gerstel & Gallagher, 1993)
- Gerstel & Gallagher findings:
  - On average, kinkeepers provide almost a FULL WEEK of work each month to kinkeeping (almost 34 hours per month)
  - The more activities the kinkeeper took on AND the more kin they helped → more stress and higher levels of depression
  - This study also included caregiving activities (providing transportation, making repairs, providing meals, etc.) in addition to traditional kinkeeping activities

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

EMPTY NEST
- Also called: Post-parental period (Dennerstein, Dudley & Guthrie, 2002)
- Definition: The time period when children are grown up and have LEFT home
- For most parents this occurs during MIDLIFE
- Recognized as a NORMATIVE EVENT — parents are aware their children will become adults and eventually leave (Mitchell & Lovegreen, 2009)
- Creates COMPLEX EMOTIONS — both positive and negative — for many parents
- Two competing theoretical perspectives:
  1. Role Loss Hypothesis
  2. Role Strain Relief Hypothesis

EMPTY NEST — ROLE LOSS HYPOTHESIS
- Predicts: When people lose an important role, they experience a DECREASE in emotional well-being
- Led to the concept of EMPTY NEST SYNDROME
- Empty nest syndrome: Great emotional distress experienced by parents (typically MOTHERS) after children have left home
- Linked to the ABSENCE of alternative roles for the parent in which they could establish their identity (Borland, 1982)
- Bouchard's (2013) review: FEW parents reported loneliness or a big sense of loss once all their children had left home
  - This finding does NOT strongly support the empty nest syndrome

EMPTY NEST — ROLE STRAIN RELIEF HYPOTHESIS
- Predicts: The empty nest period should lead to MORE POSITIVE changes for parents because the responsibility of raising children has been lifted
- Supported by MANY studies in Bouchard's (2013) review
- Consistent finding in research: Raising children has a NEGATIVE impact on the quality of marital relationships (Ahlborg, Misvaer, & Möller, 2009; Bouchard, 2013)
- Several studies: Marital satisfaction often INCREASES during the launching phase of the empty nest period
- This increased marital satisfaction ENDURES long after the last child has left home (Gorchoff, John, & Helson, 2008)

EMPTY NEST — CROSS-CULTURAL CONSIDERATIONS (CHINA)
- Most research on the post-parental period has been with AMERICAN PARENTS
- Studies in China (especially rural areas): Empty-nesters report GREATER LONELINESS and DEPRESSION than counterparts with children still at home (Wu et al., 2010)
- Family support for the elderly by their children is a CHERISHED CHINESE TRADITION (Wong & Leung, 2012)
- Children moving from rural communities to larger cities for education and employment may explain the more pessimistic reaction of Chinese parents compared to American samples
- Loss of adult child in rural region may mean LOSS OF FAMILY INCOME for aging parents
- Urban Chinese empty-nesters did NOT report the same degree of distress (Su et al., 2012)
- Conclusion: It is not so much the EVENT of children leaving, but the ADDITIONAL HARDSHIPS this may place on aging parents that drives distress

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

BOOMERANG KIDS
- Definition: Young adults who RETURN to live with parents after having lived independently outside the home
- Young adults are living with parents for longer durations and in greater numbers than previous generations
- 63% of 18–34 year-olds know someone who has returned to live with their parents
- Reasons for returning:
  - Financial reasons (primary driver, same as for delayed exit)
  - Emotional distress, such as mental health issues (Sandberg-Thoma, Snyder, & Jang, 2015)

BOOMERANG KIDS — EFFECT ON PARENTS
- Research shows stress of raising children negatively impacts parents' well-being; when children leave, many couples experience less stress and greater life satisfaction
- Early research (1980s–1990s): Boomerang children AND those failing to exit placed greater FINANCIAL HARDSHIP on parents; parents reported more NEGATIVE PERCEPTIONS of this living arrangement (Aquilino, 1991)
- Recent surveys: Today's parents are MORE TOLERANT of boomerang kids — this is becoming a more normative experience
- Children who return are more likely to have had GOOD RELATIONSHIPS with their parents growing up → less stress
- Parents of young adults who moved back for ECONOMIC REASONS report being JUST AS SATISFIED with life as parents whose adult children live independently (Parker, 2012)

BOOMERANG KIDS — ADDITIONAL PARKER (2012) FINDINGS
- Adult children age 25 and older are more likely to:
  - Contribute financially to the family
  - Complete chores and other household duties
- Living in a multigenerational household may act as an ECONOMIC SAFETY NET for young adults
- Young adults living WITH parents: 10% in poverty
- Young adults living OUTSIDE the home: 17% in poverty
- Those living with parents were LESS likely to be living in poverty

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

LINKED LIVES AND ADULT CHILDREN'S PROBLEMS
- Context: Effect on parents who have adult children dealing with personal problems (alcoholism, chronic health concerns, mental health issues, trouble with the law, poor social relationships, academic or job-related problems) — even if NOT living at home
- Concept: LINKED LIVES (from the life course perspective)
- Definition: People in important relationships (e.g., children and parents) mutually influence each other's developmental pathways (Greenfield & Marks, 2006)
- The parent-child relationship is BIDIRECTIONAL — children affect parents just as parents affect children

LINKED LIVES — GREENFIELD & MARKS (2006) FINDINGS
- Study of middle-aged parents and their adult children
- Parents whose children were dealing with personal problems reported:
  - More negative affect
  - Lower self-acceptance
  - Poorer parent-child interactions
  - More family relationship stress
- The more problems adult children faced → the WORSE the lives and emotional health of their parents
- SINGLE PARENTS fared the WORST

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
1. SANDWICH GENERATION: 47% of middle-aged adults; 15% financially supporting aging parent while raising/supporting own children; 27% are primary support for grown children (Parker & Patten, 2013)
2. SANDWICH GENERATION AGE BREAKDOWN: 71% age 40–59; 19% under 40; 10% age 60+
3. ETHNICITY AND SANDWICH GENERATION: Hispanics (31%) > Whites (24%) > Blacks (21%) in supporting two generations
4. GENDER AND CAREGIVING: Women more likely to provide personal care to aging parents in U.S. and Germany; Italy exception — men and women equally likely
5. SANDWICH GENERATION HAPPINESS: Just as happy as non-sandwich adults BUT experience greater financial strain (28% comfortable vs. 41% of non-sandwich)
6. KINKEEPER: Person who keeps family connected; 86% of respondents named a woman; provides ~34 hours/month (~full week); more activities/kin helped = more stress and depression
7. EMPTY NEST SYNDROME: Role loss hypothesis; few parents actually report major distress per Bouchard (2013)
8. ROLE STRAIN RELIEF: Supported by more studies; marital satisfaction INCREASES at launching phase and endures (Gorchoff et al., 2008)
9. CHINA EMPTY NEST: Rural Chinese empty-nesters report more loneliness/depression; urban Chinese do not show same distress; linked to economic hardship not just the departure itself
10. BOOMERANG KIDS: Return for financial AND emotional reasons; today's parents more tolerant; living with parents = 10% poverty vs. 17% for those living independently (Parker, 2012)
11. LINKED LIVES: Bidirectional influence of parent-child relationships; adult children's problems → parents' negative affect, lower self-acceptance, more stress; single parents fare worst (Greenfield & Marks, 2006)
12. KINKEEPING HOURS: Almost 34 hours per month (nearly a full week of work)
13. EMPTY NEST NORMATIVE EVENT: Recognized as normative because parents expect children to leave (Mitchell & Lovegreen, 2009)
14. EMPTY NEST SYNDROME linked to absence of alternative roles for identity (Borland, 1982)
15. BOOMERANG EARLY RESEARCH: Aquilino (1991) — financial hardship and negative parental perceptions; more recent research shows greater parental tolerance

notes.title: Erikson: Generativity vs. Stagnation | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
ERIKSON: GENERATIVITY VS. STAGNATION

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

DEFINITION AND SCOPE OF GENERATIVITY

- According to Erikson (1982), generativity encompasses THREE core elements:
  1. Procreativity
  2. Productivity
  3. Creativity
- This stage includes the generation of:
  • New beings
  • New products
  • New ideas
  • Self-generation concerned with further identity development
- Erikson believed the stage of generativity — during which one established a family and career — was the LONGEST of all the stages
- Individuals at midlife are primarily concerned with leaving a POSITIVE LEGACY of themselves
- According to Erikson (1950), PARENTHOOD is the primary generative type
- Erikson understood that work and family relationships may be in CONFLICT due to obligations and responsibilities of each
- Despite potential conflicts, he believed it was OVERALL A POSITIVE developmental time
- In addition to being parents and working, Erikson described individuals being involved in the COMMUNITY during this stage

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

STAGNATION

- A sense of STAGNATION occurs when one is NOT active in generative matters
- However, stagnation can MOTIVATE a person to redirect energies into more meaningful activities
- Not feeling needed or challenged may also result in stagnation
- Individuals should NOT fully withdraw from generative tasks as they enter Erikson's last stage in late adulthood, because complete withdrawal risks stagnation

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

THE VIRTUE OF CARE

- Erikson identified "virtues" for each of his EIGHT stages
- Virtues refer to what the individual ACHIEVES when the stage is successfully reconciled
- The virtue emerging when one achieves generativity is: "CARE"
- Erikson believed those in middle adulthood should "take care of the persons, the products, and the ideas one has learned to care for" (Erikson, 1982, p. 67)
- Erikson believed the STRENGTHS gained from the SIX EARLIER STAGES are essential for the generational task of cultivating strength in the next generation
- Erikson further argued that generativity occurred BEST after the individual had resolved issues of:
  1. Identity
  2. Intimacy
  (Peterson & Duncan, 2007)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

RESEARCH ON GENERATIVE ADULTS — CHARACTERISTICS AND OUTCOMES

- Research has demonstrated that generative adults possess many POSITIVE characteristics, including:
  • Good cultural knowledge
  • Healthy adaptation to the world
  (Peterson & Duncan, 2007)

BIG 5 PERSONALITY TRAITS IN GENERATIVE INDIVIDUALS:
- Generative women AND men scored HIGH on:
  • Conscientiousness
  • Extraversion
  • Agreeableness
  • Openness to experience
- Generative women AND men scored LOW on:
  • Neuroticism
  (de St. Aubin & McAdams, 1995; Peterson, Smirles, & Wentworth, 1997)

LONGITUDINAL FINDINGS — WOMEN:
- Women scoring HIGH in generativity at age 52 were rated high in:
  • Positive personality characteristics
  • Satisfaction with marriage and motherhood
  • Successful aging at age 62
  (Peterson & Duncan, 2007)

LONGITUDINAL FINDINGS — MEN:
- Men rated HIGHER in generativity at midlife were associated with:
  • Stronger global cognitive functioning:
    - Memory
    - Attention
    - Calculation
  • Stronger executive functioning:
    - Response inhibition
    - Abstract thinking
    - Cognitive flexibility
  • LOWER levels of depression in late adulthood
  (Malone, Liu, Vaillant, Rentz, & Waldinger, 2016)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

WITHDRAWAL AT END OF STAGE AND TRANSITION TO LATE ADULTHOOD

- Erikson (1982) indicated that at the END of this demanding stage, individuals may WITHDRAW as generativity is no longer expected in late adulthood
- This releases elders from the task of:
  • Caretaking
  • Working
- Caveat: not feeling needed or challenged may result in STAGNATION
- Therefore, one should NOT fully withdraw from generative tasks as they enter Erikson's LAST STAGE in late adulthood

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

KEY RESEARCHERS AND CITATIONS

- Erikson (1950) — parenthood as primary generative type
- Erikson (1982) — definition of generativity; virtue of care quote (p. 67); withdrawal at end of stage
- Peterson & Duncan (2007) — positive characteristics of generative adults; generativity best after identity and intimacy resolution; women's longitudinal outcomes
- de St. Aubin & McAdams (1995) — Big 5 personality traits and generativity
- Peterson, Smirles, & Wentworth (1997) — Big 5 personality traits and generativity
- Malone, Liu, Vaillant, Rentz, & Waldinger (2016) — men's cognitive functioning and depression outcomes in late adulthood

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS

1. Generativity (per Erikson, 1982) = procreativity + productivity + creativity
2. This stage is considered the LONGEST of all eight Erikson stages
3. Primary generative type (Erikson, 1950) = PARENTHOOD
4. The VIRTUE associated with successful resolution of this stage = CARE
5. Direct quote: "take care of the persons, the products, and the ideas one has learned to care for" (Erikson, 1982, p. 67)
6. Generativity is best achieved AFTER resolving identity AND intimacy (Peterson & Duncan, 2007)
7. Strengths from the SIX earlier stages are needed for generativity
8. Stagnation occurs when NOT active in generative matters — but can motivate redirection to meaningful activities
9. Big 5 findings: generative individuals score HIGH on conscientiousness, extraversion, agreeableness, openness; LOW on neuroticism
10. Women high in generativity at age 52 → positive personality, marital satisfaction, motherhood satisfaction, successful aging at 62
11. Men high in generativity at midlife → stronger global cognitive function (memory, attention, calculation), stronger executive function (response inhibition, abstract thinking, cognitive flexibility), LOWER depression in late adulthood (Malone et al., 2016)
12. Complete withdrawal from generative tasks in late adulthood risks STAGNATION — partial continuation is recommended
13. Involvement in COMMUNITY (not just work and family) is part of generativity

notes.title: Stress | Lifespan Development – Complete Study Notes
notes.body:
STRESS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

DEFINITION OF STRESS
- Stress: a pattern of physical and psychological responses in an organism after it perceives a threatening event that disturbs its homeostasis and taxes its abilities to cope with the event (Hooker & Pressman, 2016)
- The term was originally derived from the field of MECHANICS, where it described materials under pressure
- First used in a PSYCHOLOGICAL manner by researcher HANS SELYE
  - Selye was examining the effect of an ovarian hormone he thought caused sickness in rats
  - He noticed that almost ANY injected hormone produced the same sickness
  - Key realization: it was NOT the hormone causing problems — instead, the AVERSIVE EXPERIENCE of being handled and injected by researchers led to high physiological arousal and eventually to health problems like ULCERS

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

GENERAL ADAPTATION SYNDROME (GAS)
- SELYE (1946) coined the term STRESSOR to label a stimulus that causes stress on the body
- Developed the model: GENERAL ADAPTATION SYNDROME
- Definition: a THREE-PHASE model of stress response
  - Phase 1: MOBILIZATION OF PHYSIOLOGICAL RESOURCES
  - Phase 2: COPING
  - Phase 3: EXHAUSTION — when an organism fails to cope adequately and depletes its resources
- Referenced as Figure 8.24 in source material

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

TYPES AND SOURCES OF STRESS
- Stress can result from:
  - Negative events
  - Chronically difficult situations
  - A biological FIGHT-OR-FLIGHT response
  - Clinical illness such as POST-TRAUMATIC STRESS DISORDER (PTSD)
- Major life stressors (e.g., family death, natural disaster) increase likelihood of getting sick
- Even SMALL DAILY HASSLES (e.g., getting stuck in traffic, fighting with a friend) can:
  - Raise blood pressure
  - Alter stress hormones
  - Suppress immune system function
  - (DeLongis, Folkman, & Lazarus, 1988; Twisk, Snel, Kemper, & van Machelen, 1999)
- Stress is one of the most important and well-studied psychological correlates of illness
- Excessive stress causes potentially damaging WEAR AND TEAR on the body and can influence almost any disease process

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

DISPOSITIONS AND STRESS
- Negative dispositions and personality traits have been strongly tied to an array of health risks
- One of the earliest negative trait-to-health connections discovered in the 1950s by TWO CARDIOLOGISTS (Friedman & Rosenman, 1959)
  - They noticed common behavioral and psychological patterns among heart patients not present in other patient samples
  - Pattern included: being COMPETITIVE, IMPATIENT, HOSTILE, and TIME URGENT
  - Labeled it: TYPE A BEHAVIOR
  - TYPE A associated with DOUBLE THE RISK of heart disease compared to TYPE B Behavior
  - TYPE B Behavior = absence of Type A behaviors

- Since the 1950s, researchers found that HOSTILITY and COMPETITIVENESS components of Type A are especially harmful to heart health:
  - (Iribarren et al., 2000)
  - (Matthews, Glass, Rosenman, & Bortner, 1977)
  - (Miller, Smith, Turner, Guijarro, & Hallet, 1996)
- Hostile individuals are quick to get upset → angry arousal can DAMAGE THE ARTERIES OF THE HEART
- Hostile people often LACK a health-protective supportive social network due to their negative personality style

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SOCIAL RELATIONSHIPS AND STRESS
- Impact of SOCIAL ISOLATION on risk for disease and death is SIMILAR IN MAGNITUDE to the risk associated with SMOKING REGULARLY (Holt-Lunstad, Smith, & Layton, 2010; House, Landis, & Umberson, 1988)
- Some scientists believe the body has developed a physiological system that encourages seeking out relationships, especially in times of stress (Taylor et al., 2000)
- SOCIAL INTEGRATION: the concept used to describe the NUMBER OF SOCIAL ROLES that you have (Cohen & Willis, 1985)
  - Example roles: daughter, basketball team member, Humane Society volunteer, coworker, student
  - Maintaining different roles can improve health via encouragement from others to maintain a healthy lifestyle
- SOCIAL SUPPORT: provided by those in your social network when you are under stress
  - Types of social support:
    - EMOTIONAL help (e.g., a hug when you need it)
    - TANGIBLE help (e.g., lending money)
    - ADVICE
- Social relationships can have a powerful, PROTECTIVE impact on health
- In some cases, might even help people with serious illnesses stay alive longer (Spiegel, Kraemer, Bloom, & Gottheil, 1989)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

CAREGIVING AND STRESS

OVERALL STATISTICS:
- According to the National Alliance for Caregiving (2015): 40 MILLION AMERICANS provide unpaid caregiving
- Typical caregiver profile:
  - A 49-year-old FEMALE
  - Currently caring for a 69-year-old FEMALE
  - Care needed because of a long-term physical condition

CAREGIVER DEMOGRAPHICS BY AGE OF RECIPIENT:
- Care recipients aged 18–49:
  - Typical caregiver: FEMALE (61%)
  - Caring mostly for her own CHILD (32%)
  - Followed by a SPOUSE or PARTNER (17%)
- Care recipients aged 50+:
  - Typical caregiver: FEMALE (60%)
  - Caring for a PARENT (47%)
  - Caring for a SPOUSE (10%)

Impact of Caregiving on Health:
- Caregiving places ENORMOUS STRESS on the caregiver
- Caregiving for a young or adult child with special needs associated with:
  - Poorer global health
  - More physical symptoms among BOTH fathers and mothers
  - (Seltzer, Floyd, Song, Greenberg, & Hong, 2011)

GENDER DIFFERENCES IN CAREGIVING FOR CHILD WITH SPECIAL NEEDS:
- FATHERS who were caregivers: identified more chronic health conditions than non-caregiving fathers, REGARDLESS of marital quality
- MOTHERS who were caregivers: reported higher levels of chronic conditions when they reported HIGH LEVEL OF MARITAL STRAIN (Kang & Marks, 2014)

AGE DIFFERENCES IN CAREGIVING FOR DISABLED CHILD:
- Using data from the Study of Midlife in the United States, Ha, Hong, Seltzer, and Greenberg (2008) found:
  - OLDER parents were significantly LESS LIKELY to experience negative effects of having a disabled child than younger parents
  - Concluded: age-related WEAKENING OF THE STRESS occurred over time
  - Consistent with greater emotional stability noted at midlife

ADULT CHILDREN CARING FOR PARENTS:
- Currently 25% of adult children (mainly baby boomers) provide personal or financial care to a parent (Metlife, 2011)
- DAUGHTERS more likely to provide BASIC CARE
- SONS more likely to provide FINANCIAL ASSISTANCE
- Adult children 50+ who work AND provide care to a parent are more likely to have FAIR OR POOR HEALTH compared to non-caregiving peers
- Cost of leaving the workforce early to care for a parent (Metlife, 2011):
  - FEMALES: lost wages and social security benefits = $324,044
  - MALES: lost wages and social security benefits = $283,716
- This loss can JEOPARDIZE the adult child's financial future
- Result: need for greater WORKPLACE FLEXIBILITY for working caregivers

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SPOUSAL CARE

BEACH, SCHULZ, YEE & JACKSON (2000) STUDY — FOUR GROUPS:
- GROUP 1: Spouses with NO caregiving needed
- GROUP 2: Living with a disabled spouse but NOT providing care
- GROUP 3: Living with a disabled spouse AND providing care (no caregiver strain)
- GROUP 4: Helping a disabled spouse while reporting CAREGIVER STRAIN (elevated emotional and physical stress)

Results:
- GROUP 4 (strain): LEAST healthy — identified:
  - Poorer perceived health
  - Increase in health-risk behaviors
  - Increase in anxiety and depression symptoms
- GROUP 3 (caregiving, no strain): Actually identified DECREASED levels of anxiety and depression compared to Group 2; similar to Group 1
