  - Conclusion: greater caregiving involvement = BETTER MENTAL HEALTH as long as the caregiving spouse did not feel strain
- Consistent with previous research (Krause, Herzog, & Baker, 1992; Schulz et al., 1997)

GENDER DIFFERENCES IN SPOUSAL CAREGIVING:
- FEMALE caregivers of a spouse with DEMENTIA experienced (Gibbons et al., 2014):
  - More BURDEN
  - Poorer mental and physical health
  - Increased depressive symptomatology
  - Fewer health-promoting activities
  - Received FEWER HOURS OF HELP than male caregivers
- Consistent with previous research findings that WOMEN experience more caregiving burden than men despite similar caregiving situations (Torti, Gwyther, Reed, Friedman, & Schulman, 2004; Yeager, Hyer, Hobbs, & Coyne, 2010)

Reasons why women don't use more external support:
- Women's expectations that they SHOULD ASSUME CAREGIVING ROLES (Torti et al., 2004)
- Concerns with the OPINIONS OF OTHERS (Arai, Sugiura, Miura, Washio, & Kudo, 2000)

Additional factor:
- DISABLED MALES are more AGGRESSIVE than females
- Especially males with dementia who display more PHYSICAL AND SEXUAL AGGRESSION toward their caregivers (Eastley & Wilcock, 1997; Zuidema, de Jonghe, Verhey, & Koopmans, 2009)
- Female caregivers are CERTAINLY AT RISK for negative consequences; greater support needs to be available to them

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

STRESS MANAGEMENT
- About 20% of Americans report having stress
- AGE 18–33-year-olds report the HIGHEST LEVELS of stress (American Psychological Association, 2012)
- Sources of stress are often difficult to change (e.g., personal finances, current job)
- Interventions designed to reduce aversive responses to stress:

  1. RELAXATION ACTIVITIES and FORMS OF MEDITATION
     - Techniques: breathing exercises, muscle relaxation, and mental imagery

  2. BIOFEEDBACK
     - A technique where the individual is shown bodily information NOT normally available to them (e.g., heart rate)
     - Then taught strategies to alter this signal
     - Has shown promise in reducing HEART and HYPERTENSION RISK, and other serious conditions (Moravec, 2008; Patel, Marmot, & Terry, 1981)

  3. EXERCISE
     - A great stress reduction activity (Salmon, 2001)
     - Has a myriad of health benefits
     - Stress reduction does not have to be complicated

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

COPING STRATEGIES
- Coping classified into TWO CATEGORIES (Carver, Scheier, & Weintraub, 1989):

  1. PROBLEM-FOCUSED COPING
     - Actively addressing the event that is causing stress in an effort to SOLVE THE ISSUE AT HAND
     - Example: upcoming exam → spend additional time over the weekend studying to understand all material
     - Has the GREATEST IMPACT ON MENTAL WELLNESS (Billings & Moos, 1981; Herman-Stabl, Stemmler, & Petersen, 1995)

  2. EMOTION-FOCUSED COPING
     - Regulates the EMOTIONS that come with stress
     - Example: upcoming exam → watch a funny movie to take your mind off anxiety
     - In the SHORT TERM, may reduce feelings of stress
     - Best used when events are UNCONTROLLABLE (e.g., the death of a loved one) — managing feelings first might be the better strategy

- KEY PRINCIPLE: Always important to consider the MATCH OF THE STRESSOR TO THE COPING STRATEGY when evaluating plausible benefits

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
1. DEFINITION: Stress = pattern of physical and psychological responses after perceiving a threatening event that disturbs homeostasis and taxes coping ability (Hooker & Pressman, 2016)
2. HANS SELYE first used stress psychologically; discovered it was aversive experience (handling/injection), not the hormone itself, causing sickness in rats; coined 'stressor'
3. GENERAL ADAPTATION SYNDROME (Selye, 1946) = 3 phases: (1) Mobilization of physiological resources, (2) Coping, (3) Exhaustion
4. Even DAILY HASSLES can raise blood pressure, alter stress hormones, suppress immune function (DeLongis et al., 1988; Twisk et al., 1999)
5. TYPE A BEHAVIOR = competitive, impatient, hostile, time urgent → DOUBLE the risk of heart disease vs. Type B (Friedman & Rosenman, 1959); HOSTILITY and COMPETITIVENESS are most harmful components
6. SOCIAL ISOLATION risk for disease/death is SIMILAR IN MAGNITUDE to smoking regularly (Holt-Lunstad et al., 2010; House et al., 1988)
7. SOCIAL INTEGRATION = number of social roles; SOCIAL SUPPORT = emotional, tangible, or advice support from network
8. 40 MILLION Americans provide unpaid caregiving; typical caregiver is a 49-year-old female caring for a 69-year-old female (National Alliance for Caregiving, 2015)
9. 25% of adult children care for a parent; females lose $324,044 and males lose $283,716 in wages/benefits if leaving workforce early (Metlife, 2011)
10. SPOUSAL CAREGIVING: caregiving without strain = DECREASED anxiety/depression (Group 3 similar to Group 1); caregiving WITH strain = worst outcomes (Group 4) (Beach et al., 2000)
11. FEMALE spousal caregivers experience MORE BURDEN, poorer health, more depression than male caregivers (Gibbons et al., 2014)
12. OLDER PARENTS less negatively affected by having a disabled child than younger parents — age-related weakening of stress (Ha et al., 2008)
13. 20% of Americans report stress; 18–33-year-olds report HIGHEST LEVELS (APA, 2012)
14. BIOFEEDBACK: shows normally unavailable bodily info (e.g., heart rate) and teaches strategies to alter it; reduces heart/hypertension risk (Moravec, 2008; Patel et al., 1981)
15. PROBLEM-FOCUSED COPING = best for controllable stressors, greatest impact on mental wellness; EMOTION-FOCUSED COPING = better for UNCONTROLLABLE stressors (e.g., death of loved one)
16. MATCH of stressor to coping strategy is always important to evaluate

notes.title: Midlife Crisis? | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
MIDLIFE CRISIS?
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

DANIEL LEVINSON'S THEORY OF ADULT DEVELOPMENT
- In 1978, Daniel Levinson published a book entitled "The Seasons of a Man's Life"
- Presented a theory of development in adulthood
- Research was based on in-depth interviews with 40 men between the ages of 35–45
- Levinson (1978) indicated that adults go through stages and have an image of the future that motivates them
- This motivating image is called "THE DREAM"
  - For the men interviewed, "the dream" was a dream of how their career paths would progress and where they would be at midlife

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

LEVINSON'S STAGES OF MIDLIFE
- MIDLIFE TRANSITION (Ages 40–45):
  - A time of reevaluating previous commitments
  - Making dramatic changes if necessary
  - Giving expression to previously ignored talents or aspirations
  - Feeling more of a sense of urgency about life and its meaning
- MIDDLE ADULTHOOD (Ages 45–50):
  - By this stage, men believed they committed to the new choices made
  - They placed their energies into these new commitments

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

LEVINSON'S VIEW ON MIDLIFE CRISIS
- Levinson believed that a midlife crisis was a NORMAL part of development
- The person becomes more aware of:
  - How much time has gone by
  - How much time is left
- The future focus of early adulthood gives way to an emphasis on the PRESENT in midlife
- Men interviewed had difficulty reconciling:
  - "The dream" they held about the future
  - The reality they actually experienced
- Consequently, they felt impatient and were no longer willing to postpone things they had always wanted to do

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

CRITICISMS OF LEVINSON'S RESEARCH
- Although Levinson believed his research demonstrated the existence of a midlife crisis, his study has been criticized for:
  - Small sample size (only 40 men)
  - Similar ages of participants (35–45)
  - Concerns about a cohort effect
- Other research does NOT support his theory of the midlife crisis

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

VAILLANT'S CRITIQUE AND THE HARVARD STUDY
- George Vaillant (2012) believed it was the CROSS-SECTIONAL DESIGN of Levinson's study that led to the erroneous conclusion of an inevitable midlife crisis
- Vaillant believed that LONGITUDINAL STUDIES of an individual's entire life were needed to determine the factors associated with optimum health and potential
- Vaillant was one of the main researchers in the 75-YEAR-OLD HARVARD STUDY OF ADULT DEVELOPMENT
- Vaillant (1977) considered a midlife crisis to be a RARE OCCURRENCE among the participants of the Harvard Study
- Additional findings of this longitudinal study are discussed in the next chapter on late adulthood

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

MACARTHUR FOUNDATION RESEARCH: MIDLIFE WELL-BEING
- Most research suggests that MOST people in the United States today do NOT experience a midlife crisis
- Study: 10-year study conducted by the MacArthur Foundation Research Network on Successful Midlife Development
- Method: Telephone interviews with over 3,000 midlife adults
- Key Finding: The years between 40 and 60 are marked by a sense of WELL-BEING
- Only 23% of participants reported experiencing a midlife crisis
- The midlife crisis tended to occur among the HIGHLY EDUCATED
- It was triggered by a MAJOR LIFE EVENT rather than out of a fear of aging
- Source: Research Network on Successful Midlife Development, 2007

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- Daniel Levinson (1978): "The Seasons of a Man's Life" — based on 40 men ages 35–45
- Levinson's motivating image = "THE DREAM" (career-focused for his male participants)
- Midlife Transition (40–45): reevaluation, urgency, dramatic change, ignored aspirations
- Middle Adulthood (45–50): commitment to new choices made during transition
- Levinson viewed midlife crisis as NORMAL and INEVITABLE
- Criticisms of Levinson: small sample, similar ages, cohort effect, cross-sectional design
- Vaillant (2012): cross-sectional design = flaw; longitudinal studies needed
- Harvard Study of Adult Development = 75-year longitudinal study; Vaillant was a main researcher
- Vaillant (1977): midlife crisis is RARE, not inevitable
- MacArthur Foundation Study: 3,000+ adults, 10 years, ages 40–60 = generally well-being
- Only 23% reported a midlife crisis
- Midlife crisis linked to: HIGH EDUCATION level + triggered by MAJOR LIFE EVENTS (not fear of aging)

notes.title: Leisure & Psychosocial Development in Middle Adulthood | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
LEISURE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

- Leisure = time off from work and duties available to middle-aged adults
- As most developed nations restrict the number of hours an employer can demand that an employee work per week AND require employers to offer paid vacation time, leisure time becomes a meaningful area of study
- Around the world, the most common leisure activity in both EARLY and MIDDLE adulthood is WATCHING TELEVISION (Marketing Charts Staff, 2014)
- On average, middle-aged adults spend 2–3 HOURS PER DAY watching TV (Gripsrud, 2007)
- Watching TV accounts for MORE THAN HALF of all leisure time

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
GENDER AND AGE DIFFERENCES IN LEISURE TIME (U.S.)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

- In the United States, MEN spend about 5 HOURS MORE PER WEEK in leisure activities than women, especially on weekends (Drake, 2013; U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, 2016)
- The leisure gap between MOTHERS and FATHERS is slightly smaller — about 3 HOURS A WEEK — than among those without children under age 18 (Drake, 2013)
- Those aged 35–44 spend LESS TIME on leisure activities than ANY OTHER AGE GROUP (age 15 or older) (U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics, 2016)
- Reason: This age group is more likely to be parents AND still working up the career ladder, so they feel they have less time for leisure

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
U.S. VS. OTHER DEVELOPED NATIONS — LEISURE TIME AND VACATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

- Americans have LESS LEISURE TIME than people in many other developed nations
- There are NO LAWS in many U.S. job sectors guaranteeing paid vacation time (see Figure 8.22: Legally Mandated Time Off)
- Ray, Sanes, and Schmitt (2013): Several other nations provide ADDITIONAL TIME OFF for:
  • Young workers
  • Older workers
  • Shift workers
- In the U.S., those in HIGHER-PAYING JOBS and jobs covered by a UNION CONTRACT are more likely to have paid vacation time and holidays (Ray & Schmitt, 2007)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
DO U.S. WORKERS TAKE THEIR TIME OFF? (Project Time-Off, 2016)
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

- 55% of U.S. workers in 2015 did NOT take all of their paid vacation and holiday leave
- A large percentage of this leave is LOST — cannot be rolled over into the next year OR paid out
- Total of 658 MILLION VACATION DAYS lost in 2015
- Average of 2 VACATION DAYS PER WORKER was lost in 2015

REASONS MOST OFTEN GIVEN FOR NOT TAKING TIME OFF:
  1. Worry that there would be a mountain of work to return to — 40%
  2. Concern that no one else could do the job — 35%
  3. Not being able to afford a vacation — 33%
  4. Feeling it was harder to take time away when you have or are moving up in the company — 33%
  5. Not wanting to seem replaceable — 22%

ADDITIONAL STATISTICS:
- Since 2000, MORE American workers are willing to WORK FOR FREE rather than take allowed time off
- Lack of support from boss and colleagues is often a DRIVING FORCE in deciding to forgo time off
- 80% of survey respondents said they WOULD take time away if they felt they had support from their boss
- Two-thirds reported hearing NOTHING, MIXED MESSAGES, or DISCOURAGING REMARKS about taking time off
- Almost a third (31%) feel they should CONTACT THEIR WORKPLACE even while on vacation

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
BENEFITS OF TAKING TIME AWAY FROM WORK
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

- Several studies have noted the benefits of taking time away from work:
  • REDUCES JOB STRESS BURNOUT (Nimrod, Kleiber, & Berdychevesky, 2012)
  • IMPROVES MENTAL HEALTH (Qian, Yarnal, & Almeida, 2013)
  • IMPROVES PHYSICAL HEALTH (Stern & Konno, 2009), especially if leisure time includes MODERATE PHYSICAL ACTIVITY (Lee et al., 2015)
  • IMPROVES PRODUCTIVITY AND JOB SATISFACTION (Kühnel & Sonnentag, 2011)
  • Helps adults deal with BALANCING FAMILY AND WORK OBLIGATIONS (Lee et al., 2015)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
LEARNING OBJECTIVES: PSYCHOSOCIAL DEVELOPMENT IN MIDDLE ADULTHOOD
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

1. Explain the controversy surrounding the concept of a midlife crisis
2. Explain the sources of stress confronting adults in midlife and the strategies to cope
3. Summarize Erikson's seventh psychosocial task of generativity vs. stagnation
4. Describe the relationships middle-aged adults have with their children, parents, and other family members
5. Describe singlehood, marriage, divorce, and remarriage at midlife
6. Describe the contemporary roles of grandparents
7. Describe friendships at midlife
8. Explain how women are uniquely affected at midlife
9. Explain the role of religion at midlife

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
SOCIOEMOTIONAL CHANGES IN MIDDLE ADULTHOOD — PERCEPTION OF SELF AND TIME
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

- There are many socioemotional changes in how middle-aged adults perceive themselves
- People in their EARLY 20s may emphasize how OLD they are to gain respect or be viewed as experienced
- By the time people reach their 40s, they tend to emphasize how YOUNG they are
  • Example: Few 40-year-olds cut each other down for being too young by saying things like: 'You're only 43? I'm 48!'
- A previous focus on THE FUTURE gives way to an emphasis on THE PRESENT
- NEUGARTEN (1968): In midlife, people no longer think of their lives in terms of HOW LONG THEY HAVE LIVED
  • Instead, life is thought of in terms of HOW MANY YEARS ARE LEFT

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ KEY EXAM POINTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ Most common leisure activity worldwide in early AND middle adulthood = WATCHING TELEVISION (Marketing Charts Staff, 2014)
★ Middle-aged adults watch TV an average of 2–3 HOURS PER DAY; TV = more than HALF of all leisure time (Gripsrud, 2007)
★ U.S. men have ~5 MORE HOURS/WEEK of leisure than women; gap narrows to ~3 hours/week for parents (Drake, 2013)
★ Age group 35–44 has the LEAST leisure time of any group 15+ (BLS, 2016) — attributed to parenting + career climbing
★ 55% of U.S. workers in 2015 did NOT use all their paid time off (Project Time-Off, 2016)
★ 658 MILLION vacation days lost in 2015; average of 2 days per worker lost
★ Top reason for not taking vacation: worry about a mountain of work to return to (40%)
★ 80% would take time off IF they had boss support; 2/3 hear discouraging messages about taking leave
★ Benefits of leisure: reduces burnout (Nimrod et al., 2012), improves mental health (Qian et al., 2013), improves physical health (Stern & Konno, 2009), boosts productivity/job satisfaction (Kühnel & Sonnentag, 2011), helps work-family balance (Lee et al., 2015)
★ NEUGARTEN (1968): Key midlife shift — people stop thinking about years lived and start thinking about YEARS LEFT
★ Erikson's 7th psychosocial task = GENERATIVITY vs. STAGNATION (listed in learning objectives as a key topic)
★ Middle adulthood self-perception shifts from emphasizing age for credibility (early adulthood) to emphasizing youthfulness
★ U.S. has NO federally mandated paid vacation in many job sectors; higher-paying and unionized jobs more likely to include it (Ray & Schmitt, 2007)

notes.title: Work at Midlife | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
WHO IS THE U.S. WORKFORCE?
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- The civilian, non-institutionalized workforce = population aged 16 and older who are employed
- Workforce participation has steadily declined since its peak in the late 1990s
  • Late 1990s peak: 67% of civilian workforce population was employed
  • 2012: dropped to 64%
  • 2022 (projected): expected to decline to 62%
- U.S. population expected to grow more slowly based on census projections
- Adults age 16–24 (new labor force entrants) are the ONLY group projected to SHRINK in size — by nearly half a percent
- Adults age 55+ will GROW by 2.3% over current rates
- Adults age 65–74 will grow by nearly 4%
- Source: Monthly Labor Review (MLR), 2013
- In 1992: 26% of population was 55+
- By 2022 (projected): 38% of population will be 55+

BABY BOOMER CONTEXT:
- In 2002, baby boomers were between ages 38–56 (prime employment group)
- In 2012, youngest baby boomers were 48; oldest had just retired (age 66)
- These changes help explain steady decline in work participation as this large cohort ages out of the workforce

GENDER BREAKDOWN:
- In 2012, 53% of the workforce was male
- For both genders and most age groups, labor force participation declined from 2002 to 2012 and is projected to decline further by 2022
- EXCEPTION: older middle-age groups (baby boomers), especially women 55 and older

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
TABLE 8.8 — PERCENTAGE OF NON-INSTITUTIONALIZED CIVILIAN WORKFORCE EMPLOYED BY GENDER & AGE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
MALES:
- Ages 16–19: 2002=47.5%, 2012=34%, 2022*=27.8%
- Ages 20–24: 2002=80.7%, 2012=74.5%, 2022*=69.9%
- Ages 25–34: 2002=92.4%, 2012=89.5%, 2022*=88.8%
- Ages 35–44: 2002=92.1%, 2012=90.7%, 2022*=90.4%
- Ages 45–54: 2002=88.5%, 2012=86.1%, 2022*=85.1%
- Ages 55–59: 2002=78%, 2012=78%, 2022*=77.8%
- Ages 60–64: 2002=57.6%, 2012=60.5%, 2022*=64.3%
- Ages 16+ totals: 2002=74.1%, 2012=70.2%, 2022*=67.6%

FEMALES:
- Ages 16–19: 2002=47.3%, 2012=34.6%, 2022*=26.7%
- Ages 20–24: 2002=72.1%, 2012=67.4%, 2022*=64.7%
- Ages 25–34: 2002=75.1%, 2012=74.1%, 2022*=73.4%
- Ages 35–44: 2002=76.4%, 2012=74.8%, 2022*=73.3%
- Ages 45–54: 2002=76%, 2012=74.7%, 2022*=74.9%
- Ages 55–59: 2002=63.8%, 2012=67.3%, 2022*=73.3%
- Ages 60–64: 2002=44.1%, 2012=50.4%, 2022*=55.6%
- Ages 16+ totals: 2002=59.6%, 2012=57.7%, 2022*=56%

*Projected rates of employment (adapted from Monthly Labor Review, 2013)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
RACE & ETHNICITY IN THE WORKFORCE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- Hispanic males have the HIGHEST rate of labor force participation
  • 2012 rates for males (ages 16+):
    - Hispanic: 76%
    - White: 71%
    - Asian: 72%
    - Black: 64%
- Among WOMEN (2012 rates):
  • Black women: 58% (most likely to be participating)
  • Hispanic women: ~57%
  • Asian women: ~57%
  • White women: 55%
- Rates for ALL racial and ethnic groups are expected to decline by 2022 (MLR, 2013)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CLIMATE IN THE WORKPLACE FOR MIDDLE-AGED ADULTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
JOB SATISFACTION PEAKS IN MIDDLE ADULTHOOD:
- Multiple studies confirm this peak (Besen, Matz-Costa, Brown, Smyer, & Pitt-Catsouphers, 2013; Easterlin, 2006)
- Reasons for peak in job satisfaction:
  1. Higher wages
  2. Greater involvement in decisions affecting the workplace (transition from worker to supervisor or manager)
  3. Greater effectiveness and productivity after years of experience
  4. At midlife, many adults LOWER their expectations and goals (Tangri, Thomas, & Mednick, 2003)
     - Middle-aged employees may realize they have reached the highest they are likely to in their career
- Outcomes of higher job satisfaction:
  • Lower absenteeism
  • Greater productivity
  • Less job hopping compared to younger adults (Easterlin, 2006)

NEGATIVE ASPECTS OF THE WORKPLACE CLIMATE:
- GLASS CEILING: organizational discrimination in the workplace that limits the career advancement of women
  • Females employed at large corporations are TWICE as likely to quit their jobs as men (Barreto, Ryan, & Schmitt, 2009)
- JOB BURNOUT: becoming disillusioned and frustrated at work
  • American workers may experience more burnout than workers in many other developed nations
  • Most developed nations guarantee by law a set number of paid vacation days (International Labour Organization, ILO, 2011)
  • The United States does NOT guarantee paid vacation days (U.S. Department of Labor, 2016)
- Not all employees are covered under overtime pay laws (U.S. Department of Labor, 2016)

THE 40-HOUR WORK WEEK MYTH:
- The 40-hour work week is described as a MYTH for most Americans
- Only 4 in 10 U.S. workers work the typical 40-hour work week
- The average work week for many is almost a full day longer: ~47 hours
- 39% of workers work 50 or more hours per week (Saad, 2014)
- Americans work more hours per year than most European nations, especially western and northern European nations
- Americans work FEWER hours than workers in some other nations, especially Mexico
- Reference: Figure 8.19 — Average Annual Hours Actually Worked per Worker
- Source: Organisation for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD), 2016

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
CHALLENGES IN THE WORKPLACE FOR MIDDLE-AGED ADULTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
ECONOMIC DOWNTURNS:
- Middle-aged adults challenged by economic recessions starting in 2001 and again in 2008
- 55% of adults reported some problems in the workplace during the most recent economic recession (Pew Research Center, 2010a)
  • Examples: fewer hours, pay cuts, switching to part-time, etc.
- Reference: Figure 8.20 (Pew Research Center, 2010a)

IMPACT BY AGE GROUP:
- Young adults took the BIGGEST hit in terms of levels of unemployment
- Middle-aged adults saw their overall financial resources suffer:
  • Retirement nest eggs disappeared
  • House values shrank
  • Foreclosures increased (Pew Research Center, 2010b)
- Adults age 50–64 reported the recession hit them worse than other age groups

UNEMPLOYMENT CHALLENGES:
- Middle-aged adults who become unemployed are LIKELY TO REMAIN UNEMPLOYED LONGER than those in early adulthood (U.S. Government Accountability Office, 2012)
- Reasons employers may prefer younger workers:
  1. More cost-effective — younger adults start at lower pay scale levels
  2. Hiring a 25-year-old (many work years ahead) vs. a 55-year-old (likely to retire in ~10 years) — longevity consideration (Lachman, 2004)

GLOBAL AND TECHNOLOGICAL COMPETITION:
- American workers compete with global markets and rapid changes in technology
- Those who can keep up with changes OR are willing to relocate (within country or globally) have better chances of finding work
- Decision to move is EASIER for younger people who have fewer obligations to others

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ KEY EXAM POINTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. U.S. civilian workforce participation PEAKED in the late 1990s at 67%; declined to 64% by 2012 and projected 62% by 2022.
2. Adults 16–24 are the ONLY group projected to shrink in the labor force; adults 55+ will grow the most (65–74 by ~4%).
3. In 1992, 26% of population was 55+; by 2022 it is projected to be 38%.
4. In 2012, 53% of the workforce was male.
5. Hispanic males have the highest labor force participation rate (76% in 2012); Black women have the highest rate among women (58%).
6. JOB SATISFACTION PEAKS IN MIDDLE ADULTHOOD — due to higher wages, more decision-making power, greater experience, and lowered expectations.
7. Key researchers: Besen et al. (2013), Easterlin (2006), Tangri, Thomas & Mednick (2003).
8. GLASS CEILING = organizational discrimination limiting career advancement for women; women at large corporations are TWICE as likely to quit as men (Barreto, Ryan & Schmitt, 2009).
9. JOB BURNOUT is more common in the U.S. partly because the U.S. does NOT legally guarantee paid vacation days.
10. Only 4 in 10 Americans work a 40-hour week; average is ~47 hours; 39% work 50+ hours/week (Saad, 2014).
11. Americans work MORE hours per year than most European nations but FEWER than Mexico (OECD, 2016).
12. 55% of adults reported workplace problems during the most recent recession (Pew Research Center, 2010a).
13. Middle-aged unemployed adults remain unemployed LONGER than young adults (U.S. GAO, 2012).
14. Employers may prefer younger workers due to lower starting pay and longer projected work tenure (Lachman, 2004).
15. Baby boomers in 2002 were ages 38–56; in 2012 youngest were 48 and oldest had just retired (66) — this cohort aging out largely explains declining workforce participation rates.

notes.title: Gaining Expertise: The Novice and the Expert | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
GAINING EXPERTISE: THE NOVICE AND THE EXPERT
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

KEY DEFINITIONS
- Expertise: Specialized skills and knowledge that pertain to a particular topic or activity.
- Novice: Someone who has limited experiences with a particular task.
- Everyone develops some level of "selective" expertise in things that are personally meaningful to them (e.g., making bread, quilting, computer programming, diagnosing illness).

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

CHARACTERISTICS OF EXPERT THOUGHT
- Expert thought is often characterized as: INTUITIVE, AUTOMATIC, STRATEGIC, and FLEXIBLE.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

INTUITIVE
- Novices follow particular steps and rules when problem solving.
- Experts can call upon a vast amount of knowledge and past experience.
- As a result, experts' actions appear more intuitive than formulaic.
- Example: A novice cook may slavishly follow a recipe step by step, while a chef may glance at recipes for ideas and then follow her own procedure.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

AUTOMATIC
- Complex thoughts and actions become more routine for experts.
- Experts' reactions appear instinctive over time.
- Expertise allows us to process information faster and more effectively.
- Researcher cited: Crawford & Channon, 2002.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

STRATEGIC
- Experts have more effective strategies than non-experts.
- Example (medical): Both skilled and novice doctors generate several hypotheses within minutes of an encounter with a patient, BUT the more skilled clinicians' conclusions are likely to be MORE ACCURATE.
  - Skilled clinicians generate better hypotheses than the novice.
  - They are able to discount misleading symptoms and other distractors.
  - They hone in on the most likely problem the patient is experiencing.
  - Researcher cited: Norman, 2005.
- Example (academic note-taking): Over time, students do not write down everything the instructor says, but focus on the more central ideas. Experts may develop their own shorthand/short forms for commonly mentioned words, allowing faster and more efficient note-taking compared to a novice academic note taker.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

FLEXIBLE
- Experts in ALL fields are more curious and creative.
- They enjoy a challenge and experiment with new ideas or procedures.
- The only way for experts to grow in their knowledge is to take on more challenging, rather than routine, tasks.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

EXPERTISE TAKES TIME
- Expertise is a long process resulting from experience and practice.
- Researchers cited: Ericsson, Feltovich, & Prietula, 2006.
- Middle-aged adults, with their store of knowledge and experience, are likely to find that when faced with a problem, they have likely faced something similar before.
  - This allows them to IGNORE the irrelevant and FOCUS on the important aspects of the issue.
- Expertise is one reason why many people often reach the TOP OF THEIR CAREER in middle adulthood.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

LIMITATIONS OF EXPERTISE WITH AGING
- Expertise CANNOT fully make up for all losses in general cognitive functioning as we age.
- The superior performance of older adults in comparison to younger novices appears to be TASK SPECIFIC.
  - Researcher cited: Charness & Krampe, 2006.
- As we age, we need to be MORE DELIBERATE in our practice of skills in order to maintain them.
- Charness & Krampe (2006) — in their review of the literature on aging and expertise — note that the RATE OF RETURN for effort diminishes as we age.
  - In other words: Increasing practice does NOT recoup the same advances in older adults as similar efforts do at younger ages.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- Expertise = specialized skills/knowledge in a particular area; Novice = limited experience.
- Four characteristics of expert thought: Intuitive, Automatic, Strategic, Flexible.
- INTUITIVE: Experts draw on past experience rather than following rigid steps (novice cook vs. chef example).
- AUTOMATIC: Expertise speeds up information processing — Crawford & Channon, 2002.
- STRATEGIC: Experts generate more accurate hypotheses; they discount distractors (medical example — Norman, 2005).
- FLEXIBLE: Experts are more curious and creative; must seek challenging (not routine) tasks to continue growing.
- Expertise is a LONG, EXPERIENCE-BASED process — Ericsson, Feltovich, & Prietula, 2006.
- Many people reach career peaks in MIDDLE ADULTHOOD due to accumulated expertise.
- Expertise is TASK SPECIFIC in older adults — Charness & Krampe, 2006.
- With aging: must be more DELIBERATE in practicing skills; rate of return for practice effort DIMINISHES with age.
- Expertise cannot FULLY compensate for general age-related cognitive decline.

notes.title: Middle Adults Returning to Education | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
MIDDLE ADULTS RETURNING TO EDUCATION
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

ENROLLMENT TRENDS
- Midlife adults in the United States are frequently found in college classrooms
- The rate of enrollment for older Americans entering college is rising FASTER than traditionally aged students
- Older adults often enroll part-time or in the evenings
- Students over age 35 accounted for 17% of all college and graduate students in 2009
- Students over age 35 are expected to comprise 19% of all college and graduate students by 2020
- Source: Holland, 2014

REASONS FOR RETURNING TO EDUCATION
- Developing skills and expertise to launch a SECOND CAREER
- Taking their existing career in a NEW DIRECTION
- Sharpening particular skills
- Retooling and reentering the workplace
- Pursuing interests that have previously been neglected

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

HOW OLDER STUDENTS APPROACH LEARNING DIFFERENTLY
- Older students tend to approach the learning process DIFFERENTLY than younger college students
- Source: Knowles, Holton, & Swanson, 1998

COGNITIVE MECHANICS AND DECLINE
- The mechanics of cognition, such as WORKING MEMORY and SPEED OF PROCESSING, gradually decline with age
- However, these declines can be easily COMPENSATED FOR through the use of higher order cognitive skills
  - Examples of compensation strategies:
    - Forming strategies to enhance memory
    - Summarizing and comparing ideas
    - Relying LESS on rote memorization
- Source: Lachman, 2004

MEMORY RETENTION IN OLDER LEARNERS
- Although older students may take a BIT LONGER to learn material, they are LESS LIKELY to forget it quickly

RELEVANCE AND MEANING IN LEARNING
- Adult learners tend to look for RELEVANCE and MEANING when learning information
- Older adults have the HARDEST TIME learning material that is MEANINGLESS or UNFAMILIAR
- They are more likely to ask themselves: "Why is this important?" when:
  - Being introduced to new information
  - Trying to memorize concepts or facts

TASK-ORIENTED AND PROBLEM-SOLVING FOCUS
- Older adults are more TASK-ORIENTED learners
- They want to organize their activity around PROBLEM-SOLVING

FUTURE GENERATIONAL CHANGES
- These differences between older and younger learners may DECLINE as new generations, equipped with HIGHER LEVELS OF EDUCATION, begin to enter midlife

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

THE PLUS 50 INITIATIVE
- Developed by: The American Association of Community Colleges (AACC) in 2016
- Purpose: To address the educational needs of those OVER 50
- Assists community colleges in CREATING or EXPANDING programs
- Focus: Workforce training and new careers for the PLUS-50 POPULATION

PLUS 50 INITIATIVE — STATISTICS AND REACH
- In operation since 2008
- Has provided grants for programs to 138 COMMUNITY COLLEGES
- Has affected over 37,000 STUDENTS

CAREER FIELDS OFFERED THROUGH PLUS 50 PROGRAMS
- Early childhood educators
- Certified nursing assistants
- Substance abuse counselors
- Adult basic education instructors
- Human resources specialists

WHY THESE PROGRAMS ARE ESPECIALLY BENEFICIAL
- 80% of people over the age of 50 say they will:
  - Retire LATER IN LIFE than their parents, OR
  - CONTINUE TO WORK in retirement, including in a new field

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- Students over 35 = 17% of college/grad students in 2009; projected 19% by 2020 (Holland, 2014)
- Older learner cognition: working memory & processing speed decline BUT can be compensated with higher-order strategies (Lachman, 2004)
- Older students take longer to learn but are LESS LIKELY to forget material quickly
- Key adult learning characteristics: seek RELEVANCE & MEANING; struggle with meaningless/unfamiliar material; ask "Why is this important?"
- Older adult learners are TASK-ORIENTED and PROBLEM-SOLVING focused
- Differences between older/younger learners may decrease as more educated generations enter midlife
- Knowles, Holton, & Swanson (1998) — older vs. younger student learning approach differences
- AACC Plus 50 Initiative: since 2008, 138 community colleges, 37,000+ students
- 80% of adults 50+ plan to retire later or work in retirement (including new fields)
- Career fields targeted: early childhood education, certified nursing assisting, substance abuse counseling, adult basic education instruction, human resources

notes.title: Crystallized versus Fluid Intelligence | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
CRYSTALLIZED VERSUS FLUID INTELLIGENCE
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

OVERVIEW OF INTELLIGENCE AND INFLUENCING FACTORS
- Intelligence is influenced by: heredity, culture, social contexts, personal choices, and age
- Two distinct types of intelligence are noted in adulthood: fluid intelligence and crystallized intelligence

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

FLUID INTELLIGENCE
- Definition: The capacity to learn new ways of solving problems and performing activities QUICKLY and ABSTRACTLY
- Tends to DECREASE with age
- Favors the YOUNG
- Source: Salthouse (2004)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

CRYSTALLIZED INTELLIGENCE
- Definition: The accumulated knowledge of the world we have acquired throughout our lives
- INCREASES with age
- Sources: Horn, Donaldson, & Engstrom (1981); Salthouse (2004)
- These two intelligences are DISTINCT from one another

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

RESEARCH ON CRYSTALLIZED INTELLIGENCE AND OLDER ADULTS
- Older adults have MORE crystallized intelligence as reflected in:
  • Semantic knowledge
  • Vocabulary
  • Language
- Adults generally OUTPERFORM younger people on measures of:
  • History
  • Geography
  • Crossword puzzles
- Source: Salthouse (2004)
- Older adults' advantage of WISDOM comes from:
  • Superior knowledge (crystallized intelligence)
  • Slower and more complete processing style
  • More sophisticated understanding of the workings of the world
- Sources: Baltes, Staudinger, & Lindenberger (1999); Scheibe, Kunzmann, & Baltes (2009)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

CRYSTALLIZED VS. FLUID INTELLIGENCE: PRACTICAL IMPLICATIONS
- Differential changes in crystallized vs. fluid intelligence explain why older adults do NOT necessarily show poorer performance on tasks that also require EXPERIENCE (crystallized intelligence)
- Although older adults show POORER MEMORY overall, experience compensates
- Example: A young chess player may think more quickly, but a more experienced chess player has MORE KNOWLEDGE to draw on

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SEATTLE LONGITUDINAL STUDY
- Has tracked cognitive abilities of adults since 1956
- Every SEVEN YEARS: current participants are evaluated AND new individuals are added
- Approximately 6,000 people have participated thus far
- 26 people from the ORIGINAL group are still in the study today
- Sources: Schaie (2005); Willis & Schaie (1999)

KEY FINDINGS — COGNITIVE ABILITIES THAT INCREASE WITH AGE (until one's 70s):
  1. Verbal memory
  2. Spatial skills
  3. Inductive reasoning (generalizing from particular examples)
  4. Vocabulary
- Middle-aged adults perform BETTER on 4 out of 6 cognitive tasks than those same individuals did when they were YOUNG ADULTS

KEY FINDINGS — COGNITIVE ABILITIES THAT DECLINE IN MIDDLE AND LATE ADULTHOOD:
  1. Numerical computation
  2. Perceptual speed
- See Figure 8.17: Seattle Longitudinal Study ages 25 to 88

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

COGNITIVE SKILLS IN AGING PILOTS (APPLIED RESEARCH)
- Cognitive skills in the aging brain studied extensively in PILOTS
- Results similar to the Seattle Longitudinal Study findings
- Older pilots show DECLINES in:
  • Processing speed
  • Memory capacity
- However, their OVERALL PERFORMANCE seems to remain intact
- Source: Phillips (2011)
- Study details:
  • Pilots tested: age 40 to 69
  • Method: flight simulators
  • Finding: Older pilots took LONGER to learn to use the simulators
  • BUT older pilots performed BETTER than younger pilots at AVOIDING COLLISIONS

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

FLOW
- Definition: The mental state of being COMPLETELY PRESENT and FULLY ABSORBED in a task
- Source: Csikszentmihalyi (1990)
- Characteristics of the flow state:
  • Individual is able to BLOCK outside distractions
  • Mind is fully open to producing
  • Person achieves great JOY or intellectual SATISFACTION from the activity
  • Person is accomplishing a goal
  • Individual is NOT concerned with EXTRINSIC REWARDS
- Csikszentmihalyi (1996) used flow theory to research HIGH LEVELS OF CREATIVITY
  • Believed flow is an IMPORTANT FACTOR to creativity
  • Source: Kaufman & Gregoire (2016)
- Other characteristics of CREATIVE PEOPLE identified by Csikszentmihalyi (1996):
  • Curiosity and drive
  • A value for intellectual endeavors
  • An ability to lose our sense of self and feel a part of something greater
- Csikszentmihalyi believed:
  • The TORTURED CREATIVE PERSON was a MYTH
  • Creative people were very HAPPY with their lives
- According to Nakamura and Csikszentmihalyi (2002):
  • People describe flow as the HEIGHT OF ENJOYMENT
  • The MORE they experience flow, the more they judge their lives to be GRATIFYING
- The qualities that allow for flow are WELL-DEVELOPED in MIDDLE ADULTHOOD

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

TACIT KNOWLEDGE
- Definition: Knowledge that is PRAGMATIC or PRACTICAL and learned through EXPERIENCE rather than explicitly taught
- Also INCREASES WITH AGE
- Source: Hedlund, Antonakis, & Sternberg (2002)
- Also described as:
  • 'Know-how'
  • 'Professional instinct'
- Called TACIT because it CANNOT be codified or written down
- Does NOT involve academic knowledge
- Involves being able to:
  • Use skills
  • Problem-solve in practical ways
- Applicable in the workplace; used by BLUE COLLAR WORKERS, such as:
  • Carpenters
  • Chefs
  • Hair dressers

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- FLUID INTELLIGENCE = capacity to learn new ways of solving problems quickly and abstractly → DECREASES with age; favors the YOUNG
- CRYSTALLIZED INTELLIGENCE = accumulated knowledge of the world → INCREASES with age
- Source for both: Salthouse (2004); Horn, Donaldson, & Engstrom (1981)
- Older adults OUTPERFORM younger adults on history, geography, and crossword puzzles (crystallized advantage)
- 'Wisdom' in older adults = superior knowledge + slower/more complete processing + sophisticated worldview (Baltes et al., 1999; Scheibe et al., 2009)
- Chess example: young player thinks faster; experienced player draws on more knowledge
- SEATTLE LONGITUDINAL STUDY: started 1956; every 7 years; ~6,000 participants; 26 original members remain
- 4 abilities that INCREASE until 70s: verbal memory, spatial skills, inductive reasoning, vocabulary
- 2 abilities that DECLINE in middle/late adulthood: numerical computation, perceptual speed
- PILOT STUDY (Phillips, 2011): older pilots (40–69) slower to learn simulators BUT better at avoiding collisions
- FLOW (Csikszentmihalyi, 1990): complete presence + full absorption in a task; no concern for extrinsic rewards; linked to creativity; flow is the height of enjoyment (Nakamura & Csikszentmihalyi, 2002); tortured creative person = MYTH
- Flow qualities are WELL-DEVELOPED in MIDDLE ADULTHOOD
- TACIT KNOWLEDGE (Hedlund, Antonakis, & Sternberg, 2002): practical, experience-based, cannot be codified; increases with age; 'know-how'; used by blue collar workers (carpenters, chefs, hair dressers)

notes.title: Brain Functioning & Cognitive Development in Middle Adulthood | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
BRAIN FUNCTIONING IN MIDDLE ADULTHOOD
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- The brain at midlife has been shown to NOT ONLY maintain many of the abilities of young adults, but also GAIN NEW ONES
- Some individuals in middle age actually have IMPROVED COGNITIVE FUNCTIONING (Phillips, 2011)
- The brain continues to demonstrate PLASTICITY and rewires itself in middle age based on experiences
- Research has demonstrated that OLDER ADULTS USE MORE OF THEIR BRAINS than younger adults
- Older adults who perform the BEST on tasks are MORE LIKELY TO DEMONSTRATE BILATERALIZATION than those who perform worst
- The amount of WHITE MATTER in the brain, which is responsible for forming connections among neurons, INCREASES INTO THE 50s before it declines

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
EMOTIONAL FUNCTIONING OF THE MIDDLE-AGED BRAIN
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- The middle-aged brain is:
  • CALMER
  • LESS NEUROTIC
  • MORE CAPABLE OF MANAGING EMOTIONS
  • BETTER ABLE TO NEGOTIATE SOCIAL SITUATIONS
  (Phillips, 2011)
- Older adults tend to focus MORE ON POSITIVE INFORMATION and LESS ON NEGATIVE INFORMATION than younger individuals
- Older adults also REMEMBER POSITIVE IMAGES BETTER than younger individuals
- The older adult's AMYGDALA RESPONDS LESS TO NEGATIVE STIMULI
- Adults in middle adulthood make BETTER FINANCIAL DECISIONS — peaks at AGE 53
- Middle adults show BETTER ECONOMIC UNDERSTANDING
- Greater COGNITIVE VARIABILITY occurs among middle adults compared to those both younger and older
- Those in midlife with cognitive improvements tend to be more:
  • PHYSICALLY ACTIVE
  • COGNITIVELY ACTIVE
  • SOCIALLY ACTIVE

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
LEARNING OBJECTIVES: COGNITIVE DEVELOPMENT IN MIDDLE ADULTHOOD
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
1. Describe CRYSTALIZED versus FLUID INTELLIGENCE
2. Describe research from the SEATTLE LONGITUDINAL STUDY
3. Explain the importance of FLOW to creativity and life satisfaction
4. Describe how middle adults are turning to COLLEGE FOR ADVANCED TRAINING
5. Describe the difference between an EXPERT and a NOVICE
6. Describe the changes in the U.S. WORK FORCE, especially among middle adults
7. Explain the importance of LEISURE to mental health and a successful retirement

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
KEY RESEARCHER CITATIONS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- PHILLIPS (2011): Primary researcher cited for brain functioning improvements, emotional regulation, and cognitive gains in middle adulthood

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
★ KEY EXAM POINTS
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━
- The middle-aged brain GAINS new abilities — not just maintains old ones (Phillips, 2011)
- BILATERALIZATION: high-performing older adults are MORE likely to use both sides of the brain
- WHITE MATTER increases into the 50s before declining — supports neural connectivity
- The AMYGDALA in older adults responds LESS to negative stimuli — key for emotional regulation
- Best financial decision-making peaks at AGE 53 in middle adulthood
- POSITIVITY BIAS: older adults focus on, and better remember, POSITIVE information
- Middle adults with cognitive improvements are MORE physically, cognitively, and socially active
- Key cognitive development topics for middle adulthood include: crystallized vs. fluid intelligence, Seattle Longitudinal Study, flow, college re-entry, expert vs. novice, workforce changes, and leisure

notes.title: The Climacteric and Sexuality | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
THE CLIMACTERIC AND SEXUALITY
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SEXUALITY IN MIDDLE AND OLDER ADULTHOOD — OVERVIEW
- Sexuality is an important part of people's lives at ANY age
- Many older adults are very interested in staying sexually active (Dimah & Dimah, 2004)
- Sexual activity does not simply cease with aging; middle adults remain substantially sexually active

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

NATIONAL SURVEY OF SEXUAL HEALTH AND BEHAVIOR (NSSHB) STATISTICS
- Source: Center for Sexual Health Promotion, 2010
- Ages 40–49 engaged in vaginal intercourse during the previous year:
  • 74% of MALES
  • 70% of FEMALES
- Ages 50–59 engaged in vaginal intercourse during the previous year:
  • 58% of MALES
  • 51% of FEMALES
- These percentages indicate that middle adults remain sexually active despite aging

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

AGE-RELATED PHYSICAL CHANGES AFFECTING SEXUAL FUNCTIONING
- Despite continued sexual activity, age-related physical changes CAN affect sexual functioning

CHANGES IN WOMEN:
- Decreased sexual desire (Schick et al., 2010)
- Pain during vaginal intercourse due to menopausal changes (Schick et al., 2010)
- Less vaginal lubrication during arousal, which can affect overall pleasure (Carroll, 2016)

CHANGES IN MEN:
- May require MORE direct stimulation for an erection (Carroll, 2016)
- Erection may be DELAYED or LESS FIRM (Carroll, 2016)
- May experience erectile dysfunction
- May experience medical conditions (e.g., diabetes, heart disease) that impact sexual functioning

ADAPTATIONS FOR COUPLES:
- Couples can continue to enjoy physical intimacy
- May engage in MORE foreplay, oral sex, and other forms of sexual expression
- Less exclusive focus on sexual intercourse as the primary form of intimacy

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

PREGNANCY RISK AND CONTRACEPTION
- Risk of pregnancy CONTINUES until a woman has been without menstruation for at least 12 MONTHS
- Couples should CONTINUE to use contraception until this threshold is met
- Menopause is only confirmed after 12 consecutive months without a period

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SEXUALLY TRANSMITTED INFECTIONS (STIs) IN MIDDLE AND OLDER ADULTS
- People continue to be at risk of contracting sexually transmitted infections at any age
- Examples of STIs mentioned:
  • Genital herpes
  • Chlamydia
  • Genital warts
  • HIV

HIV STATISTICS (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention, 2014e):
- In 2014: 16.7% of the country's NEW HIV diagnoses were among people aged 50 and OLDER
  • That equals 7,391 out of 44,071 new diagnoses
- In 2005: 15.4% of new HIV diagnoses were among people 50 and older
- This represents an INCREASE from 2005 to 2014 (15.4% → 16.7%)

CONDOM USE RATES:
- Adults over age 40 have the LOWEST rates of condom use (Center for Sexual Health Promotion, 2010)
- This low rate of condom use suggests the need to ENHANCE EDUCATION EFFORTS for older individuals regarding:
  • STI risks
  • STI prevention
- Practicing safe sex is important at ANY AGE

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

IMPLICATIONS FOR RELATIONSHIPS AND COMMUNICATION
- When partners understand how aging affects sexual expression:
  • They will be LESS LIKELY to misinterpret physical changes as a lack of sexual interest
  • They will be LESS LIKELY to misinterpret changes as displeasure in the partner
  • They will be MORE ABLE to continue to have satisfying AND safe sexual relationships
- Education and communication are key to healthy sexual relationships in middle and later adulthood

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

KEY RESEARCHERS AND SOURCES CITED
- Dimah & Dimah (2004) — older adults and interest in sexual activity
- Center for Sexual Health Promotion (2010) — NSSHB statistics on sexual behavior and condom use
- Schick et al. (2010) — decreased sexual desire and pain in women due to menopausal changes
- Carroll (2016) — vaginal lubrication changes; male erection changes
- Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (2014e) — HIV diagnoses statistics among adults 50+

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
1. NSSHB (2010): 74% of males and 70% of females aged 40–49 had vaginal intercourse in the past year; 58% males and 51% females aged 50–59 did so.
2. Women's sexual changes with aging: decreased desire, pain during intercourse, reduced vaginal lubrication (Schick et al., 2010; Carroll, 2016).
3. Men's sexual changes with aging: need for more direct stimulation, delayed or less firm erections, possible erectile dysfunction (Carroll, 2016).
4. Pregnancy risk persists until 12 CONSECUTIVE MONTHS without menstruation — contraception still needed.
5. In 2014, 16.7% of new HIV diagnoses were in people aged 50+ (7,391 of 44,071) — up from 15.4% in 2005 (CDC, 2014e).
6. Adults over 40 have the LOWEST rates of condom use — education about STI prevention is critically needed.
7. Understanding aging-related sexual changes helps prevent misinterpretation of these changes as loss of attraction or interest.
8. Couples may shift toward more foreplay, oral sex, and non-intercourse forms of intimacy as adaptations to physical changes.
9. STIs mentioned: genital herpes, chlamydia, genital warts, HIV — all remain risks in middle and older adulthood.

notes.title: Climacteric | Lifespan Development (Lumen Learning)
notes.body:
CLIMACTERIC — OVERVIEW
- The climacteric = the midlife transition when fertility declines
- Biologically based but impacted by the environment
- During midlife, men may experience a REDUCTION in their ability to reproduce
- Women LOSE their ability to reproduce once they reach menopause

━━━

FEMALE SEXUAL AND REPRODUCTIVE HEALTH: PERIMENOPAUSE
- Perimenopause = a period of transition in which a woman's ovaries stop releasing eggs and levels of estrogen and progesterone DECREASE
- Menopause is DEFINED as 12 months without menstruation
- Average age of menopause: approximately 51
- Many women begin experiencing symptoms in their 40s
- These symptoms occur during perimenopause, which can occur 2 to 8 years before menopause (Huang, 2007)
- A woman may first notice that her periods are MORE or LESS frequent than before
- After ONE YEAR without menstruation → woman is considered menopausal and no longer capable of reproduction

━━━

SYMPTOMS OF PERIMENOPAUSE AND MENOPAUSE
- Symptoms are typically caused by DECREASED production of estrogen and progesterone (North American Menopause Society, 2016)
- Shifting hormones → inability to fall asleep
- Declining estrogen → more susceptible to environmental factors and stressors that disrupt sleep

HOT FLASHES:
- A hot flash = a surge of adrenaline that can awaken the brain from sleep
- Often produces sweat and a change of temperature → disruptive to sleep and comfort
- May take time for adrenaline to recede before sleep can occur again (National Sleep Foundation, 2016)

VAGINAL CHANGES:
- Loss of estrogen → vaginal lubrication diminishes, becomes waterier
- Can contribute to PAIN during intercourse
- Vaginal wall becomes THINNER and LESS ELASTIC

BONE HEALTH:
- Estrogen is important for bone formation and growth
- Decreased estrogen → osteoporosis → decreased bone mass

WEIGHT, MOOD, AND DEPRESSION:
- Depression, irritability, and weight gain are OFTEN ASSOCIATED with menopause but are NOT menopausal (Avis, Stellato & Crawford, 2001; Rossi, 2004)
- Weight gain: increase in intra-abdominal fat followed by loss of lean body mass after menopause (Morita et al., 2006)
- Women may need to change their lifestyle to counter weight gain
- Depression and mood swings are MORE COMMON during menopause in women who have PRIOR HISTORIES of these conditions
- Incidence of depression and mood swings is NOT greater among menopausal women than non-menopausal women
- Most American women go through menopause with FEW PROBLEMS (Carroll, 2016)
- Overall, menopause is NOT seen as universally distressing (Lachman, 2004)

NOTE: Figure 8.12 identifies symptoms experienced by women during menopause; women vary greatly in the extent to which symptoms are experienced.

━━━

HORMONE REPLACEMENT THERAPY (HRT)
- Concerns about effects of hormone replacement have changed the frequency with which estrogen replacement and HRT are prescribed
- Estrogen replacement therapy was ONCE COMMONLY USED to treat menopausal symptoms
- More recently, HRT has been associated with:
  • Breast cancer
  • Stroke
  • Development of blood clots (NIH, 2007)
- MOST women do NOT have symptoms severe enough to warrant estrogen or HRT
- If needed: treated with LOWER DOSES of estrogen and monitored with MORE FREQUENT breast and pelvic exams

OTHER WAYS TO REDUCE SYMPTOMS (non-hormonal):
- Avoiding caffeine and alcohol
- Eating soy
- Remaining sexually active
- Practicing relaxation techniques
- Using water-based lubricants during intercourse

━━━

MENOPAUSE AND ETHNICITY
- Symptoms varied greatly across countries, geographic regions, and ethnic groups within the same region (Palacios, Henderson, & Siseles, 2010)

SWAN STUDY (Study of Women's Health across the Nation):
- Examined 14,906 women: white, African American, Hispanic, Japanese American, and Chinese American
- Conducted by Avis et al., 2001
- Controlled for: age, educational level, general health status, and economic stressors

Findings:
- WHITE WOMEN: more likely to disclose symptoms of depression, irritability, forgetfulness, and headaches
- AFRICAN AMERICAN WOMEN: experienced more night sweats (varied across research sites); more vasomotor symptoms overall
- CHINESE AMERICAN and JAPANESE AMERICAN: reported FEWER menopausal symptoms compared to other groups
- Overall: Chinese and Japanese group reported the FEWEST symptoms; White women reported more PSYCHOSOMATIC symptoms; African American women reported more VASOMOTOR symptoms

━━━

CULTURAL DIFFERENCES IN MENOPAUSE EXPERIENCE
- Cultural influences play a role in the way menopause is experienced
- Prevalence of language specific to menopause is an important indicator of the occurrence of menopausal symptoms in a culture

Hmong tribal women (Australia) and Mayan women:
- NO WORD for "hot flashes" in their languages
- Both groups did NOT experience hot flashes (Yick-Flanagan, 2013)
- Hmong women reported lighter or no periods
- Reported NO emotional symptoms
- Found the concept of emotional difficulties caused by menopause AMUSING (Thurston & Vissandjee, 2005)

First Nation women in Canada:
- No single word for "menopause" in Oji-Cree or Ojibway languages
- Women referred to menopause only as "that time when periods stop" (Madden, St Pierre-Hansen & Kelly, 2010)

Perspectives by culture:
- Some women (Western): focus on menopause as a LOSS of youth, womanhood, and physical attractiveness
- Career-oriented women: tend to think of menopause as a LIBERATING EXPERIENCE
- JAPANESE WOMEN: perceive menopause as a transition from motherhood to a more whole person; no longer feel obligated to fulfill certain expected social roles such as the duty to be a mother (Kagawa-Singer, Wu, & Kawanishi, 2002)
- INDIA: 94% of women said they WELCOMED menopause; aging women gain STATUS and PRESTIGE; no longer have to go through menstrual cycle obligations

notes.title: Exercise, Nutrition, and Weight | Lifespan Development (Middle Adulthood)
notes.body:
EXERCISE, NUTRITION, AND WEIGHT — MIDDLE ADULTHOOD

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

THE IMPACT OF EXERCISE

- Exercise is a powerful way to combat the changes we associate with aging.
- Benefits of exercise:
  • Builds muscle
  • Increases metabolism
  • Helps control blood sugar
  • Increases bone density
  • Relieves stress
- STATISTIC: Fewer than half of midlife adults exercise.
- Only about 20% exercise frequently and strenuously enough to achieve health benefits.
- Many people stop exercising soon after they begin a program, particularly those who are very overweight.
- The best exercise programs are those that are engaged in REGULARLY, regardless of the specific activity.
- A well-rounded, easy-to-follow program includes: walking and weight training.
- Having a safe, enjoyable place to walk can make the difference in whether someone walks regularly.
- Weight lifting and stretching exercises at home can also be part of an effective program.
- Exercise is particularly helpful in reducing stress in midlife.
  • Walking, jogging, cycling, or swimming can release tension caused by stressors.
  • Learning relaxation techniques can also have healthful benefits.
- Exercise can be thought of as PREVENTATIVE HEALTH CARE.
- Promoting exercise for the 78 million "baby boomers" may be one of the best ways to reduce health care costs and improve quality of life (Shure & Cahan, 1998).

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

EXERCISE GUIDELINES FOR ADULTS (Office of Disease Prevention and Health Promotion, 2008)

1. Adults should AVOID being inactive. ANY activity will result in SOME health benefits.
2. For SUBSTANTIAL health benefits:
   • At least 150 minutes per week of MODERATE intensity exercise
   • OR at least 75 minutes of VIGOROUS intensity aerobic activity
   • Aerobic activity should occur for at least 10 minutes at a time and preferably spread throughout the week.
3. For MORE EXTENSIVE health benefits:
   • Adults can increase aerobic activity to 300 minutes per week of MODERATE intensity
   • OR 150 minutes per week of VIGOROUS intensity aerobic activity.
4. Adults should also participate in MUSCLE-STRENGTHENING activities:
   • Moderate or high intensity
   • Involve all major muscle groups
   • On TWO OR MORE days per week

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

NUTRITIONAL CONCERNS

- Aging brings about a REDUCTION in the number of calories a person requires.
- Many Americans respond to weight gain by DIETING.
- However, eating less does NOT typically mean eating right.
- People often suffer VITAMIN AND MINERAL DEFICIENCIES as a result of dieting.
- All adults need to be especially cognizant of the amount of SODIUM, SUGAR, and FAT they are ingesting.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

TABLE 8.7 — ESTIMATED CALORIE NEEDS PER DAY, BY AGE, SEX, & PHYSICAL ACTIVITY LEVEL
(Source: Adapted from 2015-2020 Dietary Guidelines for Americans)

MALES:
- Age 36–40: Sedentary 2400 | Moderately Active 2600 | Active 2800
- Age 41–45: Sedentary 2200 | Moderately Active 2600 | Active 2800
- Age 46–50: Sedentary 2200 | Moderately Active 2400 | Active 2800
- Age 51–55: Sedentary 2200 | Moderately Active 2400 | Active 2800
- Age 56–60: Sedentary 2200 | Moderately Active 2400 | Active 2600
- Age 61–65: Sedentary 2000 | Moderately Active 2400 | Active 2600

FEMALES (does NOT include pregnant or breastfeeding women):
- Age 36–40: Sedentary 1800 | Moderately Active 2000 | Active 2200
- Age 41–45: Sedentary 1800 | Moderately Active 2000 | Active 2200
- Age 46–50: Sedentary 1800 | Moderately Active 2000 | Active 2200
- Age 51–55: Sedentary 1600 | Moderately Active 1800 | Active 2200
- Age 56–60: Sedentary 1600 | Moderately Active 1800 | Active 2200
- Age 61–65: Sedentary 1600 | Moderately Active 1800 | Active 2000

ACTIVITY LEVEL DEFINITIONS:
- Sedentary: lifestyle that includes only the physical activity of independent living
- Moderately Active: lifestyle that includes physical activity equivalent to walking more than 1.5 to 3 miles per day at 3 to 4 miles per hour, in addition to activities of independent living
- Active: lifestyle that includes physical activity of walking more than 3 miles per day at 3 to 4 miles per hour, in addition to activities of independent living

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

EXCESS SODIUM

- Dietary guideline: adults should consume LESS THAN 2,300mg (1 teaspoon) per day of sodium.
- American Heart Association (2016): the average sodium intake among Americans is 3,440mg per day.
- PROCESSED FOODS are the main culprits of excess sodium.
- High sodium levels are CORRELATED WITH INCREASED BLOOD PRESSURE.
- Reduction in sodium does show corresponding drops in blood pressure.
- Adults with HIGH BLOOD PRESSURE are strongly encouraged to reduce sodium intake to 1,500mg.
  (Source: USHHS & USDA, 2015)

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

EXCESS FAT

- Dietary guideline: adults should consume LESS THAN 10% of calories per day from SATURATED FATS.
- American Heart Association (2016): optimally, aim for a dietary pattern that achieves 5% to 6% of calories from saturated fat.
  • In a 2,000 calorie diet, that is about 120 calories from saturated fat.
- Average American diet: about 34.3% of diet comes from FAT, with 15.0% from SATURATED FAT (Berglund et al., 1999).
- Diets high in fat:
  • Contribute to weight gain
  • Linked to heart disease
  • Linked to stroke
  • Linked to high cholesterol

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

ADDED SUGAR

- Dietary guideline (USHHS & USDA, 2015): adults should consume LESS THAN 10% of calories per day from ADDED SUGARS.
- Currently, about 15% of calories in the American adult diet come from added sugars.
- That equals approximately 22 teaspoons of sugar per day (NIH, 2014c).
- Excess sugar contributes to:
  • Weight gain
  • Diabetes
  • Other health problems

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

METABOLISM AND WEIGHT GAIN

- One of the most common complaints of midlife adults is WEIGHT GAIN.
- Especially accumulation of fat in the abdomen — often referred to as the MIDDLE-AGED SPREAD (Lachman, 2004).
- Fat distribution by sex:
  • Men: tend to gain fat on upper abdomen and back
  • Women: tend to gain fat on waist and upper arms
- Many adults are surprised by weight gain because their diet hasn't changed — but their METABOLISM HAS SLOWED.
- METABOLISM: the process by which the body converts food and drink into energy.
  • Calories consumed are combined with oxygen to release the energy needed to function (Mayo Clinic, 2014b).
- People who have MORE MUSCLE burn more calories, even at rest → higher metabolism.
- As you get older, the amount of MUSCLE DECREASES.
  • Consequently, FAT accounts for more of one's weight in midlife.
  • This slows down the number of calories burned.
- To compensate, midlife adults must:
  • Increase their level of exercise
  • Eat less
  • Watch their nutrition to maintain their earlier physique

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

OBESITY

- Obesity is a significant health concern for adults throughout the world, especially America.
- Obesity rates continue to increase.
- Being overweight is associated with a myriad of health conditions:
  • Diabetes
  • High blood pressure
  • Heart disease
- NEW RESEARCH: linking obesity to ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE.

CHANG ET AL. (2016) STUDY — Obesity and Alzheimer's Disease:
- Being overweight in midlife was associated with EARLIER ONSET of Alzheimer's disease.
- Study sample: 1,394 men and women who were part of the BALTIMORE LONGITUDINAL STUDY OF AGING.
- Average age of participants: around 60 years old.
- Follow-up period: 14 years.
- Results:
  • People with the HIGHEST BMI at age 50 were more likely to develop Alzheimer's disease.
  • Each ONE-POINT INCREASE in BMI was associated with getting Alzheimer's SIX TO SEVEN MONTHS EARLIER.
  • Those with the highest BMIs also had more BRAIN CHANGES typical of Alzheimer's, even without symptoms of the disease.
- Scientists speculate: FAT CELLS may produce harmful chemicals that promote INFLAMMATION in blood vessels throughout the body, including in the brain.
- Conclusion: A HEALTHY BMI AT MIDLIFE MAY DELAY THE ONSET OF ALZHEIMER'S DISEASE.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

CONCLUDING THOUGHTS

- Many changes in midlife can be easily compensated for (e.g., buying glasses, exercising, watching diet).
- HOWEVER, the percentage of middle adults who have a significant health concern has INCREASED in the past 15 years.

2016 UNITED HEALTH FOUNDATION'S AMERICA'S HEALTH RANKINGS SENIOR REPORT:
- Finding: the NEXT generation of seniors will be LESS HEALTHY than the current seniors.
- Study compared health of middle-aged Americans (50–64 years) in 2014 vs. 1999.
- In the past 15 years:
  • Prevalence of DIABETES increased by 55%
  • Prevalence of OBESITY increased by 25%
- State rankings:
  • Massachusetts ranked FIRST for healthy seniors
  • Louisiana ranked LAST
  • Illinois ranked 36th
  • Wisconsin ranked 13th (higher/healthier)

KEY CONCLUSION (Lachman, 2004):
- Lifestyle has a STRONG IMPACT on the health status of midlife adults.
- Midlife adults who are most likely to enjoy a plateau of good health are those who:
  • Have a strong sense of MASTERY AND CONTROL over their lives
  • Engage in challenging PHYSICAL AND MENTAL ACTIVITY
  • Engage in WEIGHT-BEARING EXERCISE
  • MONITOR THEIR NUTRITION
  • Receive ADEQUATE SLEEP
  • Make use of SOCIAL RESOURCES

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS

1. Only about 20% of midlife adults exercise frequently and strenuously enough to achieve health benefits.
2. Exercise guidelines (ODPHP, 2008): 150 min/week moderate OR 75 min/week vigorous for substantial benefits; 300 min/week moderate OR 150 min/week vigorous for more extensive benefits; muscle-strengthening 2+ days/week.
3. Sodium: recommended <2,300mg/day; Americans average 3,440mg/day; high blood pressure patients should aim for ≤1,500mg/day.
4. Saturated fat: recommended <10% of daily calories; AHA says ideally 5–6%; average American diet = 15% saturated fat.
5. Added sugar: recommended <10% of daily calories; Americans currently consume ~15% (about 22 teaspoons/day).
6. Middle-aged spread = accumulation of abdominal fat; men gain fat on upper abdomen and back; women on waist and upper arms.
7. Metabolism slows in midlife due to decrease in muscle mass; muscle burns more calories at rest.
8. Chang et al. (2016): each 1-point increase in BMI at age 50 = Alzheimer's onset 6–7 months earlier; study used Baltimore Longitudinal Study of Aging, n=1,394, followed 14 years.
9. United Health Foundation (2016): next generation of seniors will be less healthy; diabetes up 55%, obesity up 25% over 15 years.
10. 78 million baby boomers — promoting exercise among them may be one of the best ways to reduce healthcare costs (Shure & Cahan, 1998).
11. Lachman (2004): midlife adults with mastery/control, physical/mental activity, weight-bearing exercise, monitored nutrition, adequate sleep, and social resources are most likely to maintain good health.
12. State health rankings: Massachusetts = #1 healthiest seniors; Louisiana = last; Illinois = 36th; Wisconsin = 13th.

notes.title: Sleep | Lifespan Development (Middle Adulthood) – Complete Study Notes
notes.body:
SLEEP
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

RECOMMENDED SLEEP AMOUNTS FOR ADULTS
- American Academy of Sleep Medicine (Kasper, 2015): Adults require AT LEAST 7 hours of sleep per night to avoid health risks associated with chronic sleep deprivation.
- National Sleep Foundation (2015): Less than 6 hours AND more than 10 hours is NOT recommended for those in middle adulthood.
- Recommended range: 7–9 hours per night.

AMERICAN SLEEP STATISTICS (JONES, 2013 / NATIONAL SLEEP FOUNDATION)
- In 2013: Only 59% of U.S. adults met the 7–9 hour standard.
- In 1942: 84% of U.S. adults met the standard.
- This means 41% of Americans receive LESS than the recommended nightly sleep.
- In 1993: 67% of Americans felt they were getting enough sleep.
- In 2013: Only 56% felt they received as much sleep as needed.
- In 2013: 43% of Americans believed they would feel better with more sleep.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SLEEP PROBLEMS
- Source: Sleep in America poll (National Sleep Foundation, 2015)
- 9% of Americans report being diagnosed with a sleep disorder.
  - Of those diagnosed: 71% have SLEEP APNEA.
  - Of those diagnosed: 24% suffer from INSOMNIA.

PAIN AND SLEEP DEBT
- Pain is a contributing factor in the gap between needed and actual sleep.
- Chronic pain → average of 42 MINUTES of sleep debt per night.
- Acute pain (suffered in the past week) → 14 MINUTES of sleep debt per night.

OTHER CONTRIBUTING FACTORS TO POOR SLEEP
- Stress and overall poor health → key components of shorter sleep durations and worse sleep quality.
- Those in midlife with LOWER life satisfaction → experienced greater DELAY in the onset of sleep compared to those with higher life satisfaction.
- Delayed sleep onset could be the result of WORRY and ANXIETY during midlife.
- Improvements in worry/anxiety should improve sleep onset.
- MENOPAUSE can affect a woman's sleep duration and quality (National Sleep Foundation, 2016).

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

CHILDREN IN THE HOME AND SLEEP (TABLE 8.6)
- Source: 2016 National Center for Health Statistics analysis (CDC, 2016)
- Having children at home DECREASES the amount of sleep an individual receives.
- Having a PARTNER can IMPROVE the amount of sleep for both males and females.
- Table 8.6 illustrates the percentage of individuals NOT receiving seven hours of sleep per night based on parental role.

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

NEGATIVE CONSEQUENCES OF INSUFFICIENT SLEEP
- Sleep deprivation affects physical, cognitive, AND emotional functioning.

IMMUNE SYSTEM & DISEASE RISK
- Sleep deprivation SUPPRESSES immune responses that fight off infection.
- Can lead to: obesity, memory impairment, hypertension (Ferrie et al., 2007; Kushida, 2005).
- Insufficient sleep is linked to increased risk for:
  • Colon cancer
  • Breast cancer
  • Heart disease
  • Type 2 diabetes
  (Pattison, 2015)

STRESS HORMONES & BLOOD PRESSURE
- Lack of sleep → CORTISOL (stress hormone) remains ELEVATED.
- Elevated cortisol keeps the body in a state of ALERTNESS and HYPERAROUSAL.
- Hyperarousal → INCREASES BLOOD PRESSURE.

SLEEP AND LONGEVITY
- Sleep is associated with LONGEVITY.
- Dew et al. (2003): Older adults who had BETTER SLEEP PATTERNS also LIVED LONGER.

DEEP SLEEP AND GROWTH HORMONE
- During DEEP SLEEP: a GROWTH HORMONE is released.
- Growth hormone functions:
  • Stimulates PROTEIN SYNTHESIS
  • Breaks down FAT that supplies energy
  • Stimulates CELL DIVISION
- A decrease in deep sleep → LESS growth hormone released → contributes to PHYSICAL DECLINE seen in aging (Pattison, 2015).

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

SLEEP DISTURBANCES AND GLUCOSE FUNCTIONING IN MIDLIFE WOMEN
- Sleep disturbances can IMPAIR GLUCOSE FUNCTIONING in middle adulthood.

STUDY DETAILS — SWAN (Study of Women's Health across the Nation) Sleep Study
- Participants: Caucasian, African American, and Chinese non-shift-working women.
- Age range: 48–58 years old.
- Exclusion: Not taking insulin-related medications.
- Method: Participants examined at one point, then again approximately 5 YEARS LATER.
- Researchers: Taylor et al. (2016)
- Measures: Body Mass Index (BMI) and INSULIN RESISTANCE at two time points.

KEY FINDINGS (TAYLOR ET AL., 2016)
- Irregular sleep schedules — including:
  • Highly variable BEDTIMES
  • Staying up much LATER than usual
  → Associated with INSULIN RESISTANCE in midlife women.
- Insulin resistance = important indicator of METABOLIC HEALTH, including DIABETES RISK.
- Diabetes risk INCREASES in midlife women; irregular sleep schedules may be an important reason.

MECHANISM: HOW IRREGULAR SLEEP DISRUPTS METABOLISM
- Irregular bedtime schedules expose the body to VARYING LEVELS OF LIGHT.
- Light = the MOST IMPORTANT TIMING CUE for the body's CIRCADIAN CLOCK.
- By disrupting CIRCADIAN TIMING → bedtime variability may IMPAIR:
  • GLUCOSE METABOLISM
  • ENERGY HOMEOSTASIS

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
1. The American Academy of Sleep Medicine (Kasper, 2015) recommends AT LEAST 7 hours of sleep per night for adults; less than 6 or more than 10 hours is not recommended (National Sleep Foundation, 2015).
2. In 2013 only 59% of U.S. adults got enough sleep, compared to 84% in 1942 — a major historical decline.
3. 41% of Americans currently receive LESS than recommended nightly sleep.
4. Of those diagnosed with a sleep disorder: 71% have sleep apnea and 24% have insomnia (National Sleep Foundation, 2015).
5. Chronic pain causes ~42 minutes of sleep debt; acute pain causes ~14 minutes.
6. Menopause, stress, low life satisfaction, and worry/anxiety all negatively affect sleep in midlife.
7. Having children DECREASES sleep; having a partner IMPROVES sleep (CDC, 2016).
8. Insufficient sleep → suppressed immunity, obesity, memory impairment, hypertension, and increased cancer/heart disease/diabetes risk.
9. Cortisol elevation from poor sleep → hyperarousal → elevated blood pressure.
10. Deep sleep releases GROWTH HORMONE → protein synthesis, fat breakdown, cell division; less deep sleep = more physical decline in aging (Pattison, 2015).
11. Dew et al. (2003): Better sleep patterns in older adults = LONGER LIFE.
12. SWAN Sleep Study (Taylor et al., 2016): Irregular bedtimes in midlife women (ages 48–58) → insulin resistance → increased diabetes risk.
13. Light is the MOST IMPORTANT TIMING CUE for the circadian clock; variable bedtimes disrupt this and impair glucose metabolism and energy homeostasis.

notes.title: Digestive Issues | Lifespan Development
notes.body:
DIGESTIVE ISSUES
━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

FIGURE REFERENCE
- Figure 8.9: Digestive System

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

HEARTBURN (ACID INDIGESTION / PYROSIS)
- Also called: acid indigestion or pyrosis
- Definition: A common digestive problem in adults resulting from stomach acid backing up into the esophagus
- Mechanism: Prolonged contact with digestive juices injures the lining of the esophagus and causes discomfort
- More frequent occurrences may indicate GERD (gastroesophageal reflux disease)

NORMAL ESOPHAGEAL FUNCTION vs. GERD:
- Normally: The lower sphincter muscle in the esophagus keeps acid in the stomach from entering the esophagus
- In GERD: This muscle relaxes too frequently, allowing stomach acid to flow into the esophagus

U.S. STATISTICS (American College of Gastroenterology, 2016a):
- 60 million people experience heartburn at least once a month
- 15 million people experience heartburn every day

COMPLICATIONS:
- Prolonged heartburn problems can lead to more serious complications
- Including esophageal cancer — described as one of the most lethal forms of cancer in the U.S.

RISK FACTORS / CONTRIBUTING FACTORS FOR HEARTBURN:
- Eating fatty or spicy foods
- Caffeine
- Smoking
- Eating before bedtime
- Source: American College of Gastroenterology, 2016a

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

GALLSTONES
- Definition: Hard particles that can develop in the gallbladder
- Composition: Fatty materials, bile pigments, and calcium deposits
- Size range: From a grain of sand to a golf ball
- Development timeline: Typically take years to develop; in some people develop over the course of a few months

SYMPTOMS:
- About 75% of gallstones do NOT create any symptoms
- Those that do may cause sporadic upper abdominal pain when stones block bile or pancreatic ducts
- If stones become lodged in the ducts: may necessitate surgery or other medical intervention
- Can become life-threatening if left untreated
- Source: American College of Gastroenterology, 2016b

PREVALENCE (American College of Gastroenterology, 2016b):
- Present in about 20% of women over the age of 55
- Present in about 10% of men over the age of 55

RISK FACTORS FOR GALLSTONES:
- Family history of gallstones
- Diets high in calories and refined carbohydrates (e.g., white bread and rice)
- Diabetes
- Metabolic syndrome
- Crohn's disease
- Obesity — increases cholesterol in the bile, thus increasing the risk of developing gallstones
- Source: NIH, 2013

━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━

★ KEY EXAM POINTS
- Heartburn = stomach acid backing up into the esophagus; also called acid indigestion or pyrosis
- GERD occurs when the lower esophageal sphincter muscle relaxes too frequently
- 60 million Americans experience heartburn at least once a month; 15 million daily
- Prolonged heartburn → risk of esophageal cancer (one of the most lethal cancers in the U.S.)
- Heartburn triggers: fatty/spicy foods, caffeine, smoking, eating before bedtime
- Gallstones are composed of fatty materials, bile pigments, and calcium deposits
- 75% of gallstones are asymptomatic
- Gallstones present in ~20% of women and ~10% of men over age 55
- Gallstone risk factors: family history, high-calorie/refined carb diet, diabetes, metabolic syndrome, Crohn's disease, obesity
- Obesity increases cholesterol in bile → increases gallstone risk
- Blocked bile/pancreatic ducts from gallstones can be life-threatening if untreated
- Key sources: American College of Gastroenterology (2016a, 2016b); NIH (2013)

notes.title: Health Concerns | Lifespan Development — Complete Study Notes
notes.body:
HEALTH CONCERNS
